explanation: The full annual payment is a liability until service is delivered. Each month, $99 moves from Deferred Revenue to Subscription Revenue as the service obligation is fulfilled.

title: Multi-Year Contract — Upfront Payment (Deferral)
description: An enterprise client pays for 3 years of SaaS access upfront at a discounted rate.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, the deferred revenue is split between current (due within 12 months) and non-current. The full $30,000 is earned over 36 months at $833/month.

title: Usage-Based / Metered Billing Revenue
description: Recognizing revenue for API calls, data storage, or message volume consumed during the month.
explanation: Metered revenue is recognized as usage occurs. The invoice is raised at month-end based on actual consumption data from the platform.

title: Professional Services / Implementation Revenue
description: Recognizing revenue from a paid onboarding or implementation engagement.
explanation: Professional services are a distinct performance obligation under IFRS 15 and are recognized separately from subscription revenue. Revenue is recognized as the services are delivered.

title: Setup / Onboarding Fee — Deferral
description: Collecting a one-time setup fee that must be spread over the expected customer lifetime.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, a setup fee is not a separate performance obligation if it doesn't transfer a distinct service to the customer. It is deferred and recognized over the expected subscription period.

title: Mid-Term Upsell — Contract Modification
description: A customer upgrades from Basic to Enterprise mid-contract; the contract is modified to reflect the new price.
explanation: A contract modification that adds distinct services is treated as a new contract. The old deferred balance is closed and a new one opened at the upgraded price. The customer pays the incremental difference.

title: Free Trial Conversion to Paid
description: A free trial user enters credit card details and converts to a paying subscriber.
explanation: No revenue is recorded during a free trial. Revenue recognition begins at the moment the first paid billing cycle starts and payment is collected.

title: Enterprise Contract — Milestone-Based Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue for an enterprise deal where go-live is a contractual milestone for revenue.
explanation: For some enterprise contracts, revenue is gated by specific milestones (e.g., customer go-live). The full prepaid amount stays deferred until the milestone is achieved.

title: Volume Discount / Tier Pricing Adjustment
description: Issuing a credit note when a customer reaches a usage tier that qualifies for a retroactive discount.
explanation: Volume discounts are a form of variable consideration under IFRS 15. If a customer's cumulative usage triggers a lower tier price, a credit is issued for the retroactive difference.

title: Downgrade — Credit Note and Rebill at Lower Price
description: A customer downgrades from a higher to a lower plan; the unused portion of the old plan is credited.
explanation: A downgrade is a contract modification. The excess deferred revenue is either refunded or applied as a credit. Future revenue recognition follows the new lower rate.

title: Sales Commission Expense Accrual
description: Accruing the monthly commission owed to the sales team for deals closed during the period.
explanation: Commission is matched to the period in which the sale was closed, not when the cash is collected from the customer. This follows the matching principle.

title: Sales Commission Capitalized (IFRS 15 / ASC 606)
description: Capitalizing sales commissions as a Contract Cost asset amortized over the customer lifetime.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, incremental costs to obtain a contract (like sales commissions) must be capitalized if they are expected to be recovered. They are then amortized over the expected customer lifetime (e.g., 3 years).

title: Sales Commission — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the capitalized sales commission over the expected customer lifetime.
explanation: A $15,000 commission capitalized over a 3-year customer lifetime is amortized at $417/month. This prevents front-loading expenses in the month of the deal.

title: Paid Digital Advertising (Google / Meta)
description: Recording the monthly spend on paid acquisition channels.
explanation: Paid advertising is expensed in the month the ads run. CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost) is tracked by dividing this spend by the number of new customers acquired.

title: Content Marketing & SEO Spend
description: Paying a content agency and SEO tools for inbound marketing activities.
explanation: Content and SEO are period costs expensed as incurred. While they create long-term brand value, they do not meet the criteria for capitalization under IAS 38.

title: Trade Show / Conference Expense
description: Booking a booth at SaaStr Annual or a regional tech conference for demand generation.
explanation: Conference and event marketing costs are expensed in the period the event occurs. Pre-payments for future events are recorded as prepaid assets.

title: Partner / Reseller Commission Expense
description: Recording commissions earned by a channel partner for referring or reselling the SaaS product.
explanation: Reseller commissions are matched to the period of the associated sale. Under IFRS 15, they may also qualify for capitalization as contract costs if incremental and recoverable.

title: Affiliate Commission Payable
description: Accruing commissions owed to affiliate marketers for customer sign-ups generated through their links.
explanation: Affiliate commissions are recognized when the qualifying sign-up occurs, even if the payout is made 30-60 days later. The accrual ensures the expense matches the revenue period.

title: Customer Success Onboarding Cost
description: Recording the internal labor cost of a customer success manager running an enterprise onboarding.
explanation: Onboarding costs are a Cost of Revenue item for SaaS companies. They reduce gross margin and are tracked separately to monitor the efficiency of the customer success function.

title: Customer Churn — Deferred Revenue Released
description: Releasing remaining deferred revenue when a customer cancels at the end of their billing period with no refund.
explanation: If a customer cancels at period end (not mid-period) and is not entitled to a refund, the remaining deferred balance is simply recognized as earned revenue for the final period of service.

title: Reactivation of Churned Customer
description: A previously churned customer reactivates their subscription after a lapse.
explanation: A reactivation is treated as a new subscription. No special accounting is required — it is simply a new revenue event. Tracking reactivation MRR separately is a SaaS-specific KPI practice.

title: Dunning Failure — Bad Debt Write-Off
description: Writing off a subscription receivable after all dunning attempts (automated retries) have failed.
explanation: Failed payment recovery is a unique challenge in SaaS. After exhausting dunning retries, the outstanding amount is written off. This also reverses any previously recognized revenue if still in deferred status.

title: Expected Credit Loss Provision on ARR Receivables
description: Provisioning for potential non-collection of outstanding subscription invoices based on aging and historical data.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, SaaS companies must estimate expected credit losses on their receivables portfolio, especially for enterprise customers with 30-60 day payment terms.

title: Retention Coupon / Discount Offered to At-Risk Customer
description: Offering a 20% discount to prevent a customer who requested cancellation from churning.
explanation: Retention discounts reduce net revenue. They are tracked separately from acquisition discounts to allow LTV (lifetime value) analysis and churn prevention ROI measurement.

title: AWS / GCP Compute Cost — COGS vs Overhead Split
description: Allocating cloud compute costs between Cost of Revenue (serving customers) and R&D (dev/test environments).
explanation: Cloud costs must be split between production (Cost of Revenue — impacts gross margin) and non-production (R&D — below the gross margin line). This split directly affects how investors evaluate the business.

title: CDN & Bandwidth Costs
description: Recording monthly charges for content delivery network and data transfer costs.
explanation: CDN and bandwidth are direct costs of serving customers and belong in Cost of Revenue. They scale with usage and directly affect the gross margin percentage.

title: Third-Party SaaS Tooling (Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid)
description: Recording monthly costs for payment processing, SMS, and email delivery APIs embedded in the product.
explanation: Third-party API costs that are directly consumed when delivering the product are Cost of Revenue. They reduce gross margin and are critical to track per-customer unit economics.

title: Database Hosting Cost (RDS / Mongo Atlas)
description: Monthly charge for managed database services used to power the SaaS platform.
explanation: Managed database services are a core infrastructure cost classified as Cost of Revenue. This keeps hosting costs correctly below the gross profit line.

title: Security & Compliance Tools (SOC2 / ISO 27001)
description: Annual subscription for a security compliance platform and penetration testing services.
explanation: Annual compliance tool subscriptions are prepaid assets amortized monthly. For B2B SaaS, SOC2 compliance is a prerequisite for enterprise sales and is a legitimate Cost of Revenue or G&A cost.

title: Disaster Recovery & Backup Infrastructure
description: Monthly cost of maintaining a backup environment and data replication for business continuity.
explanation: DR infrastructure is a non-negotiable part of running a production SaaS product. It is a Cost of Revenue expense that protects the SLA commitments made to customers.

title: Customer Support Tooling (Zendesk / Intercom)
description: Monthly subscription for the customer support and live chat platform used to manage tickets.
explanation: Support tooling is classified as Cost of Revenue because it is directly used to deliver customer service, which is part of the subscription obligation.

title: Monthly Payroll — Engineering Team
description: Recording the gross salary expense for the software development and infrastructure team.
explanation: Engineering salaries are classified as R&D (Research & Development) expense in SaaS P&Ls, not Cost of Revenue. This is a key distinction for SaaS financial reporting and investor analysis.

title: Monthly Payroll — G&A Team
description: Recording gross salaries for finance, HR, legal, and executive staff.
explanation: General & Administrative (G&A) salaries are operating expenses below the gross profit line. They are tracked separately from Sales & Marketing and R&D to give investors a clear view of the cost structure.

title: Employer Payroll Taxes & Social Contributions
description: Recording the employer's share of payroll taxes and social security contributions.
explanation: Employer payroll taxes are allocated to the same expense line as the related salary (R&D, S&M, or G&A). They are part of the true cost of each employee.

title: New ESOP Grant — Fair Value Determination
description: Recording the fair value of a new batch of stock options granted to employees at grant date.
explanation: At grant date, the total fair value of options (Black-Scholes) is determined and disclosed. No expense is recognized immediately — the total is amortized over the vesting period.

title: ESOP Exercise — Employee Buys Shares
description: Recording the cash received and shares issued when an employee exercises their stock options.
explanation: When an option is exercised, the accumulated APIC from the option grant is reclassified to Common Stock and APIC-Common Stock. Cash is received at the exercise price.

title: RSU Vesting — Monthly Expense
description: Recording the monthly non-cash compensation expense as Restricted Stock Units vest.
explanation: RSUs are valued at the share price on the grant date and expensed straight-line over the vesting schedule (typically 4 years with a 1-year cliff). No cash leaves the company — it is a non-cash equity compensation.

title: Phantom Share / LTIP Cash Accrual
description: Accruing a cash-settled long-term incentive plan for employees who are not eligible for equity.
explanation: Unlike equity-settled SBC, cash-settled schemes (phantom shares, SARs) are recorded as a liability at fair value each month. The liability grows until the payout date.

title: Contractor / Freelancer Invoice
description: Recording a development or design invoice from an external contractor.
explanation: Contractor costs are expensed in the period the work is performed. If the work results in a qualifying software development deliverable meeting capitalization criteria, it may be capitalized instead.

title: R&D Phase Transition — Start of Capitalization
description: The moment a product moves from preliminary research to the application development stage, triggering capitalization of internal costs.
explanation: Under IAS 38 / ASC 350, costs before 'technical feasibility' are expensed. Once the product concept is proven and management commits to a development plan, subsequent costs are capitalized. This entry marks the switch.

title: Capitalized Internal Labor — Development Team
description: Capitalizing the portion of engineering salaries attributable to qualifying software development.
explanation: Engineers spending time on qualifying development work have that time capitalized rather than expensed. Time tracking data is required to support this allocation.

title: Capitalized External Contractor — Software Development
description: Capitalizing a development agency invoice for building a new product feature.
explanation: External development costs for qualifying projects are capitalized in the same way as internal labor. The work must be in the application development stage and the project must be technically feasible.

title: Capitalized Software — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing capitalized development costs over the product's estimated useful life.
explanation: A $250,000 capitalized software asset with a 5-year useful life is amortized at $4,167/month. Amortization begins when the product is available for use, not when development starts.

title: Open Source / Third-Party License Capitalization
description: Capitalizing a perpetual third-party software license used as a component in the SaaS product.
explanation: Perpetual licenses for technology embedded in the product are capitalized as intangible assets. Annual/subscription licenses are expensed monthly as a Cost of Revenue.

title: Seed Round — Cash Received from Investors
description: Recording the cash from a pre-Series A seed funding round.
explanation: Each fundraising round increases cash and equity. The split between par value and APIC depends on the authorized share structure. Seed rounds are often structured as SAFEs or convertible notes before formal equity.

title: Series A Equity Raise
description: Recording the cash and share issuance from a formal priced equity round led by a VC.
explanation: Series A rounds typically issue preferred shares with liquidation preferences. Preferred stock is shown separately from common stock in the equity section of the balance sheet.

title: Convertible Note Issuance
description: Recording cash received from an angel investor via a convertible note with a discount and cap.
explanation: Convertible notes are debt until they convert. They are recorded as a liability at inception. At a future priced round, the note principal (plus any discount / cap benefit) converts to equity.

title: Convertible Note Conversion to Equity
description: Converting the outstanding note balance to preferred shares at the Series A round.
explanation: At conversion, the note (including accrued interest and discount benefit) is reclassified from liability to equity. No cash changes hands — it is a balance sheet reclassification.

title: SAFE Note Received (Simple Agreement for Future Equity)
description: Recording cash received from an investor via a SAFE instrument.
explanation: SAFEs are classified differently depending on their terms. Post-money SAFEs with a fixed cap are often treated as equity instruments (mezzanine section). Pre-money SAFEs may be liabilities. The classification requires legal and accounting judgment.

title: Investor Due Diligence & Legal Costs
description: Recording legal and accounting fees incurred during a fundraising process.
explanation: Due diligence costs incurred before a successful raise are expensed as G&A. If directly attributable to a completed equity raise, they may be offset against the proceeds (deducted from APIC).

title: Transaction Costs on Equity Raise (APIC Deduction)
description: Deducting legal and placement agent fees directly from the equity raise proceeds.
explanation: Under IAS 32, directly attributable costs of issuing equity instruments are deducted from equity. They do not hit the income statement — they reduce the net proceeds received.

title: Monthly Bank Charges & Wire Fees
description: Recording bank account maintenance fees and international wire transfer costs.
explanation: Bank charges are a recurring G&A expense. International SaaS companies often pay higher fees for multi-currency accounts and frequent cross-border transfers.

title: Venture Debt Drawdown
description: Drawing down a venture debt facility from a specialist lender (e.g., Silicon Valley Bank, TriplePoint).
explanation: Venture debt is non-dilutive capital used to extend runway between equity rounds. It typically carries a higher interest rate than bank debt and may include warrants issued to the lender.

title: Venture Debt Monthly Interest
description: Recording monthly interest on an outstanding venture debt facility.
explanation: Venture debt carries a fixed interest rate (typically 8-15%). Interest accrues monthly and is usually paid monthly or at the end of the interest-only period.

title: FX Gain on USD-Denominated Receivables
description: Recording an exchange gain when a USD invoice is collected at a more favorable rate than when it was raised.
explanation: Common for non-US SaaS companies that price in USD. When the local currency weakens against USD, receivables become worth more at settlement, generating an FX gain.

title: FX Loss on USD-Denominated Payables
description: Recording an exchange loss when a USD vendor invoice (AWS, Google) is paid at a less favorable rate.
explanation: Non-US SaaS companies typically pay AWS and other cloud vendors in USD. If the local currency has weakened since the invoice date, they pay more in local currency terms — an FX loss.

title: Office Lease Security Deposit
description: Paying a refundable deposit when signing an office lease.
explanation: Security deposits are assets, not expenses. They are returned at the end of the lease if the premises are in good condition.

title: Prepaid Annual SaaS Tools (Notion, Figma, Slack)
description: Paying for annual subscriptions to productivity tools used by the team.
explanation: Annual tool subscriptions covering more than one month are prepaid assets. Each month, $400 is recognized as G&A expense. This is the same principle as any other prepaid expense.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the estimated tax liability on the company's annual taxable profit.
explanation: Early-stage SaaS companies often have tax losses and pay no income tax. As they reach profitability, a tax provision becomes necessary. Stock-based compensation may create timing differences between book and taxable income.

title: Deferred Tax Asset — Tax Loss Carryforward
description: Recognizing a deferred tax asset for accumulated tax losses that can offset future taxable profits.
explanation: A DTA for tax losses is only recognized if it is probable that sufficient future taxable profits will exist to utilize the losses. For early-stage SaaS, this recognition is often deferred until profitability is foreseeable.

title: R&D Tax Credit Received
description: Recording a government R&D tax incentive or credit for qualifying development expenditure.
explanation: R&D tax credits reduce the effective tax burden for SaaS companies with qualifying development spend. They are recognized when there is reasonable assurance the credit will be received.

title: VAT on B2B SaaS Invoice (Output Tax)
description: Charging 16% VAT on a SaaS subscription invoice to a Jordanian business customer.
explanation: SaaS services supplied to businesses in Jordan attract 16% Sales Tax. The tax is charged on top of the subscription fee and remitted to the ISTD monthly.

title: VAT Reverse Charge on Foreign SaaS (AWS, Google)
description: Self-assessing VAT on cloud service invoices from foreign providers under the reverse charge mechanism.
explanation: In Jordan, a registered SaaS company buying digital services from AWS, Google, or other foreign providers must self-assess VAT. The input and output entries net to zero but both must appear on the return.

title: US Sales Tax Collected (Economic Nexus)
description: Collecting and accruing state sales tax from US-based customers where the company has economic nexus.
explanation: After the South Dakota v. Wayfair ruling, SaaS companies with sufficient sales in a US state must collect and remit state sales tax. Rates vary by state (typically 4-10%).

title: Withholding Tax on Overseas Revenue
description: Recording withholding tax deducted by a foreign corporate customer before remitting payment.
explanation: Some countries require corporate buyers to withhold a percentage (5-15%) of software subscription payments before remitting. The withheld amount is either recoverable under a tax treaty or expensed as a cost of doing business.

title: Warrant Issuance to Venture Debt Lender
description: Recording the fair value of warrants issued to a lender as part of a venture debt package.
explanation: Warrants given to lenders are a form of additional compensation. Their fair value is a debt discount that is amortized to interest expense over the loan term using the effective interest rate method.

title: Warrant Debt Discount — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the warrant-related debt discount into interest expense over the loan period.
explanation: The effective interest rate method is used to amortize the debt discount. This increases the interest expense above the cash coupon rate to reflect the full economic cost of the debt including the warrant.

title: Intercompany Revenue — Parent Licenses to Subsidiary
description: Billing a foreign subsidiary for use of the parent company's SaaS platform on an arm's-length basis.
explanation: Transfer pricing rules require intercompany software licenses to be priced at arm's length. This creates internal revenue for the IP-owning entity and an expense for the subsidiary.

title: Deferred Revenue Roll-Forward (Month-End)
description: Summarizing the movement in deferred revenue — opening balance, new billings, and revenue recognized.
explanation: The deferred revenue roll-forward is a critical SaaS month-end reconciliation. It proves that opening balance + new billings - recognized revenue = closing balance. Auditors scrutinize this schedule closely.

title: MRR Movement Entry (New + Expansion - Churn - Contraction)
description: Summarizing the monthly MRR movements for management reporting purposes.
explanation: This management reporting entry breaks down MRR into its constituent movements — essential for understanding the growth engine of a SaaS business. Investors track New MRR, Expansion, Churn, and Contraction separately.

title: Direct Online Sale — Card Payment (Gross Method)
description: Recording a standard customer order paid by credit card through the website.
explanation: Always record the full sale price as revenue. The payment gateway fee (Stripe, HyperPay) is a separate selling expense. Recording only the net amount understates revenue and distorts gross margin.

title: COD Order — Revenue Recognized at Delivery
description: Recognizing revenue at the moment the courier confirms successful delivery of a cash-on-delivery order.
explanation: Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously at point of delivery. Control passes to the customer when the courier confirms handover, not when the order is placed.

title: COD Courier Settlement — Cash Received from Aramex / DHL
description: Recording the weekly batch payout from the courier company for all COD orders collected.
explanation: The courier deducts their delivery fees before remitting. The receivable is cleared, cash is deposited, and the delivery cost is recognized. Never record only the net as revenue — it was already recognized at delivery.

title: Marketplace Sale — Noon / Amazon.ae (Gross)
description: Recording a product sale made through a third-party marketplace where the store is the principal.
explanation: When the store controls the goods and is the principal (not an agent), the full sale price is recognized as revenue. The marketplace receivable represents money owed before commissions are deducted.

title: Marketplace Commission Deducted from Settlement
description: Recording the platform commission deducted by Noon or Amazon before paying out.
explanation: Marketplace commissions (typically 8-20%) are a selling expense, not a revenue reduction. Recording them separately allows tracking of the true cost of each sales channel.

title: Marketplace Weekly Cash Settlement
description: Receiving the net payout from the marketplace after commissions and fees.
explanation: This clears the marketplace receivable when cash is deposited into the bank account. Revenue and commission were already recorded separately when each sale and deduction occurred.

title: Buy-Now-Pay-Later Sale (Tabby / Tamara)
description: Recording a sale where the customer pays in instalments via a BNPL provider.
explanation: With BNPL, the merchant receives the full amount from Tabby/Tamara (minus a fee). The customer pays the BNPL provider in instalments — that is not the merchant's concern. Revenue is recognized at point of sale.

title: BNPL Merchant Fee Deduction (Tabby / Tamara)
description: Recording the fee charged by the BNPL provider for facilitating the instalment payment.
explanation: BNPL providers charge merchants a fee (typically 3-8%) for the convenience of offering instalment payments. This is a selling expense deducted before the merchant receives their settlement.

title: Digital Product / Download Sale
description: Recording a sale of a digital product (e-book, preset, template) with instant delivery.
explanation: Digital products have no COGS in the traditional sense — no inventory is consumed. Revenue is recognized immediately at point of sale since delivery is instant and the performance obligation is fully satisfied.

title: E-commerce Gift Card Sale
description: Customer purchases a store gift card online.
explanation: A gift card is not a sale — it is a liability. The store owes the holder products worth JOD 100. Revenue is only recognized when the card is redeemed for actual products.

title: Gift Card Redemption — Product Purchase
description: Customer uses a gift card to buy products from the online store.
explanation: Redemption converts the liability to earned revenue and simultaneously recognizes COGS. Both must be recorded at the same time.

title: Gift Card Breakage (Unredeemed)
description: Recognizing revenue from gift card balances statistically unlikely to ever be redeemed.
explanation: Based on historical redemption data, a percentage of gift cards expire unused. Under IFRS 15, this estimated 'breakage' amount is recognized as Other Income using the proportional method over time.

title: Bundle Sale — Revenue Allocation Across Items
description: Splitting the revenue from a discounted product bundle across individual items based on standalone selling prices.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, when selling a bundle containing multiple distinct goods, revenue is allocated based on the relative standalone selling prices of each item — not just split equally.

title: Inventory Purchase from Supplier (Credit Terms)
description: Receiving a batch of products from a supplier on 30-day credit terms.
explanation: Inventory is an asset the moment it arrives at the warehouse regardless of when the supplier is paid. The credit terms create a liability to the supplier.

title: Inventory Purchase — Cash Payment
description: Buying a new product line directly from a manufacturer with immediate cash payment.
explanation: Cash purchases of inventory immediately reduce the bank balance and create an asset. The inventory stays on the balance sheet until it is sold, at which point it becomes Cost of Goods Sold.

title: Landed Cost — Freight, Duty & Clearing Fees
description: Adding the full cost of getting inventory to the warehouse (shipping, insurance, customs, clearance).
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be recorded at its full cost to bring it to its present location and condition. Freight, insurance, import duties, and clearance fees are all capitalized into inventory cost.

title: COGS Recognition — Order Fulfilled
description: Transferring the cost of sold inventory to Cost of Goods Sold when an order ships.
explanation: COGS is recognized at the moment of sale (shipment / delivery). Each order fulfilled reduces inventory and increases the cost recognized on the income statement.

title: Inventory Write-Down to Net Realizable Value
description: Reducing the value of inventory when the selling price falls below its cost (e.g., electronics, fashion).
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be reported at the lower of cost or NRV. For fast-moving e-commerce categories like electronics or fashion, price drops can quickly push NRV below cost.

title: Inventory Shrinkage — Physical Count Adjustment
description: Adjusting the books when the physical warehouse count shows fewer units than the system balance.
explanation: Shrinkage in e-commerce warehouses results from picking errors, damage, or theft. Regular cycle counts identify discrepancies that must be written off to keep the balance sheet accurate.

title: Slow-Moving Inventory Reserve (Provision)
description: Creating a reserve for stock that has not sold in over 180 days and may need to be discounted.
explanation: The prudence principle requires recognizing potential inventory losses before they are confirmed. An allowance account is used so the original cost remains visible in the ledger.

title: Dead Stock Disposal / Write-Off
description: Writing off inventory that is completely unsellable and must be disposed of.
explanation: When physically disposed, the inventory and its associated provision are both removed. If no provision exists, the full cost is expensed directly to Inventory Loss Expense.

title: Consignment Stock Received (Inbound)
description: Receiving products from a vendor for sale — ownership stays with the vendor until sold.
explanation: Consignment stock is NOT your asset — you hold it on behalf of the vendor. It is tracked in a memo or off-balance-sheet account. No purchase is recorded until a sale occurs.

title: Consignment Sale — Revenue and Vendor Payout
description: Recording revenue from selling a consignment item and the liability to the vendor.
explanation: When the item sells, only the store's commission is revenue. The vendor's share is a liability. The consignment memo account is also cleared at this point.

title: Dropshipping — Cost Recognition at Sale
description: Recording revenue and the corresponding supplier cost when a dropshipped order is placed.
explanation: In dropshipping, inventory is never held. When an order is placed, the cost owed to the supplier is recognized immediately as COGS. There is no inventory asset at any point.

title: Kitting / Bundling — Components to Finished Bundle
description: Assembling individual products into a bundled SKU in the warehouse.
explanation: Kitting transfers the cost of individual components into a new bundle SKU. The total inventory value remains unchanged — it is simply reclassified from component items to a bundle item.

title: Outbound Shipping Cost (Courier Expense)
description: Recording the cost of shipping a customer order via a courier company.
explanation: Outbound shipping costs are a selling expense. They are tracked separately from COGS to analyze total fulfillment cost and whether free shipping promotions are economically viable.

title: Shipping Fee Charged to Customer
description: Recording the shipping fee collected from a customer who opted for express delivery.
explanation: When customers pay for shipping, it is recognized as a separate revenue stream. This helps the store measure whether shipping fees cover the actual courier costs.

title: Third-Party Logistics (3PL) Monthly Fee
description: Paying a 3PL warehouse for storage, pick-and-pack, and fulfilment services.
explanation: 3PL costs replace the need for owned warehouse space and staff. They are a key variable cost that scales with order volume and are tracked as part of the total fulfilment cost per order.

title: Warehouse Storage Fee (Per Pallet / Sqm)
description: Monthly charge for storing inventory in a third-party warehouse based on space used.
explanation: Storage fees are a direct cost of holding inventory. High storage costs on slow-moving inventory are a key signal that the product mix needs to be optimized.

title: Pick-and-Pack Fee Per Order
description: Recording the per-order handling fee charged by the 3PL for picking, packing, and preparing shipments.
explanation: Pick-and-pack fees are variable costs scaling with order volume. Tracking this separately from storage allows accurate calculation of the cost-to-serve per order.

title: Prepaid Shipping Label Credit
description: Loading credit onto a shipping account (Aramex, DHL) for label printing.
explanation: Bulk top-ups to a courier account are prepaid assets. The balance is expensed as labels are consumed for each shipment, ensuring the cost is matched to the orders it relates to.

title: Return Shipping Cost Absorbed by Store
description: Recording the cost of a prepaid return label provided to a customer.
explanation: Free returns are a customer retention tool but a real cost. Tracking return shipping separately enables the business to measure the true cost of its returns policy.

title: Delivery Damage Claim Against Courier
description: Recording a receivable from the courier for goods damaged in transit.
explanation: When a courier damages goods in transit and accepts liability, the claim receivable offsets the inventory loss previously recorded. The claim is recognized when the courier confirms acceptance.

title: Customer Return — Product Restocked
description: Processing a return where the product is in perfect condition and goes back into inventory.
explanation: A clean return reverses both the revenue and the COGS. The product re-enters inventory at its original cost. Using Sales Returns (not a direct debit to Revenue) allows tracking of return volume as a KPI.

title: Customer Return — Damaged Product (Not Restocked)
description: Processing a return where the product is damaged and cannot be resold.
explanation: Revenue is reversed but the product cannot go back into inventory. COGS is also reversed, but the cost of the damaged item is recognized as an Inventory Loss expense — the net effect is still a loss on the order.

title: Partial Refund / Goodwill Allowance
description: Issuing a 30% partial refund to a customer who received a slightly damaged item but kept it.
explanation: A partial refund does not reverse the full sale or the COGS — the customer keeps the product. Only the refunded amount reduces revenue as a Sales Allowance.

title: Marketplace-Initiated Refund (Noon / Amazon)
description: Recording when a marketplace automatically refunds a customer and charges the merchant.
explanation: Marketplaces often issue refunds directly to customers and deduct the amount from the merchant's next settlement. The deduction reduces the marketplace receivable balance.

title: COD Return — Order Rejected at Door
description: Recording a COD order that was returned because the customer refused delivery.
explanation: If a COD customer refuses the parcel, the courier returns it. Revenue and COGS are both reversed and inventory is restocked. The courier receivable that was set up at dispatch is also cleared.

title: HyperPay / PayTabs Settlement (Net of Fee)
description: Recording the weekly bank deposit from a regional payment gateway after processing fees.
explanation: Regional payment gateways like HyperPay and PayTabs hold collected funds and settle weekly. The gross receivable is cleared, the fee is expensed, and the net cash hits the bank.

title: PayPal Payout Reconciliation
description: Transferring accumulated PayPal balance to the main bank account.
explanation: The PayPal balance is a separate asset account. When funds are withdrawn to the bank, it is a transfer between asset accounts — not a revenue event. Revenue was recognized at the time of each sale.

title: Currency Conversion Fee (USD to JOD)
description: Recording the bank's fee when converting USD sales proceeds to local currency.
explanation: International e-commerce stores collecting USD must convert to local currency for operations. The conversion spread or fixed fee is a finance cost, not a revenue reduction.

title: Payment Gateway Dispute Hold / Reserve
description: Recording funds held by Stripe or PayPal as a rolling reserve due to high chargeback rates.
explanation: Payment gateways may hold a rolling reserve (typically 5-10% of volume for 90 days) for merchants with elevated chargeback risk. This is still the merchant's money — reclassified as restricted cash.

title: Failed Payment Reversal
description: Reversing a sale when a card payment fails after initial authorization.
explanation: If a payment is initially authorized but subsequently fails (insufficient funds, fraud detection), the revenue entry must be reversed. The order is cancelled and inventory is not released.

title: In-House Instalment Plan — Receivable
description: Recording a sale where the store offers its own 4-instalment payment plan without a BNPL provider.
explanation: When the store itself offers instalment plans, full revenue is recognized at point of sale (control has passed). The receivable is split between current (due in 12 months) and long-term portions.

title: Monthly Ad Spend Recognized (From Prepaid)
description: Moving one month of the prepaid advertising campaign from asset to expense.
explanation: A prepaid ad campaign is released to expense each month as the ads run. This matches the cost to the revenue it generates in each period.

title: Google Shopping / Performance Max Campaign
description: Recording a direct monthly Google Ads invoice for product listing campaigns.
explanation: Pay-per-click and shopping campaign costs are expensed in the month the ads ran. Tracking Google Ads spend separately from social media spend enables channel-level ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) analysis.

title: SMS & Email Marketing Tool Subscription
description: Monthly subscription for a marketing automation platform (Klaviyo, Mailchimp).
explanation: Email and SMS marketing tools are recurring operating costs. They are expensed monthly as a selling & marketing expense.

title: Customer Loyalty Points Earned — Revenue Deferral
description: Deferring a portion of revenue when a customer earns loyalty points on a purchase.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, loyalty points that customers can redeem are a separate performance obligation. The estimated redemption value (5% of sale) is deferred as a liability until points are used or expire.

title: Loyalty Points Redeemed on Purchase
description: A customer applies accumulated points to get a discount on their next order.
explanation: The points liability is converted to revenue when redeemed. The customer pays the difference in cash. Full COGS is recognized on the total sale price.

title: Loyalty Points Breakage (Expired Points)
description: Recognizing revenue from loyalty points that expired without being redeemed.
explanation: Points that expire are an obligation that no longer exists. The liability is released to Other Income. The amount is estimated using historical expiry patterns.

title: Flash Sale / Promotional Discount
description: Recording sales during a Black Friday or Eid sale at a significantly reduced price.
explanation: Recording the full price and a promotional discount separately preserves the gross revenue figure for performance analysis. Management can see that JOD 39.60 per unit was given away in promotions.

title: Referral Reward — Cash Paid to Referrer
description: Paying a customer JOD 10 cash for referring a friend who made a first purchase.
explanation: Cash referral rewards are a customer acquisition cost. They are expensed when paid, not when the referral link was created. They should be tracked against the LTV of referred customers.

title: Warehouse Staff Monthly Payroll
description: Recording gross wages for warehouse pickers, packers, and receiving staff.
explanation: Warehouse labor is classified as a Cost of Revenue (fulfilment cost) in e-commerce P&Ls, not a G&A expense. Tracking it separately enables calculation of the total cost-to-fulfill per order.

title: Customer Service Team Payroll
description: Recording the monthly wages for the customer support and returns team.
explanation: Customer service is either classified as Cost of Revenue or S&M depending on the business model. High return rates increase CS costs significantly — tracking separately enables root cause analysis.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Warehouse Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service benefit for Jordanian warehouse employees.
explanation: EOSB for warehouse staff is part of the fulfilment cost. It must be accrued monthly to avoid a large unexpected liability when employees leave.

title: SSC Employer Share — Warehouse & Ops Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the employer's 14.25% social security contribution for operations staff.
explanation: SSC contributions are part of the true cost of each employee. For warehouse staff, it is a fulfilment cost; for admin staff, it is G&A overhead.

title: Packing Materials & Supplies
description: Recording the cost of boxes, bubble wrap, tape, and branded packaging consumed weekly.
explanation: Packaging materials are a direct fulfilment cost. Branded packaging (custom boxes, inserts, tissue paper) can be a significant expense for lifestyle and beauty e-commerce brands.

title: Shopify / WooCommerce Monthly Subscription
description: Recording the monthly platform fee for hosting the online store.
explanation: E-commerce platform subscriptions are a recurring G&A or technology expense. Unlike infrastructure costs, they are not classified as Cost of Revenue.

title: Annual App / Plugin Subscription
description: Paying for annual subscriptions to essential store plugins (reviews, upsell, loyalty apps).
explanation: Annual plugin subscriptions are prepaid assets amortized at JOD 100/month. Multiple small subscriptions for store apps are a growing cost for Shopify-based businesses.

title: Domain Name & SSL Certificate Renewal
description: Annual payment for domain registration and SSL security certificate.
explanation: Domain and SSL costs are minor annual expenses typically expensed in the month they are renewed. A multi-year domain registration would be prepaid and amortized.

title: ERP / OMS Implementation Cost
description: Capitalizing the cost of implementing an Order Management System or ERP for the e-commerce operation.
explanation: ERP and OMS implementation costs meeting the IAS 38 capitalization criteria are intangible assets. They are amortized over the expected useful life (typically 5-7 years).

title: Monthly Amortization of ERP / Platform System
description: Amortizing the capitalized ERP implementation cost monthly.
explanation: A JOD 35,000 ERP system amortized over 5 years generates JOD 583/month of amortization. This expense is a G&A technology cost.

title: VAT Output Tax on Domestic Sales (Jordan)
description: Recording 16% VAT on taxable product sales to Jordanian customers.
explanation: E-commerce stores registered for VAT in Jordan must charge 16% on taxable goods. Some product categories (basic foods, medicines) are exempt. The tax is collected on behalf of the ISTD.

title: VAT Input Tax on Inventory Purchases
description: Recording recoverable 16% VAT paid to suppliers on inventory purchases.
explanation: VAT paid on inventory is a recoverable input tax — not part of the inventory cost. It is offset against VAT collected on sales when filing the monthly return.

title: VAT on Imported Goods (Customs Border)
description: Recording VAT paid at customs when importing goods into Jordan.
explanation: VAT paid at customs on imported goods is a separate input tax from the supplier invoice VAT. It is recorded as a recoverable asset and offset against VAT output on future sales.

title: VAT Reverse Charge on Digital Advertising
description: Self-assessing VAT on Google, Meta, and TikTok advertising invoices from foreign entities.
explanation: E-commerce businesses buying digital advertising from foreign platforms must self-assess VAT in Jordan. The input and output entries typically net to zero but are required on the VAT return.

title: Monthly VAT Remittance to ISTD
description: Paying the net VAT liability (output minus input) to the Income & Sales Tax Department.
explanation: The net VAT payable = Output VAT collected - Input VAT recoverable. Only the difference is remitted to the ISTD. If input exceeds output, a refund claim is submitted.

title: Customs Duty Capitalized into Inventory
description: Adding customs duties and import fees to the cost of imported inventory.
explanation: Import duties are part of the landed cost of inventory and must be capitalized under IAS 2. They increase the inventory cost and therefore the COGS when items are eventually sold.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the annual income tax on the e-commerce company's taxable profit.
explanation: Income tax is accrued at year-end based on taxable profit. Taxable income may differ from accounting profit due to non-deductible expenses and timing differences.

title: Withholding Tax on Foreign Marketplace Fees
description: Withholding tax when paying commissions to a foreign marketplace (Amazon, Noon) under Jordan's tax law.
explanation: Payments to foreign entities for services (commissions, platform fees) may require withholding tax deduction. Jordan has tax treaties with several countries that reduce or eliminate this obligation.

title: Inventory Financing — Trade Credit Line
description: Drawing down a revolving credit facility to purchase inventory ahead of peak season.
explanation: Inventory financing allows e-commerce businesses to stock up for peak periods (Ramadan, Black Friday) without tying up cash. The facility is repaid as stock sells.

title: Supplier Early Payment Discount
description: Capturing a 2% discount from a supplier for paying within 10 days instead of 30.
explanation: Early payment discounts reduce the effective cost of inventory. The saving is credited back to inventory (perpetual system) as the goods cost less than originally recorded.

title: Merchant Cash Advance — Drawdown
description: Receiving a lump-sum advance against future sales revenue from a fintech lender.
explanation: Merchant cash advances charge a factor rate (e.g., 1.15x) on the amount advanced. The total repayable ($23,000) is the liability; the $3,000 cost is a finance charge amortized as repayments are made.

title: Merchant Cash Advance — Daily Revenue Deduction
description: Recording the daily automatic deduction from sales revenue to repay the merchant cash advance.
explanation: MCAs are typically repaid via a fixed daily percentage of sales. Each repayment reduces the liability and amortizes part of the finance cost. The split between principal and cost follows the effective interest method.

title: Bank Overdraft Utilization — Peak Season
description: Recording inventory payments made when the bank balance goes negative during Ramadan peak stocking.
explanation: When the bank balance goes below zero due to peak inventory purchases, the negative balance becomes a current liability (Bank Overdraft) — not a negative asset.

title: FX Gain on USD Inventory Purchase
description: Recording a foreign exchange gain when a USD supplier invoice is settled at a more favorable rate.
explanation: If the JOD strengthened against USD between the invoice date and payment date, the store pays less in local currency than expected. The saving is an FX gain on the income statement.

title: FX Loss on USD Inventory Purchase
description: Recording a foreign exchange loss when the JOD weakened against USD at payment date.
explanation: Common for Jordanian e-commerce businesses buying products priced in USD. If the dinar weakens before settlement, the store pays more in local currency — an FX loss.

title: Cash Donation Box Collection
description: Recording the cash counted from a collection box at a mosque, church, or public event.
explanation: Cash collected from donation boxes is unrestricted revenue — the donor placed no conditions. It is recognized when counted and verified by two authorized signatories per internal controls.

title: Online Donation — Website / PayPal / Stripe
description: Recording a donation made through the NGO's online giving page, net of platform fee.
explanation: Gross donation is recognized as revenue. The processing fee is a fundraising expense. Recording only the net amount ($97) would understate both revenue and expense.

title: Recurring Monthly Donor — First Charge
description: Recording the first installment from a donor who has set up a monthly standing order.
explanation: A recurring donation pledge does not create a receivable until each payment is due and collectible. Revenue is recognized monthly as each charge succeeds.

title: Recurring Donor Cancellation — Pledge Write-Off
description: Writing off the remaining expected future donations when a monthly donor cancels their commitment.
explanation: If the NGO had previously recognized a multi-period pledge receivable, the uncollected future amounts must be written off when the donor cancels. If no receivable was set up, no entry is needed.

title: Corporate Matching Gift Received
description: Recording an employer matching donation triggered by an employee's personal gift.
explanation: Matching gifts are recorded when received from the employer, not when the employee makes their personal gift. They are typically unrestricted unless the corporation specifies a purpose.

title: Donation Refund to Donor
description: Returning a donation to a donor because the restricted project was cancelled.
explanation: If a restricted project cannot proceed and the donor requests or is entitled to a refund, the previously recognized revenue is reversed and cash is returned.

title: In-Kind Donation — Vehicle
description: Recording a car donated by a supporter for program use.
explanation: A donated vehicle is recorded at its fair market value on the donation date. The asset is then depreciated over its useful life just like a purchased vehicle.

title: In-Kind Donation — Real Estate / Land
description: Recording land donated by a benefactor for the NGO's permanent offices.
explanation: Donated real estate is recorded at appraised fair value. Land is never depreciated. If the donor restricts the property to a specific use, the contribution is permanently restricted.

title: Stock / Securities Donated (Fair Value)
description: Recording publicly traded shares donated by a supporter.
explanation: Donated securities are recorded at their fair market value on the date of the gift (the closing price of the stock). The original cost basis of the donor is irrelevant for the NGO's books.

title: Donated Securities Sold After Receipt
description: Selling donated shares on the same day or shortly after receipt.
explanation: NGOs typically liquidate donated securities immediately per their investment policy. Any difference between the recorded fair value and the sale price is a small gain or loss.

title: Cryptocurrency Donation Received
description: Recording Bitcoin or other crypto received as a donation, valued at fair market value at receipt.
explanation: Under current guidance, donated cryptocurrency is treated as a non-cash gift recorded at fair market value on the donation date. The NGO should convert to cash promptly to avoid price volatility risk.

title: Bequest Received from Estate
description: Recording a cash legacy received following the death of a donor.
explanation: A bequest is recognized as revenue when the probate process is sufficiently complete and collection is virtually certain. If the will is contested, recognition is deferred until the uncertainty is resolved.

title: Government Grant Received Upfront (Full Amount)
description: Receiving a full-year government grant in a single disbursement at the start of the grant period.
explanation: An upfront government grant is deferred as a liability until conditions are met and expenses are incurred. Revenue is recognized as qualifying project expenditure occurs.

title: Grant Milestone Billing — Invoice to Donor
description: Raising an invoice to a grant-making organization when a project milestone is achieved.
explanation: For milestone-based grants, revenue is recognized when the milestone is achieved and verified. The receivable is set up to track the amount due from the grantor.

title: Grant Indirect Cost Recovery (Overhead)
description: Recovering an agreed percentage of direct costs as indirect cost overhead from a grant.
explanation: Many institutional donors allow NGOs to recover a percentage (10-25%) of direct project costs as overhead. This is tracked as a separate revenue line to show cost recovery against actual overhead incurred.

title: Grant Close-Out — Unused Funds Returned
description: Returning the unspent balance of a grant to the donor at project close.
explanation: Unused grant funds must be returned to the donor unless the grant agreement permits carrying forward. The deferred liability is cleared and cash is refunded.

title: Grant Audit Finding — Disallowed Cost
description: Recording the repayment of a grant amount disallowed by the donor's auditor.
explanation: If an auditor disallows a cost (e.g., deemed not eligible under the grant), the NGO must repay it. This is a current-period expense — previously recognized grant revenue is not reversed.

title: Cost-Share / Matching Contribution (NGO's Own Funds)
description: Recording the NGO's own matching contribution required as a condition of a donor grant.
explanation: When the NGO is required to contribute a matching amount, both the expense and a corresponding revenue entry are recorded to satisfy grant reporting requirements. The net effect on the income statement is zero.

title: Multi-Donor Pooled Fund Contribution
description: Receiving a contribution into a pooled fund managed on behalf of multiple donors.
explanation: Pooled funds are managed as a single portfolio. Each donor's contribution is tracked individually in subsidiary records, but all flows through one restricted revenue account on the statement of activities.

title: Grant Receivable Written Off (Non-Payment)
description: Writing off a grant receivable when the grantor fails to honour a committed grant.
explanation: If a previously recognized grant receivable becomes uncollectible (donor defaults, project cancelled), it is written off as a bad debt expense in the period the loss is confirmed.

title: Direct Program Expense — Supplies
description: Purchasing educational materials, food kits, or medical supplies for direct program use.
explanation: Direct program supplies are expenses in the period they are distributed to beneficiaries. They are classified under the specific program they serve for functional expense reporting.

title: Beneficiary Cash Transfer (Direct Aid)
description: Distributing cash grants directly to qualifying beneficiaries.
explanation: Cash transfers to beneficiaries are direct program expenses. They are immediately expensed when disbursed. Strong documentation (recipient lists, signatures) is required for grant accountability.

title: Beneficiary Voucher / In-Kind Distribution
description: Issuing food vouchers or commodity packages to beneficiaries.
explanation: Vouchers create a liability when issued and are cleared when redeemed at partner vendors. Physical goods (food parcels, hygiene kits) are expensed at cost when distributed.

title: Staff Field Mission Costs
description: Paying for travel, accommodation, and per diem for staff on a field assessment mission.
explanation: Field missions are direct program costs. Per diem rates are typically set by the organization's travel policy. Advances issued before travel are cleared against actual expenses upon staff return.

title: Program Vehicle Fuel & Maintenance
description: Recording fuel and routine maintenance for vehicles used exclusively for program activities.
explanation: Vehicles used purely for programs are a direct program cost. Shared-use vehicles require cost allocation between program, fundraising, and management based on mileage or usage logs.

title: Training & Capacity Building Program Cost
description: Paying for a workshop, trainer fees, and materials for a beneficiary capacity building program.
explanation: Capacity building is a direct program expense. Trainer fees, venue hire, materials, and travel for participants are all included. Donors often require granular reporting on these costs.

title: Program Equipment Purchased (Grant-Funded)
description: Buying laptops, medical equipment, or solar panels for a specific program.
explanation: Equipment purchased with grant funds is capitalized as a fixed asset. Donors usually require the NGO to track grant-funded assets separately and report on their status.

title: Program Equipment — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation charge on equipment used for program delivery.
explanation: Depreciation of program assets is classified as a program expense, not management overhead. This ensures the full cost of program delivery is captured in functional expense reporting.

title: Monitoring & Evaluation (M&E) Expense
description: Paying for independent monitoring visits, data collection, and program evaluation.
explanation: M&E is a required grant expense for most institutional donors. It is classified as a direct program cost since it supports evidence of impact.

title: Project-Specific Audit Expense
description: Paying for a donor-required financial audit of a specific project.
explanation: When a donor mandates and funds a project audit, the cost is a direct program expense charged to that grant. The audit fee may be partially recoverable as an indirect cost.

title: Monthly Staff Payroll (Gross)
description: Recording the gross salary expense for all NGO staff — program, admin, and fundraising.
explanation: NGOs must allocate payroll across functional categories (Program, M&G, Fundraising) for the Statement of Functional Expenses. The allocation is based on time sheets or activity-based estimates.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — NGO Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service gratuity for Jordanian NGO employees.
explanation: EOSB is allocated across functional categories just like salaries. The provision ensures the liability is built up gradually rather than creating a large unexpected cost when an employee leaves.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — NGO Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the employer's 14.25% Social Security contribution for all Jordanian staff.
explanation: SSC is allocated proportionally across functional categories. The employer share is part of the true cost of employment and must be reflected in grant budgets and donor reporting.

title: Expat Staff Housing Allowance
description: Paying the monthly housing allowance for an international staff member stationed in Jordan.
explanation: Housing allowances for expat staff are a staff cost classified under the program they serve. International NGOs must include these in grant budgets as they are a significant and donor-scrutinized cost.

title: Volunteer Meal & Transport Reimbursement
description: Reimbursing volunteers for meal and transportation costs incurred while supporting program activities.
explanation: Out-of-pocket reimbursements to volunteers are a legitimate program expense. They must be supported by receipts and are subject to the same approval controls as staff expenses.

title: Consultancy Contract Payment
description: Paying an external consultant for technical expertise on a specific project.
explanation: Consultants provide specific technical skills for defined deliverables. Their fees are a direct program cost charged to the grant or program they support. Withholding tax obligations apply in Jordan.

title: Staff Redundancy / Severance Payment
description: Recording a one-time payment to an employee whose position was eliminated due to grant funding ending.
explanation: When a grant ends, positions are often eliminated. The EOSB provision covers part of the payment; any amount above the provision is an additional expense in the period of termination.

title: Endowment Investment Purchase
description: Investing endowment funds in a diversified portfolio of stocks and bonds.
explanation: Endowment cash is invested to generate returns for operations. The investment is a restricted asset — it remains permanently in the endowment fund. Only the income it generates is available for spending.

title: Endowment Investment Sale (Rebalancing)
description: Selling a portion of the endowment portfolio to rebalance asset allocation.
explanation: Investment rebalancing does not change total endowment value — it exchanges one asset for another. Realized gains remain restricted within the endowment unless the board appropriates them for spending.

title: Endowment Interest Income Earned
description: Recording interest earned on endowment fixed-income investments.
explanation: Interest on endowment investments is restricted — it stays within the endowment fund unless appropriated by the board for operations. It increases the net assets with donor restrictions.

title: Endowment Dividend Income
description: Recording dividends received on stocks held in the endowment portfolio.
explanation: Dividends received by the endowment fund are restricted income. They accumulate within the fund until the board decides on the annual spending distribution.

title: Endowment Realized Gain on Investment Sale
description: Recording a capital gain when endowment stocks are sold above their original cost.
explanation: Realized gains on endowment investments are classified based on donor restrictions. Without specific donor instruction, most jurisdictions treat realized gains as part of the endowment corpus or temporarily restricted income.

title: Endowment Realized Loss on Investment Sale
description: Recording a loss when an endowment investment is sold below its carrying value.
explanation: Losses reduce the endowment's net asset balance. Persistent losses can make an endowment 'underwater' — where the fair value falls below the original gift amount (historic dollar value).

title: Endowment Annual Spending Distribution to Operations
description: Transferring the board-approved annual spending amount from the endowment to the operating fund.
explanation: The board authorizes an annual spending rate (typically 4-5% of endowment value). The transfer moves cash from the restricted endowment pool to the unrestricted operating account and releases the net asset restriction.

title: Quasi-Endowment Established by Board
description: The board votes to invest a portion of unrestricted reserves as a board-designated quasi-endowment.
explanation: A quasi-endowment is created by the board from unrestricted funds — not from donor restrictions. It can be undesignated by the board at any time, unlike true endowments which are permanently restricted.

title: Annuity Payment to Donor (Split-Interest)
description: Making the periodic annuity payment to a donor who established a Charitable Gift Annuity.
explanation: Each annuity payment reduces the liability recognized when the gift annuity was set up. The liability decreases with each payment until the donor's death, at which point the remaining balance becomes unrestricted.

title: Purchase of Office Equipment
description: Buying computers, printers, and office furniture for the NGO's headquarters.
explanation: Equipment over the capitalization threshold is a fixed asset. The cost is allocated between Management & General and Program based on how the equipment is used.

title: Monthly Depreciation — Office & Program Assets
description: Recording monthly depreciation across all fixed assets used by the NGO.
explanation: Depreciation is allocated across functional categories based on how each asset is used. This ensures the Statement of Functional Expenses reflects the true cost of delivering programs versus running the organization.

title: Donated Asset — Subsequent Depreciation
description: Depreciating a vehicle that was donated and recorded at fair value.
explanation: Donated assets are depreciated the same way as purchased assets — starting from the recorded fair value. A vehicle worth JOD 8,000 with a 5-year life generates JOD 133/month of depreciation.

title: Leasehold Improvement — Program Office
description: Capitalizing fit-out costs for a rented program office.
explanation: Permanent improvements to rented space are capitalized and depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or useful life. For an NGO, these are typically program or management expenses.

title: Capital Campaign Expenditure — Construction Draw
description: Using restricted capital campaign funds to pay a construction contractor.
explanation: As capital campaign funds are spent on the building, the restriction is released. The cash decreases and the CIP asset increases. Restrictions are released in proportion to spending.

title: Asset Write-Off — Fully Depreciated
description: Removing a fully depreciated computer or printer that is no longer in use.
explanation: When an asset is fully depreciated and disposed of, both the cost and accumulated depreciation are removed. No gain or loss arises if the asset has zero book value.

title: Grant-Funded Asset — Deferred Income (IAS 20)
description: Recording a government grant received to buy program equipment as deferred income.
explanation: Under IAS 20, grants for assets are deferred and recognized as income over the same period the asset is depreciated. Each month, a portion of the deferred grant income is released to match the depreciation expense.

title: Month-End Net Asset Reconciliation
description: Verifying that the three net asset classes (Unrestricted, Temporarily Restricted, Permanently Restricted) balance.
explanation: This is a reconciliation check entry, not a transaction. The CFO or Finance Manager verifies that the sum of all three net asset classes equals total assets minus total liabilities at month-end.

title: Time-Based Restriction Release
description: Releasing a time-restricted donation when the specified time period has arrived.
explanation: Some donors specify that funds may only be used after a certain date. When that date arrives, the time-restriction is automatically lifted with this reclassification entry.

title: Correction of Prior Year Misclassification
description: Correcting an error where a restricted grant was incorrectly recorded as unrestricted.
explanation: Net asset misclassifications from prior periods are corrected by adjusting the opening balances. If material, this may require restating prior year financial statements.

title: Foreign Currency Translation — Field Office
description: Translating a field office's monthly activity from USD to JOD for consolidation.
explanation: Field offices report in their local currency. At consolidation, exchange rate differences between the transaction date and reporting date create a cumulative translation adjustment recorded in net assets.

title: Bank Reconciliation Adjustment — Interest Earned
description: Adjusting the general ledger for interest income appearing on the bank statement.
explanation: Bank interest is unrestricted income unless the board has placed restrictions on specific accounts. It is recognized when it appears on the bank statement during reconciliation.

title: Provision for Outstanding Grant Commitments
description: Accruing the estimated future expenditure committed under active grant agreements.
explanation: NGOs with signed grant agreements have future spending obligations. Provisioning at year-end ensures the financial statements reflect the full cost of existing commitments.

title: Accrued Annual Audit Fee
description: Accruing the auditor's fee at year-end before the invoice is received.
explanation: The annual audit relates to the fiscal year just ended. Accruing the fee ensures the expense is matched to the period being audited, not the following year when the invoice arrives.

title: Annual NGO Registration / License Renewal
description: Paying the annual renewal fee to the Ministry of Social Development or relevant authority.
explanation: NGO registration renewals are mandatory operating costs. They are classified under Management & General as they benefit the entire organization rather than a specific program.

title: Statutory Audit Fee Payment
description: Paying the external auditor's invoice for the annual financial statement audit.
explanation: This clears the accrued audit fee liability set up at year-end. The expense was already recognized — this is simply the cash settlement.

title: Legal & Compliance Expense
description: Paying a lawyer for reviewing grant agreements and employment contracts.
explanation: Legal fees for organizational compliance, contract review, and employment matters are Management & General expenses. Legal fees directly related to a specific program would be a program expense.

title: UBIT Tax Actual Payment
description: Paying the Unrelated Business Income Tax accrued in a prior period.
explanation: This clears the UBIT liability recognized when the income was earned. The payment is made to the tax authority — in Jordan, to the ISTD.

title: Withholding Tax on Foreign Grant Receipts
description: Recording withholding tax deducted by a foreign donor before disbursing a grant.
explanation: Some bilateral donors are required to withhold a percentage before disbursing. The NGO recognizes the gross grant as revenue. The withholding is a tax expense unless recoverable under a tax treaty.

title: Purchase — VAT Exempt Status (No Reclaim)
description: Recording a purchase where the NGO cannot reclaim VAT due to its exempt status.
explanation: NGOs with tax-exempt status cannot reclaim input VAT. The full amount including VAT is expensed or capitalized. This is different from commercial entities where VAT is a recoverable asset.

title: Annual Filing Fee — Ministry / Regulator
description: Paying the annual reporting and filing fee to the NGO regulatory authority.
explanation: Annual filing fees are a standard compliance cost for registered NGOs in Jordan. They are classified as Management & General since they benefit the whole organization.

title: Fundraising Gala — Ticket Sales Collected
description: Recording advance ticket sales for an annual fundraising gala.
explanation: Ticket sales before the event are deferred — the performance obligation (providing the event experience) has not yet been met. Revenue is recognized on the night of the event.

title: Fundraising Gala — Event Night Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing ticket revenue and recording event costs on the night of the gala.
explanation: On event night, deferred revenue converts to earned revenue. Direct costs (venue, catering, entertainment) are expensed simultaneously to show the net fundraising proceeds.

title: Online Crowdfunding Campaign Proceeds
description: Recording net proceeds from a GoFundMe or LaunchGood crowdfunding campaign.
explanation: Crowdfunding platforms deduct their fees before disbursing. Gross donations are recorded as revenue and the platform fee is a fundraising expense. Revenue classification depends on whether the campaign specifies a purpose.

title: Peer-to-Peer Fundraising Payout
description: Receiving funds raised by individual volunteer fundraisers on behalf of the NGO.
explanation: P2P fundraisers collect donations on the NGO's behalf. The funds are recognized as contributions when received by the NGO. The fundraiser acts as an agent — their collections are the NGO's revenue.

title: Donor Stewardship Expense
description: Recording costs of donor recognition events, impact reports, and thank-you gifts.
explanation: Donor stewardship costs (annual reports, thank-you events, impact visits) are classified as fundraising expenses. They are essential for donor retention but must be monitored as a percentage of fundraising revenue.

title: Gala Cancellation — Refund to Ticket Holders
description: Refunding ticket holders when an event is cancelled.
explanation: If the event is cancelled, the deferred revenue liability is cleared by refunding ticket holders. No revenue is recognized since the performance obligation will not be fulfilled.

title: Corporate Sponsorship Revenue
description: Recording a corporate sponsor's payment for naming rights or event sponsorship.
explanation: If the sponsor receives something in return (logo placement, speaking slot), it is an exchange transaction — not a contribution. It is recognized as revenue when the benefit is delivered.

title: Fundraising Raffle / Lottery Proceeds
description: Recording ticket sales from a charity raffle event.
explanation: Raffle proceeds are fundraising revenue. The cost of prizes is a fundraising expense. Net proceeds flow to the program or general fund. Local gaming/lottery laws must be observed.

title: Cash Sale at POS (No Card)
description: Recording a straightforward over-the-counter cash sale.
explanation: The most fundamental retail entry. Cash is collected, revenue is recognized, and the VAT element is separated as a liability to be remitted to the ISTD.

title: Debit Card Sale at POS
description: Recording a sale paid by debit card where the bank settles the same day.
explanation: Debit card transactions settle directly to the bank account, net of the POS terminal fee. The full sale price is revenue; the processing fee is a selling expense.

title: Contactless / Apple Pay / Samsung Pay Sale
description: Recording a tap-to-pay transaction at a contactless-enabled POS terminal.
explanation: Contactless payments are processed the same way as card payments. The fee percentage may differ slightly from chip-and-PIN depending on the acquiring bank agreement.

title: Split Payment — Cash and Card
description: A customer pays part of their bill in cash and the rest on a credit card.
explanation: Split payments require recording each payment method separately. Revenue and VAT remain unchanged — only the collection method is split.

title: Layaway Plan — Deposit Received
description: A customer puts an item on layaway by paying a 20% deposit to reserve it.
explanation: A layaway deposit is a liability — the store owes the customer either the goods or their money back. No revenue is recognized until the item is fully paid and collected.

title: Layaway — Final Payment and Revenue Recognition
description: Customer makes the final instalment, collects the item, and revenue is recognized.
explanation: At collection, the deposit liability is cleared, the remaining cash is received, and the full revenue and COGS are recognized together. Control passes at the point of physical handover.

title: Layaway Cancellation — Deposit Forfeited
description: Customer cancels their layaway after the forfeiture period and loses their deposit.
explanation: If the store's policy allows deposit forfeiture after a period of inactivity, the deposit is recognized as Other Income. The item is returned to the sales floor and no COGS is recorded.

title: Trade-In Allowance on Purchase
description: A customer brings in an old item and receives a trade-in discount on a new product.
explanation: The trade-in value is treated as partial payment. The used item enters inventory at its estimated resale value. The full retail price of the new item is the revenue base for VAT.

title: Wholesale / Bulk Sale to Business Customer
description: Selling a large quantity to a business customer at a negotiated wholesale price.
explanation: Wholesale sales are invoiced on credit terms rather than collected at POS. Revenue and COGS are recognized at point of delivery when control transfers to the buyer.

title: Buy Online, Pick Up In Store (BOPIS)
description: Customer orders online and pays upfront; revenue is recognized when they collect in store.
explanation: Payment is collected online but the item has not yet been handed over. Revenue is deferred until the customer collects. If not collected within the policy window, the deferred amount is released to revenue.

title: BOPIS — Customer Collects Item In Store
description: Recognizing revenue when the customer picks up their pre-paid online order.
explanation: Control passes at the point of physical handover. Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously when the customer collects the item.

title: Customer Account / House Account Sale
description: A VIP or corporate customer purchases on a store account settled monthly.
explanation: Store accounts allow trusted customers to purchase and settle at month-end. Revenue is recognized at sale; the receivable represents the amount owed on account.

title: Customer Return — Full Cash Refund
description: A customer returns an item within the return policy window and receives a full cash refund.
explanation: A full return reverses both the revenue and the COGS, and the item is restocked. VAT is also reversed since no taxable supply ultimately occurred.

title: Customer Return — Exchange for Different Item
description: Customer exchanges a returned item for a different product of the same value.

entries.account: Cost of Goods Sold — Item A (Reversal)
Cost of Goods Sold — Item B

explanation: An exchange is two transactions: a return of the original item and a new sale of the replacement. No cash changes hands if the values are equal.

title: Customer Return — Store Credit Issued
description: Customer returns an item but chooses store credit instead of a cash refund.
explanation: Store credit is a liability — the store owes the customer merchandise. Revenue is reversed on the original sale, and the credit balance is tracked until the customer spends it.

title: Customer Return — Damaged Item (Not Restocked)
description: Customer returns a damaged item that cannot be resold.
