explanation: Revenue is reversed but the item cannot return to stock. The COGS reversal moves the cost out of COGS and into Inventory Loss — reflecting the economic reality that the goods are gone with no resale value.

title: Price Adjustment / Allowance (No Return)
description: Issuing a partial refund when a customer finds the same item cheaper elsewhere within the price-match window.
explanation: A price adjustment does not involve returning goods. Only the price difference is refunded. COGS is unchanged since the goods remain with the customer.

title: Sales Return Provision — End of Period
description: Estimating and provisioning for returns expected in the next period on current-period sales.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, variable consideration (expected returns) must be estimated and deducted from revenue. For high-return categories (fashion, electronics), this is material and auditor-scrutinized.

title: Warranty Exchange — No Charge to Customer
description: Replacing a faulty product under warranty at no cost to the customer.
explanation: Warranty replacements are charged against the warranty provision set up at the original point of sale. No revenue or COGS entry is made — the cost was already anticipated in the provision.

title: Staff / Employee Discount Sale
description: Recording a sale where a staff member purchases goods at a 30% employee discount.
explanation: Revenue is recorded at the full retail price. The employee discount is a staff benefit expense, not a revenue reduction. This keeps the gross margin analysis clean and tracks the true cost of the benefit.

title: Senior Citizen / Loyalty Member Discount
description: Recording a sale with a permanent 10% discount for a loyalty programme member.
explanation: Loyalty discounts are contra-revenue — they reduce net sales without affecting the VAT base, which is calculated on the actual amount paid by the customer.

title: Bundle / Multi-Buy Promotion (3 for 2)
description: Customer buys three items in a 3-for-2 promotion, effectively getting one free.

entries.account: Sales Revenue (3 Items at Full Price)

explanation: Recording the promotion as a discount rather than a reduced price preserves the full revenue figure for analytics. Management can see both gross revenue and the cost of the promotion separately.

title: Buy-One-Get-One-Free (BOGO) Sale
description: Customer buys one item at full price and receives a second identical item at no charge.

entries.account: Sales Revenue (2 units at full price)

explanation: BOGO promotions are recorded using the full price for both units. The free item's value is recorded as a discount. COGS reflects both units consumed regardless of the promotional structure.

title: Clearance Sale — Markdown Below Cost
description: Selling end-of-season inventory at a price below cost to clear shelf space.
explanation: When selling below cost, COGS exceeds revenue — a deliberate decision to recover partial value. If an NRV write-down was previously recorded, the COGS entry would be at the written-down value instead.

title: Seasonal Markdown — Inventory Write-Down
description: Writing down winter clothing inventory at season-end to its expected clearance sale price.
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be reported at the lower of cost or NRV. At season-end, when a collection can only be sold at a significant discount, an NRV write-down is required before the next reporting date.

title: Promotional Gift With Purchase (GWP)
description: Giving a free promotional item to customers who spend over a threshold amount.
explanation: The promotional gift is removed from inventory at cost and expensed as a marketing cost. No revenue is recorded for the free item. This preserves the integrity of the COGS margin on the main purchase.

title: Flash Sale / Weekend Promotion Discount
description: Recording high-volume sales during a 24-hour flash sale at 40% off.
explanation: Flash sales are tracked as contra-revenue to preserve the visibility of gross revenue in management reports. This enables calculation of the true revenue foregone during promotional periods.

title: Referral Discount — New Customer First Purchase
description: Giving a new customer a 15% discount because they were referred by an existing customer.
explanation: Referral discounts are a customer acquisition tool. Tracking them separately as contra-revenue allows measurement of the cost-per-acquisition through the referral channel.

title: Loyalty Points Earned — Revenue Deferral
description: Deferring a portion of revenue when a customer earns redeemable points on a purchase.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, loyalty points with standalone value are a separate performance obligation. The estimated redemption value is deferred as a liability until points are used or expire.

title: Loyalty Points Redeemed on Purchase
description: Customer uses accumulated points to get a discount on their next purchase.
explanation: Redemption converts the deferred points liability into earned revenue. The customer pays the balance in cash and the transaction is closed.

title: Loyalty Points Breakage (Expired Points)
description: Recognizing revenue from loyalty points that have expired without being redeemed.
explanation: Points that pass their expiry date without redemption are an obligation that no longer exists. The liability is released to Other Income using historical expiry rate assumptions.

title: Gift Card — Partial Redemption with Cash Top-Up
description: Customer partially uses a gift card and pays the remaining balance in cash.
explanation: The gift card value offsets part of the sale. The remainder is paid in cash. Only the portion of the gift card actually used converts to revenue — the remaining balance stays as a liability.

title: Store Credit Balance Issued to Customer
description: Issuing a store credit note to a customer instead of a cash refund.
explanation: A store credit is a liability — the store owes the customer merchandise or services. Revenue is reversed and the credit balance is held until the customer spends it.

title: Store Credit Redeemed on Purchase
description: Customer uses their store credit balance to pay for a new purchase.
explanation: When credit is redeemed, the liability converts to earned revenue and COGS is recognized. No cash changes hands — the transaction is settled entirely through the credit balance.

title: Inventory Purchase — Cash Payment
description: Buying stock directly from a supplier with immediate cash payment.
explanation: Inventory purchased for cash is immediately an asset at cost. It remains on the balance sheet until sold, when it transfers to Cost of Goods Sold.

title: Inventory Purchase — Credit Terms (30 days)
description: Receiving a stock delivery from a supplier on 30-day credit terms.
explanation: Inventory arrives and is immediately an asset regardless of when the supplier is paid. VAT on the purchase is a separate recoverable asset, not part of the inventory cost.

title: Goods Received — Quantity Discrepancy Claim
description: Raising a debit note when fewer units arrive than stated on the supplier's invoice.
explanation: A quantity discrepancy is resolved by reducing both the inventory asset and the payable by the value of the missing goods. A formal debit note is sent to the supplier.

title: Freight-In Capitalized into Inventory Cost
description: Adding inbound freight charges to the cost of delivered inventory.
explanation: Under IAS 2, all costs to bring inventory to its present location must be included in its cost. Freight-in is capitalized into inventory, increasing COGS when the items are eventually sold.

title: Consignment Inventory — Outbound Shipment to Dealer
description: Sending goods to a dealer's showroom on consignment — ownership is retained by the store.
explanation: Consignment outbound is a transfer between two asset accounts — not a sale. The goods are still the store's property until the dealer makes a sale.

title: Inter-Store Inventory Transfer
description: Moving stock from a warehouse or flagship store to a branch with low inventory.
explanation: Inter-store transfers are internal movements with no P&L impact. Total inventory value is unchanged — it is simply reclassified between locations for stock management purposes.

title: Drop-Shipment — Supplier Ships Directly to Customer
description: Recording a sale where the supplier ships directly and the store never holds the goods.
explanation: In dropshipping, no inventory asset is ever created. Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously at the time of the customer order.

title: Advance Payment to Supplier for Future Stock
description: Paying a supplier in advance to secure a large seasonal stock order.
explanation: Supplier advances are current assets — not expenses. They convert to inventory when the goods are delivered and the supplier's invoice is received.

title: Supplier Advance Settled on Delivery
description: Offsetting the advance against the supplier invoice when goods are received.
explanation: When the goods arrive, the advance converts to inventory. If the actual invoice differs from the advance amount, cash or a payable balances the difference.

title: COGS Recognition — Periodic Inventory System
description: Calculating and recording cost of goods sold at the end of a period using the periodic method.
explanation: Under the periodic system, COGS = Opening Inventory + Purchases - Closing Inventory. The closing inventory figure comes from the physical count. COGS is calculated and recorded as one entry at period-end.

title: COGS Recognition — Perpetual Inventory System (Per Sale)
description: Recognizing cost of goods sold at the point of each individual sale in a perpetual system.
explanation: In a perpetual system (used with barcode/POS systems), COGS is recognized on every sale transaction. The inventory balance is updated in real-time.

title: Inventory NRV Write-Down
description: Writing down the value of inventory when net realizable value falls below cost.
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be reported at the lower of cost or NRV. This is especially relevant for fashion, electronics, and perishable goods where market prices can drop quickly.

title: Inventory Damage — Insurance Claim Receivable
description: Recording stock destroyed by flooding and the corresponding insurance claim.
explanation: The inventory loss and the insurance recovery are recorded separately. The loss is the full inventory value; the claim is what the insurer agrees to pay. Any shortfall is a net loss.

title: Cycle Count Overage — Inventory Surplus
description: Adjusting the books when a physical count finds more inventory than the system shows.
explanation: An overage means the system underestimated stock — perhaps due to a missed receiving entry. The inventory is adjusted upward and the gain is recognized as Other Income.

title: Dead Stock Write-Off (Fully Obsolete)
description: Removing completely unsellable inventory from the books after exhausting all markdown options.
explanation: When dead stock is physically disposed of, both the inventory and any previously established allowance are removed. If no provision exists, the full cost is expensed directly.

title: Monthly Store Rent — Prepaid Release
description: Recognizing one month of rent expense from a prepaid annual rent payment.
explanation: Common in Jordan where landlords require 12 months upfront. The prepaid balance is released at JOD 2,500/month to match the occupancy cost to each trading period.

title: Store Utilities — Monthly Accrual
description: Estimating and accruing electricity and water costs before the bill arrives.
explanation: Under the accrual basis, utilities consumed during the month must be recognized even if the bill arrives in the following month.

title: Store Cleaning & Maintenance Service
description: Monthly payment to a cleaning company and routine store maintenance.
explanation: Routine cleaning and maintenance keep the store presentable and functional. These are operating expenses recognized in the month the service is provided.

title: Store Security System — Monthly Fee
description: Monthly monitoring subscription for the alarm system and CCTV.
explanation: Security monitoring is a necessary overhead for any physical retail store. It protects against theft and reduces shrinkage losses.

title: Shopping Bags & Packaging Materials
description: Purchasing branded shopping bags, tissue paper, and boxes for customer purchases.
explanation: Packaging materials are a selling cost expensed when purchased (or when supplies are consumed). Premium packaging for luxury retail is a meaningful cost tracked for margin analysis.

title: Store Signage & Display Materials
description: Printing seasonal promotional signage and window display materials.
explanation: In-store signage and window displays are marketing costs expensed in the period they are used. Permanent signage above a capitalization threshold would be capitalized as a leasehold improvement.

title: In-Store Music Licensing Fee
description: Annual copyright license fee for playing background music in the retail store.
explanation: Playing music commercially requires a license. The annual fee is a prepaid asset amortized monthly at JOD 50.

title: ATM / Cash Handling Service Fee
description: Paying the bank for cash counting, coin rolling, and change provision services.
explanation: High-volume cash retailers pay banks to count and process cash. This is an operating expense discovered on the bank statement during reconciliation.

title: Sales Floor Staff Monthly Payroll (Gross)
description: Recording the gross wage expense for all floor staff — sales associates, cashiers, supervisors.
explanation: Gross payroll is recognized as an expense in the month earned. The net amount is paid after deducting SSC employee share and any income tax withholding.

title: Commission-Based Staff Payout
description: Paying monthly sales commissions to sales staff based on their individual sales targets achieved.
explanation: Commissions are earned when the sale occurs and matched to that period. They motivate sales performance and are a variable labour cost that scales with revenue.

title: Part-Time / Seasonal Staff Wages
description: Recording wages for temporary staff hired for Ramadan and holiday shopping seasons.
explanation: Seasonal hiring is common in retail. These wages are expensed in the period worked. Short-duration staff typically do not accumulate EOSB entitlements under Jordanian law.

title: SSC Employer Share — Retail Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the employer's 14.25% SSC contribution for retail store employees.
explanation: The employer's SSC contribution is a mandatory labour cost in Jordan. It is recognized in the same period as the related wages expense.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Retail Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service gratuity for permanent retail employees.
explanation: Jordanian labour law requires a gratuity payment when an employee resigns or is terminated. Monthly provisioning avoids a large unexpected cost and ensures accurate period reporting.

title: Staff Uniform Purchase
description: Buying branded polo shirts, aprons, and name badges for all store staff.
explanation: Uniforms are expensed in the period purchased. They are a staff welfare and brand presentation cost classified under HR or selling expenses.

title: Staff Training & Development Cost
description: Paying for customer service, sales technique, and product knowledge training for store staff.
explanation: Training is an operating expense. It improves staff capability but cannot be capitalized as an asset since the company does not own the knowledge.

title: Store Fit-Out / Leasehold Improvement
description: Capitalizing the cost of fitting out a new store location — flooring, shelving, lighting.
explanation: Permanent improvements to a rented store are capitalized as a fixed asset. They are depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or useful life — typically 5-10 years.

title: Leasehold Improvement — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation charge on the store fit-out.
explanation: An JOD 80,000 fit-out over a 5-year lease generates JOD 1,333/month of depreciation. This is a significant fixed cost that must be covered before the store is profitable.

title: POS Terminal Purchase & Setup
description: Buying and setting up point-of-sale hardware for a new store.
explanation: POS terminals are fixed assets capitalized at cost and depreciated over their useful life (typically 3-5 years).

title: POS Terminal Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on POS hardware.
explanation: A JOD 3,500 POS system with a 3-year life generates JOD 97/month depreciation. POS hardware depreciates faster than structural fit-out.

title: Shelving & Display Fixtures Capitalization
description: Capitalizing the cost of branded shelving units, display cases, and gondolas.
explanation: Significant fixtures above the capitalization threshold are fixed assets. Smaller items (hangers, price tags) are expensed immediately as store supplies.

title: Furniture & Equipment Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on store furniture, display cases, and shelving.
explanation: Retail fixtures are depreciated over 5 years (straight-line). The monthly charge is part of the fixed overhead that management must monitor relative to store sales volume.

title: Store Renovation — Capital vs. Expense Decision
description: Repainting the store interior — expensed because it restores rather than enhances.
explanation: Routine repainting is maintenance — it restores the asset to its original condition but does not enhance or extend its life. It is expensed immediately. A full store refit that adds new lighting or extends the layout would be capitalized.

title: Fully Depreciated Fixture Write-Off
description: Removing old display shelving from the books when it is replaced after its useful life.
explanation: When a fully depreciated asset is disposed of, both the cost and its accumulated depreciation are removed. No gain or loss arises since the net book value is zero.

title: VAT Output on Retail Sales (Jordan 16%)
description: Summarizing total VAT collected on retail sales during the month for ISTD filing.
explanation: Net VAT payable = Output VAT (collected from customers) - Input VAT (paid on purchases). Only the net difference is remitted to the ISTD monthly.

title: VAT Input on Inventory Purchases
description: Recording the recoverable VAT paid on inventory purchased from a registered supplier.
explanation: VAT on inventory is a recoverable asset, not a cost. It is offset against VAT collected on sales when calculating the monthly net VAT payment to the ISTD.

title: VAT Monthly Remittance to ISTD
description: Paying the net VAT liability to the ISTD by the filing deadline.
explanation: This is the final step in the VAT cycle — transferring the collected net VAT to the government. Late payment attracts penalties and interest.

title: VAT Exempt Goods Sale (Zero-Rated)
description: Recording the sale of a product category exempt from sales tax in Jordan (e.g., basic foods).
explanation: Exempt goods are sold without adding VAT. No Sales Tax Payable entry is made. Retailers must clearly separate exempt and taxable sales for accurate VAT return filing.

title: Customs Duty on Imported Goods
description: Capitalizing customs duties into the cost of imported inventory.
explanation: Import duties are part of the inventory's landed cost and are capitalized under IAS 2. They increase COGS when items are sold and directly affect the gross margin.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the year-end income tax on the store's taxable profit.
explanation: Income tax is accrued based on taxable profit. Differences between accounting profit and taxable income create deferred tax assets or liabilities.

title: Municipality License & Trade Fee
description: Paying the annual commercial license and municipality fees to operate the retail store.
explanation: Annual licenses are prepaid assets amortized monthly. Without a valid trade license, the store cannot legally operate.

title: Withholding Tax on Rent — Individual Landlord (Jordan)
description: Deducting 5% WHT from rent paid to an individual (non-company) landlord.
explanation: Jordanian law requires a company to deduct 5% from rent paid to an individual landlord and remit it to the ISTD. The landlord receives 95% and the company remits the 5% on their behalf.

title: Monthly Bank & POS Terminal Charges
description: Recording monthly bank account maintenance fees and POS terminal rental charges.
explanation: POS terminal rental and bank service fees are recurring operating costs. They are typically discovered during bank reconciliation.

title: End-of-Day Cash Count and Bank Deposit
description: Transferring the day's cash takings from the store till to the bank night safe.
explanation: The daily deposit transfers cash between two asset accounts — from the physical till to the bank account. This is not a revenue or expense event.

title: Bank Overdraft — Short-Term Working Capital
description: Using the store's overdraft facility to pay suppliers during a slow trading period.
explanation: When the bank balance goes negative, the liability shifts from Accounts Payable to Bank Overdraft. The bank charges interest on the overdrawn balance.

title: Trade Finance / Inventory Credit Line Draw-Down
description: Drawing on a revolving inventory finance facility to fund a large seasonal stock purchase.
explanation: Inventory credit lines allow retailers to stock up for peak seasons without tying up their own cash. The facility is repaid as stock is sold, matching the cash flow to the sales cycle.

title: Supplier Invoice — Early Payment Discount Captured
description: Paying a supplier early within the discount window to capture a 2% settlement discount.
explanation: Early payment discounts reduce the effective cost of inventory. The JOD 200 saving is credited back to inventory under the net method — it lowers COGS when those goods are sold.

title: FX Loss on USD-Priced Imported Goods
description: Recording a foreign exchange loss when the JOD weakens between the supplier invoice date and payment date.
explanation: Jordanian retailers importing goods priced in USD are exposed to FX risk. If the JOD weakens between invoice and payment, the store pays more in local currency — an FX loss on the income statement.

title: Seismic Survey Costs — E&E Asset
description: Capitalizing the cost of a 3D seismic survey conducted during the exploration phase.
explanation: Under IFRS 6, exploration and evaluation expenditures may be capitalized as E&E assets. Seismic surveys generate data about subsurface geology and are a direct exploration cost. The policy choice to capitalize or expense must be consistent and disclosed.

title: Exploratory Drilling — E&E Asset
description: Capitalizing the cost of drilling an exploratory well to test the presence of hydrocarbons.
explanation: Exploratory wells are drilled to test if oil or gas is present. The cost is capitalized pending determination of whether commercially viable reserves exist. If reserves are confirmed, the cost is reclassified to development; if not, it is written off.

title: Dry Hole Write-Off
description: Writing off the cost of an exploratory well that found no commercially viable hydrocarbons.
explanation: A dry hole is an exploration failure. When a well proves non-commercial, all associated E&E costs must be written off immediately. This is one of the highest-risk aspects of oil & gas accounting — explorers may write off many wells before finding a commercial discovery.

title: E&E Asset Reclassified to Development Asset
description: Reclassifying an exploratory well to a development asset after commercial viability is determined.
explanation: Once a discovery is confirmed as technically and commercially viable, the E&E asset is reclassified to PP&E or Intangible assets depending on the nature of the rights. Depletion then commences when production begins.

title: E&E Asset Impairment — Trigger Event
description: Writing down an E&E asset when indicators of impairment exist (e.g., licence expiry, decision to abandon).
explanation: IFRS 6 requires E&E assets to be tested for impairment when facts and circumstances suggest the carrying amount may exceed the recoverable amount — e.g., licence expiry approaching, no plans to develop, or the area is being relinquished.

title: Development Well — Capitalized Cost
description: Capitalizing the drilling and completion costs for a development well in a proven field.
explanation: Development wells are drilled after commercial reserves are confirmed. Their costs are capitalized to the oil and gas property and depleted over the proved developed reserves using the Units of Production (UOP) method.

title: Production Asset Depletion — Units of Production
description: Recording monthly depletion of the oil & gas asset based on barrels produced.
explanation: Unlike straight-line depreciation, oil & gas assets are depleted using UOP: (Cost / Proved Developed Reserves) × Barrels Produced. In a high-production month, depletion is higher; in a low month, it is lower. This matches cost to revenue generation.

title: Lifting Costs — Operating Expense
description: Recording the day-to-day costs of extracting oil from a producing well (lease operating expenses).
explanation: Lifting costs (pumping, maintenance, chemicals, water disposal) are the direct costs of producing oil and are expensed as incurred. They are distinct from development costs which are capitalized. The lifting cost per barrel ($/bbl) is a key operational metric.

title: Production Sharing Contract — Government Take
description: Recording the government's share of production under a Production Sharing Contract (PSC).
explanation: Under a PSC, the company first recovers costs ('cost oil'), then splits the remainder ('profit oil') with the government. The government's share is recorded as an expense, not a tax. The company only recognizes its own share as revenue.

title: Royalty Expense — Government / Land Owner
description: Recording the royalty payable on gross production to the government or mineral rights owner.
explanation: Royalties are typically 10-20% of gross production value, paid to the party that owns the mineral rights. They are expensed as production occurs and represent the cost of accessing the resource.

title: Crude Oil Inventory — Production Recognized
description: Recording crude oil extracted and placed into storage tanks as inventory.
explanation: Produced oil enters inventory at its cost of production (lifting costs). Revenue is NOT recognized until the oil is sold and control passes to the buyer. Inventory is carried at the lower of cost or NRV.

title: Pipeline Tariff Income
description: Earning tariff revenue from third-party producers who use the company's pipeline for transportation.
explanation: Midstream pipeline tariffs are fee-based revenue recognized as volumes flow through the pipeline. This is lower-risk revenue than upstream production as it does not depend on commodity prices.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) — Initial Recognition
description: Recording the present value of the estimated future cost to decommission an oil platform or well.

entries.account: Oil & Gas Properties (ARO Added to Asset)

explanation: IAS 37 / IFRS requires recognition of the decommissioning obligation at the start of production. The liability is the present value of estimated future decommissioning costs. An equal amount is added to the asset and depleted over the reserve life.

title: ARO Accretion Expense (Unwinding of Discount)
description: Recording the annual unwinding of the discount on the ARO liability.
explanation: The ARO is initially recorded at present value. Each year, as decommissioning approaches, the discount unwinds — increasing the liability. This 'accretion' is a financing cost, similar to interest expense.

title: ARO Revision — Change in Cost Estimate
description: Revising the ARO liability upward when new engineering studies increase decommissioning cost estimates.
explanation: ARO estimates are revised regularly as technology, regulations, and cost indices change. Upward revisions increase both the asset and liability. The additional asset cost is depleted over the remaining reserve life.

title: Actual Decommissioning Expenditure
description: Recording costs incurred to plug a well and restore the site at the end of its productive life.
explanation: When actual decommissioning occurs, the ARO liability is settled. If actual cost differs from the provision, the difference is a gain or loss. Effective ARO estimation can result in significant gains or losses.

title: Crude Oil Sale — Revenue at Market Price
description: Recording the sale of crude oil cargo at the prevailing market price.
explanation: Revenue is recognized when control of the crude cargo passes to the buyer — typically at the point of loading (FOB) or delivery. The significant margin between production cost and market price is the upstream business model.

title: Natural Gas Sales Revenue
description: Recording monthly gas sales under a long-term gas supply agreement.
explanation: Gas is typically sold under long-term take-or-pay contracts at a formula price. Revenue is recognized monthly as gas is delivered. Gas pricing formulas often link to oil prices or market indices.

title: Take-or-Pay Contract — Minimum Payment Revenue
description: Recognizing the minimum payment under a take-or-pay contract when the buyer fails to take contracted volumes.
explanation: Under a take-or-pay contract, the buyer must pay whether they take the gas or not. The payment is deferred as a liability until: (a) the make-up period expires, or (b) the buyer subsequently takes the volumes they paid for.

title: Oil Price Hedge — Swap Contract Fair Value
description: Revaluing an oil price swap used to protect against falling crude prices.
explanation: When a company hedges its oil production revenue using swaps, the derivative is marked to market monthly. For an effective cash flow hedge, the gain/loss goes to OCI and is recycled to the P&L when the hedged production is sold.

title: Oil Price Hedge — Settlement Received
description: Receiving a settlement payment when the hedge is settled and oil prices fell below the hedged price.
explanation: At settlement, the hedge gain is recycled from OCI to the income statement to offset the lower oil revenue received. The net effect is that the company receives approximately the hedged price regardless of market movements.

title: Underlift / Overlift Adjustment (JV Production)
description: Recording an underlift position where the company lifted less crude than its entitlement share.
explanation: In joint venture fields, each partner must lift exactly their share of production or an imbalance arises. An underlift means you have a right to take more oil later. Revenue is deferred until the physical barrels are lifted.

title: JV Cash Call — Paid to Operator
description: Making a cash call payment to the JV operator to fund development drilling.
explanation: In a joint operating agreement (JOA), the operator periodically issues cash calls to all partners. The non-operator records this as an advance that is replaced by actual capital expenditure once the JV accounting statements are received.

title: JV Cash Call — Received by Operator from Partners
description: The JV operator receives cash calls from non-operating partners to fund operations.
explanation: From the operator's perspective, cash calls received are a liability until the costs are incurred and charged to the JV. The operator is a fiduciary — the funds belong to the JV, not to the operating company.

title: JV Partner Billings — Monthly Cost Statement
description: The operator bills non-operating partners for their share of monthly JV operating costs.
explanation: The operator prepares monthly cost statements (JV billing statements) allocating actual costs to each partner per their working interest. The advance (liability) is converted to a billing receivable as costs are allocated.

title: Cost Recovery Entitlement — PSC
description: Recording the company's entitlement to 'cost oil' under a Production Sharing Contract.
explanation: Under a PSC, the company first recovers its capital and operating costs from a portion of gross production ('cost oil'). The cost recovery entitlement is recognized as revenue as the costs are recovered in each period.

title: Profit Oil Entitlement — PSC Share
description: Recording the company's share of 'profit oil' after cost recovery under a PSC.
explanation: After cost recovery, the remaining production ('profit oil') is split between the company and the government per the agreed PSC formula — often on a sliding scale based on cumulative production or R-factor (return on investment).

title: Farm-Out Transaction — Partial Interest Sold
description: The company sells a 30% working interest in a license to a third party in exchange for a carried interest.
explanation: A farm-out partially divests a license interest. The gain is the consideration received less the book value of the portion of asset disposed. Farm-outs are a key risk management and capital recycling tool in exploration.

title: Petroleum Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the special petroleum income tax applicable in GCC and MENA producing countries.
explanation: Oil & gas companies typically pay petroleum income tax at higher rates than standard corporate tax (55-85% in some jurisdictions). The tax is calculated on the 'ring-fenced' petroleum income from each license area.

title: Deferred Tax on E&E Assets
description: Recognizing the deferred tax liability arising from the temporary difference on capitalized E&E costs.
explanation: E&E costs are often immediately deductible for tax purposes but capitalized for accounting. This creates a taxable temporary difference. The deferred tax liability (DTL) is recognized at the applicable petroleum tax rate.

title: Royalty — Tax vs Accounting Treatment
description: Recording the difference between the royalty expense as per accounts and its tax treatment.
explanation: In some PSC structures, royalties are treated differently for tax and accounting purposes — creating timing differences. A deferred tax asset arises when the tax deduction occurs later than the accounting expense.

title: Resource Rent Tax (Petroleum Resource Rent Tax)
description: Accruing a resource rent tax that applies once the project achieves a certain rate of return.
explanation: Some jurisdictions impose a profit-based resource rent tax on top of royalties and income tax. It is triggered once the project achieves a specified threshold rate of return (the 'uplift' rate), capturing super-profits for the government.

title: Ring-Fencing — Separate Tax Calculation Per License
description: Maintaining separate taxable income calculations for each license area to prevent cross-subsidy.
explanation: Ring-fencing prevents companies from using losses from a new exploration area to reduce tax on a profitable producing field. Each license is taxed independently. This is a major feature of most petroleum fiscal regimes.

title: Geological & Geophysical (G&G) Studies — Expensed
description: Expensing geological mapping and regional basin studies before an exploration license is secured.
explanation: Pre-license G&G studies that do not relate to a specific exploration area with rights are expensed as incurred. They cannot be capitalized as E&E assets under IFRS 6 because no exploration right yet exists.

title: Exploration License Acquisition Cost Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the signature bonus and legal costs paid to acquire an exploration permit/block.
explanation: The cost of acquiring the legal right to explore is directly attributable to finding oil and gas resources. Under IFRS 6, it is capitalized as an E&E asset from the date the license is granted.

title: Exploration License — Annual Rental Payment
description: Paying the annual license rental to the host government to maintain the exploration permit in good standing.
explanation: Annual license rentals are paid to maintain the exploration right. They are capitalized to the E&E asset as a cost of holding the license. The full E&E balance is tested for impairment at the end of each period.

title: Exploratory Well — Suspended Pending Determination
description: Keeping an exploratory well's costs capitalized while the company assesses whether reserves are commercial.
explanation: Under IFRS 6, exploratory well costs can remain capitalized beyond one year if (a) reserves have been found and (b) management is actively assessing commerciality. A suspended well over 12 months triggers enhanced disclosure requirements.

title: Suspended Well — Written Off After Determination
description: Writing off a previously suspended exploratory well after management decides not to develop.
explanation: Once management decides a suspended well is non-commercial or there is insufficient justification to continue, the E&E asset is written off immediately. Delayed write-offs are an audit risk area in the oil and gas sector.

title: Stratigraphic Test Well — Cost Treatment
description: Recording a well drilled purely to obtain geological information with no production intent.
explanation: Stratigraphic test wells drilled solely for geological data with no drilling intention beyond that purpose are expensed under successful efforts accounting. Under full cost accounting, they are capitalized to the cost pool.

title: Exploration Overhead Allocation to E&E Asset
description: Allocating a portion of exploration department salaries and overhead to the E&E asset.
explanation: Only directly attributable exploration overhead can be capitalized to E&E assets. General administrative overhead cannot. The allocation basis (time records, project codes) must be defensible to auditors.

title: E&E Farm-Out — Partial Interest Sold Pre-Discovery
description: Selling a 40% working interest in an exploration block before any discovery is made.
explanation: Pre-discovery farm-outs monetize acreage value before the drilling risk is resolved. The gain is the consideration less the proportional book value of the E&E asset. Some operators use a full-cost pool method that eliminates the gain.

title: Offshore Platform / Production Facility — Construction
description: Capitalizing construction costs for a fixed offshore production platform.
explanation: Production platforms are the most capital-intensive assets in offshore E&P. Costs accumulate in Assets Under Construction and transfer to PPE when the facility is commissioned. The ARO for eventual decommissioning is recognized simultaneously.

title: Subsea Infrastructure Capitalized
description: Capitalizing subsea pipelines, wellheads, manifolds, and umbilicals connecting wells to the platform.
explanation: Subsea infrastructure connects the reservoir to surface processing. It is a development cost capitalized under the field's development plan. Depletion follows UOP based on the proven developed reserves.

title: Gas Processing Plant Construction — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the cost of building a gas treatment and compression facility.
explanation: Gas processing plants treat raw gas for pipeline or LNG export. They are long-lived assets (30+ years) depreciated on a straight-line or UOP basis. Capacity expansions are capitalized; routine maintenance is expensed.

title: Water / Gas Injection Well — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the cost of drilling and completing an injection well for pressure maintenance.
explanation: Injection wells are development assets — they maintain reservoir pressure to maximize production from the field. Their cost is capitalized and depleted using UOP over the associated producing reserves.

title: Development Borrowing Cost Capitalized (IAS 23)
description: Capitalizing interest on a project finance facility specifically funding a qualifying development asset.
explanation: Under IAS 23, borrowing costs directly attributable to a qualifying development asset are capitalized. For large offshore projects funded by project finance, this can be a substantial addition to the asset cost.

title: Facilities Turnaround / Shutdown — Major Maintenance
description: Recording the cost of a planned major maintenance turnaround on a production facility.
explanation: Major turnarounds (typically every 4-6 years) involve complete shutdown for inspection and refurbishment. Under component accounting (IAS 16), turnaround costs are capitalized as a separate component and depreciated over the interval to the next turnaround.

title: Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) — Cost Treatment
description: Recording the cost of a chemical EOR program injecting surfactants to increase oil recovery.
explanation: EOR costs are capitalized when the injection program can be demonstrated to increase total recoverable reserves. If the expenditure only maintains current production rates, it is expensed as a lifting cost.

title: DD&A — Facilities and Equipment (Units of Production)
description: Monthly depletion, depreciation, and amortization of all producing assets using the UOP method.
explanation: All capitalized development costs (platforms, pipelines, processing) are depleted using UOP. The rate is: (Net Book Value / Remaining Proved Developed Reserves) × Production Volume. High production months yield higher DD&A.

title: DD&A Rate Revision — Reserve Estimate Change
description: Revising the DD&A rate prospectively after an upward revision to proved developed reserves.
explanation: Reserve estimate changes are treated as changes in accounting estimates (IAS 8 — prospective treatment). An upward revision lowers the future DD&A rate, improving profitability. A downward revision increases the rate — a major earnings impact.

title: Gas Processing Plant — Straight-Line Depreciation
description: Depreciating a gas plant on a straight-line basis over its 30-year useful life.
explanation: When a gas plant serves multiple fields with different reserve lives, straight-line depreciation over its economic life is more appropriate than UOP. An JOD 85M plant over 30 years depreciates at JOD 236,111/month.

title: Impairment Test — Producing Field CGU (IAS 36)
description: Recording an impairment charge when the recoverable amount of a producing field falls below book value.
explanation: Under IAS 36, each producing field is a Cash Generating Unit (CGU). When oil prices fall or reserves are revised down, the recoverable amount (higher of VIU and FVLCTS) may fall below the carrying amount, triggering impairment.

title: Impairment Reversal — Proved Reserves Upgrade
description: Reversing part of a prior impairment charge following a significant reserve upgrade.
explanation: Under IAS 36, impairment reversals are permitted when the recoverable amount subsequently increases. The reversal cannot exceed the original impairment amount. This is triggered by reserve upgrades, higher oil prices, or improved development plans.

title: Gas Flaring Cost and Regulatory Penalty
description: Recording the cost of flaring associated gas and any regulatory fine for exceeding the flaring limit.
explanation: Gas flaring is both an operating expense (lost resource value) and a potential regulatory liability. Many GCC and MENA regulators are tightening flaring limits. Penalties are expensed and disclosed separately.

title: Produced Water Treatment & Disposal
description: Recording the cost of treating and disposing of water produced alongside oil and gas.
explanation: Produced water volumes often exceed oil production volumes in mature fields. Treatment and disposal costs are a significant operating expense (Lease Operating Expense) that increases as a field matures.

title: Crude Oil Quality Adjustment — Price Differential
description: Recording a price discount on heavy, high-sulphur crude relative to the Brent benchmark.
explanation: Crude oil prices are quoted relative to benchmark crudes (Brent, WTI, Dubai). Heavy or sour crudes trade at a discount that is contractually defined. The differential is presented as a reduction to revenue.

title: Oil Stock Gain / Loss — Tank Calibration Adjustment
description: Adjusting for the difference between metered volumes and physical tank dip measurements.
explanation: Tank calibration, thermal expansion, and measurement uncertainty create differences between fiscal meters and physical stock counts. Material variances are investigated; minor differences are recognized as stock gains or losses.

title: LNG Liquefaction Cost
description: Recording the cost of liquefying natural gas at the LNG train for export.
explanation: LNG liquefaction is an energy-intensive process. The cost is a direct production expense. LNG producers with tolling arrangements (where a third party liquefies the gas) record a tolling fee rather than the underlying costs.

title: LNG Cargo Sale — Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue from the sale of an LNG cargo to an Asian buyer.
explanation: LNG cargo sales are recognized when control passes to the buyer — typically at the ship's rail (FOB) or upon delivery (DES). Cargos are often sold under long-term SPAs (Sale and Purchase Agreements) with oil-linked price formulas.

title: Condensate Sale — Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue from selling condensate (light liquid hydrocarbons) separated from gas.
explanation: Condensate is a high-value by-product that requires separate metering and pricing. It is typically priced at a premium to crude oil and reported as a distinct revenue stream from natural gas.

title: NGL Separation & Sale
description: Recording revenue from natural gas liquids (ethane, propane, butane) extracted from raw gas.
explanation: NGLs are separated at a gas processing plant and sold as individual components. NGL extraction improves the economics of gas production. Revenue is allocated to each NGL product based on its market price.

title: Crude Oil Transportation — Tanker Hire
description: Paying a tanker hire charge to transport crude from the loading terminal to the customer's port.
explanation: Transportation costs are expensed as incurred. Whether classified as LOE or a reduction to revenue (netback pricing) depends on the commercial terms — CIF sales include transport in the revenue; FOB sales show it as a separate expense.

title: Offshore Installation Insurance Premium
description: Annual insurance premium for an offshore production platform covering property damage and well control.
explanation: Offshore insurance (energy package, well control, liability) is a significant operating cost. Annual premiums are prepaid assets amortized monthly. Offshore insurance is a specialized market dominated by Lloyd's of London syndicates.

title: Oil Price Formula — Retroactive Adjustment
description: Adjusting revenue when the final price is calculated retroactively based on a monthly average index.
explanation: Many oil sale agreements use a provisional invoice price with final pricing based on a monthly average. The retroactive adjustment reconciles the provisional and final prices. IFRS 15 constrains recognition of variable consideration until the uncertainty is resolved.

title: Make-Up Gas Delivery — Take-or-Pay Fulfillment
description: Delivering make-up gas to a buyer who previously paid but did not take their contracted volumes.
explanation: When previously deferred take-or-pay revenue is finally earned by delivering the gas, the liability is released to revenue. The make-up period may span several years depending on the gas sales agreement terms.

title: Capacity Reservation Fee Income
description: Earning a fee from a third party reserving pipeline capacity regardless of actual throughput.
explanation: Capacity reservation fees provide stable, predictable income for pipeline and processing owners. They are recognized over the reservation period regardless of whether the capacity is used.

title: Pipeline Tariff — Annual Escalation Clause
description: Applying the annual CPI-linked escalation to the pipeline tariff charged to shippers.
explanation: Long-term gas and pipeline agreements typically include annual escalation clauses linked to CPI or producer price indices. The tariff increase is applied prospectively from the contract anniversary date.

title: Force Majeure — Revenue Suspension
description: Suspending revenue recognition during a force majeure event (natural disaster, conflict) that halts production.
explanation: Force majeure events suspend contractual obligations. Revenue that cannot be earned due to force majeure is reversed if it was previously accrued. Insurance claims may partially offset the lost revenue.

title: Crude Blending Revenue
description: Earning a premium by blending heavy and light crude streams to produce a marketable blend grade.
explanation: Blending adds value by creating a product grade with broader market appeal. The blending margin (revenue less component costs) is the economic benefit. This is a common practice for companies managing multiple reservoir qualities.

title: Non-Operator — Share of JV Development Capex
description: Recording the non-operator's proportional share of development capex based on the JV billing statement.
explanation: When the non-operator receives the JV billing statement, the previously paid cash call advance is reclassified to the appropriate asset (PPE, E&E, or expense) based on the nature of each cost line in the statement.

title: JV Audit Adjustment — Prior Period Cost Correction
description: Recording a prior period adjustment following the annual JV audit that identified a misallocation.
explanation: Annual JV audits often identify prior period cost misallocations. If material, these are accounted for as prior period errors per IAS 8. If immaterial, they are recorded in the current period as an expense adjustment.

title: JV Partner Default — Non-Consent Penalty
description: Recording the penalty uplift when a JV partner elects not to participate in a well, triggering a non-consent penalty.
explanation: JOAs include non-consent provisions where a partner who elects not to fund a well forfeits a contractually defined penalty (typically 200-500% of their share). The consenting partners recover this uplift before the non-consenting partner receives any production.

title: JV Carried Interest — Funding on Behalf of Carry Partner
description: Recording the carrying of a partner's share of exploration costs in exchange for a promoted interest.
explanation: In a carry arrangement, one party funds another's share in exchange for a larger stake in production economics. The carrier capitalizes its own share to E&E and records a receivable for the carried amounts — recoverable from future production.

title: Technical Assistance Fee to Operator
description: Non-operator paying a technical services fee to the operator for managing the JV.
explanation: Operators typically charge a technical or management fee to the JV for their services. This is part of the non-operator's share of JV overhead. It is expensed unless it relates directly to a capitalized asset.

title: JV Cash Call Surplus — Returned to Partners
description: Recording the return of excess cash call funds after a budget underspend.
explanation: When actual JV expenditure is less than the cash call, the operator returns the surplus. The non-operator reduces their advance receivable. No P&L impact — it is a balance sheet adjustment only.

title: Farm-Out — Carry Obligation Fulfilled
description: Recording when the carry obligation (funding the partner's share) is satisfied and the receivable is recovered from production.
explanation: When production begins, the carrier deducts the carry amount from the carry partner's production entitlement. The carry receivable is reduced and the recovered amount flows to revenue — it is effectively a pre-payment of the carry partner's future production.

title: Refinery — Crude Oil Intake (Feedstock Purchase)
description: Recording the purchase of crude oil as feedstock for refinery processing.
explanation: The refinery purchases crude at market prices. Crude is held in storage as inventory until processed. FIFO or weighted average cost methods are typically used for crude inventory valuation.

title: Refined Product Inventory — Gasoline and Diesel
description: Transferring refined products from the refinery production process into finished goods inventory.
explanation: Refinery output transfers from WIP to finished goods at production cost. Yields are measured by volume and quality testing. The refining margin (product revenue less crude cost plus processing cost) is the key profitability metric.

title: Refinery Processing Cost — Operating Expense
description: Recording the monthly refinery operating costs: energy, catalysts, maintenance, and labour.
explanation: Refinery operating costs are production expenses — they go through WIP inventory and are ultimately part of the cost of finished products. Energy (catalytic cracking, distillation) is typically the largest single cost.

title: Refining Margin — Product Sale vs Crude Cost
description: Recording the sale of gasoline and diesel and recognizing the refining margin.
explanation: The refining margin (crack spread) is the difference between product revenue and feedstock cost. It varies with supply/demand for specific products and is the primary performance metric for downstream operations.

title: Pipeline Ship-or-Pay — Minimum Revenue Recognized
description: Recognizing minimum ship-or-pay revenue when a shipper fails to use their booked capacity.
explanation: Ship-or-pay contracts guarantee minimum revenue for pipeline operators. The payment is deferred if the shipper has a make-up right to use capacity later. If no make-up right exists, revenue is recognized immediately.

title: Storage Terminal Fee Income
description: Earning monthly storage fees from third parties storing oil products in the terminal.
explanation: Storage terminals earn fee-based income from third-party throughput and storage. This is low-risk, stable revenue compared to upstream production — making midstream assets increasingly valued by infrastructure investors.

title: LPG Bottling and Distribution Cost
description: Recording the cost of bottling LPG into cylinders and distributing to retail customers.
explanation: LPG distribution is a downstream retail business. Bottling, cylinder management, and last-mile delivery are the major costs. This is particularly important in Jordan and GCC where household LPG usage is significant.

title: Environmental Remediation Provision (IAS 37)
description: Recognizing a provision for contaminated soil remediation at an onshore production site.
explanation: When contamination exists and remediation is legally or constructively obligatory, a provision is recognized under IAS 37. The estimate must be the best estimate of the expenditure required. It is separate from the ARO (which covers planned end-of-life decommissioning).

title: Environmental Remediation — Actual Expenditure
description: Recording actual remediation spending against the previously established provision.
explanation: As remediation work is carried out, the provision is drawn down. If actual costs exceed the provision, additional expense is recognized. If below, a provision release reduces the expense.

title: Carbon Credit Purchased
description: Purchasing voluntary carbon credits to offset the company's Scope 1 and 2 emissions.
explanation: Carbon credits are intangible assets held for the purpose of surrendering against emissions obligations. They are carried at cost or NRV. As sustainability reporting becomes mandatory, carbon credit accounting is an increasingly important area.

title: Carbon Credit Surrendered (Obligation Met)
description: Surrendering carbon credits to meet a regulatory or voluntary emissions obligation.
explanation: When carbon credits are surrendered to a regulatory authority or carbon registry, the obligation is discharged and the intangible asset is derecognized. The provision set up at year-end for the emissions obligation is cleared.

title: HSE Training & Compliance Cost
description: Recording the cost of mandatory health, safety, and environment training programs.
explanation: HSE training is a mandatory regulatory requirement for oil and gas operations. It is classified as a direct operating expense. Safety performance is a key KPI for operators — poor HSE records increase insurance costs and regulatory scrutiny.

title: Community Investment / Social Obligation
description: Recording mandatory or voluntary social investment spending required under the concession agreement.
explanation: Many concession agreements in MENA and GCC require operators to invest in local communities — schools, clinics, roads. These are expensed as a cost of operating the concession. Social license obligations are increasingly material.

title: Oil Spill Cleanup Cost
description: Recording emergency costs to contain and clean up an oil spill from a pipeline rupture.
explanation: Oil spill costs are expensed immediately. Insurance recovery is recognized separately when virtually certain. Gross presentation (expense and recovery disclosed separately) provides transparency. Regulatory fines are not covered by insurance.

title: Expatriate Staff Payroll — Operations
description: Recording monthly gross salaries for expatriate drilling engineers, geologists, and production staff.
explanation: Expatriate staff in oil and gas operations are a major cost, particularly in exploration and early-stage production. Their salaries, including housing and allowances, are typically classified as LOE or capitalized if directly related to construction.

title: Local Staff Payroll and SSC (Jordan)
description: Recording monthly payroll and SSC for Jordanian national employees in oil operations.
explanation: Jordanian nationals employed in oil operations are subject to standard Jordanian labor law, including SSC obligations. Localization programs in GCC countries require increasing proportions of national staff.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — O&G Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing end-of-service benefits for Jordanian employees in oil and gas operations.
explanation: EOSB accrual is required under Jordanian labor law. For staff working on capitalized projects, the EOSB cost is capitalized proportionally to the project; for operational staff, it is an operating expense.

title: Expat Housing and Relocation Cost
description: Paying for expatriate staff accommodation and one-time relocation costs.
explanation: Expat housing in remote oil operations can be a significant cost. Permanent camp facilities for offshore workers are usually capitalized; temporary accommodation during a specific project is expensed.

title: Remote Operations — Camp / Accommodation Cost
description: Monthly cost of running accommodation camp facilities for workers at a remote onshore oil field.
explanation: Remote oil field operations require full accommodation, catering, and welfare facilities. These are a direct LOE cost. The cost per man-day is a key efficiency metric for field operations.

title: Helicopter and Offshore Logistics Cost
description: Monthly cost of helicopter crew changes and supply vessel logistics to an offshore platform.
explanation: Offshore operations depend entirely on helicopters and supply vessels. These are major recurring LOE costs. In Jordan's nascent offshore sector or for GCC operations, this is one of the largest operational cost items.

title: Reserve-Based Lending (RBL) — Facility Drawdown
description: Drawing down a reserve-based lending facility to fund development drilling.
explanation: Reserve-based lending is the primary debt financing instrument for E&P companies. The borrowing base is determined by the NPV of proved reserves and is redetermined semi-annually. Unlike corporate loans, RBL repayment is tied to production cash flows.

title: RBL — Semi-Annual Borrowing Base Redetermination
description: Reducing the RBL borrowing base after a downward reserve revision forces partial repayment.
explanation: If the borrowing base falls below the outstanding RBL balance at redetermination, the company must repay the shortfall within a defined cure period. This is a key liquidity risk for leveraged E&P companies.

title: Oil Price Collar — Put Option Premium Paid
description: Paying the upfront premium for a put option protecting against oil prices falling below a floor.
explanation: A price collar combines a put option (price floor protection) with a sold call (cap on upside). The put premium is paid upfront and recognized as a derivative asset. The collar provides budget certainty for RBL debt service.

title: Oil Price Collar — Fair Value Movement
description: Revaluing the oil price collar at month-end as oil prices move.
explanation: As oil prices fall, the put option gains value, protecting revenue. For an effective cash flow hedge, the gain is recorded in OCI and recycled to revenue when the hedged production is sold — offsetting the lower oil price received.

title: FX Loss on USD-Denominated Operating Costs
description: Recording FX losses when local currency weakens and USD-priced services cost more.
explanation: Oil and gas services (drilling, seismic, aviation) are predominantly priced in USD. When the local currency weakens between invoice and payment, the company records an FX loss. Hedging USD payables is an important treasury function.

title: Project Finance — Equity Bridge Loan
description: Drawing an equity bridge loan to fund the equity portion of a project before long-term financing closes.
explanation: Equity bridge loans are temporary financing used during project construction before long-term project finance is fully drawn. They are classified as current liabilities and repaid when the long-term financing is in place.

title: E&P Subsidiary Dividend to Parent Company
description: An oil-producing subsidiary declares and pays a dividend to its parent holding company.
explanation: Cash repatriation from oil-producing subsidiaries to parent companies is a key cash flow event. Withholding taxes, currency restrictions, and loan covenants may limit the timing and amount of dividends.

title: Petroleum Income Tax — Quarterly Installment Payment
description: Making a quarterly advance tax payment based on estimated annual petroleum income tax.
explanation: Petroleum income tax is often paid in quarterly installments based on estimated annual profits. The installments are prepaid assets offset against the annual tax provision. Under- or over-payments are settled at year-end.

title: Deferred Tax — ARO Liability
description: Recognizing a deferred tax asset on the ARO liability where the decommissioning expenditure is tax-deductible when incurred.
explanation: The ARO liability creates an accounting expense (accretion) but no current tax deduction — the deduction only comes when decommissioning occurs. This deductible temporary difference creates a DTA recognized over the asset's producing life.

title: Deferred Tax — Development Capex (Accelerated Tax Depreciation)
description: Recording the deferred tax liability where accelerated tax depreciation on development assets exceeds accounting DD&A.
explanation: Many petroleum fiscal regimes allow 100% first-year capital allowance on development spending. Where accounting DD&A is spread over reserves life, the faster tax deduction creates a taxable temporary difference and DTL.

title: Tax Loss Carryforward — Deferred Tax Asset
description: Recognizing a DTA for exploration-phase tax losses expected to be utilized against future production profits.
explanation: E&P companies generate large tax losses during exploration and development phases. When there is convincing evidence that future taxable profits will be sufficient to absorb these losses, a DTA is recognized. First oil events often trigger DTA recognition.

title: Withholding Tax on Service Contractor Payments
description: Withholding tax on payments to foreign drilling and oilfield service companies.
explanation: Payments to foreign oilfield service companies (Schlumberger, Halliburton, Baker Hughes) are typically subject to WHT. The applicable rate depends on the host country's tax law and any relevant double taxation treaty.

title: Customs Duty on Imported Drilling Equipment
description: Capitalizing customs duties and import taxes on a drilling rig imported for exploration.
explanation: Import duties on drilling equipment form part of the asset's cost under IAS 2/IAS 16. Some concession agreements include customs duty exemptions for petroleum operations — reducing the effective cost of imported equipment.

title: VAT Refund Claim on Exploration Costs
description: Filing a VAT refund claim for input tax paid on goods and services used in exploration.
explanation: Many countries exempt petroleum activities from VAT or allow full input tax recovery. The refund claim is filed periodically. In Jordan, the ISTD processes VAT refunds for qualifying petroleum operations.

title: Government Royalty in Kind (RIK)
description: Recording the government taking its royalty share in physical oil rather than cash.

entries.account: Crude Oil Inventory (Reduced by RIK Volume)

explanation: Some governments elect to take their royalty as physical oil lifted from the field. The RIK is measured at fair value on the lifting date and reduces the company's inventory. Revenue is not recognized on the government's share.

title: Proposal & Bid Cost Expensed
description: Expensing internal staff time and out-of-pocket costs preparing an RFP or bid submission.
explanation: Pre-contract bid costs do not meet the IFRS 15 capitalization criteria because no contract exists yet. They are expensed immediately. Only incremental costs to obtain a contract that is actually won are eligible for capitalization.

title: Incremental Contract Acquisition Cost Capitalized (IFRS 15)
description: Capitalizing the sales commission paid only upon winning a multi-year professional services contract.
explanation: Under IFRS 15.91, incremental costs to obtain a contract (commissions paid only on winning) are capitalized if they are recoverable and the contract exceeds 12 months. They are amortized over the expected contract period — here 24 months at JOD 250/month.

title: Contract Acquisition Cost — Monthly Amortization
description: Releasing one month of the capitalized sales commission over the service contract term.
explanation: A JOD 6,000 capitalized acquisition cost over a 24-month contract amortizes at JOD 250/month. This prevents front-loading the commission expense in month one and matches cost to the revenue stream it generated.

title: Contract Fulfilment Cost Capitalized (Setup / Mobilization)
description: Capitalizing pre-delivery setup costs that directly relate to a specific client engagement — such as system configuration, onboarding scripts, and initial staff training.
explanation: Under IFRS 15.95, costs that directly relate to satisfying a future performance obligation and generate resources to be used in that satisfaction may be capitalized. Setup costs for a client-specific outsourcing engagement meet this test; general training does not.

title: Client Advance / Mobilization Deposit Received
description: Recording an upfront cash deposit from a new client before any services are delivered.
explanation: Until services are delivered, the advance is a contract liability. Revenue is recognized only as work is performed. If the engagement is cancelled before services begin, the deposit is refundable (unless the contract specifies otherwise).

title: Client Advance Offset Against Progress Invoice
description: Applying the client's advance deposit against the first billing milestone.
explanation: When the milestone is reached, the full revenue is recognized. The advance is offset, leaving only the remaining balance as a receivable. The client owes JOD 5,000 in cash (the invoice less the deposit already held).

title: Time-and-Materials Revenue — Weekly Billing
description: Recognizing weekly revenue under a T&M engagement where the firm bills hours at agreed rates.
explanation: Under a T&M contract, each hour worked is a satisfied performance obligation and revenue is recognized as hours are delivered. 40 hours × JOD 180/hr = JOD 7,200. Invoicing is triggered by the approved timesheet.

title: Fixed-Fee Engagement — Revenue Over Time (% Completion)
description: Recognizing a portion of a fixed fee based on project completion percentage at month-end.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, fixed-fee contracts where the firm delivers a continuous service are recognized over time. Revenue = (costs incurred to date / total estimated costs) × total contract fee. A contract asset is used when revenue outpaces billing milestones.

title: Fixed-Fee Milestone Invoice Raised
description: Billing the client when a contractual milestone is achieved, converting the contract asset to a receivable.
explanation: The milestone invoice converts previously recognized contract asset revenue to a receivable, and recognizes any additional revenue earned since the last reporting date. The right to bill is now unconditional.

title: WIP Accrual — Month-End Timesheet Hours Not Yet Billed
description: Accruing month-end work in progress for hours worked by fee earners but not yet invoiced to the client.
explanation: Accrual-basis firms must recognize revenue as work is performed, not when billed. WIP represents hours approved on timesheets at the month-end but for which no invoice has yet been raised. It is reclassified to Accounts Receivable when the invoice is issued.

title: WIP Converted to Accounts Receivable on Invoicing
description: Reclassifying the WIP contract asset to a trade receivable when the client invoice is issued.
explanation: Issuing the invoice makes the right to payment unconditional. The contract asset (conditional right) is reclassified to a trade receivable (unconditional right). No revenue is recognized at this point — it was already booked when the work was performed.

title: Direct Staff Cost Allocated to Engagement
description: Allocating a consultant's salary cost to a specific client project based on time recorded on the timesheet.
explanation: Professional services cost of revenue is primarily staff time. Time recorded against a billable project code is the direct cost matched to that engagement's revenue. The remaining unallocated salary is overhead.

title: Overhead Allocation to Engagements (Indirect Cost)
description: Apportioning office rent, IT, and support staff costs across client engagements using a standard overhead rate.
explanation: Indirect costs (office, IT, admin) are pooled and then allocated to engagements using a predetermined rate — often as a percentage of direct labor hours or cost. This ensures each engagement bears its fair share of the cost structure.

title: Sub-Consultant / Sub-Contractor Cost
description: Recording the cost of an external specialist engaged to support a client project.
explanation: Sub-consultant fees are a direct project cost matched to the engagement revenue. If the firm passes the cost through to the client with a markup, the gross amount is billed as revenue and the sub-consultant fee is a COGS item. Withholding tax obligations apply in Jordan.

title: Sub-Consultant Cost Passed Through to Client (at Cost)
description: Billing the client exactly the sub-consultant's cost with no markup as a direct recharge.
explanation: Where the contract specifies pass-through at cost with no margin, the firm is acting as an agent for the specific sub-service. Under IFRS 15, if the firm is principal (controls the sub's output), gross revenue is recognized. If agent, only the margin is revenue. Pass-through at cost with no markup typically results in agent treatment and zero net margin on this line.

title: Disbursements / Out-of-Pocket Expenses — Client Recharged
description: Recharging travel, printing, courier, and filing fees to the client under the engagement letter.
explanation: Disbursements are presented gross — full expense and full recovery revenue. Netting is not permitted under IFRS 15 if the firm controls the service (e.g., arranges travel on behalf of the client). Gross presentation shows the true cost and recovery.

title: Non-Rechargeable Disbursements Absorbed by Firm
description: Recording out-of-pocket costs that the engagement letter does not permit the firm to recover from the client.
explanation: Where the contract caps disbursements or excludes certain categories (e.g., internal printing, local taxis), those costs are absorbed as an operating expense reducing the engagement margin. They are not billed to the client.

title: VAT on Professional Fee Invoice (Jordan 16%)
description: Adding 16% Sales Tax to a professional services invoice issued to a Jordanian corporate client.
explanation: Professional services (legal, accounting, consulting, engineering) are subject to Jordan's 16% General Sales Tax when supplied to registered businesses. The VAT collected is a liability to the ISTD and is not the firm's revenue.

title: Withholding Tax Deducted by Client (Jordan)
description: Recording the WHT deducted by the client before remitting payment for professional services.
explanation: In Jordan, companies paying professional fees to individuals or certain entities must withhold 5% income tax and remit it to the ISTD. The firm records the full revenue, receives the net cash, and holds the WHT as a prepaid tax asset creditable against its annual income tax.

title: Credit Note Issued — Fee Reduction Agreed with Client
description: Issuing a credit note when the firm agrees to reduce fees following a client dispute over service quality.
explanation: A credit note reduces revenue and VAT in the period it is issued. If the original invoice was in a prior period, the credit is recognized in the current period — not a prior-period restatement unless material. VAT on the credit note is also reversed.

title: WIP Write-Down — Irrecoverable Engagement Hours
description: Writing down WIP when hours worked cannot be billed because the engagement was terminated or the work product is rejected.
explanation: When billed hours become uncollectable (client refuses to pay, scope dispute, or engagement terminated), the WIP is impaired. The write-down reverses previously recognized revenue. This is a key risk management issue for professional firms billing under T&M contracts.

title: Allowance for Doubtful Client Receivables
description: Provisioning for professional fee invoices where collection is uncertain due to client financial difficulty.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, trade receivables must be assessed for expected credit losses. When a client enters financial distress or disputes an invoice, a specific provision is raised. The allowance reduces the net receivable on the balance sheet without removing the legal claim.

title: Client Receivable Written Off (Unrecoverable)
description: Writing off a client invoice confirmed as unrecoverable after all collection efforts have failed.
explanation: The write-off removes the receivable from the balance sheet. No new expense is recognized if the allowance was already set up. The legal claim against the client may continue even after write-off.

title: Client Debt Recovered After Write-Off
description: Recording unexpected payment from a client whose invoice was previously written off.
explanation: Recovered amounts from previously written-off clients are recognized as Other Income (or a reduction in bad debt expense) when cash is received. They are not credited back to the original revenue line.

title: Onerous Fixed-Fee Contract — Loss Provision
description: Recognizing an immediate loss when the estimated cost to complete a fixed-fee engagement exceeds the remaining fee to be earned.
explanation: When a fixed-fee engagement becomes loss-making (scope creep, underestimated complexity), the expected total loss must be recognized immediately under IAS 37 and IFRS 15. Waiting until the engagement closes understates current-period liabilities.

title: Early Payment Discount Offered to Client
description: Offering a 2% discount if the client pays within 10 days of invoice date.
explanation: Early payment discounts are contra-revenue. Using a contra account instead of directly reducing revenue allows management to track the cost of the discount policy. Under IFRS 15, variable consideration (including discounts) must be estimated at contract inception.

title: Retainer / Subscription Service — Annual Upfront Payment
description: A client pays a full-year retainer for on-call legal or advisory services billed annually in advance.
explanation: Annual retainer payments received before services are delivered are deferred revenue. JOD 3,000 is recognized each month as the firm stands ready to provide services. The firm's obligation is availability, not a specific deliverable.

title: Monthly Retainer Revenue Recognition
description: Releasing one month of the deferred annual retainer to earned revenue.
explanation: Each month, the performance obligation (being available and providing services on-call) is satisfied. The deferred liability decreases and revenue is recognized. Unused retainer hours do not roll over unless the contract explicitly allows it.

title: Retainer Breakage — Unused Hours Lapsed
description: Recognizing revenue from retainer hours that expired unused at the end of the contract period.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, breakage revenue (lapsed unused entitlements) is recognized when the likelihood of the client exercising remaining rights becomes remote. Historical data on usage patterns is used to estimate the breakage rate.

title: Contingent Success Fee — Constraint Lifted at Deal Close
description: Recognizing a fully contingent M&A success fee once the transaction completes and the fee is no longer variable.
explanation: Under IFRS 15.56, highly variable success fees are constrained until the uncertainty is resolved. Upon deal close (the triggering event), the constraint is lifted and the full fee is recognized immediately. No revenue was recognized during the advisory process.

title: Performance Bonus — Variable Fee Earned
description: Recognizing a performance bonus when the client confirms the firm exceeded agreed KPI targets.
explanation: Variable consideration (performance bonuses, KPI uplifts) is recognized only when it is highly probable the amount will not be significantly reversed. Formal client confirmation that KPIs were met lifts the revenue constraint.

title: Contract Modification — Additional Scope Agreed
description: Recording additional revenue when a change order adds new services to an existing engagement.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, a contract modification adding distinct services at their standalone selling price is treated as a new contract. The additional revenue is recognized as the new services are delivered. If the price is not at SSP, the modification adjusts the original contract prospectively or as a catch-up.

title: Partner Profit Distribution (Partnership)
description: Recording the quarterly distribution of profits to equity partners in a professional services partnership.
