Also confirm that policy loan receivables are correctly netted against the policy liability in the regulatory (statutory) financial statements, as required in many jurisdictions.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy loan application signed by policyholder, CSV statement at the time of loan, loan agreement with interest rate and repayment terms, current loan balance and interest accrual schedule, and confirmation that the loan is within the maximum allowed limit.

title: Deferred Acquisition Cost (DAC) — Initial Recognition
description: Deferring upfront agent commissions paid to acquire new insurance policies.
explanation: Under IFRS 17 and IFRS 4, incremental costs of acquiring contracts (primarily agent commissions) are deferred and amortized over the contract period. This matches the acquisition cost to the revenue it generates.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Under IFRS 17, DAC is replaced by the concept of 'insurance acquisition cash flows' which are included in the measurement of the insurance contract liability (reducing the CSM or, for PAA, as a separate asset). Only incremental costs — those that would not be incurred if the policy were not written — qualify for deferral. In practice, agent commissions are the main qualifying cost. The DAC asset is calculated in the policy admin system and amortised over the policy period. For IFRS 17 PAA contracts, the insurance acquisition cashflow asset is a separate line item on the balance sheet.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that only incremental acquisition costs are deferred — overhead costs allocated to acquisition (e.g., underwriting staff time) do not qualify for deferral under IFRS 17. The DAC asset must be tested for recoverability (part of the onerous contract test) — if the contract group becomes onerous, the DAC must be written off first before a loss component is recognised. Verify that DAC amortisation is proportionate to premium earning and that cancelled policies have their DAC written off immediately.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Commission schedule by product and agent tier, incremental cost analysis supporting DAC qualification, DAC roll-forward schedule (opening balance, new business, amortisation, write-offs for cancellations, closing balance), and recoverability test as part of the onerous contract assessment.

title: Deferred Acquisition Cost (DAC) — Monthly Amortization
description: Releasing one month of the deferred acquisition cost to the income statement.
explanation: DAC amortization spreads the cost of acquiring the policy over its life. JOD 18,000 DAC on a 12-month policy releases at JOD 1,500/month, matching the cost to the earned premium.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: DAC amortisation is automatically calculated by the policy admin system in line with the premium earning pattern. For straight-line policies (most short-duration general insurance), the monthly amortisation is the DAC balance divided by the remaining policy months. The amortisation is posted to the GL as a monthly journal entry. The DAC balance per policy is tracked in the billing or policy sub-ledger. For cancelled policies, the remaining unamortised DAC balance is written off in the month of cancellation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile the DAC balance on the balance sheet to the underlying policy-level DAC sub-ledger. The amortisation rate should match the UPR earning rate — any divergence between DAC amortisation and premium earning requires explanation. Verify that the DAC balance is zero or nil for expired policies and that cancelled policies have been properly written off. The DAC balance should be tested for recoverability as part of the liability adequacy test.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy-level DAC schedule (opening balance, monthly amortisation, cancellation write-offs, closing balance), reconciliation of DAC sub-ledger to GL, amortisation methodology confirmation, and liability adequacy test supporting the DAC carrying value.

title: Agent / Broker Commission Expense
description: Recording commissions paid to insurance agents and brokers for new and renewal business.
explanation: Commissions are typically 10-25% of written premium depending on the line of business. Motor and health commissions are lower; liability and specialty lines are higher. Renewal commissions are typically lower than new business.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Agent and broker commissions are calculated automatically by the policy admin system based on the commission rate configured for each agent/product combination. The commission payable is generated at policy issuance and cleared when payment is made to the agent. Some systems allow for instalment commission (paid as premium instalments are received) versus upfront commission. The commission payable sub-ledger must be maintained by agent to support the payment run and reconciliation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that commission rates are within the regulatory maximum permitted rates (Jordan IRC sets maximum commission rates by line of business). Test that commission is calculated on the net premium (after applicable taxes and fees), not the gross amount collected. Review the commissions payable aging for any disputed or unclaimed commissions. Confirm that commission expense is correctly classified as an acquisition cost and that any portion qualifying as incremental is treated as DAC under IFRS 17.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed agency or brokerage agreement specifying commission rates, commission calculation report from the policy admin system, commissions payable aging schedule, payment run confirmation, and IRC maximum commission rate schedule for compliance confirmation.

title: Underwriting Expense (Salaries & Overhead)
description: Recording the monthly cost of the underwriting and risk assessment department.
explanation: Underwriting expenses include staff salaries, software, risk data subscriptions, and office costs. They are tracked separately from claims and DAC to calculate the Expense Ratio — a key insurance KPI.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Underwriting expenses are recorded in the GL using a dedicated cost centre for the underwriting department. Payroll is processed through the HR/payroll system (SAP HR, Oracle HCM) and allocated to the underwriting cost centre. For IFRS 17 reporting, management expenses must be allocated between 'insurance service expenses' (underwriting and policy admin) and 'investment expenses' using a documented allocation methodology. The expense ratio (underwriting expenses / net earned premium) is a key KPI reported to the Board.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the expense allocation methodology between underwriting, claims, and investment functions for IFRS 17 compliance. Confirm that expenses classified as underwriting (and hence as part of the insurance service result) are genuinely attributable to underwriting activities. Test that one-off or non-recurring costs (system implementation, restructuring) are not being buried in the recurring underwriting expense line. Verify payroll postings against HR records for accuracy.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Departmental payroll report, cost centre allocation schedule, overhead allocation methodology documentation, expense ratio trend analysis for reasonableness, and IFRS 17 expense attribution analysis.

title: Policy Administration System Annual Subscription
description: Paying the annual fee for the core insurance policy administration and billing platform.
explanation: Policy admin systems are the core technology of an insurer. The annual fee is prepaid and amortized monthly. These systems are increasingly cloud-based SaaS platforms.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The annual subscription is paid upfront and recorded as a prepaid asset, then amortised monthly (JOD 120,000 / 12 = JOD 10,000/month). The prepaid is tracked in the GL prepayments module with a scheduled amortisation run. For multi-year SaaS arrangements, IFRS 16 must be assessed — if the arrangement contains a lease component (right to use an identified asset), it may need to be recognised as a right-of-use asset. Pure SaaS subscriptions (where the vendor controls the underlying infrastructure) are typically expensed over the service period.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether multi-year IT subscriptions should be assessed under IFRS 16 as a lease or as a service contract. Key question: does the insurer have the right to use a specifically identified asset (i.e., dedicated servers)? If yes, IFRS 16 applies. If no (cloud/SaaS), it is a service contract and expensed. Also confirm that the amortisation period matches the subscription period and that the prepaid balance is supported by the vendor invoice.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Vendor invoice and subscription agreement, IFRS 16 assessment (lease vs. service contract), amortisation schedule, and confirmation that the system is operational and in use (to support the prepaid carrying value).

title: Motor Third-Party Liability (TPL) Premium Written
description: Recording the premium for a compulsory third-party liability motor policy.
explanation: Motor TPL is compulsory in Jordan and most GCC countries. The premium is written at policy inception. The UPR is calculated and deferred separately. TPL covers bodily injury and property damage to third parties.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Motor TPL premiums in Jordan are regulated by the Jordan Insurance Commission (IRC) — the premium rates are set by regulation based on vehicle type, engine capacity, and use. The policy admin system is configured with the regulatory tariff, and the TPL premium is computed automatically. Integration with the Jordan Motor Vehicle Licensing system may be required for policy validation. The TPL policy certificate is a legally mandated document that must be issued at point of sale.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that all written Motor TPL premiums are charged at the IRC-approved minimum tariff rates — charging below the minimum is a regulatory violation. Confirm that every motor vehicle registered in the period has valid TPL cover (cross-check with the vehicle licensing authority if data is available). Test for policies written without a corresponding TPL certificate issuance. The IRC conducts its own regulatory audits of motor TPL compliance.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IRC-approved motor TPL tariff schedule, policy issuance confirmation, TPL certificate (Shahadat Ta'meen), vehicle registration document (teskira), and proof of premium collection.

title: Motor Comprehensive Premium Written
description: Recording the annual premium for a comprehensive motor policy covering own damage and TPL.
explanation: Comprehensive motor covers own damage, theft, fire, and TPL. It is the highest-premium motor product. In Jordan, the comprehensive rate is typically 2-4% of the vehicle value.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Comprehensive motor premiums are calculated by the policy admin system based on the vehicle's agreed value (or market value), the rate applied by the underwriter, and applicable loadings or discounts (NCB, driver age, usage type). The agreed value must be confirmed by a vehicle assessment or market valuation at inception. The premium includes the mandatory TPL component plus the own damage premium. The system issues the comprehensive certificate alongside the mandatory TPL certificate.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the accuracy of the sum insured (vehicle value) against market valuations — overstatement of vehicle value inflates both the premium and potential claims exposure. Verify that the NCB discount applied matches the claims history for the vehicle or driver. Test the application of the agreed rate to vehicle value across a sample of policies. Confirm that instalment arrangements have proper credit terms and that overdue instalments are tracked and escalated.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Vehicle valuation report or market value confirmation, policy schedule with agreed value and premium computation, NCB certificate or claims history from prior insurer, and instalment schedule if applicable.

title: Motor Fleet Policy — Group Premium
description: Recording a single fleet premium for a company insuring 50 vehicles on one policy.
explanation: Fleet policies cover multiple vehicles under one contract at a negotiated group rate. The premium is written in full at inception; the UPR release follows the policy period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Fleet policies are set up in the policy admin system as a single policy with multiple vehicle schedules attached. Each vehicle carries its own sum insured, premium allocation, and TPL certificate. Mid-term vehicle additions and deletions generate endorsement premiums or return premiums. The fleet premium is often negotiated as a blended rate across all vehicles, requiring the underwriting system to record both the overall fleet policy and the individual vehicle breakdown for TPL certificate issuance.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that all vehicles listed in the fleet schedule exist and are registered to the fleet operator. Verify that mid-term vehicle additions have been captured with the correct pro-rata additional premium. Fleet discounts must be within the underwriter's delegated authority. Cross-check the fleet premium to the underlying vehicle schedule total — unexplained differences may indicate vehicles being added or removed without proper endorsement.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fleet schedule listing all vehicles, vehicle registration documents for each vehicle, fleet premium computation, mid-term endorsements for vehicle additions/deletions, and the negotiated fleet rate authority approval.

title: Motor Own Damage — Total Loss Claim Paid
description: Settling a total loss motor claim where the vehicle is written off.
explanation: A total loss is declared when repair costs exceed a threshold (typically 75%) of market value. The insured is paid the insured value less any excess. The salvage vehicle passes to the insurer.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Total loss declarations are made in the claims management system following the loss adjuster's assessment. The system calculates the net settlement (insured value less policy excess and any outstanding premium instalments). The salvage vehicle is transferred to the insurer's salvage inventory in the system, and the net claim payment is initiated through AP. The salvage is tracked as an inventory asset until disposed of. Integration between the claims system and the motor licensing authority may be required to cancel the vehicle's registration.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the total loss threshold (percentage of vehicle value) is consistently applied in line with the policy wording. Verify that the insured value used for settlement reflects the agreed policy value, not a higher or lower amount. Test that the policy excess has been collected before releasing the net settlement. Confirm that salvage vehicles are registered in the insurer's name and tracked to disposal — unaccounted salvage is a fraud risk. Any remaining premium instalments due must be offset against the claim settlement.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Loss adjuster's total loss report, vehicle valuation at time of loss, salvage transfer documentation, excess collection confirmation, net settlement calculation, and payment authorisation.

title: Motor Own Damage — Partial Repair Claim
description: Paying a garage directly for repairing a damaged vehicle under a comprehensive policy.
explanation: Partial repairs are settled directly with authorized garages. The reserve set at FNOL is cleared when the repair invoice is received. Any shortfall or excess goes through claims development.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Network garage repair claims are managed through the direct repair programme (DRP) in the claims system. The garage submits a repair estimate via the insurer's portal or manual process; the claims handler reviews and approves. Upon completion, the final invoice is matched against the approved estimate in the AP module. Any variance between the approved estimate and final invoice above a threshold requires re-approval. Payment is made directly to the garage, not to the insured.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that payments are made only to approved network garages on the authorised supplier list. Verify that the repair invoice matches the claims handler's approved estimate — unusually large variances warrant investigation. Check for duplicate repair payments on the same claim. Confirm that the policy excess has been collected from the insured by the garage before the insurer pays the net repair cost. Review the garage payment terms to identify any suppliers receiving preferential treatment.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Repair estimate approved by claims handler, final repair invoice from the garage, excess collection confirmation (from garage), payment instruction and bank transfer confirmation, and closed claim confirmation in the claims system.

title: Third-Party Bodily Injury Reserve Setup
description: Setting a large reserve for a serious TPL bodily injury claim with potential long-term liability.
explanation: Serious bodily injury claims are long-tail liabilities. The initial reserve is a conservative estimate that may take years to settle. Actuaries review these reserves regularly as medical reports, court judgments, and negotiation progress.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Serious bodily injury (BI) claims are flagged in the claims system as 'large loss' or 'complex claims' and assigned to a specialist claims handler. The reserve is set based on initial medical reports and legal opinion on quantum. The claims file in the system accumulates all documents — police reports, medical assessments, legal correspondence, court filings. Reserve changes must be approved by the senior claims manager above a threshold (e.g., JOD 50,000 change). Legal fees (LAE) are tracked separately on the same claim file.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: BI reserves are a critical audit area due to their size, long-tail nature, and estimation uncertainty. Auditors review the largest open BI reserves individually with the claims manager. Key tests include reviewing the legal opinion on liability and quantum, comparing reserves to similar settled claims (internal benchmarks), and assessing whether the IBNR for unreported BI claims is adequate. In Jordan, the courts play a significant role in determining BI damages — auditors review pending court judgments and their implications for the reserve.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Police report, medical reports (initial and follow-up), legal counsel's opinion on liability and quantum, court filings and judgment history, reserve history log showing all changes and justifications, and large loss notification to reinsurer.

title: Third-Party Property Damage — Claim Paid
description: Paying a third party directly for vehicle or property damage caused by the insured.
explanation: TPL property damage claims are typically settled faster than bodily injury. Payment goes directly to the damaged third party, not to the insured policyholder.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: TPL property damage claims are processed through the claims management system with the third party recorded as the payee (not the insured). This requires the AP module to support payments to third-party claimants — a different vendor category from the insured policyholder. The third party's bank details must be verified before payment. The claims system tracks the third party's identity, vehicle details, and damage assessment independently from the insured's policy file.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that payments are made directly to the damaged third party and not through the insured (to avoid fraud risk). Verify third-party identity and ownership of the damaged property before payment. Test that the damage assessment was carried out by an approved assessor. In Jordan, third-party property damage claims arising from traffic accidents must be supported by a police report (taqrir shurta) — auditors check that this document is on file for all TPL PD claims.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Police report confirming the accident and liability, third-party vehicle damage assessment, third-party ownership proof, bank account verification for payment, settlement agreement signed by the third party, and claim payment confirmation.

title: Motor Policy Excess Collected from Insured
description: Collecting the policy excess (deductible) from the insured before releasing the claim payment.
explanation: The excess is the first amount of any claim borne by the insured. Collecting it reduces the net claim payment. The claims reserve should reflect the gross loss; the excess collection offsets part of the settlement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The policy excess is recorded in the policy admin system against the policy and automatically applied in the claims management system when the claim is settled. The claims handler receives a notification to collect the excess before releasing the net payment. In direct repair programmes, the garage collects the excess from the insured on the insurer's behalf. Cash excess collections are posted in the AR or claims module and reconciled to the bank statement.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the excess amount matches the policy schedule — excess should not be waived without documented management approval. Test that the claims reserve reflects the gross loss (before deducting the excess) and that the excess is recorded as a separate collection, not as a reduction to the gross reserve at inception. Verify that excess collection is consistently applied — selective waiver of the excess can indicate fraud or a policy to improve customer satisfaction without appropriate approval.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy schedule confirming the excess amount, excess collection receipt, claims settlement calculation showing gross loss, excess deducted, and net payment, and bank deposit confirmation.

title: No-Claim Bonus (NCB) Provision
description: Accruing the liability for premium discounts that will be applied at renewal for claim-free policyholders.
explanation: NCB is a loyalty pricing mechanism — policyholders earn up to 40-50% discount for consecutive claim-free years. The projected NCB cost is provisioned at year-end to match the benefit to the period it was earned.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: NCB levels are tracked in the policy admin system per policyholder, automatically stepping up each claim-free year at renewal (e.g., Year 1: 10%, Year 2: 20%, up to a maximum). The NCB provision is estimated at year-end based on the in-force portfolio's expected NCB at the next renewal, multiplied by the expected retention rate. This is an actuarial estimate posted as a top-side journal. At renewal, the actual NCB discount applied is compared to the provision to determine any over/under-provision.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the reasonableness of the NCB provision methodology and the expected retention rate assumption. An unusually high or low retention rate assumption will distort the provision. Test a sample of renewed policies to confirm that the NCB was applied correctly (correct step-up or step-down based on claims experience). Verify that policyholders who made claims in the year have had their NCB correctly stepped down, not maintained.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: NCB schedule per policy (from policy admin system), NCB provision calculation methodology, retention rate assumption and basis, actuarial sign-off on the provision, and year-on-year comparison of NCB provision movements.

title: Motor Salvage Vehicle Sold
description: Recording proceeds from selling a total-loss vehicle that became the insurer's property.
explanation: After a total loss settlement, the salvage vehicle is taken over and sold at auction. The proceeds reduce the net claims cost. Salvage management is an important cost recovery process for motor insurers.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Salvage vehicles are transferred to a salvage inventory account in the fixed assets or inventory module at the assessed salvage value. The salvage is tracked by vehicle ID (chassis number) in the system. When sold at auction, the sale proceeds are posted in the GL, the inventory is derecognised, and the gain or loss on sale is recognised. Many insurers use specialist salvage management companies who maintain their own platform integrated with the insurer's claims system.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile the salvage inventory to the list of total-loss claims settled during the year — every total-loss settlement should result in a salvage vehicle entry, and every salvage sale should clear an existing inventory item. Test the ownership transfer documentation — the vehicle must be legally registered in the insurer's name before sale. Confirm that salvage proceeds are properly banked and not diverted. Verify that the salvage value used in the inventory represents a reasonable estimate (cross-check to auction results).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Total-loss settlement file with salvage takeover confirmation, vehicle ownership transfer documents (in the insurer's name), salvage inventory listing, auction sale confirmation with hammer price, and cash receipt.

title: Motor Subrogation Recovery — Third-Party at Fault
description: Recovering a paid claim from a third party who was legally responsible for the accident.
explanation: After paying the insured, the insurer can pursue the at-fault party for recovery (subrogation rights). Recoveries are recognized when virtually certain and reduce the net claims cost for the period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Subrogation rights are tracked in the claims management system as a component of the claim file, separate from the original paid claim amount. The subrogation receivable is only raised when recovery is virtually certain — typically when the at-fault party has admitted liability or a judgment has been obtained. Some insurers use a specialist subrogation unit or outsource to a recovery agent. The recovery reduces claims expense in the period it is received or becomes virtually certain.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that the 'virtually certain' threshold is met before recognising a subrogation receivable — premature recognition inflates investment returns and understates claims expense. Review the aging of subrogation receivables for amounts that have been outstanding for more than 12 months without progress. Confirm that where a reinsurer has also recovered a portion of the claim, the subrogation recovery is appropriately split between the cedant and reinsurer according to the treaty.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Subrogation demand letter, police report or court judgment confirming third-party liability, admission of liability or settlement agreement, cash receipt if already collected, and claims file confirming the original claim payment amount.

title: Green Card / International Motor Extension Premium
description: Charging additional premium for extending motor cover internationally.
explanation: International extensions (Green Card) are add-on premiums for short-term cover abroad. They are written at full value and recognized over the extension period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Green Card extensions are issued as an endorsement to the main motor policy in the policy admin system. The Green Card certificate is generated as a standardised international document per the Green Card Bureau requirements. The additional premium is written at the endorsement date and recognised over the cover period. In Jordan, Green Card cover is managed through the Jordanian Motor Insurers' Bureau (JMIB), and the insurer must be a member to issue Green Cards.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the insurer is a JMIB member in good standing and authorised to issue Green Cards. Verify that Green Card premiums are paid to the JMIB for the foreign country coverage element, and that the insurer retains only its domestic portion. Test that the Green Card period does not exceed the policy period and that the premium rate is at least the minimum set by the JMIB.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: JMIB membership confirmation, Green Card certificate (conforming to Council of Bureaux standard), endorsement premium calculation, and confirmation of any JMIB levy payment for the foreign country coverage element.

title: Commercial Property Premium Written
description: Underwriting an annual fire and perils policy on a commercial warehouse.
explanation: Commercial property policies cover buildings and contents against fire, explosion, natural perils, and accidental damage. Premium rates are set by the underwriter based on construction, occupancy, protection, and exposure.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Commercial property underwriting data (COPE — construction, occupancy, protection, exposure) is entered into the underwriting system to generate the premium. Large commercial risks may require facultative reinsurance placement before the policy is bound. The policy admin system generates the policy schedule, which includes the full perils list, sub-limits for specific covers, and the premium breakdown. Premium is typically collected upfront or via a structured instalment plan agreed at inception.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the sum insured represents the reinstatement value of the property (not market value for property policies), and that it has been confirmed by a professional valuation for high-value assets. For risks requiring facultative reinsurance, confirm that the reinsurance was placed before the policy was bound — binding a risk without reinsurance cover creates an unintended net exposure. Confirm that the underwriter operated within their delegated underwriting authority (DUA) for this risk.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: COPE data sheet, risk survey report, premium computation and rate justification, facultative reinsurance placement confirmation (if applicable), underwriting authority sign-off, and signed policy schedule.

title: Residential Homeowners Policy Premium
description: Writing a homeowners policy for a residential property including building and contents.
explanation: Homeowners policies are personal lines products covering building, contents, and liability. They are written in high volumes at low individual premiums — making data analytics critical for pricing.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Homeowners policies are typically written through a high-volume, automated underwriting process (straight-through processing) using an online portal or agent desktop. Pricing is driven by rating factors (location, construction type, building age, sum insured) embedded in the rating engine. The policy admin system issues the policy and certificate automatically upon premium payment. Aggregation management tools monitor the accumulation of residential risks in flood or earthquake zones.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the rating engine's output against the filed tariff to confirm premium accuracy. For regulated markets, confirm that premium rates are filed with and approved by the IRC. Review the portfolio for geographic concentrations that may not be adequately addressed in the reinsurance programme. Verify that the sum insured for buildings represents reinstatement cost (including demolition and professional fees), not market value.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Rating engine output, approved tariff schedule, property details (location, construction, age, size), sum insured basis confirmation, and reinsurance programme aggregate mapping.

title: Property Fire Claim — Building Damage Reserve
description: Setting up a reserve for a major fire loss at a commercial property.
explanation: Large property losses are immediately notified to reinsurers and a substantial case reserve is set. The loss adjuster's preliminary estimate forms the basis of the initial reserve.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Large property fire claims are treated as 'large loss' in the claims management system, triggering immediate escalation to senior management and automatic reinsurance notification if above the large loss threshold. The loss adjuster's preliminary report feeds the initial reserve. As the adjuster's investigation progresses (cause of loss, scope of damage, business interruption assessment), the reserve is refined. The system tracks all reserve movements with timestamps and authorisation records for audit.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors scrutinise large fire claims for completeness of the loss adjuster investigation — was the cause of fire independently confirmed? For insured values close to or exceeding the sum insured, auditors test for underinsurance (average clause application). Confirm that the reinsurer was notified within the treaty's required timeframe. For BI claims attached to the property loss, verify that the BI coverage period and indemnity limits are correctly applied to the BI reserve.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fire brigade report, loss adjuster's preliminary and final report, cause of loss investigation report, reinsurance large loss notification with acknowledgement, reserve history, and BI assessment if applicable.

title: Business Interruption Claim — Loss of Profit
description: Paying a business interruption claim for lost profits following a fire at the insured's premises.
explanation: BI claims compensate the insured for lost gross profit and increased cost of working during the indemnity period. They are complex to settle as they require detailed financial analysis of the insured's accounts.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: BI claims are linked in the claims management system to the underlying property damage claim (they are interdependent — BI only triggers after a covered property loss). A specialist BI loss adjuster (forensic accountant) is appointed to assess the financial loss. Interim payments on account are common for long BI claims, with each interim payment posted as a partial reserve release. The final settlement is based on the agreed gross profit loss calculation, and any difference from the reserve is a development entry.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: BI claims are among the most contentious and complex to audit. Auditors assess the loss adjuster's gross profit calculation methodology against the policy definition (gross profit = turnover minus specified working expenses). Key issues include the correct indemnity period, the appropriateness of the annual turnover base, and the insured's obligation to mitigate the loss. Verify that the sum insured for BI is adequate (often underinsured due to an outdated gross profit figure used at inception).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Underlying property damage claim file, BI policy schedule confirming coverage and indemnity period, forensic accountant's report with financial loss calculation, insured's audited accounts and management accounts, interim payment schedule, and final settlement agreement.

title: Property Risk Survey Fee
description: Paying an independent surveyor to inspect and assess a high-value property risk.
explanation: Risk surveys are essential for large commercial properties. The cost is an underwriting expense. Some insurers charge the survey fee to the insured as a condition of cover.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Survey fees are posted in the AP module with reference to the risk/policy being surveyed. If the survey fee is charged to the insured, it is collected as a separate fee (not part of the premium) and posted to a fee income account with any applicable VAT. The survey report is stored in the document management system linked to the underwriting file. Some insurers use an approved panel of surveyors whose invoices are processed through a pre-approved vendor list.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that survey fees charged to insureds are properly reflected as fee income (not netted against the survey cost). Verify that the surveyor is on the approved panel and their report has been received and reviewed by the underwriter. For risks where the survey recommended protective improvements, confirm that the insurer has a mechanism to track whether the recommendations were implemented — non-compliance may affect the validity of cover.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Survey appointment letter, surveyor's invoice, survey report with risk findings and recommendations, underwriter's review note, and (if charged to insured) the fee collection confirmation.

title: Property Co-Insurance Penalty — Underinsurance
description: Applying the average clause when the insured's sum insured is below the actual property value.

entries.account: Cash — Net Claim Paid to Insured

explanation: If a building insured for JOD 500,000 is worth JOD 800,000, the insured receives only 500/800 of any claim (the 'average' principle). The insurer effectively only pays 62.5% of the loss. The difference reduces the net claims cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The average clause calculation is performed by the loss adjuster and must be explicitly approved by the claims manager before application. The calculation is documented in the claims management system: the proportion (sum insured / actual value at risk) is applied to the gross loss to determine the net payable amount. The insured must be notified of the average clause application in writing. Some jurisdictions and policy wordings require 'agreed value' policies that preclude the average clause — the policy type must be checked before applying.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that the average clause is applied consistently and correctly when underinsurance is identified. Verify that the actual value at risk used in the calculation is independently established (e.g., by a professional valuer or the loss adjuster), not just the insured's declaration. Confirm that the insured has been formally notified of the underinsurance and average clause application. Audit any cases where the average clause was waived to assess whether this was within management's authority.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Loss adjuster's report confirming the actual value at risk, average clause calculation worksheet (sum insured, actual value, proportion applied), insured's written notification of average clause application, and management approval for any waiver.

title: Property Reinstatement Value — Inflation Uplift
description: Adjusting the sum insured and premium upward mid-term for construction cost inflation.
explanation: Rising construction costs mean buildings become underinsured if sums insured are not indexed. Automatic indexing clauses generate additional mid-term premium that is recognized over the remaining policy period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Automatic indexation clauses are configured in the policy admin system to apply an index (typically a construction cost index) to the sum insured at each policy anniversary or mid-term review. The uplift premium is generated as an automatic endorsement, calculated as the additional premium on the increased sum insured for the remaining policy period (pro-rata). The insured should receive a revised policy schedule reflecting the updated sum insured and premium.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the index applied is consistent with the policy wording (the specific index referenced — e.g., Jordan Chamber of Construction and Building Contractors index). Verify that the indexation was applied to all qualifying policies and that the additional premium was collected. Test that the UPR is recalculated based on the total premium including the uplift. Review the portfolio for properties where the sum insured has not been indexed and may be significantly underinsured.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy wording confirming the indexation clause, index source and current index value, endorsement premium calculation, updated policy schedule with revised sum insured, and premium collection confirmation.

title: Group Medical Policy Premium Written
description: Underwriting an annual group medical scheme for a company covering 200 employees.
explanation: Group medical is the fastest-growing insurance line in Jordan and the GCC. Premiums are set annually based on the group's prior claims experience, demographics, and market trends. High claims ratios in medical make reserving and pricing critical.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Group medical policies are set up in the policy admin system with a member schedule listing all covered employees (and dependants if included). The premium is rated based on the group's prior claims experience (loss ratio, utilisation data), demographics (age/gender profile), and benefit structure (network tier, deductible, co-pay, annual limit). Member additions and deletions generate mid-term endorsements with pro-rata adjustments. The TPA system (if applicable) is integrated with the claims system for real-time utilisation tracking.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the completeness of the member schedule — the premium should match the enrolled member count. Verify that the loss ratio used for renewal pricing reflects the actual claims experience, and that premium adjustments at renewal are properly documented. Group medical loss ratios in Jordan frequently exceed 80-90%; auditors assess whether the UPR is adequate to cover expected future claims and whether the policy group is onerous under IFRS 17.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Member schedule (all insured names, dates of birth, relationship to employee), claims experience report for the prior period, loss ratio calculation, renewal premium computation, and group policy schedule.

title: Individual Health Insurance Premium Written
description: Writing an individual health policy for a self-employed customer.
explanation: Individual health policies are more expensive per head than group schemes due to adverse selection risk. Medical underwriting (health declarations) is applied at inception.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Individual health underwriting requires a completed health declaration form from the applicant. The underwriting system evaluates the declared conditions and applies loadings, exclusions, or declines based on underwriting guidelines. Pre-existing conditions are typically excluded for the first 12 months or permanently, depending on severity. The policy admin system records all exclusions on the policy schedule. HIPAA-equivalent data privacy controls must be applied to medical information.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the underwriting file for individual health policies to confirm that health declarations were obtained and assessed before the policy was issued. Test that pre-existing condition exclusions are clearly documented on the policy and enforced in the claims process. Review any claims that were denied on pre-existing condition grounds — the claims denial must be supported by the policy exclusion wording. Confirm that data privacy controls are applied to health data in the system.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Completed health declaration form signed by the applicant, underwriting assessment note documenting any exclusions or loadings applied, signed policy schedule clearly listing exclusions, premium computation, and data privacy consent form.

title: Medical Claim — Hospital Direct Billing
description: Recording a hospital invoice under a direct billing arrangement when an insured is admitted.
explanation: Direct billing networks allow insured patients to receive treatment without upfront payment. The hospital bills the insurer directly. Claims are expensed when the service is rendered, based on pre-authorization approvals.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Direct billing claims flow from the TPA's pre-authorisation system to the insurer's claims management system upon discharge. The hospital submits the final invoice electronically via the TPA portal. The claims/TPA system validates the invoice against the pre-authorisation: approved diagnosis, treatment codes, and amounts. Validated invoices are posted to AP as payable to the network hospital. Invoices with discrepancies are flagged for manual review. Batch payment to hospitals is processed monthly or bi-monthly.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the control over pre-authorisation vs. final invoice matching — a material proportion of invoices exceeding the pre-authorisation amount without approval is a control weakness. Test a sample of claims for eligibility (is the member listed on the policy, was the treatment within the benefit limits?). Review the hospital network agreement for the agreed fee schedule — invoices above the agreed rates should be rejected or renegotiated. Also check for duplicate claims submissions.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Pre-authorisation approval, hospital discharge summary, final treatment invoice, TPA validation report, hospital network agreement (fee schedule), and AP payment batch report.

title: Medical Claim — Reimbursement to Insured
description: Reimbursing an insured who paid out-of-pocket for treatment outside the network.
explanation: Out-of-network reimbursements typically require the insured to submit original invoices. The claim is validated against the policy benefits and paid subject to co-payment, deductibles, and benefit limits.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Reimbursement claims are submitted by the insured via a claim form (paper or online portal) with original invoices attached. The claims handler validates the eligibility (is the treatment covered, is the insured within the annual limit?), applies the co-payment and deductible, and approves the net reimbursement amount. The payment is made directly to the insured's bank account via the AP module. Original invoices must be retained for audit purposes — they should not be returned to the insured.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that original invoices are held on file (not copies), as reimbursement fraud often involves submitting invoices multiple times or to multiple insurers. Verify that the annual benefit limit tracking is accurate — overreimbursement beyond the limit is a claims leakage issue. Test that the co-payment and deductible are correctly deducted from every reimbursement. Confirm that payments are made to the insured (not a third party) unless a signed assignment has been obtained.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Claim form signed by insured, original medical invoices and prescriptions, treating physician's report, benefit limit tracking showing remaining annual entitlement, co-payment and deductible calculation, and bank transfer confirmation to insured.

title: Medical Pre-Authorization Liability Accrual
description: Accruing a liability for approved hospital admissions not yet invoiced.
explanation: When a hospital admission is pre-authorized, the insurer has a present obligation even before the invoice arrives. Accruing approved but unbilled claims improves IBNR accuracy and ensures liabilities are not understated.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The TPA system maintains a real-time register of all open pre-authorisations (approved but not yet invoiced). At month-end, this register is extracted and used to calculate the pre-authorisation liability — essentially unbilled claims where the obligation exists. The accrual is posted as a top-side journal to the GL and reverses in the following month when the actual invoices are received and posted. This accrual is a key component of the medical IBNR estimation process.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors obtain the open pre-authorisation register directly from the TPA and independently verify that all approved admissions above a threshold are included in the accrual. The risk of understatement is significant if pre-authorisations are not systematically tracked. Test that the accrual reverses correctly in the following period and that actual invoices match or exceed the accrued amount. Assess whether the pre-authorisation accrual is adequately supplemented by additional IBNR for claims not yet pre-authorised.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Open pre-authorisation register from the TPA system as at the reporting date, pre-authorisation accrual calculation methodology, reversal posting in the subsequent period, and comparison of accrual amounts to subsequent invoices received.

title: Medical Claims Inflation Reserve Top-Up
description: Increasing reserves to account for rising medical treatment costs mid-year.
explanation: Medical inflation consistently exceeds general inflation. Actuaries increase IBNR and outstanding reserves during the year when observed inflation threatens to make existing reserves inadequate.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Medical inflation adjustments are made by the actuarial team based on analysis of the observed claims cost trend (comparing cost per claim and frequency against the prior year and budget). The trend analysis is produced from claims data extracted from the claims/TPA system. The inflation loading is applied to the outstanding reserve estimate and posted as a top-side adjustment. The adjustment is reviewed at each quarterly reporting cycle.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors request the actuarial trend analysis supporting the inflation adjustment. Key tests include verifying the claims cost data quality (complete and accurate extraction from TPA), the trend calculation methodology, and the appropriateness of the medical inflation rate applied (compare to published healthcare cost inflation indices for Jordan). Assess whether the inflation reserve is also reflected in the premium pricing for renewal business — persistent underpricing combined with high medical inflation is a profitability concern.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Actuarial trend analysis report, claims cost data extract from TPA, medical inflation rate source (Ministry of Health or private healthcare cost data), outstanding reserve before and after inflation adjustment, and actuarial sign-off on the adjusted reserve.

title: Medical Co-Pay and Deductible Collected
description: Collecting the insured's co-payment share directly from the hospital network.
explanation: Co-payments and deductibles are the insured's share of each claim. In direct billing networks, the hospital collects the co-pay and remits confirmation to the insurer. This reduces the net claims cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Co-pay amounts are configured in the TPA/claims system per plan type and treatment category (e.g., 10% co-pay for specialist consultations, JOD 20 fixed co-pay for GP visits). The TPA system calculates the co-pay amount per pre-authorisation and instructs the hospital to collect it from the insured. The hospital confirms collection via the TPA portal. The co-pay confirmation reduces the net invoice payable to the hospital. Some hospitals net the co-pay from their invoice; others report it separately — the accounting treatment must match the billing method.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether co-pays are actually being collected at the point of service — hospitals may waive co-pays informally to attract patients, which is a form of premium evasion and a moral hazard issue. Test that the co-pay amounts in the claims system match the plan design. Review any patterns of co-pay waivers. Confirm that the co-pay logic in the TPA system matches the policy wording for all plan types — errors in co-pay configuration lead to systematic over- or under-charging of the insured.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy schedule confirming co-pay structure by benefit type, TPA system configuration of co-pay rates, co-pay collection confirmation per claim from the hospital, and net invoice reconciliation showing gross treatment cost, co-pay deducted, and net amount payable by insurer.

title: Third-Party Administrator (TPA) Fee Expense
description: Paying a TPA for managing the medical network, pre-authorizations, and claims processing.
explanation: TPAs manage medical claims operationally for insurers who do not have their own networks. The fee is typically a percentage of premium or a per-member-per-month (PMPM) rate. It is a direct expense of the medical line.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: TPA fees are calculated per the TPA service agreement — either as a percentage of gross premium written, a PMPM rate multiplied by average member months, or a fee per claim processed. The TPA submits a monthly invoice with the fee calculation detail. The invoice is posted to AP and the TPA management fee expense account. For IFRS 17, TPA fees are a direct administrative cost of the medical contracts and are included in the fulfillment cash flows for PAA/GMM measurement.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the TPA fee is calculated in accordance with the signed service agreement and test the calculation for a sample of months. Review the TPA performance against service level agreements (SLAs) — if the TPA is not meeting SLAs (e.g., pre-authorisation response time, claims processing time), penalty provisions in the agreement may apply and should be reflected in the fee. Assess whether the TPA relationship represents value for money by comparing the PMPM rate to market benchmarks.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TPA service agreement specifying fee structure, monthly TPA invoice with member count and fee calculation, SLA performance report, payment confirmation, and market benchmark comparison if available.

title: Network Hospital Settlement Payment
description: Settling the monthly batch invoice from a network hospital for all direct billing claims.
explanation: Network hospitals are paid in batches — typically monthly or bi-monthly. The payment clears the accounts payable that accumulated from individual pre-authorized admissions throughout the period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Network hospital payments are processed through the AP payment run, which consolidates all approved and validated invoices for each hospital into a single payment instruction. The payment run is authorised by the finance manager and processed through the bank's payment system (SWIFT, JoPACC in Jordan). The hospital receives a detailed remittance advice listing all invoices included in the payment. The AP sub-ledger is updated automatically on payment, clearing the individual invoice payables.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that hospital payments are within the contractual payment terms (e.g., 30-45 days from invoice). Late payment to hospitals can damage network relationships and result in network withdrawal. Verify that the payment run only includes validated and approved invoices — unapproved invoices must not be paid. Reconcile the total hospital payments for the period to the direct billing claims expense to confirm completeness. Investigate any hospitals receiving payments significantly above or below their expected share of claims volume.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Monthly AP payment run report, remittance advice sent to each hospital, bank transfer confirmations, hospital invoice reconciliation (invoices received vs. paid vs. disputed), and network hospital agreement payment terms.

title: Life Insurance — Term Policy Premium Written
description: Writing an annual term life policy providing a death benefit with no cash value.
explanation: Term life is the simplest life product — pure protection with no savings component. The UPR is calculated and deferred. If the insured survives the term, the premium is fully earned with no benefit paid.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Term life premiums are processed through the life policy admin system, with the premium rate determined by the actuarial mortality table and the insured's age, gender, health status, and sum at risk. Annual premiums are written at inception for 12-month term policies. For longer-term renewable policies, each annual renewal generates a new written premium. The life policy system calculates and maintains the UPR (or equivalent IFRS 17 LRC) for each in-force policy.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the mortality rate used in pricing is current and based on a recognised actuarial table (e.g., the Jordan national life table or a recognised international table). For IFRS 17 purposes, term life policies are assessed for PAA eligibility — most annual term policies qualify for PAA, simplifying the measurement. Verify that the mortality risk transferred is significant (a condition for IFRS 17 classification as an insurance contract).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy schedule with sum at risk, premium computation showing mortality rate applied, actuarial table used, health declaration (if applicable for the sum at risk threshold), and IFRS 17 contract classification assessment.

title: Life Insurance — Whole Life / Endowment Premium
description: Recording the annual premium for a 20-year endowment policy with savings and protection components.
explanation: Long-term life products require complex reserve calculations. Under IFRS 17, the premium is not simply written — it feeds into the cash flow projections for the CSM and BEL liability measurement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Endowment policy accounting requires a specialist life actuarial system (Prophet, ALFA, MoSes, or equivalent) to calculate the policy liability (BEL, risk adjustment, and CSM) under IFRS 17 GMM. The annual premium is not recognised as revenue on receipt — it is allocated between the repayment of the insurance acquisition cash flows, the BEL (savings component), and the insurance service (coverage component). This requires a full actuarial model run at each reporting date, making endowment accounting significantly more complex than general insurance.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors appoint their own actuaries to independently review the actuarial assumptions used in the long-duration life reserve model: mortality, morbidity, lapse rates, investment return, and expense assumptions. The IFRS 17 CSM roll-forward must be reconciled in full. Key risk areas include lapse rate assumptions (if actual lapses differ materially from the model, the reserve is misstated) and the investment return assumption for the savings element. Policyholder bonus declarations for participating policies must be confirmed as within actuarial surplus.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Actuarial model documentation, assumption basis (mortality, lapses, expenses, investment return), IFRS 17 GMM calculation output (BEL, RA, CSM), CSM roll-forward schedule, independent actuary's review report, and policy schedule.

title: Takaful Contribution Received — Participants Fund
description: Recording a takaful contribution paid by a participant into the Participants' Risk Fund.
explanation: In takaful, contributions are not 'premiums' — they are donations to a shared risk pool (the Participants' Fund). The fund belongs to participants, not the operator. The operator is a custodian/manager of the fund.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Takaful operators maintain two distinct sets of books: the Participants' Fund (tabarru' pool) and the Operator's Fund (shareholders' equity). Most ERP systems require a separate company code or fund accounting structure to maintain this segregation. The contribution received is recorded as a cash inflow in the Participants' Fund, not the operator's revenue. The operator must ensure that the Participants' Fund is maintained in a completely segregated bank account and investment portfolio.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the strict segregation of the Participants' Fund from the Operator's Fund — commingling is a Shari'a compliance violation and a regulatory breach. Confirm that the operator's bank accounts are clearly segregated. The Participants' Fund financial statements must be presented separately from the operator's financial statements in the annual report. Review the Shari'a Supervisory Board's report for any findings on contribution accounting or fund segregation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Participants' Fund bank account statements (separate from operator accounts), Shari'a Supervisory Board resolution on contribution accounting, contribution receipts issued to participants, fund financial statements (separate from operator statements), and regulatory filing confirming fund segregation.

title: Takaful Wakala Fee Income to Operator
description: Recording the operator's management fee (wakala fee) charged to the Participants' Fund.
explanation: The takaful operator charges a wakala (agency) fee of typically 20-35% of contributions for managing the risk pool. This is the operator's revenue — similar in concept to an insurance company's expense loading.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The wakala fee is calculated as a percentage of contributions received into the Participants' Fund, as specified in the wakala agreement. The fee is deducted from the Participants' Fund and transferred to the Operator's Fund. This inter-fund transfer must be recorded in both sets of books. The wakala fee percentage must be disclosed in the participant's certificate (equivalent of a policy schedule) and approved by the Shari'a Supervisory Board.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the wakala fee rate is consistent with the rate disclosed to participants and approved by the Shari'a board. Confirm that the fee is applied only to actual contributions received (not projected or budgeted contributions). The Shari'a Supervisory Board's report should confirm that the wakala fee arrangement is compliant. Assess whether the wakala fee adequately covers the operator's actual management costs — if the wakala fee is set too low, the operator may seek to increase it improperly through other charges.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Wakala agreement specifying the fee rate, Shari'a Supervisory Board approval of the wakala fee, participant certificate disclosing the fee, wakala fee calculation schedule, and inter-fund transfer documentation.

title: Takaful Mudarabah — Operator's Profit Share
description: Recording the operator's share of investment profit earned on the Participants' Fund assets.
explanation: In the mudarabah arrangement, the operator invests the participants' funds and shares the investment profit — typically 30% to the operator and 70% to participants. This is a second revenue stream for the takaful operator.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Investment income earned on the Participants' Fund assets is calculated by the investment accounting system and allocated between the operator (mudarib) and participants (rabb al-mal) per the mudarabah ratio specified in the founding documents. Only actual investment profit (not return of capital) is split. Investment losses are borne entirely by the participants' fund — the operator does not share in losses. The mudarabah profit share is transferred from the Participants' Fund to the Operator's Fund via an inter-fund transfer.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that only genuine investment profit (realised and unrealised as per the mudarabah agreement) is subject to the profit share — not contributions or capital returns. Verify that the mudarabah ratio applied matches the founding documents and Shari'a board approval. Confirm that the Shari'a board has reviewed and approved the investment policy for the Participants' Fund (Shari'a-compliant instruments only — no interest-bearing bonds). The investment income split must be clearly presented in the Participants' Fund financial statements.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Mudarabah agreement specifying the profit sharing ratio, Shari'a Supervisory Board investment policy approval, investment portfolio statement for the Participants' Fund, investment income calculation and split, and inter-fund transfer documentation.

title: Takaful Actuarial Reserve — Life Policy Liability
description: Calculating and recording the actuarial reserve for long-term family takaful policies.
explanation: Long-term takaful policies (family takaful) require actuarial reserves for the accumulated savings component and mortality liabilities. The reserve increases as the savings element grows.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Family takaful reserves are calculated by the appointed actuary using a life actuarial system (Prophet, ALFA, or AXIS). The reserve comprises two components: the tabarru' (risk) reserve (equivalent to a mortality reserve) and the participant investment account (PIA) (the accumulated savings/investment component). The PIA is a participant-specific account maintained in the policy admin system. Under IFRS 17, the takaful contract is assessed for its classification — the savings component may require unbundling if it is not closely interrelated with the insurance component.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors appoint an independent actuary to review the family takaful reserve. Key areas include the mortality assumption (is it based on a Shari'a-compliant takaful experience or a conventional life table?), the investment return assumption for the PIA, and the lapse/surrender rate assumption. For IFRS 17, auditors assess whether the savings component of family takaful contracts must be unbundled and accounted for separately under IFRS 9 (as an investment contract) with only the risk component under IFRS 17.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Actuarial valuation report (takaful-specific), assumption basis for tabarru' rate and investment return, PIA balance per participant (policy admin system), Shari'a board review of actuarial basis, IFRS 17 contract boundary and unbundling assessment, and independent actuary's review.

title: Life Policy Surrender Value Paid
description: Paying a policyholder who surrenders their whole life or endowment policy before maturity.
explanation: Surrender values are less than the accumulated policy reserve — the difference (surrender penalty) is the insurer's gain for early termination. Surrenders are a major source of profitability or loss in life insurance.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Surrender values are calculated by the life policy admin system based on the surrender value table specified in the policy contract (or actuarially calculated using the reserve minus a surrender penalty). The surrender is processed upon receipt of the surrender request form and any required documents. The payment is released through the AP module to the policyholder's bank account. The policy is marked as surrendered in the system, stopping any further premium collection. Reinsurance recovery on the surrender (if any treaty protection applies) is calculated simultaneously.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that surrender values are calculated in accordance with the policy contract and the IRC-approved surrender value basis. Verify that the surrender gain (penalty retained) is correctly calculated as the difference between the actuarial reserve and the surrender value paid. Review large surrenders that occur shortly after policy issue — they may indicate mis-selling or churning (replacing policies unnecessarily). For family takaful, confirm that the participant investment account (PIA) is correctly calculated and fully returned to the participant, with only the tabarru' portion retained.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Surrender request form signed by policyholder, identity verification documents, surrender value calculation worksheet, policy admin system surrender confirmation, payment instruction and bank transfer confirmation, and reinsurance recovery calculation (if applicable).

title: Death Benefit Claim Paid
description: Settling a life insurance claim following the death of the insured.
explanation: Death claims are the core liability of a life insurer. They are paid to the nominated beneficiary upon production of the death certificate and completion of the claims form. Reinsurance recovery applies for large sum-insured cases.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Death claims are registered in the claims management system upon notification. The claims handler verifies the claim against the policy: is the insured deceased (death certificate required), is the policy in force (no lapse), has the contestability period (typically 2 years) passed? The claim is assessed for any exclusions (suicide in the first year, pre-existing condition if applicable). Payment is made to the nominated beneficiary — if no beneficiary is nominated, the estate representative must be verified. Reinsurance recovery is triggered automatically if the sum insured exceeds the treaty retention.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that death claims are supported by official death certificates and, where required, a post-mortem report. For claims within the contestability period, confirm that full underwriting review has been conducted — the insurer has the right to void the policy for material misrepresentation during the contestability period. Test that payments are made to the correct nominated beneficiary, not to the insured's estate without proper probate documentation. Confirm that reinsurance recovery is submitted and collected for large sum-insured claims.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Death certificate (official, not copy), claims form signed by beneficiary, identity verification of beneficiary, beneficiary nomination form from policy file, policy in-force confirmation at date of death, contestability review note, reinsurance recovery claim (if applicable), and payment confirmation.

title: Life Policy Maturity Benefit Paid
description: Paying the maturity benefit on a 20-year endowment policy at its expiry date.
explanation: At maturity, the policy reserve is fully released and paid to the policyholder. If the actual payment equals the reserve, there is no P&L impact — it is a pure balance sheet settlement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maturity payments are scheduled in the life policy admin system at inception — the system generates a maturity alert when the policy expiry date approaches. The payment is prepared in advance and released on or shortly after the maturity date. The policy liability is closed simultaneously in the actuarial system. Some policies offer the policyholder options at maturity (cash, paid-up additions, extended term) — the system must record the option elected. Any policyholder bonus (for participating policies) is added to the maturity amount.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the maturity payment amount matches the sum assured plus any accumulated bonuses as per the policy terms. Test that maturity payments are made only to the policyholder and that identity has been verified. For a large volume of maturing policies, auditors test that the actuarial reserve for maturing policies has been fully extinguished and no orphan reserves remain on the balance sheet. Confirm that policies that have lapsed before maturity are not incorrectly processed as maturities.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policy schedule confirming the maturity date and sum assured, maturity payment calculation (sum assured plus bonuses), identity verification of the policyholder, payment instruction and bank transfer confirmation, policy admin system closure of the policy, and actuarial reserve release confirmation.

title: Policyholder Dividend — Participating Policy
description: Distributing a share of the insurer's surplus to participating policyholders.
explanation: Participating policies entitle holders to share in the insurer's investment and mortality surplus. The dividend is declared annually and can be paid in cash, used to buy additional cover, or left to accumulate with interest.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Policyholder dividends (bonuses) are declared by the Board following the actuarial valuation and surplus distribution analysis. The bonus rate per sum assured is configured in the life policy admin system, which calculates the total bonus for each eligible policy. Policyholders are notified of their bonus via the annual statement. In the GL, the bonus is recognised as an expense and a liability (dividends payable). The liability is cleared when the bonus is paid in cash, applied to reduce premiums, or added to the sum assured (reversionary bonus).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the surplus available for distribution has been correctly calculated by the actuary and that the declared dividend does not impair the insurer's minimum solvency margin. The dividend policy must comply with regulatory requirements — some regulators require approval of the dividend declaration. Test that all eligible participating policies have received the correct bonus amount. Confirm that the policyholder's chosen option (cash, reduction of premium, additional coverage) has been correctly implemented in the policy admin system.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Board resolution declaring the bonus rate, actuarial surplus analysis confirming the distributable surplus, policyholder bonus calculation schedule, individual policy bonus certificates, solvency margin calculation post-dividend, and regulatory approval (if required).

title: Takaful Surplus Distribution to Participants
description: Distributing the annual underwriting surplus of the Participants' Fund back to eligible participants.
explanation: If the takaful risk pool has a surplus after paying claims and expenses, it is distributed to participants who did not claim — reinforcing the mutual aid principle. This is a key differentiator from conventional insurance.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The Participants' Fund surplus is calculated at year-end after all claims, wakala fees, and reserves are deducted from total contributions and investment income. The surplus distribution formula and eligibility criteria (e.g., claim-free participants only, or all participants pro-rata to contribution) must be approved by the Shari'a Supervisory Board. The policy admin system identifies eligible participants and calculates each participant's share. Some operators retain a portion of the surplus in a reserve for future deficit coverage (the Qard Hasan fund).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the Participants' Fund surplus calculation is actuarially sound and that all liabilities (IBNR, outstanding claims, URR) have been fully provided for before declaring a surplus. The Shari'a Supervisory Board must approve both the surplus amount and the distribution methodology. Test the participant eligibility criteria — if only claim-free participants qualify, confirm that claims records are accurately maintained. Verify that the declared surplus is within the operator's regulatory surplus distribution framework.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Participants' Fund financial statements for the year, surplus calculation workings (contributions + investment income - claims - reserves - wakala fees), Shari'a Supervisory Board approval of surplus distribution, participant eligibility list, individual surplus distribution calculations, and regulatory notification if required.

title: General Liability Premium Written
description: Underwriting a commercial general liability (CGL) policy for a manufacturing company.
explanation: CGL policies cover third-party bodily injury and property damage arising from business operations. Claims are often long-tail — bodily injury suits may take years to settle.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: CGL underwriting data is entered into the underwriting system including the insured's operations description, turnover, payroll (for employers' liability), and prior claims history. The premium is rated using the underwriter's manual or a proprietary rating model. CGL is typically a claims-made or occurrence policy — the coverage trigger (claims-made vs. occurrence) must be correctly configured in the policy system as it determines which policy responds to future claims. Long-tail IBNR reserves are significant for CGL.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the coverage trigger type (occurrence vs. claims-made) for each CGL policy — this is critical for determining which year's reserves respond to late-reported claims. For occurrence policies, IBNR for unreported claims arising in the underwriting year is a significant reserve. For claims-made policies, the run-off provision for extended reporting periods must be assessed. Verify that the underwriter is operating within their DUA, especially for large limits of indemnity.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Proposal form (insured's operations and risk details), premium computation and rate justification, coverage trigger confirmation (occurrence vs. claims-made), underwriter's authority sign-off, prior claims history, and signed policy schedule with limits and sublimits.

title: Professional Indemnity (PI) Premium Written
description: Underwriting a professional indemnity policy for an accounting or law firm.
explanation: PI covers professionals against claims for negligence in their professional services. Claims are made-and-reported basis — the policy year when the claim is reported controls which policy responds.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: PI policies are written on a claims-made basis — the policy admin system must be configured to correctly record the retroactive date and the claims-made year. The retroactive date determines how far back in time an insured event can trigger the current policy. When a PI claim is reported, the claims system identifies the correct policy year based on the notification date. Run-off provisions (for extended reporting periods after policy expiry) are a significant reserve item for PI.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the retroactive date and claims-made structure are correctly recorded for each PI policy. Test that claims are assigned to the correct policy year based on the notification date, not the date the negligent act occurred. Run-off reserves for expired PI policies with open claims must be adequately maintained. For professions with regulatory reporting requirements (e.g., accountants, lawyers, engineers), confirm that minimum mandatory limit requirements are met.
