practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Proposal form with profession details, fee income (premium basis for PI), retroactive date confirmation, claims-made basis disclosure in the policy schedule, prior claims history, run-off reserve schedule for expired policies, and regulatory minimum limit compliance confirmation.

title: Directors & Officers (D&O) Premium Written
description: Underwriting a D&O policy for a publicly listed company's board members.
explanation: D&O insures directors and officers against personal liability for corporate decisions. GCC demand has grown significantly following regulatory increases in directors' accountability.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: D&O policies are written on a claims-made basis. The policy admin system records all three coverage sides: Side A (personal asset protection for directors), Side B (company reimbursement for indemnifying directors), and Side C (entity coverage for securities claims, for listed companies). The premium is split between the sides for accounting purposes. Large D&O programs for listed companies often involve co-insurance or layered coverage, requiring coordination with lead and following insurers.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the D&O policy covers the current board composition (new directors added during the year may need to be endorsed). Verify the claims-made trigger and retroactive date. D&O claims are often high-profile and high-value — auditors review the largest open D&O reserves carefully. For listed company D&O, confirm that securities coverage (Side C) limits are appropriate given the company's market capitalisation. Assess whether the D&O coverage interacts with any regulatory investigations or enforcement actions involving the company.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Completed D&O proposal form with board composition, company financials, and litigation history; signed policy schedule with Side A/B/C split; premium computation; retroactive date confirmation; co-insurance or layering documentation if applicable; and a board resolution confirming the purchase of D&O coverage.

title: Marine Cargo Premium Written
description: Underwriting a marine cargo open cover policy for a trader's annual import shipments.
explanation: Marine cargo covers goods in transit by sea, air, or land. Open covers automatically insure each shipment declared. Premium is typically based on cargo value × rate per mille.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Marine cargo open covers are set up in the policy admin system as an annual framework policy. Each individual shipment is declared by the insured, generating a certificate of insurance. The premium for each shipment (cargo value × rate per mille) is accumulated and billed monthly or quarterly. The UPR for open covers is complex — it represents the unearned portion of the estimated annual premium, adjusted for actual declarations to date. The system must track cumulative declarations against the annual declaration limit.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that all shipments above the minimum declaration threshold have been declared — non-declaration by the insured does not eliminate the cover but creates a premium receivable. Test the rate per mille applied to a sample of declarations. Verify that the UPR is calculated on the basis of expected future declarations for the remaining policy period, adjusted for seasonality. For high-value or sensitive cargo (electronics, pharmaceuticals), confirm that the appropriate Institute Cargo Clauses (A, B, or C) have been specified.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Open cover policy schedule, shipment declaration forms, monthly premium accumulation schedule, certificate of insurance for each declared shipment, UPR calculation methodology for open covers, and rate per mille confirmation.

title: Marine Hull Premium Written
description: Underwriting a marine hull policy on a commercial vessel.
explanation: Marine hull covers physical damage to the vessel. It is a specialist line requiring knowledge of international maritime law, Institute Clauses, and Lloyd's market practices.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Marine hull policies are typically placed through the London market or specialist reinsurers, often on a co-insurance basis. The policy admin system records the vessel details (name, IMO number, type, gross tonnage, age, flag, classification society). The agreed value of the vessel is the basis for both premium and claims. Hull policies are written for 12 months and include Protection & Indemnity (P&I) liability coverage separately through a P&I club (mutual arrangement, not recorded in the insurance policy admin system).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the vessel details in the policy match the vessel's class certificate from a recognised classification society (Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV). If the vessel's class has been suspended or the vessel is operating in a trading area excluded by the policy, coverage may be void. For high-value vessels, confirm that facultative reinsurance was placed before binding the risk. Test that the agreed value in the policy is a reasonable estimate of the vessel's market value.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Vessel class certificate, IMO registration, agreed value survey, Institute Yacht Clauses or Institute Hull Clauses (appropriate to vessel type), co-insurance slip if applicable, facultative reinsurance placement confirmation, and signed policy schedule.

title: Construction All Risk (CAR) Premium Written
description: Underwriting a CAR policy for a major construction project in Jordan.
explanation: CAR policies cover construction works in progress, plant, equipment, and third-party liability on construction sites. The premium is typically earned proportional to the construction schedule.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: CAR policies are project-specific and written for the duration of the construction period (which may span multiple years). The policy admin system must be configured to handle multi-year policies with mid-term adjustments as the contract value changes (due to variations, inflation, scope changes). The premium is typically based on the final contract value plus plant and machinery values. UPR for CAR policies is calculated based on the construction schedule (S-curve) rather than a simple straight-line method, as risk is front-loaded during the active construction phase.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the sum insured reflects the full reinstatement value of the works at any point during construction (not just the final contract value). Test that mid-term adjustments in contract value have been endorsed and additional premium collected. For multi-year CAR projects, verify that the UPR methodology reflects the actual exposure at each reporting date (using the S-curve or completion percentage). Confirm that the Defects Liability Period (DLP) is included in the policy period if required.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Construction contract (confirming contract value and duration), project S-curve (construction schedule), CAR premium computation, mid-term endorsements for contract value changes, reinsurance placement confirmation, and DLP extension confirmation.

title: Workers Compensation Premium Written
description: Underwriting an employer's liability / workers compensation policy.
explanation: Workers comp covers employees injured at work. Premium is based on payroll and industry hazard class. Claims can be long-tail due to permanent disability and periodic payment orders.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Workers compensation premiums are calculated based on the insured's declared payroll multiplied by an industry hazard rate class. Most workers comp policies include an audit clause — the premium is adjusted at year-end based on the actual payroll (not the estimate used at inception). The policy admin system must support the premium adjustment workflow. In Jordan, workers compensation is governed by the Labour Law, which sets minimum benefit levels. Claims are often long-tail — permanent disability claims may involve periodic payments over many years.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the audit premium adjustment is performed after year-end based on the insured's certified payroll records. Confirm that the hazard class applied is appropriate for the insured's industry — misclassification of a high-hazard industry as low-hazard is a common fraud risk. For long-tail permanent disability claims, verify that the reserve reflects the present value of future periodic payments (PPO equivalent). Test that claims payments comply with the Labour Law minimum benefit schedule.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Payroll estimate at inception, industry hazard class documentation, audit endorsement with actual payroll and premium adjustment, workers' payroll records (for audit), permanent disability assessment, and reserve calculation for long-tail claims.

title: Cyber Insurance Premium Written
description: Underwriting a cyber liability policy covering data breach and ransomware for a fintech company.
explanation: Cyber insurance is the fastest-growing specialty line globally. It covers first-party losses (ransomware, data recovery) and third-party liability (customer data breach notification, regulatory fines coverage).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Cyber underwriting requires a detailed cyber risk assessment of the insured's IT infrastructure, security controls, and data holdings. Many insurers use third-party cyber risk scoring tools (BitSight, SecurityScorecard) integrated with the underwriting system. The policy structure is complex — first-party and third-party coverages may be sub-limited separately in the system. Cyber IBNR is extremely uncertain given the rapidly evolving threat landscape and limited historical data — actuarial reserving for cyber requires specialist techniques.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the underwriter has adequate information on the insured's cyber risk posture to price the risk. Verify that the cyber policy wording clearly defines covered events (ransomware, social engineering, data breach) — ambiguous wordings create coverage disputes. Confirm that systemic/accumulation risk (e.g., a single software vulnerability affecting thousands of insured entities simultaneously) is adequately addressed in the reinsurance programme. Cyber IBNR assumptions should be stress-tested given the potential for large, correlated losses.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Cyber risk assessment form and scoring output, IT security controls confirmation, policy schedule with first- and third-party sub-limits, cyber accumulation management data, reinsurance cyber aggregate tracking, and actuarial IBNR justification for cyber.

title: Broker Commission Payable — Outward
description: Accruing the commission owed to the placing broker for new business written through the broker channel.
explanation: Brokers earn commission (typically 15-25% of premium) for placing business with insurers. The commission is an acquisition cost and should be treated as DAC if IFRS 17 capitalization criteria are met.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Broker commissions are accrued in the policy admin system at the time of policy issuance, linked to the registered broker. The commissions payable sub-ledger tracks amounts owed to each broker. Payment is made monthly or quarterly as per the broker agreement. Some brokers net their commission directly from the premium remitted to the insurer — in this case, the insurer must record both the gross premium receivable and the commission payable, and reconcile the net cash received. Under IFRS 17, incremental broker commissions are DAC assets.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that broker commission rates are within the IRC maximum permitted rates and match the signed brokerage agreement. Test the accuracy of the commission calculation on a sample of policies. Confirm that commissions are not paid to unregistered brokers (an IRC regulatory requirement). For brokers who net their commission from premiums, auditors reconcile gross premium written, commission netted, and net cash received to confirm completeness.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed brokerage agreement specifying commission rates, commission calculation report, broker registration with the IRC, commissions payable aging schedule, and payment confirmations or netting reconciliation.

title: Premium Written — Broker Channel (Gross)
description: Recording a policy placed through an intermediary broker, with premium collected via the broker.
explanation: Broker-channel business often involves a credit risk on the broker (not the insured). The insurer recognizes the full gross premium as a receivable from the broker, who in turn collects from clients.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Broker-channel premiums are recorded in the AR sub-ledger with the broker as the debtor (not the underlying insured). The broker is responsible for collecting from the client and remitting to the insurer within the agreed credit period (typically 30-60 days). The policy admin system identifies broker-sourced policies by the registered intermediary code. Premium credit control is managed at the broker level — if the broker fails, the insurer may face a loss even if the underlying insured has paid.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the credit risk on broker premium receivables — large balances from a single broker should be flagged. In Jordan, the IRC requires brokers to hold client funds in separate trust accounts — auditors may request confirmation that broker premium accounts are properly segregated. Test the age of broker receivables — amounts overdue beyond the credit period should be provided for or subject to policy cancellation. Review any cases where the insurer has paid a claim on a policy where the premium has not been received from the broker.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Broker credit terms in the brokerage agreement, broker premium receivable aging schedule, broker trust account confirmation (if required by IRC), credit limit for each broker, and documentation of any overdue balances and collection actions taken.

title: Mid-Term Endorsement — Additional Premium
description: Collecting additional premium when a policyholder increases their sum insured or adds a risk mid-term.
explanation: Mid-term endorsements adjust the policy. Additional premiums are written pro-rata for the remaining policy period and deferred using the UPR mechanism.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Mid-term endorsements are processed in the policy admin system, which automatically calculates the additional premium on a pro-rata basis for the remaining days of the policy period. The endorsement generates a new billing item in the AR sub-ledger. The UPR is recalculated on the total premium (original plus endorsement) as at the endorsement effective date. The policy schedule is updated to reflect the revised cover details and is reissued to the policyholder.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that endorsement premiums are correctly calculated on a pro-rata basis from the effective date of the change to the policy expiry date, not a full-year premium. Test that the UPR is updated to reflect the endorsement premium. Verify that the endorsement has an effective date that is on or after the date of the policyholder's request — backdated endorsements require underwriter approval. For material increases in sum insured, confirm that the underwriter's acceptance note is on file.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policyholder's request for endorsement, underwriter's acceptance (for material changes), endorsement premium calculation, updated policy schedule, and billing confirmation for the additional premium.

title: Mid-Term Endorsement — Return Premium
description: Refunding a premium reduction when a policyholder reduces their coverage mid-term.
explanation: Return premiums are negative written premiums. The UPR is adjusted and the refund is processed. Short-rate penalties may apply on voluntary reductions.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Return premium endorsements are processed in the policy admin system as a negative written premium transaction, reducing both the written premium and the UPR. The refund is processed through the AP module (if by bank transfer) or as a credit against future premiums (if the policyholder has other policies). Short-rate penalty calculations (if applicable) must be configured in the system's cancellation and endorsement rating engine. The revised policy schedule is issued to the policyholder.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the return premium is calculated correctly as the unearned pro-rata premium on the reduction in cover, with any short-rate penalty correctly applied per the policy wording. Verify that the UPR is reduced by the same amount as the return premium (ensuring the accounting is symmetrical with how the original premium was deferred). Test that refunds are made only to the policyholder — return premiums should not be redirected to the broker or agent without written authorisation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Policyholder's written request for cover reduction, return premium calculation (pro-rata or short-rate), revised policy schedule, refund payment confirmation, and updated UPR schedule.

title: Renewal Notice — Premium Accrual
description: Accruing the renewal premium for a policy that has reached its anniversary date but payment is not yet received.
explanation: If the renewal terms are agreed and the risk continues, the premium can be accrued at the renewal date even if cash has not been received. This requires a credit risk assessment on the policyholder.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Renewal premiums are accrued in the policy admin system when the renewal is confirmed (either automatically for automatic renewal policies, or upon receipt of a signed renewal confirmation). The system generates the renewal written premium and UPR entries at the renewal date. Unconfirmed renewals (where the policyholder has not yet responded to the renewal notice) should not be accrued — only confirmed renewals. The system must clearly distinguish between confirmed renewals and pending renewals.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that only confirmed renewals are included in the written premium accrual at period-end. Cut-off testing is particularly important — policies with a renewal date close to year-end but where renewal confirmation has not been received should not be accrued. Verify the credit assessment of policyholders with large accrued renewal premiums outstanding. Confirm that the renewal premium rate reflects current market rates and any adjustments for claims experience — automatic renewal at the old rate may understate claims costs.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Renewal notice sent to policyholder, renewal confirmation from policyholder (signed renewal slip or confirmation email), renewal premium computation showing any rate changes, credit assessment for large accounts, and post-balance-sheet evidence of payment for cut-off testing.

title: Lapsed Policy — Unearned Premium Released
description: Releasing the remaining UPR when a policy lapses due to non-payment of premium.
explanation: When a policy lapses, the insurer's obligation to provide future cover ceases. The remaining UPR is released to earned premium. The insurer retains the earned portion regardless.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Policy lapses are processed in the policy admin system upon expiry of the grace period (typically 30 days after the premium due date for non-payment). The system automatically releases the UPR upon lapse processing, recognising the remaining unearned premium as earned (since there is no longer an obligation to provide future cover). The lapsed policy is flagged in the system, and no further claims are accepted from the lapse date. The premium receivable (for uncollected instalments) is assessed for write-off.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the lapse date is correctly determined as the end of the grace period, not the premium due date — lapsing before the grace period expires is improper. Test that the premium receivable for the lapsed policy has been properly written off or provided for — continuing to hold a receivable for an uncollectable premium is incorrect. Confirm that no claims are paid for events occurring after the lapse date. Review the lapse rate against prior periods — a spike in lapses may indicate affordability issues or customer dissatisfaction.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Premium due date, grace period confirmation per policy wording, lapse processing date in the policy admin system, UPR release calculation, premium receivable write-off assessment, and written notification of lapse sent to the policyholder.

title: Policy Reinstatement — Back Premium Collected
description: Reinstating a lapsed policy after the insured pays arrears of premium.
explanation: Reinstatement restores lapsed cover from the reinstatement date (not retroactively). The back premium covers the gap period if the insurer agrees to backdate the reinstatement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Policy reinstatements are processed in the policy admin system after the arrear premium and any reinstatement fee are collected. The reinstatement date is the date from which cover is reinstated — not the original lapse date (unless the insurer specifically agrees to backdate, which requires senior underwriting approval). The written premium for the reinstated period is recorded as a new transaction. For life policies, a declaration of health may be required for reinstatement to avoid adverse selection.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that reinstated policies are not granted cover for the gap period (between lapse and reinstatement) unless a specific backdating agreement is in place with supporting documentation and approval. For life policy reinstatements, verify that the health declaration was obtained and assessed before the policy was reinstated — reinstating a policy for a policyholder who became seriously ill during the lapse period is an adverse selection risk. Confirm that reinstatement fees are charged per the policy wording.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Reinstatement request form, arrear premium payment confirmation, reinstatement fee calculation, health declaration (for life policies), underwriting approval for backdated reinstatement (if applicable), and updated policy schedule with new coverage dates.

title: First Notification of Loss (FNOL) — Initial Reserve
description: Setting an initial reserve immediately when a new claim is reported by telephone or online.
explanation: At FNOL, limited information is available. A 'system default reserve' or initial estimate is set based on the coverage and minimal facts. This is refined as the loss adjuster investigates. Speed of FNOL capture is critical for reserve adequacy.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: FNOL is the entry point for all claims in the claims management system. Claims can be notified via phone (call centre), web portal, mobile app, or broker. The FNOL system captures the minimum required data: policyholder, policy number, date of loss, brief description, and initial estimate. The system generates a claim number and sets a default reserve based on the cover type and reported damage. An automatic task is assigned to a claims handler for follow-up. Large losses above a threshold trigger automatic escalation and reinsurance notification.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the timeliness of FNOL capture — delays in registering claims lead to understatement of claim liabilities at period-end. Review FNOL-to-registration lag statistics for each class of business. Test that default reserves at FNOL are set at a level that provides a reasonable starting estimate — systematically low default reserves require constant upward revision and mask the true liability position. Confirm that reinsurance notification triggers are correctly configured in the system.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: FNOL record in the claims system (claim number, notification date, date of loss, initial reserve, assigned handler), default reserve table by cover type, reinsurance notification confirmation for large losses, and FNOL-to-registration timeliness report.

title: Reserve Increase — Adverse Claims Development
description: Increasing a case reserve when new information indicates the claim will cost more than initially estimated.
explanation: Claims development is the process by which initial reserves are refined over time. Adverse development (reserve strengthening) is common for bodily injury and liability claims where final costs are uncertain.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Reserve increases are processed in the claims management system by the claims handler, subject to authorisation limits (e.g., handler: up to JOD 10,000 increase; manager: up to JOD 100,000; Head of Claims: above). Each reserve change is logged with a timestamp, handler ID, and justification note in the claims system audit trail. The reserve change flows automatically to the GL. Management reports track reserve development by accident year and handler to identify patterns of inadequate initial reserving.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the reserve strengthening patterns to identify systematic underreservations — if a handler or class of business consistently shows adverse development, the initial reserving process is inadequate. Test that all reserve increases above the handler's authority have the required manager approval. Confirm that the reserve increase is supported by new factual information (medical reports, legal opinion, court judgment) rather than being a precautionary uplift without basis. Reserve strengthening concentrated in the year-end period may indicate year-end reserve manipulation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Claims reserve change record in the system (showing authorisation chain), supporting documentation for the reserve increase (medical report, legal opinion, updated loss adjuster assessment), reserve development history for the claim, and management reserve development report.

title: Reserve Decrease — Favorable Claims Development
description: Reducing a case reserve when the claim settles for less than the reserve or evidence improves.
explanation: Favorable development is recognized as a credit to claims expense — reducing the expense charge for the period. Strong reserving discipline leads to more favorable development over time.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Reserve reductions are processed in the claims management system with the same authorisation controls as reserve increases. The system audit trail records the reason for the reduction (e.g., medical improvement, out-of-court settlement at a lower amount, liability dispute resolved in insurer's favour). Management reports show both upward and downward reserve movements by class and accident year. Favorable development on prior years is a key component of the underwriting year profitability analysis.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that reserve reductions are based on genuine evidence of reduced exposure, not on management pressure to improve reported results. A pattern of consistent favorable development may indicate that reserves are set conservatively (prudently), which is not prohibited but should be disclosed as an accounting policy. Auditors also check that claims closed with nil payment (where the reserve is fully released) are genuinely nil — not closed prematurely to release reserves artificially.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Claims reserve reduction record with supporting justification (medical report, settlement agreement, liability determination), authorisation chain in the claims system, reserve development history for the claim, and nil-settlement documentation for claims closed with nil payment.

title: Ex-Gratia Payment — No Legal Liability
description: Making a goodwill payment to a claimant when there is no legal obligation under the policy.
explanation: Ex-gratia payments are made for commercial, reputational, or humanitarian reasons without admitting liability. They are disclosed separately as they represent payments outside the policy contract.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Ex-gratia payments are recorded in the claims management system under a separate transaction type (not as a normal claim settlement) to clearly flag that no policy liability is admitted. The payment requires senior management authorisation (typically Head of Claims or CEO) and a formal ex-gratia authority note documenting the commercial justification. In the GL, ex-gratia payments are posted to a separate expense account (not within claims expense) for transparency in financial reporting and regulatory disclosure.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review all ex-gratia payments made during the year for proper authorisation and documentation. Ex-gratia payments without a documented commercial rationale may represent payments for claims that should have been properly assessed under the policy — or potentially fraudulent payments. Confirm that ex-gratia payments are excluded from the claims expense line and separately disclosed in the financial statements or management accounts. Test that no reinsurance recovery has been sought for ex-gratia payments (reinsurers do not cover ex-gratia).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Ex-gratia authority note signed by the approving executive, documentation of the commercial or humanitarian rationale, payment instruction, claimant's receipt (without admission of liability), and GL posting to the separate ex-gratia expense account.

title: Claim Reopened After Closure
description: Reopening and re-reserving a claim that was closed but the insured has submitted additional costs.
explanation: Claims are sometimes closed prematurely and must be reopened. Reopened claims are a quality metric — high reopen rates indicate inadequate initial investigation or premature closure.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Claim reopenings are processed in the claims management system as a status change (from 'closed' to 'open'). The system logs the reopen date, reason, and authorising handler. A new reserve is set based on the additional information received. For claims that were closed as 'nil settlement' and are now being reopened, the system must clearly reflect the original closure and the reopen transaction separately for audit trail integrity. Reopen rates are tracked as a claims quality KPI in management reporting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the reopen rate by handler and by class of business — a high reopen rate on nil settlements may indicate systematic premature closure of claims to release reserves artificially. Test that the reason for reopening is documented and supported by new information or costs. For liability classes, confirm that the statute of limitations has not expired for the claim to be validly reopened — reopening a time-barred claim may not create a valid obligation. Review any clustering of reopenings around the year-end period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Claim closure documentation (original closure reason and date), reopening request with supporting new information or costs, authorisation to reopen, new reserve justification, and claims system status change log.

title: Litigation Reserve — Disputed Claim
description: Setting a reserve for a claim where the insured has initiated legal proceedings against the insurer.
explanation: When a claim is disputed and goes to court, a litigation reserve is set based on legal opinion. Legal costs (LAE) are reserved separately. The provision follows IAS 37 criteria — probable and estimable.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Disputed claims that proceed to litigation are flagged in the claims system under a 'litigation' status and assigned to the legal department or external legal counsel. The litigation reserve is set based on legal counsel's opinion on quantum and probability of an adverse judgment. Legal costs (LAE) are tracked separately as a sub-component of the claim. The legal team provides quarterly updates on litigation progress, and the reserve is revised accordingly. Significant litigation must be disclosed in the financial statement notes under IAS 37.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors obtain confirmations from external legal counsel for all significant litigation matters — legal confirmations are a standard audit procedure confirming the counsel's assessment of the likelihood and quantum of the claim. Reserves that differ materially from legal counsel's assessment require explanation. Assess whether any litigation involves coverage disputes that may affect the validity of the insurer's reserve — if the insurer's coverage is found to be broader than expected, the reserve may be understated.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Legal counsel's opinion letter (liability, quantum, and probability of adverse outcome), litigation reserve calculation, legal cost estimate, quarterly litigation status update reports, IAS 37 disclosure assessment, and any court filings or correspondence received.

title: Large Loss Notification to Reinsurer
description: Recording the notified share of a large loss to be recovered from reinsurers.
explanation: Losses above the retention threshold are notified to reinsurers immediately. The recoverable is set up once the reinsurer acknowledges the notification. Treaty terms determine automatic vs. facultative recovery.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Large loss notifications are triggered automatically by the claims management system when a new reserve exceeds the reinsurance notification threshold (e.g., JOD 100,000 for proportional treaties, or the XL attachment point). The notification is sent to the reinsurer (or through the reinsurance broker) with the claim details. The reinsurance recoverable is set up in the reinsurance accounting module once the notification is confirmed. For XL recoveries, the system must confirm whether the cumulative loss development has exceeded the attachment point.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the completeness of large loss notifications — a claims population above the notification threshold should all have reinsurance recoverables recorded (unless the retention has not been exceeded after netting). Verify that notification was made within the treaty's required timeframe — late notification may give the reinsurer grounds to deny the claim. For XL treaties, confirm that the accumulation of losses from a single event is correctly aggregated to determine whether the attachment point is triggered.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Large loss notification letter (with date sent and reinsurer acknowledgement), treaty notification threshold confirmation, reinsurance recoverable calculation, XL accumulation report (if applicable), and reinsurer's reservation of rights letter (if any).

title: Claim Fraud Identified — Reserve Released
description: Releasing the full reserve when a claim is confirmed as fraudulent and will not be paid.
explanation: When fraud is proven, the reserve is fully released as no payment will be made. The release is a credit to claims expense. Fraud investigation costs (legal, SIU) are expensed separately.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Fraud-identified claims are processed in the claims management system under a 'repudiation on grounds of fraud' status. The full reserve is released only after a formal fraud determination — supported by the Special Investigation Unit (SIU) report and legal counsel's advice. The claim file documents the fraud evidence, the repudiation letter sent to the claimant, and the legal risk of the repudiation being challenged in court. Fraud statistics (number and value of fraud repudiations) are reported to management and the Board.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that fraud repudiations are supported by sufficient evidence — releasing a reserve on suspected fraud without sufficient evidence is inappropriate. Confirm that the repudiation letter was properly issued to the claimant, as failure to formally repudiate may create an implied acceptance. Review any claims where fraud was suspected but not pursued — the reserve should reflect the risk of having to pay even a fraudulent claim if the evidence is insufficient. Track SIU investigation costs separately and confirm they are expensed to LAE, not netted against the fraud reserve release.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SIU investigation report with fraud findings, legal counsel's advice on repudiation, formal repudiation letter sent to claimant, claims system status change to 'repudiated', reserve release calculation, and fraud statistics report.

title: Unexpired Risk Reserve (URR) — Additional Reserve
description: Setting an additional reserve when the UPR is insufficient to cover expected future claims.
explanation: The URR is set when the UPR is expected to be insufficient to cover future claims on current policies. It is required when the expected claims ratio for the remaining period exceeds 100% of the UPR.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The URR (equivalent to the Liability Adequacy Test / LAT under IFRS 4 or the onerous contract test under IFRS 17) is calculated by the actuarial team. The test compares the UPR (unearned premium) to the expected claims and expenses for the unexpired policy period. If the expected future losses exceed the UPR, an additional provision (URR) is required. This is a top-side actuarial journal posted to the GL. Under IFRS 17 PAA, the equivalent concept is the loss component for onerous contract groups.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors ensure that the LAT / URR test is performed for all material lines of business at each reporting date. The test must use the same assumptions as the IBNR calculation (consistency between prospective and retrospective reserving). For lines with deteriorating loss ratios (e.g., medical, motor TPL), the URR may be material and growing. Under IFRS 17, the onerous contract test replaces the URR — auditors verify that the transition from URR to the loss component is correctly implemented.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Actuarial URR / LAT calculation workings, expected claims ratio for the unexpired period, comparison of UPR to expected future losses by line of business, IFRS 17 onerous contract test (if applicable), and actuarial sign-off on the URR amount.

title: Discounted Claims Reserve — Long-Tail Lines
description: Discounting long-tail claims reserves (workers comp, bodily injury) to present value.
explanation: Long-tail claims may not be paid for 5-15 years. Discounting to present value reduces the reserve liability. Under IFRS 17, all long-tail reserves are discounted. Under IFRS 4, discounting is optional but must be disclosed.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Discounting is applied by the actuarial team to the undiscounted best estimate reserve, using a discount rate consistent with the IFRS 17 methodology (risk-free rate adjusted for illiquidity). The discount benefit (the difference between undiscounted and discounted reserves) is recognised as income in the period the discounting is first applied. In subsequent periods, the unwinding of the discount (as the payment date approaches) is recognised as a finance cost. This requires the claims reserve to be maintained on both a discounted and undiscounted basis in the actuarial system.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the discount rate applied is consistent with IFRS 17 requirements and is derived from an observable market yield curve. The payment pattern (timing of future claim payments) used to calculate the discount must be based on the historical claims settlement experience and actuarially reviewed. Confirm that discount unwinding is recognised as a finance cost (not as a reduction in claims expense) — incorrect classification distorts the insurance service result. For lines that were previously undiscounted, auditors assess the impact of introducing discounting and require it to be disclosed as a change in accounting policy.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Discount rate curve (source and derivation), actuarial payment pattern for each long-tail line, undiscounted reserve and discounted reserve schedules, discount benefit reconciliation (period-start to period-end), IFRS 17 finance income/expense disclosure, and comparison to prior period discounting assumptions.

title: IBNER — Incurred But Not Enough Reported
description: Increasing reserves for claims where reported information suggests the reserve is inadequate.
explanation: IBNER covers the gap between what claimants have reported and what the final settlement is expected to be. It is a component of IBNR for claims already reported but where the full extent of loss is not yet known.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: IBNER is calculated by the actuarial team as a component of the overall IBNR reserve. Technically, IBNR comprises two elements: (1) IBNR proper (claims not yet notified) and (2) IBNER (notified claims where the case reserve is insufficient). The actuarial calculation identifies the expected shortfall between case reserves and ultimate losses based on development patterns. The IBNER amount is posted as a top-side journal in the GL and forms part of the total IBNR liability on the balance sheet.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the IBNER component is separately quantified and justified — in practice, some actuaries do not split IBNR and IBNER explicitly, which makes it harder to assess reserve adequacy. Test the IBNER calculation against the case reserve development history — if case reserves consistently develop adversely by a predictable percentage, the IBNER should be calibrated to this percentage. For classes with high proportions of bodily injury claims, IBNER is typically material given the uncertainty in initial case reserves.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Actuarial IBNR/IBNER split report, case reserve development analysis (comparing initial reserves to final outcomes), IBNER calculation methodology, and comparison to prior period IBNER for trend analysis.

title: Run-Off Portfolio — Reserve Transfer
description: Transferring claims reserves for a discontinued line of business to a run-off reserve account.
explanation: When a line of business is discontinued, its remaining liabilities are placed into run-off. Segregating these reserves helps management track the remaining exposure and cost of the discontinued book.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Run-off portfolios are set up as separate line-of-business codes or legal entity codes in the claims management system and GL. The reserve transfer is a reclassification between GL accounts — it does not affect total liabilities. Claims from the run-off book continue to be managed in the claims system but under the run-off flag. A dedicated run-off manager monitors the remaining claims and optimises settlement timing. If the run-off is transferred to a third-party run-off specialist (via a Part VII transfer or portfolio transfer), the reserve is derecognised from the insurer's balance sheet.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the completeness of the run-off reserve — all claims from the discontinued line must be identified and included. Test that no new business has been written under the run-off code. For Part VII portfolio transfers, auditors verify that the regulatory transfer conditions have been met and the liability has been derecognised. Assess the run-off reserve adequacy annually — long-duration run-off reserves for bodily injury or liability claims remain subject to development.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Board resolution to discontinue the line of business, run-off reserve calculation (all outstanding and IBNR claims), regulatory notification of the run-off, Part VII transfer documentation (if applicable), and run-off management plan.

title: Actuarial Assumption Change — Prior Year Development
description: Adjusting reserves due to a change in the mortality, discount rate, or inflation assumption.
explanation: Actuarial assumptions are reviewed annually. A change in mortality rates, interest rates, or cost inflation can materially change the reserve. The impact is recognized in the period the assumption is revised.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Actuarial assumption changes are processed as a top-side journal to the GL, representing the difference between the reserve under the old and new assumptions. The actuarial team documents each assumption change with a before/after impact quantification. Under IFRS 17, assumption changes that relate to future service adjust the CSM (not P&L), while changes that relate to current or past service are recognised in P&L. This distinction is critical and requires the IFRS 17 model to correctly attribute assumption changes to the appropriate component.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors require detailed documentation of each actuarial assumption change, including the rationale and quantified impact. Changes in assumptions must be distinguished from experience variances (actual vs. expected outcomes) — they have different accounting treatments under IFRS 17. The aggregate impact of assumption changes must be disclosed under IFRS 17. Auditors assess whether assumption changes are genuine (based on new information or market data) or are being used to manage reported results.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Actuarial assumption change documentation (old assumption, new assumption, reason for change, quantified impact by assumption), IFRS 17 attribution of assumption change between CSM adjustment and P&L, disclosure in the IFRS 17 insurance contract liability roll-forward, and Board or Audit Committee approval of significant assumption changes.

title: Periodic Payment Order (PPO) Liability
description: Setting a liability for a court-ordered lifetime periodic payment to a seriously injured claimant.
explanation: PPOs replace lump-sum settlements with lifetime annuity payments. The liability is the present value of all future payments, discounted at the risk-free rate. PPOs create very long-tail, inflation-sensitive liabilities.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: PPO liabilities are maintained as separate long-tail claim records in the claims management system, distinct from the standard outstanding reserve. The present value calculation is performed by the actuarial team using the specific payment schedule per the court order, discounted at the appropriate risk-free rate, and adjusted for the claimant's life expectancy. The liability is remeasured at each reporting date as the discount rate and longevity assumptions change. PPO cash flows must also be inflation-indexed if the court order specifies index-linking.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: PPO liabilities are among the most complex and sensitive in insurance accounting — they combine longevity risk (uncertainty in the claimant's lifespan), inflation risk (payment indexation), and interest rate risk (discounting). Auditors require independent actuarial review of each PPO liability. The discount rate must be from an observable market yield curve. Confirm that the insurer has matched assets (e.g., index-linked bonds) against the PPO liability to manage the interest rate and inflation risk. For reinsurance recoveries on PPOs, the reinsurer's portion must also be discounted on the same basis.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Court order specifying the PPO amount, payment frequency, and indexation, claimant's medical assessment and life expectancy, actuarial PPO liability calculation (discounted PV of all future payments), discount rate curve, asset-liability matching analysis, reinsurance recovery calculation on the PPO, and longevity assumption basis.

title: Bond Investment Purchased at Premium
description: Buying a government bond at above par value (premium) for the investment portfolio.
explanation: A JOD 1,000,000 par bond purchased at JOD 1,050,000 carries a JOD 50,000 premium that must be amortized over the bond's remaining life using the EIR method, reducing the effective yield below the coupon rate.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Bond purchases are recorded in the investment accounting system (Bloomberg AIM, SimCorp, or similar) with the purchase price, settlement date, accrued interest at purchase, and computed EIR. The EIR is calculated by the system to equate the present value of all future cash flows (coupon and principal) to the purchase price. The EIR is then used for all subsequent interest income accruals and premium amortisation. The investment is classified at AC (amortised cost) if the business model test (hold to collect) and SPPI test are met.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the bond's classification as AC is supported by the insurer's business model documentation — AC classification requires a 'hold to collect contractual cash flows' business model with no trading intent. Test the EIR calculation for a sample of bonds purchased at premium or discount. Confirm that the SPPI test has been assessed — bonds with non-standard cash flow features (e.g., convertible bonds, step-up coupons with non-market triggers) may fail the SPPI test and must be classified at FVTPL.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bond trade confirmation, settlement statement, EIR calculation schedule, IFRS 9 business model assessment and SPPI test, investment committee approval for large purchases, and investment policy confirming AC classification is consistent with the portfolio's business model.

title: Bond Premium Amortization — Monthly
description: Amortizing the premium paid on a bond purchase, reducing interest income each month.
explanation: Premium amortization reduces the bond's carrying value toward par over its life. Each month, the carrying value decreases and effective interest income is lower than the coupon received.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Bond premium amortisation is calculated and posted automatically by the investment accounting system on a monthly basis using the pre-computed EIR. The system generates the amortisation schedule at purchase and posts the monthly journal automatically. The bond's carrying value in the GL should equal the investment system's amortised cost book value — any difference is a reconciling item requiring investigation. The amortisation schedule is recalculated only if the bond's cash flows are revised (e.g., callable bond called, floating rate bond with rate reset).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors agree the EIR amortisation schedule to the investment system and to the GL carrying value at the period end. Confirm that the amortisation is correctly reducing the carrying value toward par (not away from par). Test that the system has correctly handled any special events in the bond's life (coupon payment dates, ex-dividend dates, partial redemptions). For callable bonds, verify that the EIR is calculated to the first call date if the bond is expected to be called.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bond amortisation schedule (generated at purchase), monthly amortisation journal entries, GL carrying value reconciliation to investment system, bond coupon payment confirmations, and revised amortisation schedule for any bond modifications.

title: Equity Investment Purchase
description: Buying listed equities for the insurer's investment portfolio.
explanation: Equity investments are typically classified at FVTPL for insurers unless the insurer elects FVOCI. FVTPL means all fair value changes and dividends go through the income statement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Equity purchases are recorded in the investment accounting system on trade date, with settlement on T+2 (for most exchanges). The purchase price excludes transaction costs for FVTPL instruments (costs are expensed immediately). For FVOCI equity instruments, transaction costs are included in the initial carrying value. The FVTPL vs. FVOCI classification must be made irrevocably at initial recognition — this is an instrument-level election (not portfolio-level) under IFRS 9. The investment system must tag each equity holding with its classification to apply the correct P&L vs. OCI treatment for subsequent fair value movements.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the FVTPL/FVOCI classification decision was made at initial recognition and is documented per instrument. For FVOCI equities, transaction costs should be in the carrying value — for FVTPL equities, costs are in P&L (verify they are not incorrectly capitalised). Test that transaction costs for listed equities are reasonable (brokerage commission, stamp duty, exchange fees). Confirm that the equity is listed on an approved exchange and within the investment policy limits (e.g., maximum percentage in equities, single-stock concentration limits).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Trade confirmation, settlement statement, transaction cost invoice, IFRS 9 classification election document (FVTPL vs. FVOCI), investment committee approval, investment policy limits compliance, and portfolio valuation at period-end.

title: Equity Sold — Realized Gain
description: Selling shares from the investment portfolio at above their carrying value.
explanation: Realized gains are recognized on disposal. For FVTPL equity, this has already been partially reflected in prior fair value adjustments. The realized gain is the actual cash-out less book value.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Equity disposals are processed in the investment accounting system on trade date, with the gain/loss calculated as the difference between the sale proceeds (net of transaction costs) and the carrying value at the disposal date. For FVTPL equities, the carrying value is the most recent fair value — so the realised gain is only the difference since the last valuation date. For FVOCI equities, the cumulative OCI balance on the sold equity is transferred within equity (not recycled to P&L) at disposal under IFRS 9.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the carrying value used to compute the realized gain is the correct book value at the disposal date (not the original cost). For FVTPL equities, prior fair value movements already in P&L mean the realized gain may be a small amount. For FVOCI equities, confirm that the cumulative OCI is transferred within equity (not taken to P&L) — this is a key IFRS 9 difference from IAS 39. Test that the sale proceeds agree to the broker's contract note.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Broker's sale contract note, settlement confirmation, gain/loss calculation (proceeds less carrying value), investment system disposal record, OCI reclassification entry for FVOCI equities (within equity, not P&L), and investment committee approval for large disposals.

title: Equity Sold — Realized Loss
description: Selling shares below their carrying value, recognizing a realized loss.
explanation: Realized losses reduce investment income. Significant realized losses can trigger solvency concerns if they erode the insurer's capital base below regulatory minimums.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Equity disposal losses are processed in the investment accounting system identically to gains — the difference between sale proceeds and carrying value determines whether a gain or loss is recognised. For FVTPL equities, prior unrealised losses may already be in P&L, meaning the realised loss at disposal is only the incremental movement since the last valuation. For FVOCI equities, cumulative losses in OCI are transferred within equity at disposal (not recycled to P&L) — the P&L only sees the current period fair value movement up to the disposal date.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the impact of realized losses on the insurer's solvency margin — material losses may trigger a regulatory capital breach. Confirm that realized losses have been approved by the investment committee or Board and that they are consistent with the investment strategy. Review whether realized losses indicate impairment in the portfolio that has not yet been recognised on other holdings. For FVOCI equities, verify that losses are not being incorrectly recycled to P&L on disposal.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Broker's sale contract note, settlement confirmation, loss calculation (proceeds less carrying value), investment committee or Board approval (for large disposals), solvency impact assessment, and post-disposal portfolio review.

title: Investment in Associate — Equity Method Pickup
description: Recording the insurer's share of profit from a strategic investment in an associate company.
explanation: Large insurers often have strategic equity stakes in banks, hospitals, or other companies. Under IAS 28, significant influence (20-50% ownership) requires the equity method — the insurer's share of profit increases the investment carrying value.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Equity method investments are recorded in the investment accounting system or a separate investment module at cost at initial recognition, subsequently adjusted for the insurer's share of the associate's net profit/loss and OCI. The insurer must obtain the associate's financial statements (or management accounts) to compute its share of profit. If the associate's reporting period differs from the insurer's, the insurer may use the associate's most recent financial statements (adjusted for significant transactions in the intervening period). Dividends received from associates reduce the carrying value (not income).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that significant influence exists (20-50% ownership, or other indicators per IAS 28) for each equity method investment. Test the equity pickup calculation against the associate's financial statements — the insurer's share of profit must be based on the associate's IFRS (or equivalent) profit, adjusted for purchase price allocation differences (e.g., additional depreciation on fair value uplift at acquisition). Confirm that impairment testing (IAS 36) is performed on equity method investments when there are indicators of impairment.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Ownership percentage confirmation, signed shareholders' agreement confirming significant influence, associate's financial statements, equity pickup calculation (share of profit after PPA adjustments), dividend receipt documentation, IAS 36 impairment assessment, and disclosure in the financial statements of nature and extent of the investment.

title: Investment Property — Monthly Rental Income
description: Earning monthly rental income from commercial properties held as investments.
explanation: Rental income from investment properties is recognized over the lease term. It is disclosed separately from underwriting income to show the investment contribution to total earnings.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Rental income is recognised over the lease term using the straight-line method (or another systematic basis if more representative). Lease incentives (rent-free periods, tenant fit-out contributions) are spread over the lease term. The property management system or AR module tracks lease agreements, billing schedules, and rental receipts. Operating lease income from investment property under IFRS 16 (from the lessor's perspective) uses the IAS 17 operating lease model — rental is straight-lined.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that rental income is recognised on a straight-line basis over the lease term, including any rent-free periods. For multi-year leases with rent escalation clauses, verify that the total expected rental income is spread evenly. Obtain copies of lease agreements for all major tenants and reconcile to rental income recorded. Test for any related-party lease arrangements and confirm they are on arm's-length terms.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed lease agreements for all tenants, rent schedule showing payment amounts and dates, straight-line rental income calculation (for leases with varying payments), rental income reconciliation to cash received, and disclosure of significant lease terms in the financial statements.

title: Short-Term Deposit — Interest Accrual
description: Accruing interest earned on short-term bank deposits held for liquidity management.
explanation: Short-term deposits are a core liquidity management tool. Interest is accrued monthly. Insurers must comply with regulatory asset allocation rules — typically a minimum percentage in liquid assets.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Short-term deposit interest is accrued in the GL based on the deposit amount, agreed interest rate, and number of days. The accrued interest receivable clears on the deposit maturity date when the bank transfers the interest and principal. In SAP, deposits are managed via the Treasury module (TRM) with automatic interest accrual based on the deposit deal record. Insurers must maintain a register of all short-term deposits including institution, amount, maturity date, and rate — this is submitted to the IRC as part of the regulatory asset declaration.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm deposits and interest by obtaining bank confirmation letters directly from the depositary bank (positive confirmation). Verify that interest rates are consistent with prevailing market rates — below-market rate deposits with related-party banks require additional scrutiny. Confirm that deposits are within the concentration limits per institution as required by the IRC investment regulations. Test that deposits are classified as current assets if maturing within 12 months, and that there are no undisclosed encumbrances on the deposits (e.g., pledged as collateral).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Deposit receipts or bank certificates, deposit register (institution, amount, maturity, rate), bank confirmation letter, interest accrual calculation, IRC regulatory asset declaration, and concentration limit compliance schedule.

title: External Investment Management Fee Paid
description: Paying the quarterly fee to an external fund manager for managing the bond portfolio.
explanation: Investment management fees are an investment-related operating expense. They are netted against investment income in financial reporting to show the net investment return.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Investment management fees are typically calculated as a percentage of assets under management (AUM) and invoiced quarterly by the fund manager. The fee is posted to the investment expense account and the AP sub-ledger. For performance fee arrangements, the fee accrual is more complex — performance fees are only accrued when the performance target is virtually certain to be met. The investment management agreement must be on file and reviewed periodically by the investment committee.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the investment management fee rate matches the signed investment management agreement. For AUM-based fees, verify the AUM basis used by the fund manager against the insurer's own portfolio records. Performance fees require careful assessment — premature accrual of performance fees that may not be earned is a known audit risk. Confirm that the investment management arrangements comply with the IRC's requirements for insurer investment management (some regulators restrict external fund management of insurance assets).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed investment management agreement (specifying fee rate, basis, and performance conditions), quarterly fund manager invoice with AUM and fee calculation, IRC compliance confirmation, performance fee accrual calculation (if applicable), and investment committee review of the fund manager's performance.

title: Investment Portfolio Rebalancing
description: Selling bonds and buying equities to restore the target asset allocation.
explanation: Portfolio rebalancing maintains the insurer's target asset allocation (e.g., 60% bonds, 30% equity, 10% real estate). Rebalancing transactions trigger realized gains/losses on disposed assets.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Portfolio rebalancing is initiated by the investment manager following a review of the current vs. target asset allocation. The investment committee approves the rebalancing trades, which are then executed through the insurer's brokers and recorded in the investment accounting system. For bonds classified at AC (amortised cost), frequent sales to rebalance may raise questions about the 'hold to collect' business model — too many sales may require reclassification of the entire portfolio to FVTPL.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the frequency and volume of bond sales from the AC portfolio are inconsistent with a 'hold to collect' business model under IFRS 9. IFRS 9 allows AC classification only if more than an infrequent number of sales occur, and sales for asset-liability management purposes are permitted. However, if sales are frequent and significant, the business model must be reassessed. Confirm that rebalancing trades are within the investment policy approved by the Board and that the resulting allocation is within regulatory limits.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Investment committee approval of the rebalancing decision, target asset allocation policy, trade confirmations for all disposal and purchase transactions, realized gain/loss calculations, post-rebalancing allocation report confirming compliance with investment policy, and IFRS 9 business model reassessment if sales frequency is increasing.

title: Facultative Reinsurance — Single Risk Certificate
description: Ceding a specific large commercial property risk on a facultative (case-by-case) basis.
explanation: Facultative reinsurance is placed risk-by-risk when the risk exceeds treaty limits or requires specialist reinsurer expertise. Each certificate is a separate contract, more expensive than treaty but provides targeted capacity.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Facultative cessions are recorded in the reinsurance accounting module as individual contracts (not part of the treaty bordereaux). Each facultative certificate has its own ceded premium, UPR ceded, and claims recoverable tracking. The facultative placement must be completed (signed slip received) before the policy is bound — the reinsurer's capacity is specific to the risk. Facultative premiums are typically paid at inception (not quarterly like treaty premiums). The reinsurance payable is cleared upon payment.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the facultative certificate is signed and effective before the underlying policy inception date — binding a risk without confirmed facultative capacity is a net risk exposure. Verify that the facultative premium and terms match the signed slip. Confirm that large losses on facultatively reinsured risks have been properly notified to the facultative reinsurer per the certificate terms. Review any facultative placements with related-party reinsurers for arm's-length terms.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed facultative reinsurance certificate (slip), underlying policy schedule, facultative premium calculation, broker's placement confirmation, payment of facultative premium, and large loss notification procedure per the certificate.

title: Inward Reinsurance Accepted — Premium Income
description: Acting as a reinsurer and accepting cessions from other insurance companies.
explanation: Large insurers also act as reinsurers, accepting risk from smaller companies. This is inward (or assumed) reinsurance. The premium income and associated claims are tracked separately from the direct insurance book.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Inward (accepted) reinsurance is maintained in a separate book of business within the reinsurance accounting module, distinct from the insurer's direct writing. The assumed premium income, UPR, and claims reserves are tracked by treaty and cedant. Bordereaux received from cedants are processed to update the assumed book. The insurer may also place retrocession on the assumed book if the accumulated exposure exceeds its net retention. Regulatory reporting for both direct and assumed business must be filed separately with the IRC.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that inward reinsurance is properly segregated from direct business in all financial reporting — combined ratios and reserving must be presented separately or the combined figures disclosed. Verify that bordereaux received from cedants are reconciled to the assumed premium income. For assumed business from related-party cedants, confirm that the terms are on arm's-length basis and disclosed. Assess the quality of IBNR for the assumed book — typically more difficult due to limited access to the cedant's underlying claim data.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Accepted reinsurance treaty for each cedant, bordereaux received and reconciliation, cedant credit assessment, assumed UPR and IBNR calculations, retrocession placement confirmation, and regulatory filing for assumed business.

title: Inward Reinsurance — Claim Paid to Cedant
description: Paying a claim to the original insurer (cedant) under an accepted reinsurance treaty.
explanation: When the reinsurer accepts claims from cedants, the claims are expensed against the inward reinsurance premium income. Combined ratios are calculated separately for the direct and assumed reinsurance books.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Claims on the assumed reinsurance book are received from cedants via loss advices. The claims are entered into the reinsurance claims module and validated against the treaty terms (retention, cession percentage, attachment and limit for XL). Payment to the cedant is authorised by the claims department after treaty validation and processed through AP. The combined ratio for the assumed book is reported separately from the direct book in management accounts.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test assumed reinsurance claims against the treaty terms — the reinsurer pays only its share per the accepted treaty. Verify that the cedant's loss advice is supported by adequate documentation of the underlying loss. Confirm that the assumed claims reserves are adequate — the reinsurer's access to underlying claim data is limited, requiring greater reliance on the cedant's reserves, which may be less conservative. Review the commutation option in any assumed treaties — commuting a treaty at a discount reduces the exposure but creates a settlement gain/loss.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Loss advice from cedant, treaty terms confirming the reinsurer's share, claims validation against treaty, payment authorisation, cedant's claims documentation (summary of underlying losses), assumed claims reserve, and retrocession recovery calculation (if retrocession applies).

title: Retrocession Premium Paid
description: A reinsurer further cedes its own accepted reinsurance risk to a retrocessionnaire.
explanation: Retrocession is 'reinsurance of reinsurance.' Large reinsurers use retrocession to further distribute risk. It is the final layer of risk transfer in the global insurance/reinsurance chain.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Retrocession is accounted for in the same way as outward reinsurance from the cedant's perspective — the retrocession premium is an expense, the retrocession payable is a liability, and retrocession recoveries are assets. The retrocession module in the reinsurance accounting system calculates the retrocession cession from the net assumed position after direct writing. The retrocession premium is typically settled quarterly with the retrocessionnaire.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that retrocession contracts are valid and signed before any retrocession premiums are accrued. The risk transfer test (IFRS 17 significant insurance risk transfer) must be met for the retrocession arrangement to be accounted for as reinsurance rather than as a deposit/financing arrangement. Verify that the retrocession accounting is gross (not netted against the assumed book). For spiral or convergence retrocession arrangements (where the retrocessionnaire and cedant are ultimately part of the same market loop), additional scrutiny is warranted.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed retrocession treaty or slip, risk transfer assessment confirming the arrangement qualifies as reinsurance, retrocession premium calculation, cession bordereaux, retrocession payable settlement, and retrocession recovery amounts claimed.

title: Reinsurance Bad Debt — Reinsurer Default Provision
description: Provisioning for potential non-recovery from a reinsurer showing financial distress.
explanation: Reinsurance recoverables must be assessed for credit risk under IFRS 9. If a reinsurer is downgraded or shows distress, an ECL provision reduces the net recoverable. Reinsurer counterparty risk is a significant balance sheet concern.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The ECL provision on reinsurance recoverables is calculated by the credit risk team or actuarial team using the reinsurer's credit rating, probability of default, and loss given default. Most insurers use a simplified approach (provision matrix based on credit rating categories) rather than a full expected credit loss model. The ECL is posted as a contra-asset (allowance against the gross recoverable) and the impairment expense is recognised in P&L. The ECL assessment must be updated at each reporting date for changes in the reinsurer's credit profile.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the credit rating of all reinsurers with material recoverable balances and assess the adequacy of the ECL provision. A reinsurer downgraded to below investment grade (BB or lower) requires a significantly higher ECL provision. Confirm that the ECL model is based on IFRS 9 methodology and not merely an allowance for overdue balances (IAS 39 approach). Assess the concentration risk — a large proportion of recoverable from a single reinsurer represents significant counterparty risk that should be disclosed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Reinsurer credit rating report (from S&P, Moody's, AM Best), ECL provision model and calculation, reinsurance recoverable schedule by reinsurer, concentration analysis by reinsurer group, and comparison to prior period ECL provision.

title: Commutation Agreement — Reinsurance Settlement
description: Settling all past and future claims under a reinsurance treaty for a single lump sum.
explanation: A commutation extinguishes the reinsurance relationship for a fixed payment. It provides certainty for both parties but involves accepting a discount. The difference between the recoverable and the settlement is a gain or loss.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Commutation agreements are complex transactions requiring legal, actuarial, and financial sign-off. The commuted recoverable is derecognised from the balance sheet on the commutation effective date, and the cash received is recorded. If the commutation payment is less than the carrying value of the recoverable (net of any ECL provision), the shortfall is a commutation loss in P&L. The commutation agreement terminates the reinsurer's obligations for all past and future claims under the treaty — the insurer must ensure its own reserves are adequate after the reinsurance protection is removed.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the commutation agreement and the actuarial assessment of the liabilities being commuted. The commutation loss (or gain) should be assessed for reasonableness against the ECL provision already recognised — if the commutation loss exceeds the prior ECL provision, the prior period provisions may have been inadequate. Confirm that Board approval was obtained for the commutation, as it is a significant strategic decision. Verify that the insurer's own gross reserves are sufficient to cover the liabilities without the reinsurance protection after commutation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed commutation agreement, actuarial valuation of the liabilities being commuted, Board approval resolution, cash receipt confirmation, commutation gain/loss calculation, and post-commutation reserve adequacy assessment.

title: Underwriter & Actuary Staff Payroll
description: Recording monthly gross salaries for technical insurance staff.
explanation: Technical staff (underwriters, actuaries, risk analysts) are the core human capital of an insurer. Their salaries are typically allocated between policy acquisition and management expenses in IFRS 17 reporting.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Payroll is processed through the HR/payroll system (SAP HR, Oracle HCM, or a dedicated payroll platform) and posted to the GL via a payroll journal. Underwriting and actuarial staff costs are recorded in dedicated cost centres. For IFRS 17, time-allocation studies or activity-based costing are used to split staff costs between: (a) insurance acquisition cash flows (DAC-eligible costs for IFRS 17), (b) insurance service expenses (policyholder service and claims management costs), and (c) investment activity costs. Payroll for the actuarial team is partially an insurance service expense and partially an investment expense.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test payroll postings to the underwriting cost centre against HR records for headcount accuracy. Confirm that the IFRS 17 cost allocation between acquisition, service, and investment is based on a documented and reasonable methodology (e.g., time surveys or activity drivers). Review staff bonus accruals at year-end — confirm the basis and consistency of bonus provision. Verify that staff authorised to bind risk (underwriters) are within their individual delegated underwriting authority.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Payroll run report, HR headcount and job role classification, IFRS 17 cost allocation methodology and split, bonus provision calculation, and delegated underwriting authority (DUA) schedule for underwriting staff.

title: Claims Handlers & Loss Adjusters Payroll
description: Recording monthly salaries for the claims department.
explanation: Claims staff costs are part of Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE) and should be allocated to the claims expense line. Efficient claims handling directly reduces the combined ratio.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Claims department payroll is posted to the claims cost centre and allocated to the LAE (Unallocated Loss Adjustment Expense, or ULAE) pool. ULAE is then allocated to individual claim reserves on a systematic basis (e.g., as a percentage of outstanding claims or a ratio of paid to total claims activity).
