Usage data (CDRs — Call Detail Records) is aggregated and fed to the revenue assurance system, which validates usage against network data and produces a daily revenue recognition journal. The journal moves the consumed amount from deferred revenue to the appropriate revenue account (voice, data, SMS). Revenue is reported by service type and geography in the management information system.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the completeness of revenue recognition by reconciling CDR-based consumption data to the GL revenue entries. Revenue assurance reports (comparing network-generated traffic data to billed data) are key evidence. Test for any gap between network traffic and billed revenue — unexplained gaps may indicate fraud or system errors. Confirm that zero-rated traffic (promotions, free data bundles) is correctly excluded from revenue recognition.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: CDR aggregation report, revenue assurance reconciliation (network traffic vs. billed revenue), daily revenue recognition journal, deferred revenue movement schedule, zero-rated traffic exclusion documentation, and fraud detection report.

title: Bundle Allocation — Handset and Plan (IFRS 15)
description: Splitting a 24-month contract bundle between the handset (hardware) and the ongoing service plan.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, a handset + plan bundle is two distinct performance obligations. The transaction price is allocated based on standalone selling prices. The handset component is recognized immediately at delivery; the service plan is deferred and released monthly.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Bundle allocation under IFRS 15 requires a separate revenue allocation engine or module (often built on top of SAP RAR — Revenue Accounting and Reporting, or Zuora Revenue). The standalone selling prices (SSPs) for the handset and service plan are maintained in the revenue engine configuration. When a bundle is sold, the system automatically allocates the total contract value to each performance obligation based on SSPs and posts the handset revenue immediately while deferring the service revenue. Contract assets are created where revenue is recognised before billing.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review and challenge the SSP determination methodology — SSPs must reflect the price at which the operator would sell each element standalone. Test the allocation calculation on a sample of bundle contracts. Confirm that the contract asset (handset revenue recognised but not yet billed) is correctly presented on the balance sheet. Assess whether the SSP for the handset reflects the actual standalone device price in the market, not an artificially low figure that shifts revenue to the more lucrative service stream.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SSP determination methodology and supporting market price data, revenue allocation engine configuration, bundle contract sample with allocation calculation, contract asset schedule, IFRS 15 accounting policy documentation, and SSP annual review.

title: Data Overage Revenue
description: Billing a customer for data usage beyond their monthly plan allowance.
explanation: Overage charges are variable consideration under IFRS 15 — recognized when the usage occurs and the amount is determinable. Telecoms face constraints in recognizing overage estimates before they are confirmed.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Data overage is tracked in real-time by the OCS, which monitors each subscriber's data consumption against their plan allowance. When the allowance is exhausted, the OCS either applies the overage rate automatically or notifies the customer and requires them to opt in. Overage charges are billed at month-end as part of the postpaid invoice. The CBS generates the overage charge and posts it to the AR sub-ledger. Revenue is recognised when the usage occurs, confirmed by CDR data.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test overage revenue by reconciling CDR data to billed overage charges. Confirm that the overage rate applied matches the subscriber's tariff. For regulators that cap data overage charges, verify that the operator is compliant with the regulatory ceiling. Test that customers who have purchased additional data packs mid-month are not also charged overage for the same period. Review the volume of overage disputes — high dispute rates indicate billing accuracy issues.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Overage CDR report, CBS billing run showing overage charges by subscriber, overage rate tariff schedule, regulatory cap compliance confirmation, overage dispute register, and add-on data pack purchase records.

title: International Roaming Revenue
description: Billing a home-network customer for calls and data used while roaming on a foreign network.
explanation: Roaming revenue is charged to the home customer at the roaming rates. The home operator then settles the underlying termination cost with the visited network operator in a separate interoperator settlement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Roaming CDRs from visited networks are received through the GSMA TAP (Transferred Account Procedures) clearing system. The home operator's billing system processes the TAP files, applies the home customer's roaming tariff, and generates the charge on the customer's next invoice. Revenue is recognised when the TAP file is processed — typically one to four weeks after the actual roaming event. The delay between roaming event and billing means a month-end accrual is required for unprocessed TAP files. The interoperator settlement (paying the visited network) is a separate cost entry.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the TAP file processing completeness — all files received from visited networks should be processed and billed. Test the month-end roaming revenue accrual for unbilled TAP files. Confirm that the roaming rate applied to subscribers matches the tariff schedule. Assess the roaming revenue cut-off — late TAP files relating to prior periods must be correctly allocated. Review the receivable from the visited network (for inbound roaming) separately from the customer receivable (for outbound roaming).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TAP file receipt and processing log, roaming revenue billing report, month-end accrual for unbilled TAP files, customer tariff schedule for roaming, interoperator settlement accounts, and GSMA clearing house reconciliation.

title: Contract Modification — Customer Upgrades Plan
description: A customer mid-contract upgrades from a basic plan to a premium plan.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, an upgrade that adds distinct goods/services is treated as a new contract. The old remaining deferred balance is closed and the new higher-value plan commences.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Contract modifications are processed in the CBS as a plan change event. The revenue accounting system (SAP RAR or equivalent) identifies the modification type under IFRS 15 — either a new contract (if distinct goods/services are added at their standalone price) or a cumulative catch-up adjustment to the existing contract (if not distinct). For a straightforward upgrade, the system closes the old deferred revenue balance and creates a new deferred balance for the upgraded plan. The incremental billing is posted to the AR sub-ledger. Volume of modifications is tracked for financial reporting disclosures.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the IFRS 15 modification accounting policy applied to upgrades — is the upgrade treated as a new contract or a modification of the existing one? Test the mechanical accuracy of the deferred revenue reset for a sample of mid-contract upgrades. Confirm that any contract assets from the original bundle allocation are also reassessed at modification. Review whether customer churn patterns around mid-contract upgrades indicate that the upgrade terms are commercially incentivised.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IFRS 15 contract modification accounting policy, CBS plan change report, revenue system modification event log, deferred revenue reset journal, contract asset reassessment at modification, and modification volume and value statistics.

title: Prepaid Credit Breakage — Expired Unused Credit
description: Recognizing revenue from prepaid balances that have expired without being used.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, breakage is estimated using historical data and recognized as revenue over the expected period of use. Significant breakage is common — many customers top up more than they use.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Breakage revenue is estimated using statistical analysis of historical prepaid balance expiry data in the billing system. The breakage rate (proportion of top-ups expected never to be consumed) is calculated by customer segment and top-up denomination. Under IFRS 15, the estimated breakage is recognised in proportion to consumption patterns — not solely when balances formally expire. The revenue accounting system calculates the monthly breakage revenue release and posts it to the GL. Where expiry policies are changed (e.g., extending validity), the breakage estimate must be updated.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the breakage rate assumption against actual historical expiry data. The rate should be based on sufficient statistical evidence and updated annually. Confirm that the proportional recognition method is applied (not the expiry method) per IFRS 15. Test whether breakage estimates are being used to accelerate revenue recognition — a breakage rate that is too high overstates revenue and understates liabilities. Review regulatory requirements on unused prepaid credit — some jurisdictions require operators to refund or donate expired credit rather than recognise it as revenue.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Breakage rate analysis (historical data and statistical methodology), IFRS 15 breakage accounting policy, breakage revenue recognition schedule, regulatory compliance confirmation on expired credit treatment, prepaid balance expiry report, and annual breakage rate review.

title: Interconnect / Termination Income
description: Earning termination revenue when another operator's calls are terminated on the network.
explanation: When a caller on Network A calls a subscriber on Network B, Network A pays Network B a termination rate. This wholesale revenue is settled monthly between operators based on CDR (Call Detail Record) data.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Interconnect traffic is measured by the network mediation system, which aggregates CDRs by originating operator and traffic type (fixed, mobile, international). The interconnect billing system generates monthly statements (SDRs — Settlement Detail Records) for each interconnect partner. Revenue is accrued monthly based on internal CDR data and confirmed by exchange with counterparties. The interconnect billing system feeds to the ERP for AR posting. In Jordan, interconnect rates (MTR — Mobile Termination Rates) are set by the TRC.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test interconnect revenue by reconciling the internal CDR-based accrual to the signed monthly statements exchanged with each operator. Disputed volumes require specific assessment. Confirm that the termination rate applied matches the signed interconnect agreement or the TRC-regulated MTR. Test for any netting of interconnect income and expense in the P&L — IFRS requires gross presentation. Review the aged interconnect receivable for disputed or long-outstanding items.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: CDR aggregation report by originating operator, signed monthly settlement statements (SDRs), interconnect agreement with applicable rates, TRC MTR rate schedule, aged interconnect receivable report, and dispute log with counterparties.

title: Interconnect Expense — Origination Cost
description: Paying origination charges to another network for calls originated on their platform.
explanation: Interconnect is both an income and expense. Net interconnect position depends on traffic flows. Mobile operators in small markets often pay more in origination than they earn in termination.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Interconnect costs are accrued monthly based on CDR data showing traffic originated on the operator's network and terminated on other networks. The interconnect billing system calculates the expected payment to each counterparty and posts it to the AP sub-ledger. The expense is classified in the direct cost / cost of service line in the P&L. Settlement is typically monthly via bank transfer, with the payable cleared on payment. Disputes with counterparties on volume or rate result in provision adjustments.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that interconnect costs are presented gross (not netted against interconnect income). Test that the termination rate applied in the cost calculation matches the signed agreement with each counterparty. Confirm that CDR data underpinning the cost accrual is complete and has been reconciled to the network mediation system. Review the interconnect payable aging for long-outstanding balances that may indicate disputes. For international interconnect, test that WHT is correctly applied to payments to foreign operators.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: CDR aggregation report for originated traffic, interconnect cost accrual calculation, signed agreements with termination rates, AP sub-ledger posting, reconciliation of cost accrual to counterparty statements, dispute log, and WHT calculation for international payments.

title: National Roaming Income
description: Earning income from a smaller operator who uses the network's infrastructure for domestic coverage.
explanation: National roaming agreements allow smaller operators (like MVNOs) to use an established network. The host charges a per-minute or per-GB rate. This is pure wholesale revenue with very low incremental cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: National roaming traffic is tracked by the host network's mediation system, which captures all traffic from MVNO/roaming partner SIMs. Monthly usage is aggregated and billed to the roaming partner per the wholesale agreement. The billing system posts the receivable and revenue to the GL. National roaming revenue is classified separately from retail revenue in management reporting as it is wholesale in nature. The incremental cost of carrying the roaming partner's traffic is typically very low — making this a high-margin revenue stream.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that the wholesale rate in the billing system matches the signed national roaming agreement. Test traffic volume reconciliation between the host network's mediation system and the MVNO/partner's own traffic records. Assess whether the arrangement is accounted for correctly under IFRS 15 — if the host operator controls the network service before transferring to the MVNO, gross presentation applies. If the host is purely providing access to infrastructure, the accounting may differ.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: National roaming agreement (rates, minimum commitments, settlement terms), mediation system traffic report by partner SIM, monthly invoice to roaming partner, receivable reconciliation, IFRS 15 principal/agent assessment, and partner traffic reconciliation.

title: Wholesale Capacity Lease — IP Transit
description: Leasing internet bandwidth capacity to ISPs and enterprise clients.
explanation: Wholesale capacity leases are recurring monthly revenue. They are recognized over the lease term as bandwidth is provided. This is core to any telecom with excess network capacity.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Wholesale capacity contracts are managed in the operator's wholesale billing system, which generates monthly invoices based on contracted capacity (Mbps or Gbps) at the agreed rate. Revenue is recognised over the contract period as capacity is made available. For multi-year capacity contracts, IFRS 16 must be assessed — if the customer has the right to use a specifically identified network segment, the arrangement may contain a lease. Upfront activation or installation fees are deferred under IFRS 15 if they relate to future capacity provision.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the IFRS 16 embedded lease assessment for wholesale capacity contracts — dedicated dark fibre or identified wavelength arrangements are likely leases, while generic internet transit is typically a service. Test revenue recognition for capacity agreed but not yet activated — revenue should only commence when capacity is available to the customer. Confirm that multi-year contracts with built-in rate escalations are correctly accounted for over the contract term.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Wholesale capacity contract, IFRS 16 embedded lease assessment, monthly billing and revenue recognition schedule, capacity activation confirmation, installation fee deferral schedule, and wholesale receivable aging.

title: IRU — Indefeasible Right of Use (Fiber Capacity)
description: Selling a 20-year IRU on fiber capacity to another operator.
explanation: An IRU grants a customer the right to use fiber capacity for a long term (15-25 years). The upfront lump sum is deferred and recognized ratably over the contract term under IFRS 15.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: IRU arrangements require careful accounting analysis — they may be classified as a sale, a service contract (deferred revenue recognised over the term), or a finance lease under IFRS 16, depending on whether the customer obtains control of an identified asset for substantially all of the economic life. Most modern IRU arrangements over dedicated dark fibre are classified as a lease under IFRS 16 from the buyer's perspective and as a finance/operating lease from the seller's perspective. The classification drives whether the seller recognises upfront revenue or defers it. The IRU management system tracks each agreement's term, capacity, and revenue recognition schedule.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors perform a detailed IFRS 15/16 classification analysis for each IRU — is it a service (revenue deferred over term), a sale of goods (revenue recognised upfront), or a lease (IFRS 16 treatment)? The specific facts of the agreement (identified asset, substitution rights, direction of use) determine the conclusion. This is one of the most technically complex areas in telecom accounting. Test that the deferred revenue release matches the term of each IRU. Confirm that IRU arrangements are disclosed in the financial statement notes.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IRU agreement (term, capacity specification, substitution rights, renewal options), IFRS 15/16 classification analysis, deferred revenue recognition schedule, annual revenue release journal, IRU register with term and NBV of deferred balance, and financial statement disclosure.

title: Mobile Number Portability (MNP) Fee
description: Recording the administrative fee charged when a customer ports their number to another operator.
explanation: MNP fees are recognized immediately since the porting service is a point-in-time obligation. Some jurisdictions do not allow charging for MNP — making this entry applicable only where regulatory permitted.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: MNP fees are charged through the CBS when the porting process is initiated or completed, depending on the operator's policy and regulatory framework. In Jordan, the TRC regulates the MNP process and any applicable administrative fee. The fee is posted to the revenue account immediately as the performance obligation (processing the port) is satisfied at a point in time. The MNP administration system logs all porting requests (in and out) and interfaces with the CBS for billing.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that MNP fees are only charged where permitted by the TRC regulatory framework. Test that the fee amount matches the approved regulatory or contractual limit. Reconcile the number of MNP transactions processed to the fee income recognised. For ports-out, confirm that the service is terminated and no further subscription revenue is recognised after the effective porting date. Review the porting-out volume as a customer retention metric for management reporting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TRC regulatory confirmation of permissible MNP fee, CBS porting transaction log, MNP revenue posting, reconciliation of porting volume to fee income, porting-out subscriber deactivation records, and porting-in new subscriber activation records.

title: Spectrum License Capitalization
description: Recording the acquisition of a 4G/5G spectrum license from the regulator.
explanation: Spectrum is the most valuable asset of a mobile operator. License costs are capitalized as an intangible asset and amortized over the license period (typically 15-25 years for 4G/5G).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Spectrum license costs are capitalised in the intangible assets module of the ERP (SAP AM or Oracle Fixed Assets) as soon as the license is awarded and payment is due. Where the license fee is payable in instalments, each instalment is capitalised when due. The license is assigned a useful life equal to the license term. Amortisation commences when the network is commercially operational on the licensed spectrum (not when the license is awarded). License renewal costs are assessed separately — they may be capitalised as a new asset or treated as a continuation of the existing license.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the license cost includes all directly attributable costs (license fee, legal costs, regulatory compliance costs directly related to the license award). Test that amortisation commences on the commercial launch date, not the license award date. Assess whether the useful life assumption matches the full license term or a shorter period if renewal is uncertain. For licenses paid in instalments, confirm that the liability to the regulator is correctly classified (current vs. non-current based on payment schedule). Test for impairment indicators — spectrum covering geographic areas with lower-than-expected network utilisation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TRC license award letter, license fee payment schedule, intangible asset register entry, commercial launch date confirmation, amortisation schedule, license renewal terms, impairment assessment, and Board approval of the spectrum acquisition.

title: Spectrum License — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the spectrum license cost over its licensed period.
explanation: An JOD 80M spectrum license over 20 years amortizes at JOD 333,333/month. This is one of the largest non-cash expenses in telecom, significantly impacting EBITDA-to-EBIT conversion.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Spectrum amortisation is calculated automatically by the intangible asset module using the straight-line method over the license term. The monthly amortisation charge is posted to the intangible amortisation expense account. Spectrum amortisation is a major bridge item in the EBITDA-to-EBIT reconciliation — investors and analysts closely track this in telecom financial models. Multiple spectrum bands (800MHz, 1800MHz, 2100MHz, 3.5GHz) may have different license terms and therefore different monthly amortisation rates.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the amortisation rate against the license term (total cost / number of months in license period). Test that amortisation has not commenced before the commercial launch date. If the license is renewed at a cost, assess whether the renewal cost should be added to the existing asset (extending its life) or treated as a new asset. Test that spectrum in bands where the network is no longer commercially operated is assessed for impairment. Review the presentation of spectrum amortisation in segment reporting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Intangible asset register with spectrum license entries, amortisation schedule by spectrum band, commercial launch date for each band, license term and amortisation rate calculation, renewal cost assessment (if applicable), impairment test for underutilised spectrum, and EBITDA-to-EBIT bridge in management accounts.

title: Tower / BTS Equipment Purchase
description: Purchasing a new base transceiver station (BTS) for network expansion.
explanation: Network equipment (BTS, antennas, routers) is capitalized as PPE. Telecoms have massive ongoing capex requirements to maintain and upgrade their networks — this is the core capital-intensive nature of the industry.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Network equipment procurement is managed through the supply chain and capital project management systems (Oracle Projects or SAP PS). Each BTS installation is tracked as a capital project in the WBS (Work Breakdown Structure). Costs accumulate in a capital work-in-progress (CWIP) account during installation. On commissioning and acceptance test sign-off, the CWIP is transferred to the network equipment asset class and depreciation commences. The network asset register is maintained in the ERP and reconciled to the network management system's site inventory.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the CWIP-to-fixed-asset transfer process — assets should only be transferred on commissioning, not earlier. Confirm that capitalisation only includes directly attributable costs (equipment cost, installation labour, site preparation, commissioning). Internal labour costs capitalised must be verified against timesheets. Test the depreciation start date aligns with the commissioning date. Perform physical inspection of a sample of BTS sites to confirm existence and operational status.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Purchase order and vendor invoice, capital project WBS record, commissioning and acceptance test certificate, CWIP-to-fixed-asset transfer journal, network asset register entry, depreciation start date confirmation, and physical asset verification report.

title: Network Equipment — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on all active network infrastructure.
explanation: Network depreciation is the largest expense line for most telecoms. 4G equipment typically depreciates over 7-10 years. High depreciation drives the classic telecom EBITDA focus rather than net profit.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Network depreciation is calculated by the fixed asset module automatically for each asset, using the configured useful life per asset category (radio access network: 7–10 years, transmission: 10–15 years, core network: 7–10 years, IT systems: 3–5 years). Monthly depreciation journals are generated in batch and posted to the GL. The depreciation run is reconciled to the asset register. Technology refresh decisions are informed by the residual net book value of existing equipment — accelerated write-downs may be required when technology is retired early.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the useful life assumptions against industry norms and the operator's own technology refresh history. If the operator is deploying 5G, remaining 4G equipment useful lives should be reassessed — an unrealistically long residual life overstates asset values. Test that fully depreciated assets still in service are disclosed. Review the useful life policy for consistency across all sites. Confirm that accelerated depreciation on assets being replaced or decommissioned is recognised promptly.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fixed asset register with asset categories and useful lives, monthly depreciation run report, useful life policy document, technology refresh plan, accelerated depreciation calculations for assets being decommissioned, and EBITDA-to-EBIT bridge in management accounts.

title: Tower Site Lease — IFRS 16 Initial Recognition
description: Recognizing a Right-of-Use asset and lease liability when signing a tower site lease.
explanation: Mobile operators lease thousands of tower sites. IFRS 16 requires almost all leases to be on-balance-sheet. A large operator with 2,000 tower leases could have hundreds of millions in ROU assets and lease liabilities.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tower site leases are managed in a lease administration system (e.g., CoStar, LeaseAccelerator, or SAP RE-FX) integrated with the ERP. When a new lease is executed, the system calculates the present value of future lease payments (using the incremental borrowing rate or implicit rate) and creates the ROU asset and lease liability. The lease liability is amortised using the effective interest method. The ROU asset is depreciated straight-line over the shorter of the lease term and the useful life. Lease modifications (rent reviews, extensions) trigger remeasurement of the liability and ROU asset.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the completeness of the lease population — all tower site leases must be captured in the lease system. Test the discount rate used (IBR should reflect the operator's borrowing rate for a similar term and collateral). Confirm the lease term assumption — if renewal options are reasonably certain to be exercised, the lease term includes the renewal period. For very large tower lease portfolios, auditors apply statistical sampling techniques to test the accuracy of the lease system. Review lease modifications and reassess the accounting treatment.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed tower site lease agreements, lease administration system record, IBR determination methodology (per lease term and currency), ROU asset and lease liability roll-forward schedule, lease modification log, and IFRS 16 transition documentation.

title: Tower Site ROU Asset — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation of the right-of-use asset for a tower site lease.
explanation: An JOD 180,000 ROU asset over a 5-year tower lease depreciates at JOD 3,000/month. Tower depreciation is the largest component of lease-related depreciation for mobile operators.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: ROU asset depreciation is calculated by the lease administration system or the fixed asset module using the straight-line method over the lease term. The depreciation is posted monthly to the ROU depreciation expense account (separate from owned asset depreciation in the P&L). The ROU asset and accumulated depreciation are presented separately from owned PPE on the balance sheet, or disclosed in the notes per IFRS 16 requirements. Total ROU depreciation for tower leases is a key disclosure for investors comparing operators that own towers versus those that have sold and leased back.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that ROU depreciation uses the correct lease term (including reasonably certain renewal periods). Test that ROU depreciation is presented separately from owned asset depreciation in the P&L and balance sheet. Verify that any early termination of a lease results in immediate write-off of the remaining ROU asset. Confirm that the aggregate ROU depreciation charge is reconciled to the lease administration system. Review the sensitivity of EBITDA to the reclassification of lease costs under IFRS 16 (a key investor adjustment).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Lease administration system depreciation schedule, ROU asset register by tower site, monthly depreciation journal, early termination write-off records, IFRS 16 financial statement disclosure, and investor EBITDA reconciliation showing lease cost reclassification.

title: Network Upgrade — 4G to 5G Capitalization
description: Capitalizing the cost of upgrading existing cell sites from 4G to 5G technology.
explanation: Technology upgrades that enhance network capability are capitalized. Component accounting under IAS 16 requires removing the old 4G hardware's book value and adding the 5G equipment.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The 5G upgrade programme is tracked as a capital programme in the project management system (SAP PS or Oracle Projects). Each site upgrade generates a capital project that captures all directly attributable costs (equipment, installation, civil works, testing). On commissioning of each upgraded site, the CWIP is transferred to the network PPE. Simultaneously, the old 4G equipment being replaced must be derecognised — the net book value of the replaced components is written off (partial disposal under IAS 16 component accounting). Detailed component records in the asset register enable accurate derecognition.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test whether the replaced 4G components have been derecognised correctly — failure to remove old components results in double-counting of network assets. Component accounting requires that the asset register maintains sufficient detail to identify replaced components and their NBV. Test that only directly attributable costs are capitalised (exclude general overhead and costs of temporary workarounds). Confirm that 5G assets only commence depreciation from the commercial launch date. Review the business case for the 5G upgrade, particularly where coverage areas have low traffic density.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Capital project WBS for the 5G upgrade programme, vendor contracts and progress billing schedule, commissioning certificates per site, component derecognition records (4G equipment removed), network asset register update, CWIP balance and transfer schedule, and impairment assessment for sites with low 5G utilisation.

title: SIM Card Inventory — Purchase Cost
description: Recording the purchase of a batch of SIM cards from a manufacturer.
explanation: SIM cards are inventory items expensed when distributed to subscribers or dealers. The cost is typically very low (JOD 0.50-2 per card) but volume is high for large operators.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: SIM card inventory is managed in the inventory module of the ERP with a unique item code. SIM cards are received against purchase orders and posted to the SIM inventory account. When SIMs are issued to dealers or activated by subscribers, the cost is expensed to COGS (cost of SIM card). The SIM management system tracks each SIM by its ICCID from manufacture through distribution, activation, and eventual deactivation. Periodic physical counts of SIM card warehouse stock are reconciled to the ERP inventory balance.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile the SIM card inventory balance to the SIM management system's stock count and perform or observe a physical count. Test that unactivated SIMs held by dealers are correctly classified — dealer-held unactivated SIMs may remain in operator inventory or be treated as delivered (depending on the distribution agreement). Confirm that eSIM issuance is correctly excluded from physical SIM inventory. Test for SIM cloning or fraud by reviewing anomalous activation patterns.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SIM purchase orders and invoices, inventory receipt records, SIM management system stock report (by ICCID status), physical inventory count, dealer-held SIM reconciliation, COGS charge for activated SIMs, and eSIM issuance records.

title: Handset Subsidy Cost
description: Recording the cost of selling a handset below cost to attract a customer to a 24-month contract.
explanation: The handset loss (JOD 200) is offset by the higher-margin service plan revenue over 24 months. Under IFRS 15 bundle accounting, the handset is allocated its standalone value, reducing the subsidy loss.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Handset inventory is managed in the supply chain module. When a subsidised handset is sold as part of a bundle, the revenue allocation engine determines how much of the total contract value is allocated to the handset vs. the service plan (based on SSPs). The handset is derecognised at its full cost (COGS), with the allocated handset revenue recognised immediately. The difference between the allocated handset revenue and the price paid by the customer is recorded as a contract asset (recoverable through future service fee payments). Where the customer price is zero, the full allocated handset revenue is a contract asset.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the handset SSP used in the allocation — if the SSP is set too low, revenue is shifted away from the upfront handset recognition to the monthly service plan, which understates the current-period revenue. Test the contract asset balance — this represents handset revenue recognised but not yet billed, which will be recovered through future service payments. Confirm the contract asset is tested for recoverability (if a customer churns early, the unrecovered contract asset must be impaired). Review handset inventory shrinkage and write-downs.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Handset SSP determination methodology, revenue allocation calculation per bundle type, handset inventory records, contract asset schedule by subscriber, contract asset impairment assessment (for churned subscribers), handset COGS reconciliation, and inventory shrinkage report.

title: Dealer Commission — Subscriber Acquisition
description: Paying a retailer or dealer a commission for activating a new postpaid subscriber.
explanation: Dealer commissions are a major component of subscriber acquisition cost (SAC). They may be expensed immediately or capitalized as contract costs under IFRS 15 if incremental and recoverable.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Dealer commissions are calculated by the dealer management system (DMS) based on activations reported by each dealer. The commission is validated against the activation records in the CBS before payment. Commissions are posted to the AP sub-ledger and expensed to the SAC account. Where IFRS 15 capitalisation applies (see tel-cac-capitalized-contract), the commission is initially deferred. The dealer management system tracks commissions payable by dealer, plan type, and activation month. Monthly reconciliations between the DMS and CBS prevent over-payment for invalid activations.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test dealer commission calculations against the signed dealer agreement (rate per activation by plan type). Confirm that commissions are only paid for valid activations — test a sample of commissions against CBS activation records. Review clawback provisions — if a subscriber churns within a specified period, the dealer's commission is recovered. Test that clawback receivables are raised promptly. Assess the accounting treatment (expense vs. capitalise) and confirm it is applied consistently.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Dealer commission agreement (rates per plan type), DMS activation and commission report, CBS activation cross-reference, AP payment records, clawback receivable register, IFRS 15 capitalisation assessment for incremental costs, and dealer audit reconciliation.

title: CAC Capitalized as Contract Cost (IFRS 15)
description: Capitalizing incremental commission costs to obtain a contract customer.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, incremental costs to obtain a contract are capitalized if recoverable. For a 24-month postpaid contract, the JOD 35 dealer commission is capitalized and amortized over the 24-month contract term.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: CAC capitalisation is managed in the contract cost module of the revenue accounting system (SAP RAR or Zuora Revenue). When a commission is paid for an eligible contract, the system creates a contract cost asset. The asset is amortised over the expected contract term (or expected benefit period if longer than the initial term). The contract cost asset balance is carried on the balance sheet and disclosed in the financial statements. The total portfolio of contract cost assets requires periodic recoverability assessment — particularly when average monthly revenue per user (ARPU) trends decline.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the incremental cost criterion — only costs that would not have been incurred if the contract had not been obtained qualify (dealer commissions on new activations typically qualify; internal sales staff salaries do not). Test the recoverability assessment — if the expected future revenue from the contract is less than the unrecovered contract cost asset, the excess must be impaired. Confirm the amortisation period is the expected customer lifetime (which may extend beyond the initial minimum term if renewal is expected). Test the consistency of the practical expedient election (contracts of 12 months or less can be expensed immediately).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IFRS 15 contract cost capitalisation policy, incremental cost identification, contract cost asset register, amortisation schedule by cohort, recoverability assessment (comparing contract cost asset to future revenue), practical expedient election documentation, and financial statement disclosure.

title: CAC Contract Cost — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the capitalized customer acquisition cost over the contract period.
explanation: JOD 35 capitalized CAC over 24 months amortizes at JOD 1.46/month. This prevents the full subscriber acquisition cost hitting in month 1 of a 2-year contract, improving reported EBITDA margin.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: CAC amortisation is calculated by the contract cost module for each subscriber cohort (grouped by activation month and plan type). The monthly amortisation rate is contract cost / remaining contract months. Amortisation is posted automatically to the commission expense account and reduces the contract cost asset. When a subscriber churns, the remaining unamortised contract cost is immediately expensed. The contract cost asset and its movement (additions, amortisation, early termination write-offs) are disclosed in the financial statements.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile the contract cost asset roll-forward (opening balance + additions - amortisation - early termination write-offs = closing balance). Test that the early termination write-off is triggered promptly on subscriber churn. Review the portfolio-level recoverability test — declining ARPU trends may indicate that the total contract cost asset exceeds the recoverable amount. Confirm that the amortisation period is updated if the subscriber upgrades or extends their contract.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Contract cost asset roll-forward schedule (by cohort), monthly amortisation journal, early termination write-off records (linked to churn data), recoverability assessment, ARPU trend analysis, and financial statement note on contract cost assets.

title: Free Handset With Plan — Cost Allocation
description: Allocating the cost of a 'free' handset across the handset and service plan performance obligations.

entries.account: Cost of Goods Sold (Allocated to Handset POB)

explanation: Even a 'free' handset has a cost. IFRS 15 requires allocating the transaction price across all performance obligations. The contract asset represents the revenue already earned (on the handset) but not yet billed — to be recovered through service fees over the contract.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The 'free' handset arrangement is one of the most technically complex areas of telecom IFRS 15 accounting. The revenue allocation engine assigns the handset its SSP (e.g., JOD 250 standalone price) and the service plan its SSP. When the customer pays JOD 0 for the handset, the handset revenue (JOD 250 allocated SSP) is recognised immediately on delivery, with a corresponding contract asset of JOD 250 (the amount yet to be collected). The service plan revenue is reduced by the handset subsidy amount — so the customer is effectively paying a higher price for the service plan, recovering the handset cost over the contract term.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the full end-to-end accounting treatment for free handset bundles — this is a frequent area of error. Confirm the contract asset is recognised (not simply ignoring the handset revenue) and that the service plan revenue is correctly reduced. Test the contract asset recoverability — if a subscriber churns early, the remaining contract asset must be impaired. Review the presentation of contract assets separately from trade receivables on the balance sheet. Confirm COGS is recognised at the handset's full cost regardless of the customer price.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Free handset bundle IFRS 15 accounting policy, SSP for handset and service plan, revenue allocation calculation, contract asset schedule, COGS recognition at full handset cost, early termination impairment assessment, and balance sheet presentation of contract assets vs. trade receivables.

title: Airtime Fraud Write-Off
description: Writing off revenue lost to SIM box fraud or international revenue share fraud (IRSF).
explanation: Telecom fraud (SIM box, IRSF, subscription fraud) is a significant industry problem. When fraudulent traffic is identified, the related receivable is written off as a fraud loss. Prevention through real-time analytics is critical.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The fraud management system (FMS) identifies fraudulent traffic through real-time analytics and pattern detection. When fraud is confirmed, the revenue assurance team initiates the write-off process — reversing any revenue recognised on the fraudulent traffic and expensing the loss. In SIM box fraud, the operator loses international termination revenue (calls are disguised as local). In IRSF, the operator is liable for payments to premium rate numbers it cannot recover from the perpetrator. The FMS generates a fraud incident report that drives the accounting adjustment.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the fraud management system's detection and write-off process. Test that fraud losses are recognised promptly when identified and not left in the AR for extended periods. Assess the adequacy of the fraud provision for known fraud types (IBNR fraud equivalent) — not all fraud is detected immediately. Confirm that insurance claims have been filed where applicable. Review the fraud loss trend as a percentage of revenue — a deteriorating trend indicates inadequate fraud prevention controls.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fraud management system incident report, revenue assurance write-off calculation, AR reversal journal, fraud prevention control assessment, insurance claim documentation (if applicable), fraud loss trend analysis, and management remediation plan for systemic fraud.

title: Billing Dispute Credit Note to Subscriber
description: Issuing a credit note to a subscriber for incorrectly charged roaming or data fees.
explanation: Billing disputes are common in telecoms. Credit notes reverse previously recognized revenue. Recurring billing errors indicate system issues that must be addressed to maintain customer trust.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Billing disputes are managed through the customer care system (CRM), which logs each dispute and routes it to the billing resolution team. When a dispute is upheld, a credit note is raised in the CBS, reducing the outstanding AR and reversing the revenue. The credit note is applied to the customer's next invoice or refunded in cash. Dispute resolution data feeds into the billing quality dashboard, which tracks dispute volumes by charge type and billing system. High dispute rates on specific charges indicate systematic billing errors requiring system correction.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test credit note approvals — confirm that all credit notes are authorised by an appropriate supervisor and that the credit is justified. Analyse credit note volumes by charge type to identify systemic billing errors. Test that large credit notes are not being used to compensate customers for non-billing issues (e.g., network quality complaints) without proper classification. Confirm that the revenue reversal and the AR/liability adjustment are posted in the same period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: CRM dispute log, credit note authorisation record, billing system credit note report, revenue reversal journal, dispute root cause analysis by charge type, billing quality dashboard, and customer refund records.

title: Service Credit to Subscriber (Goodwill Gesture)
description: Crediting a customer's account with free data after a service outage.
explanation: Free credits for service failures are a customer retention cost. The credit is a liability until consumed by the subscriber. For accounting purposes, it is treated like additional airtime top-up.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Service quality credits are issued through the customer care system and posted to the subscriber's account in the CBS as a credit balance. The credit is classified as deferred revenue (similar to prepaid top-up) until consumed by the subscriber. The expense is classified as service quality expense or customer retention marketing cost, not as a revenue reversal. The OCS tracks consumption of the credit balance and releases the deferred revenue to the appropriate revenue account as the credit is used. Mass service credits (following a major outage) are processed in batch through the CBS.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether goodwill credits are correctly classified as an expense (not a revenue reversal) when they are not related to a billing error. Test that the deferred liability represents all outstanding unspent credits. For mass outage credits, confirm the total credit value is consistent with the operator's stated compensation policy and the duration and customer impact of the outage. Review whether regulatory reporting of service quality failures and associated compensation is complete.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Customer care credit approval record, CBS credit posting report, deferred credit liability schedule, outage incident report (for mass credits), regulatory service quality reporting, credit consumption tracking, and total credit expense by category.

title: Number Portability — Revenue Impact (Subscriber Loss)
description: Recognizing the revenue impact when a high-value subscriber ports out to a competitor.
explanation: When a subscriber ports out before contract end, the early termination fee (ETF) is charged and recognized. Any remaining deferred revenue for undelivered service is reversed.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: When a port-out is initiated, the CBS triggers the ETF calculation based on the remaining contract period and the plan's ETF schedule. The ETF is posted as a receivable and revenue in the CBS. Simultaneously, any deferred service revenue attributable to the remaining contract period is reversed. The subscriber's account is closed in the CBS on the effective porting date. The revenue accounting system handles the deferred revenue reversal based on the contract closeout event. The unamortised CAC contract asset is also written off at the point of porting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that ETF revenue is recognised at the point of porting (not when collected) as the constraint on variable consideration is removed. Test that the deferred revenue reversal matches the remaining months of the contract. Confirm that the CAC contract asset write-off is triggered simultaneously with the port-out event. Review ETF collectability — ETFs are often difficult to collect and may require provision. Assess whether the ETF amount complies with regulatory maximum limits on early termination charges.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Port-out notification from the MNP system, ETF calculation per the contract terms, ETF receivable posting, deferred revenue reversal journal, CAC contract asset write-off, ETF collection history and provision assessment, and TRC ETF regulatory limit compliance.

title: Late Payment Penalty Income
description: Charging interest on overdue postpaid invoices.
explanation: Late payment charges are recognized when charged and are legally enforceable per the subscriber agreement. They are Other Income rather than core service revenue.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Late payment charges are calculated automatically by the CBS billing engine at the invoice due date. The penalty rate and grace period are configured in the CBS per the subscriber agreement and regulated by the TRC in Jordan. The charge is added to the next invoice and posted to the AR sub-ledger. Revenue is recognised when the penalty is charged. The late payment income is presented separately from subscription revenue in management reporting and in the financial statements. High late payment income may indicate credit quality deterioration in the subscriber base.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the penalty rate and grace period applied are consistent with the subscriber agreement and any applicable TRC regulations. Test the collectability of late payment charges — if subscribers are disputing the charges or the collection rate is low, a provision may be required. Confirm that late payment income is separately presented and not included in core subscription revenue metrics. Review whether waiving of late fees is appropriately authorised and documented.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: CBS late payment charge configuration (rate, grace period), billing run showing late charges applied, subscriber agreement extract on late payment terms, TRC regulatory limit on late payment charges, collection rate analysis for penalty receivables, and late fee waiver register.

title: Universal Service Obligation (USO) Levy
description: Recording the mandatory contribution to the national telecommunications USO fund.
explanation: Most countries require operators to contribute to a USO fund that subsidizes connectivity in underserved areas. In Jordan, the TRC administers this. The levy is typically a percentage of gross revenues.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The USO levy is calculated quarterly (or as required by the TRC) as a percentage of the operator's qualifying gross revenue, as defined in the telecommunications law. The calculation is prepared by the regulatory affairs and finance teams, reviewed for completeness, and posted as an expense and payable in the GL. The levy is remitted to the TRC within the stipulated timeframe. Some operators are eligible for USO levy credits if they have invested in qualifying rural coverage projects — these credits reduce the net levy payable.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the USO levy base is calculated correctly per the TRC's prescribed definition of qualifying revenue (which may differ from accounting revenue). Test the levy rate applied against the current TRC determination. Confirm timely remittance. Assess eligibility for USO levy credits (rural coverage investments) and verify that credits claimed are supported by qualifying capital expenditure evidence. Disclose the USO levy separately in the financial statements as a regulatory levy.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TRC USO levy determination (rate and base definition), qualifying revenue calculation, USO levy calculation workings, TRC remittance receipt, levy credit application (if applicable), supporting capex evidence for credits, and financial statement disclosure of regulatory levies.

title: Annual Spectrum Usage Fee
description: Paying the annual frequency usage fee to the Telecommunications Regulatory Commission (TRC).
explanation: Annual spectrum usage fees are separate from the one-time license cost. They are prepaid assets amortized monthly. They represent the ongoing cost of the right to use the spectrum.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The annual spectrum usage fee is paid to the TRC at the start of the year (or as per the TRC's billing schedule) and recorded as a prepaid regulatory fee asset. The fee is amortised monthly (annual fee / 12) to the spectrum usage expense account. The prepaid balance is tracked in the prepayments sub-ledger and reconciled to the TRC fee schedule. Spectrum usage fees are different from the capitalised spectrum license cost (one-time acquisition) — they represent the annual right-to-use payment and are entirely expensed over the year.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that annual spectrum usage fees are correctly classified as prepaid expenses (not capitalised as intangible assets alongside the license cost). Test the amortisation rate against the annual fee payment. Confirm that the fee is paid on time — late payment to the TRC may result in spectrum suspension. Verify that fees for all licensed spectrum bands are captured. Test that increases in the annual fee (which TRC may revise periodically) are promptly reflected in the amortisation schedule.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TRC spectrum fee determination letter (per band), payment receipt, prepaid schedule with amortisation rate, monthly amortisation journal, spectrum band register (confirming all bands are covered), and TRC fee revision notification.

title: Regulatory Fine — TRC Penalty
description: Recording a fine imposed by the Telecommunications Regulatory Commission for quality of service violations.
explanation: Regulatory fines are expensed immediately. They are non-deductible for income tax purposes and are disclosed separately in financial statements as they indicate compliance failures.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Regulatory fines are posted to the regulatory fines and penalties expense account immediately when the fine notice is received from the TRC. The fine is a non-recurring, non-deductible expense that must be disclosed separately in the financial statements. The payable is cleared when remitted to the TRC. In Jordan, the TRC may impose fines for QoS (Quality of Service) KPI failures, consumer protection breaches, or licence condition violations. Where the operator intends to appeal the fine, an IAS 37 contingent liability assessment determines whether to accrue the full amount or a portion.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors obtain confirmation of all regulatory fines issued by the TRC during the year. Confirm that fines are immediately expensed and not spread over future periods. Test IAS 37 assessment for fines under appeal — the probability of success and the expected outcome must be assessed. Confirm that fines are disclosed separately in the financial statements. Verify that income tax computations correctly treat fines as non-deductible expenses. Review the root cause of fines — recurring QoS fines indicate a systemic network quality issue.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TRC fine notice, appeal documentation (if applicable), IAS 37 contingent liability assessment, GL expense posting, TRC remittance receipt, income tax non-deductibility confirmation, financial statement disclosure, and regulatory affairs root cause analysis.

title: Withholding Tax on International Settlements
description: Withholding tax on interconnect settlement payments to foreign operators.
explanation: Payments to foreign telecoms for interconnect, roaming settlement, and capacity leasing may be subject to withholding tax. Applicable rate depends on double taxation treaties between Jordan and the counterparty's country.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: WHT on international payments is configured in the AP module for each foreign operator vendor. The applicable WHT rate is determined by the double taxation treaty (DTT) between Jordan and the foreign operator's country — or the standard WHT rate if no treaty applies. When the payment is processed, the system automatically deducts the WHT and posts it to the WHT payable account. The WHT is remitted to the ISTD by the prescribed deadline. WHT certificates must be issued to foreign operators annually. Treaty rates must be documented with residency certificates from the foreign operator.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the WHT rate applied for each foreign operator matches the applicable DTT rate (not the higher default rate). Test that residency certificates are on file for operators claiming treaty benefits. Confirm that WHT is remitted to the ISTD on time. Verify that WHT is applied consistently to all applicable payment types (interconnect, roaming, capacity, technical services). Review whether foreign operators are disputing WHT deductions and whether these disputes create contingent receivables or liabilities.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: DTT rate schedule by country, residency certificates from foreign operators, WHT calculation per payment, ISTD remittance receipts, WHT certificates issued to foreign operators, AP vendor WHT configuration, and ISTD correspondence on any WHT disputes.

title: Telecom-Specific Tax Provision
description: Accruing telecom-specific sector taxes such as a telecoms excise tax on revenue.
explanation: Many jurisdictions impose sector-specific taxes on telecom revenue in addition to standard corporate income tax. In Jordan, this may include the general sales tax on services and specific levies on mobile services.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Telecom sector taxes in Jordan include the General Sales Tax (GST — 16%) applied to subscriber services, which is collected from subscribers and remitted to the ISTD. Additionally, Jordan imposes a special tax on mobile telephone services (5% of the price of service, per the Sales Tax Law). These are configured as output tax codes in the billing system. The taxes are collected from subscribers and flow to the tax payable accounts, not to revenue. Monthly GST returns are filed and the net tax position (output less input) is settled with the ISTD.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that GST and telecom sector taxes collected from subscribers are not included in revenue — they are pass-through liabilities. Test the completeness of GST filings against the operator's taxable revenue. Verify that input GST on eligible business costs is claimed and offset. Assess the GST treatment of bundled products (handset + service plan) — the handset portion may have a different GST treatment to the service portion. Confirm timely filing and payment of all sector-specific taxes.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: GST registration certificate, monthly GST return filings, billing system GST configuration, output tax reconciliation to GST returns, input GST claim supporting documentation, sector-specific tax calculation, and ISTD correspondence.

title: Fixed-Line / Broadband Monthly Subscription Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue for residential broadband and fixed-line telephone subscriptions.
explanation: Fixed-line broadband revenue is recognized monthly as the service is delivered. It is accounted for separately from mobile revenue as it has different cost drivers, churn rates, and margin profiles.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Fixed-line and broadband subscriptions are billed through a fixed-line billing system (which may be separate from or integrated with the mobile CBS). Monthly invoices are generated at billing cycle dates and posted to the AR sub-ledger with revenue recognised simultaneously. Fixed-line revenue is tracked in a separate cost centre and revenue account from mobile. Network costs for fixed-line (local loop, DSLAM, MSAN) are separately depreciated. Churn in fixed-line tends to be lower than mobile — the billing system flags disconnections for revenue cutoff.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test fixed-line revenue completeness by reconciling the number of active lines at month-end to the billed revenue. Confirm cut-off for lines disconnected mid-month. Test that installation fees for new broadband connections are assessed under IFRS 15 — if they relate to a set-up activity (not a distinct service), they are deferred. Review the fixed-line receivable aging — fixed-line customers tend to be more creditworthy than prepaid mobile, but corporate accounts may have longer payment terms.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fixed-line active line count report, billing system invoice report, revenue reconciliation (line count × monthly fee), disconnection report for mid-period churn, installation fee deferral assessment, and fixed-line AR aging report.

title: Fibre-to-the-Home (FTTH) Monthly Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly subscription revenue from a residential FTTH connection.
explanation: FTTH is a premium broadband product requiring significant upfront capex for fibre deployment. Monthly revenue is recognized as connectivity is provided. The high capex-to-revenue ratio makes FTTH a long-payback investment.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: FTTH revenue is recognised monthly per active subscriber. The FTTH network build (fibre cables, ONT equipment, active electronics) is capitalised as network PPE during the construction phase and depreciated over its useful life (fibre: 20-30 years; active equipment: 7-10 years). Home connection costs (drop cables, ONT at the subscriber premise) may be capitalised as subscriber connection assets or expensed depending on the operator's policy and whether the operator retains ownership of the ONT equipment. Revenue recognition commences when the subscriber is connected and service is active.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the capex-to-revenue payback period for the FTTH programme — where payback is very long (over 10 years), impairment indicators should be considered. Confirm that civil construction costs (trench works, duct installation) are capitalised and not expensed. Test the ONT capitalisation policy — if ONTs are provided to subscribers at no charge, the cost may be an upfront cost that should be recognised as a contract cost or an asset depending on ownership. Test the revenue start date aligns with the connection date in the network management system.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: FTTH capex register (fibre, active equipment, civil works), subscriber connection date from NMS, revenue recognition start date, ONT capitalisation policy assessment, FTTH programme financial model (payback period), impairment indicators assessment, and FTTH segment financial statements.

title: Enterprise Managed Services Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from a corporate customer for a fully managed network and security service.
explanation: Managed services contracts bundle connectivity, hardware, security monitoring, and helpdesk support. Under IFRS 15, each distinct performance obligation is assessed separately. Long-term contracts may have material contract modifications.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Enterprise managed services contracts are complex, multi-year arrangements with multiple performance obligations (connectivity, CPE hardware, security monitoring, managed helpdesk, SLA credits). The revenue accounting system (SAP RAR or Zuora) identifies and allocates the transaction price to each performance obligation based on SSPs. Connectivity and services are recognised monthly; CPE hardware is recognised on delivery. SLA penalty credits (variable consideration) are estimated using the expected value method and constrained to avoid revenue reversal. Enterprise contracts are managed in the CRM and ERP with dedicated project codes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors perform a detailed IFRS 15 performance obligation identification for large enterprise contracts. Test the SSP determination for each bundled element. Assess the constraint on variable consideration (SLA credits) — are the estimates reasonable? Review contract modifications for large accounts — enterprise customers frequently renegotiate terms, and each modification requires an IFRS 15 reassessment. Confirm that customer-specific customisation costs are assessed for capitalisation as contract fulfilment costs.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Enterprise contract document, IFRS 15 performance obligation identification, SSP by element, revenue allocation calculation, SLA credit variable consideration assessment, contract modification log, contract fulfilment cost assessment, and enterprise AR aging.

title: Cloud & Hosting Service Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from providing cloud infrastructure and colocation services to business customers.
explanation: Cloud and hosting is the fastest-growing B2B revenue stream for telecoms. Revenue is recognized over the service period. Upfront setup fees may be deferred under IFRS 15 if they relate to future performance obligations.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Cloud and hosting revenue is recognised monthly based on contracted capacity (servers, storage, bandwidth) or consumption (pay-per-use). For IaaS and PaaS arrangements, usage-based billing is generated from the cloud management platform and fed to the billing system. Upfront setup and implementation fees are assessed under IFRS 15 — if they relate to a set-up activity that is not distinct, they are deferred and recognised over the service term. The data centre capex (servers, cooling, power, fit-out) is capitalised and depreciated separately from telecoms network equipment.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the IFRS 15 treatment of upfront fees — setup fees for cloud environments that are not distinct performance obligations must be deferred. Test consumption-based billing completeness by reconciling metered usage data from the cloud platform to billed revenue. Confirm that data centre infrastructure is correctly classified and depreciated at appropriate rates (servers: 3-5 years; building fit-out: 10-20 years; power infrastructure: 15-25 years). Review the cloud segment's margin to assess whether data centre capex is being appropriately recovered through pricing.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Cloud service agreement, billing system consumption or capacity data, upfront fee deferral assessment, revenue recognition schedule, data centre capex register, depreciation schedule by asset class, cloud segment financial statements, and metered usage-to-billing reconciliation.

title: IoT / M2M Connectivity Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly SIM-based connectivity revenue from IoT devices (smart meters, vehicles, sensors).
explanation: IoT revenue is high-volume, low-ARPU (Average Revenue Per User). Millions of connected devices generate reliable recurring revenue. The key metric is the cost per SIM rather than per-subscriber revenue.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: IoT/M2M contracts are typically enterprise agreements covering large numbers of SIMs with very low individual monthly fees (a few cents per SIM per month). Billing is generated from the M2M platform based on active SIM counts or data consumption. Revenue is recognised monthly per the active SIM count. The M2M platform tracks SIM lifecycle (active, suspended, terminated) and feeds billing data to the CBS. IoT revenue is presented in a separate business unit segment from consumer mobile. The cost structure for IoT is primarily network capacity (low incremental cost per SIM), making it a high-margin revenue stream at scale.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile the active SIM count in the M2M platform to the billed revenue. Test that suspended or decommissioned SIMs are not billed. For large IoT contracts with minimum commitment levels, assess whether the minimum commitment should be recognised even if actual usage is below the minimum. Confirm that eSIM-based IoT connections are correctly captured in the platform. Review the contract asset/liability position for IoT contracts with upfront device activation fees.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: M2M platform active SIM count report, monthly billing reconciliation (SIM count × rate), enterprise IoT contract with minimum commitments, eSIM activation records, cost-per-SIM analysis, IoT segment revenue and margin report, and AR aging for IoT enterprise customers.

title: A2P SMS (Bulk Business Messaging) Revenue
description: Earning revenue from banks, retailers, and government agencies sending OTPs and alerts via SMS.
explanation: Application-to-Person (A2P) SMS revenue comes from businesses sending automated messages. As person-to-person SMS declines, A2P has become a growing revenue stream — especially for OTP, bank notifications, and e-commerce alerts.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: A2P SMS revenue is generated from enterprise aggregator contracts or direct enterprise agreements. The SMS platform counts messages delivered and feeds the data to the billing system at the agreed per-message rate. Revenue is recognised when messages are delivered. A2P rates are significantly higher than P2P rates — and grey route (unsanctioned) A2P traffic that bypasses the operator's legitimate A2P routes represents revenue leakage. Revenue assurance controls compare network-registered A2P traffic to legitimately billed A2P volumes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test A2P revenue by reconciling the SMS platform delivery counts to the invoiced volume. Confirm that grey route traffic detected by the revenue assurance system is quantified — this represents revenue lost to bypass. Assess whether the operator's A2P pricing complies with any regulatory or industry guidelines on bulk messaging. Test the collectability of A2P receivables — some aggregators have credit risk. Confirm the principal/agent classification where the operator uses an SMS aggregator.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SMS platform delivery count report, enterprise A2P contract rates, billing reconciliation (delivery count × rate), grey route traffic detection report, revenue assurance A2P loss quantification, A2P receivable aging, and aggregator principal/agent assessment.

title: Premium Rate / Shortcode Revenue (Revenue Share)
description: Recording the telecom's share of revenue from premium rate SMS or voice services.
explanation: The telecom acts as billing agent for premium services. Only the operator's revenue share (net of the content provider's share) is recognized as revenue. The content provider's share is a payable.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Premium rate services are managed through a shortcode management platform. The operator bills the subscriber at the premium rate and splits the revenue with the content provider per the revenue share agreement (typically 70% operator / 30% content provider, or vice versa). The operator's net share is recognised as revenue; the content provider's share is a liability until remitted. The IFRS 15 principal/agent analysis is critical here — the operator is typically an agent for the content (net presentation) but a principal for the connectivity (gross presentation), requiring careful disaggregation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors perform the IFRS 15 principal/agent analysis for each premium rate category — the operator's level of control over the content service determines gross vs. net presentation. Test that the revenue share split matches the signed content provider agreement. Confirm that premium rate charges comply with TRC regulations on consumer protection for premium services (subscriber opt-in requirements, charge caps). Test subscriber complaint data on premium rate charges — unauthorised charges indicate compliance failures.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Content provider agreement (revenue share percentage), IFRS 15 principal/agent analysis by service type, shortcode billing report (gross charges and operator/content provider split), content provider payable reconciliation, TRC premium rate regulatory compliance confirmation, and subscriber opt-in records.

title: OTT Content Partnership Revenue Share
description: Earning a share of subscription revenue from bundling a streaming service into mobile plans.
explanation: Telecoms bundle OTT content (streaming, music, games) with mobile plans. Revenue share agreements give the telecom a percentage of subscription revenue for subscribers acquired through their platform.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: OTT partnership revenue is typically calculated monthly by the OTT partner based on active subscribers acquired through the telecom's platform, multiplied by the revenue share rate per the partnership agreement. The telecom receives a revenue share statement from the OTT partner and raises an invoice or accepts the statement as the revenue basis. The revenue is posted to the OTT partnership revenue account. Where the OTT service is bundled as a free add-on to a mobile plan (no separate charge), the IFRS 15 treatment requires careful assessment of whether the OTT content is a separate performance obligation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the revenue share receivable against the OTT partner's subscriber count data and the contractual share rate. Confirm that the subscriber count used in the revenue share calculation matches the telecom's own active subscriber data. Assess the IFRS 15 treatment of OTT bundles — if the OTT content is included at no extra charge, is it a separate performance obligation? If yes, the plan's transaction price must be allocated to the OTT content. If no, the OTT content is a marketing tool and revenue from the plan is unchanged.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: OTT partnership agreement (revenue share rate, subscriber data reporting obligations), OTT partner monthly statement, telecom's own subscriber activation data, revenue share reconciliation, IFRS 15 OTT bundle performance obligation assessment, and OTT partnership revenue segment reporting.

title: Device Insurance Premium — Monthly Recurring
description: Recognizing the telecom's agent commission for selling device insurance on behalf of an insurer.
explanation: When telecoms sell device insurance as an agent of an insurance company, only the agent commission is revenue — not the full premium. Under IFRS 15, acting as an agent requires net presentation.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Device insurance is offered as an add-on in the CBS when a subscriber activates a new device. The monthly premium is collected from the subscriber with the postpaid invoice and remitted to the insurer. The telecom retains its agent commission (typically 20-30% of the premium). Only the commission is recognised as revenue (net presentation as agent). The gross premium collected is a pass-through liability until remitted. The insurer is responsible for claims processing and payment. The telecom must also comply with insurance distribution regulations in the relevant jurisdiction.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the IFRS 15 agent classification — key criteria are whether the telecom controls the insurance service before providing it to the subscriber. If the telecom does not underwrite the risk, it is typically an agent and net presentation applies. Test that gross premiums collected are correctly classified as pass-through liabilities (not revenue). Confirm that the commission rate matches the insurer agreement. Verify compliance with insurance distribution regulations — the telecom may require an insurance intermediary licence.
