practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Insurer distribution agreement (commission rate, premium remittance schedule), IFRS 15 principal/agent analysis, gross premium liability account, commission revenue recognition, insurance intermediary licence (if required), premium remittance confirmations, and subscriber opt-in records.

title: Handset Installment Plan — In-House Financing
description: The telecom offers 12-month installment payments for a handset without a third-party lender.
explanation: When the telecom provides in-house financing, the full handset revenue is recognized at delivery (control has passed). The receivable is split between current and long-term based on when installments are due.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: In-house handset financing creates a financing component in the transaction under IFRS 15. If the installment plan is interest-free or below market rate, and the period exceeds 12 months, a significant financing component must be assessed. If material, revenue is recorded at the cash price (present value) and interest income is accrued over the installment period using the effective interest rate. The receivable is split between current (due within 12 months) and non-current portions. The CBS and ERP must support the split classification and monthly interest accrual.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the installment plan contains a significant financing component — interest-free handset plans over 12 months typically do. Test the present value calculation of the cash equivalent price. Confirm that interest income is recognised over the installment period using the EIR method. Verify that the current/non-current split of the receivable matches the payment schedule. Assess the credit risk on the installment receivable — subscribers who churn may default on remaining installments.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Handset installment plan terms (period, interest rate or zero rate), significant financing component assessment, present value calculation, EIR amortisation schedule, current/non-current receivable classification, installment payment schedule, credit risk assessment and provision for defaults, and financial statement disclosure of financing receivables.

title: SIM-Only Plan Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from a SIM-only postpaid plan with no handset subsidy.
explanation: SIM-only plans are pure service contracts with no handset component — simpler IFRS 15 treatment with a single performance obligation. Higher margins than bundled plans as there is no handset cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: SIM-only plans have the simplest revenue recognition in telecoms — one performance obligation (ongoing connectivity service) recognised monthly. The CBS generates the monthly invoice and posts to the AR sub-ledger. No bundle allocation or contract asset accounting is required. Dealer commission for SIM-only activations is typically lower than for bundled contracts. SIM-only plans often have no minimum contract term (rolling monthly) — which simplifies the CAC capitalisation assessment (contracts under 12 months may use the practical expedient to expense acquisition costs immediately).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that SIM-only plans with rolling monthly terms are treated as 12-month-or-less contracts for the IFRS 15 practical expedient on CAC capitalisation. Test revenue completeness by reconciling active SIM-only plan count to monthly revenue. Confirm that SIM-only plan subscribers who port out mid-month have revenue cut off at the porting date. Review the SIM-only margin profile compared to bundled plans — SIM-only margins should be materially higher.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SIM-only plan active subscriber count, billing system revenue report, IFRS 15 practical expedient election for CAC, dealer commission rate for SIM-only plans, porting-out cut-off records, and SIM-only vs. bundled plan margin comparison.

title: Family Plan — Multi-Line Bundle Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from a family plan covering 4 lines at a group discount.
explanation: Multi-line family plans are a retention tool — the group discount creates switching cost. Under IFRS 15, each line is a separate performance obligation allocated the same SSP as individual plans.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Family plans are structured in the CBS as a master account (primary subscriber) with multiple dependent lines. Billing is consolidated on the primary subscriber's invoice. Revenue is recognised monthly based on the family plan's total fee, split across each line's performance obligation based on the individual line's SSP. Where the family plan discount is material, the total discount is allocated proportionally across all lines (reducing the effective rate per line). Each line's activation and deactivation is tracked independently in the CBS.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the SSP allocation for family plans — the group discount must be proportionally allocated, not arbitrarily assigned. Confirm that when a dependent line is deactivated, only the relevant portion of revenue ceases. Test the churn dynamic — the primary subscriber's churn triggers all dependent lines' disconnection. Verify that the family plan billing is correctly consolidated on the primary subscriber's AR. Review whether family plan subscribers are subject to ETF for the primary line's minimum commitment period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Family plan pricing and SSP by line, revenue allocation calculation, CBS master/dependent account structure, dependent line activation/deactivation records, billing consolidation on primary AR, churn impact analysis on dependent lines, and ETF terms for the primary commitment.

title: Contract Downgrade — Revenue Reduction
description: A customer mid-contract downgrades from a premium to a basic plan.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, a plan downgrade is a contract modification that reduces the transaction price. The remaining contract revenue is adjusted prospectively. No catch-up adjustment to past periods is required.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Contract downgrades are processed in the CBS as a plan change event. The revenue accounting system identifies the downgrade as a contract modification under IFRS 15 and applies the prospective adjustment. If the downgrade reduces the overall transaction price but the services are distinct at their standalone price, it is treated as a termination of the old contract and inception of a new contract at the lower price. The deferred revenue is reset to reflect the new plan's remaining value. Any contract asset from a prior bundle allocation may need to be reassessed.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the IFRS 15 modification type for downgrades — prospective adjustment (new contract inception) vs. cumulative catch-up (modification of existing contract) has a significant revenue impact. Test the mechanical accuracy of the deferred revenue reset for sample downgrades. Review whether downgrades are being offered without ETF as a retention tool — if so, the waiver of the ETF is a variable consideration element that should be assessed. Confirm that contract assets are re-evaluated at modification.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IFRS 15 contract modification policy for downgrades, CBS plan change report, revenue accounting system modification event log, deferred revenue reset journal, contract asset reassessment at downgrade, ETF waiver register, and downgrade volume and revenue impact statistics.

title: Early Termination Fee (ETF) Income
description: Charging a fee when a postpaid customer cancels their contract before the minimum term expires.
explanation: ETFs recover the unrecouped subsidy and acquisition cost when a customer leaves early. Under IFRS 15, the ETF is recognized when charged since the constraint on variable consideration is lifted at that point.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: ETF charges are calculated by the CBS at the time of contract cancellation, based on the contractual ETF schedule (typically a declining amount over the contract term). The charge is raised as a final invoice or applied to the subscriber's final bill. Revenue is recognised immediately as the performance obligation is the right to cancel — a point-in-time event. The contract cost asset (unamortised CAC) is written off simultaneously. The ETF receivable is collected along with any outstanding subscription charges.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the ETF amount matches the contractual schedule and has been correctly calculated based on remaining months. Test the collectability of ETF receivables — ETFs are often disputed or difficult to collect from customers who have already ported out. Assess whether an ETF provision is required for expected non-collection. Confirm TRC compliance — Jordan's TRC may cap ETF amounts or specify the calculation methodology. Test that CAC write-off is triggered at the same time as the ETF charge.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: ETF schedule from the subscriber agreement, CBS ETF calculation, ETF invoice, TRC ETF regulatory limit, ETF collection history and provision assessment, CAC write-off journal, and ETF dispute register.

title: Reconnection / Reactivation Fee
description: Charging a fee to reconnect a suspended postpaid or prepaid account.
explanation: Reconnection fees are recognized immediately on reactivation since the performance obligation is the restoration of service — a point-in-time event.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Reconnection fees are configured in the CBS as a one-time charge applied when a suspended account is reactivated. Suspension may be due to non-payment (involuntary) or customer request (voluntary). The CBS posts the fee to the subscriber's account and the revenue is recognised immediately. For non-payment suspensions, the reconnection fee is collected along with any outstanding arrears before service is restored. The reconnection event is logged in the CBS with a timestamp for audit trail purposes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that reconnection fees are only charged where permitted by the subscriber agreement and TRC regulations. Test that revenue is recognised at the reconnection event date. Confirm that the CBS prevents reactivation without payment of the reconnection fee and outstanding arrears. Review whether the reconnection fee rate is appropriate given the administrative cost — an excessively high fee relative to cost may attract regulatory scrutiny.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Subscriber agreement extract on reconnection terms, CBS suspension and reactivation log, fee rate schedule, TRC regulatory compliance confirmation, revenue posting at reactivation, and collection of arrears confirmation.

title: SIM Replacement Fee
description: Charging a customer for replacing a lost, stolen, or damaged SIM card.
explanation: SIM replacement fees cover the cost of the physical SIM and administrative processing. Revenue is recognized at point of issuance.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: SIM replacement is processed at the operator's retail outlet, call centre, or online portal. The fee is collected (cash, card, or charged to the subscriber's account) and the old SIM is deactivated in the subscriber management system while the new SIM is activated. Revenue is recognised immediately at issuance. The SIM inventory is reduced by the cost of the replacement SIM (COGS). For eSIM replacements (which have no physical card cost), the revenue may be lower or zero depending on the operator's policy.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test that SIM replacements are properly documented — a replacement should only occur after identity verification and the old SIM's immediate deactivation. Review the SIM replacement rate as a fraud indicator — high replacement rates may indicate SIM swap fraud (used for account takeover). Confirm that eSIM replacement fees are correctly set given the lower cost (no physical SIM). Test the SIM cost COGS charge against the inventory unit cost.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SIM replacement request record (with identity verification), old SIM deactivation log, new SIM activation record, fee collection confirmation, SIM inventory COGS charge, eSIM replacement fee policy, and SIM swap fraud monitoring report.

title: Roaming Revenue Adjustment — Interoperator Reconciliation
description: Adjusting roaming revenue after the quarterly reconciliation of CDRs with roaming partners.
explanation: Roaming revenue is initially estimated based on processed CDRs. Quarterly reconciliation with partners may reveal discrepancies requiring revenue adjustments. This is a normal part of the interoperator settlement cycle.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Roaming settlements follow the GSMA IOT (Inter-Operator Tariff) settlement process. The reconciliation compares TAP files exchanged between operators to identify volume and rate discrepancies. Disputes are resolved through the GSMA clearing house arbitration process. Revenue adjustments are posted to the GL when the reconciliation is agreed — either reducing the receivable and revenue (where the home operator over-claimed) or increasing it (where under-claimed). The reconciliation cycle is typically quarterly under GSMA guidelines.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the roaming reconciliation process to confirm that adjustments are promptly processed and revenue is not overstated in the interim periods. Assess the materiality of roaming reconciliation adjustments — large, recurring adjustments indicate inaccurate TAP file processing. Confirm that disputed items with partners are appropriately disclosed as contingent assets or liabilities. Test that the revenue adjustment is allocated to the correct accounting period (the period the roaming traffic occurred).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TAP file exchange log, GSMA clearing house reconciliation statement, agreed adjustment schedule by partner, revenue adjustment journal, dispute log with outstanding items, period allocation of adjustments, and roaming settlement provision at year-end.

title: Postpaid Receivable — Bad Debt Write-Off
description: Writing off a postpaid customer's outstanding balance after exhausting collection attempts.
explanation: Postpaid bad debt write-offs are applied against the allowance. The expense was already recognized when the provision was set up. Write-off is a balance sheet cleanup entry with no additional P&L impact.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Bad debt write-offs are approved through a collections management system or credit committee process. The write-off is applied against the existing allowance in the ERP AR module, reducing both the gross receivable and the contra-allowance account. No additional P&L charge is incurred at write-off — the expense was already recognised when the provision was raised. The subscriber's account in the CBS is flagged as a bad debtor — preventing future re-subscription without clearance of the debt. The debt may be sold to a collections agency, in which case a recovery is recognised.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the write-off approval process — all write-offs above a threshold should require credit committee approval. Confirm that write-offs are applied against the allowance (not charged directly to expense). Test the timing of write-offs — debt that is clearly uncollectable should be written off promptly, not left in the AR for years. Review debt recovery rates from collections agencies to assess whether the allowance provision rates are adequate.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Write-off approval record (credit committee minutes), AR write-off journal, CBS bad debtor flag, collections agency contract and referral schedule, debt recovery receipts, allowance adequacy assessment, and write-off ageing analysis (days from due date to write-off).

title: Allowance for Doubtful Postpaid Accounts
description: Monthly provision for expected bad debts on the outstanding postpaid receivable portfolio.
explanation: Telecoms provision for expected bad debts on postpaid balances using historical collection rates by aging bucket. This is an IFRS 9 ECL-based estimate updated monthly.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The IFRS 9 ECL provision is calculated using a provision matrix — historical bad debt rates are applied to each aging bucket (current, 30 days, 60 days, 90 days, 120+ days) of the postpaid AR. The rates are updated quarterly based on collection experience. The provision is posted monthly to the bad debt expense account. The provision model is maintained in the credit risk module of the ERP or in a spreadsheet-based tool. Post-reporting-date collections (cash collected after month-end) are used to validate the provision adequacy.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the IFRS 9 provision matrix by validating the historical default rates used for each aging bucket against actual collection data. Assess whether the provision rates reflect any deterioration in the current economic environment (IFRS 9 forward-looking adjustment). Test the provision calculation for each aging bucket on a sample basis. Confirm that the provision adequately covers specific large accounts known to be in financial difficulty. Compare the provision as a percentage of the gross AR to prior periods and industry benchmarks.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IFRS 9 provision matrix (default rates by aging bucket), AR aging report at month-end, historical collection data supporting the provision rates, forward-looking economic adjustment assessment, provision calculation workings, post-period collection validation, and large account specific provision assessment.

title: Revenue Refund — Customer Complaint Resolution
description: Issuing a credit to a customer's account to resolve a billing complaint.
explanation: Customer credits for complaints reduce recognized revenue. The credit balance is a liability until applied against a future invoice or refunded in cash. Tracking refunds by category helps identify systemic billing issues.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Complaint resolution credits are issued through the CRM system, which routes to the CBS for credit application. The credit reduces the customer's outstanding balance and creates a credit balance if it exceeds the current invoice. The revenue reversal is posted to the CBS and flows to the GL. Credits are categorised by complaint type (billing error, service quality, goodwill) to enable root cause analysis. The customer care system tracks resolution times and credit volumes by complaint category as operational KPIs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors analyse complaint credits by category to identify systemic billing errors — high volumes of credits for a specific charge type indicate a billing system error requiring a broader correction. Test approval of material credits. Review the liability balance representing unspent credits — aged credit balances that customers have not spent may eventually be recognised as breakage revenue (under IFRS 15 if the customer has no right to a cash refund). Confirm that cash refunds processed are consistent with the credit balance on the customer's account.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: CRM complaint log with resolution credit details, CBS credit application records, revenue reversal journal, credit balance liability aging report, complaint root cause analysis by charge type, cash refund records, and IFRS 15 breakage assessment for unspent credit balances.

title: Tower Lease — IFRS 16 Interest Expense
description: Recording the monthly interest cost on the lease liability for a tower site.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, each lease payment splits between principal repayment and interest expense. The interest is calculated on the outstanding lease liability using the implicit or incremental borrowing rate.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The IFRS 16 interest expense is calculated by the lease administration system using the effective interest method — the monthly interest is the outstanding lease liability × (IBR / 12). When the lease payment is made, it is split between the interest expense (P&L) and principal repayment (reduces the lease liability). The lease amortisation schedule in the lease system drives both the interest accrual and the payment allocation. For an operator with thousands of tower leases, the total lease interest is a significant finance cost that is separately disclosed from corporate debt interest.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the IBR used for lease interest calculation — the rate should reflect the operator's incremental borrowing rate at the lease commencement date for a similar term and collateral. Confirm that the effective interest method is applied (not a straight-line approximation). Test the total interest expense against the lease liability roll-forward. Verify that interest on leases is presented separately from interest on financial debt in the P&L (or at minimum disclosed in the notes). Assess whether changes in IBR following lease modifications have been correctly reflected.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Lease administration system amortisation schedule (per tower lease), IBR determination at commencement, monthly interest expense journal, lease liability roll-forward (opening balance + interest - payments = closing balance), IFRS 16 financial statement disclosure, and lease modification IBR reassessment.

title: Tower Sale-Leaseback — Derecognition and Gain
description: Selling owned towers to a TowerCo and recognizing the portion of gain relating to rights transferred.
explanation: Tower sale-leasebacks are a major capital recycling strategy for GCC telecoms. Under IFRS 16, the gain recognized is only the portion relating to the rights transferred to the buyer. The leaseback portion of the gain is deferred in the ROU asset.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tower sale-leaseback is one of the most complex transactions in telecom accounting. Under IFRS 16, a sale-leaseback is only a sale if the transfer of the tower meets the IFRS 15 sale criteria. If it is a sale, the gain is calculated as the difference between the fair value of the transferred rights and the carrying amount. The portion relating to the retained ROU asset (leaseback) is deferred within the ROU asset (reducing the recognised gain). If the proceeds differ from the fair value, an above/below market adjustment is made. A failed sale (e.g., due to a put option) is treated as a secured borrowing.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors perform a detailed IFRS 15 sale criteria assessment for tower sale-leasebacks — this is technically complex and has significant gain recognition implications. Test whether the TowerCo has control of the tower (or whether the seller retains control through the leaseback terms). Calculate the gain split between the transferred portion (recognised) and the retained ROU portion (deferred in the ROU asset). Assess whether the sale proceeds are at fair value — above-market proceeds indicate additional financing that must be recognised as a liability, not a gain. Verify that the leaseback terms are assessed under IFRS 16 and the ROU asset and lease liability are correctly measured.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Tower sale and leaseback agreement, IFRS 15 sale criteria assessment (control transfer analysis), independent fair value of towers, gain calculation (total gain, transferred portion, ROU portion), ROU asset and lease liability recognition, IFRS 16 leaseback assessment, secured borrowing assessment (if sale criteria not met), and Board approval of the transaction.

title: Passive Infrastructure Sharing Income
description: Earning rental income from a competitor sharing space on the telecom's tower sites.
explanation: Tower sharing reduces industry capex and generates incremental income for the host operator. Income is recognized monthly as the space is occupied. This is high-margin revenue with minimal incremental cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Passive infrastructure sharing is billed monthly to the sharing operator per the signed infrastructure sharing agreement. The billing is managed in the wholesale billing system and posted to the AR sub-ledger. Revenue is recognised monthly as space is made available (service period). The arrangement is assessed under IFRS 16 — if the sharing operator has the right to use a specifically identified space on an identified tower, an embedded lease may exist and the income is recognised as lease income under IFRS 16 rather than service revenue under IFRS 15. A tower sharing register tracks each sharing arrangement by tower ID, sharing operator, and monthly charge.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors perform the IFRS 15/16 classification for passive sharing arrangements — dedicated co-location on a named tower with exclusive space rights is typically a lease; non-exclusive space is a service. Test that revenue is recognised only for towers where the sharing operator is actively co-located (not from agreement inception before installation is complete). Reconcile the number of co-located sites to the monthly revenue. Confirm that the sharing agreement rates comply with any TRC regulations on infrastructure sharing pricing.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Infrastructure sharing agreement, tower sharing register (site ID, partner, monthly fee), IFRS 15/16 classification analysis, monthly billing report, co-location confirmation by site, TRC infrastructure sharing regulatory framework compliance, and AR aging for sharing partners.

title: Active RAN Sharing — Cost Split with Partner
description: Recording the cost-sharing payment under a Radio Access Network sharing agreement.
explanation: Active RAN sharing allows two operators to share radio equipment, dramatically reducing network opex. The hosting operator charges the guest operator for their share of network costs.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Active RAN sharing income is calculated based on the cost-sharing formula in the RAN sharing agreement — typically an agreed percentage of the host's network operating costs (energy, maintenance, site rental) attributable to the shared sites. The host operator invoices the guest operator monthly based on actual cost data from the network management and finance systems. The income is posted to the wholesale revenue sub-account. RAN sharing also affects network asset and depreciation accounting — if the guest operator shares ownership of active equipment, the host's capex and depreciation may need to be proportionally allocated.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the cost allocation methodology in the RAN sharing agreement is being applied correctly — test the actual costs used as the basis for the monthly charge. Assess whether the arrangement constitutes a joint operation under IFRS 11 (where both operators have rights to the assets and obligations for the liabilities) rather than a simple service arrangement. If IFRS 11 applies, the host should recognise its proportionate share of the assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses. Test that network assets shared with the partner are not fully depreciated by the host without recognising the partner's cost contribution.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: RAN sharing agreement (cost allocation formula, shared site list), IFRS 11 joint arrangement assessment, monthly cost calculation and invoice, RAN partner AR aging, network asset register for shared sites, depreciation split calculation (if IFRS 11 applies), and TRC approval of the sharing arrangement.

title: Fibre Cable Installation — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the cost of laying fibre optic cable for a new urban coverage area.
explanation: Fibre cable has a useful life of 20-30 years and is capitalized as PPE. The high upfront cost and long life make fibre a strategic infrastructure investment — the depreciation charge is low relative to the revenue potential.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Fibre cable installation is tracked as a capital project (WBS element in SAP PS or Oracle Projects) during the construction phase, accumulating in CWIP. The project captures all directly attributable costs: cable material, civil works (trenching, ducting), splicing, testing, and project management. On network acceptance and commissioning, the CWIP is transferred to the fibre cable PPE asset class in the fixed asset module. Fibre is depreciated over 20–30 years using the straight-line method. The fibre route is documented in the network GIS (Geographic Information System), which links to the asset register by route ID.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that only directly attributable costs are capitalised — allocated overheads and general administrative costs must be excluded. Test that civil work costs (trenching, reinstatement) are capitalised as part of the fibre asset, not expensed as maintenance. Verify that depreciation commences only after the fibre is commissioned and in service. For fibre shared with other operators (dark fibre sold to third parties), assess whether the portion sold should be derecognised or whether an IRU arrangement applies. Test the useful life assumption against the physical lifespan of the fibre.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Capital project WBS, contractor invoices, commissioning acceptance certificate, CWIP-to-PPE transfer journal, fibre route GIS documentation, asset register entry with useful life, depreciation schedule, and dark fibre sharing agreements (if applicable).

title: Undersea / International Cable Capacity Purchase
description: Purchasing capacity on an international submarine cable for international bandwidth.
explanation: International cable capacity is purchased as an indefeasible right of use (IRU) for a fixed term. It is capitalized as an intangible asset and amortized over the IRU period — typically 15-25 years.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Undersea cable IRU purchases are capitalised in the intangible assets module as cable capacity rights. The classification as an intangible asset vs. a right-of-use asset under IFRS 16 depends on whether the operator has the right to use a specifically identified cable segment — dedicated wavelength or fibre pair arrangements are likely IFRS 16 leases. If classified as an intangible, the capacity right is amortised over the IRU term using the straight-line method. The asset is linked to the specific cable (e.g., EASSy, SMW5, FLAG) in the asset register. Maintenance charges paid to the cable consortium are expensed separately.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors perform the IFRS 15/16 classification analysis for submarine cable IRUs — the same principles as terrestrial IRUs apply (identified asset, right to control use). Test the amortisation period against the contractual IRU term. Confirm that cable capacity right values are assessed for impairment — if traffic is re-routed away from a specific cable (due to cheaper alternatives or cable faults), the capacity right may be impaired. Verify that cable consortium annual operating charges are expensed and not capitalised into the cable capacity right.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IRU purchase agreement, IFRS 16 vs. intangible classification analysis, intangible asset register entry (with IRU term and amortisation schedule), cable consortium membership and operating cost invoices, impairment assessment, and traffic routing analysis.

title: Network Decommissioning Cost
description: Recording the cost of removing and disposing of obsolete 2G/3G network equipment.
explanation: Technology migrations require decommissioning legacy networks. If equipment is not fully depreciated at disposal, the net book value is recognized as a loss. Decommissioning costs (removal, disposal) are expensed.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Network decommissioning is managed as a programme in the project management system. Each site or equipment item decommissioned triggers a disposal transaction in the fixed asset module — the original cost and accumulated depreciation are removed, and any remaining NBV is recognised as a loss on disposal. Physical equipment removal costs (contractor labour, transport, disposal fees) are expensed separately. Salvage value recovered from selling scrap equipment is a credit against the decommissioning expense. The network decommission register tracks the status of each decommissioned site against the programme timeline.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the completeness of decommissioning write-offs — all 2G/3G equipment physically removed should be derecognised from the asset register in the same period. Confirm that the NBV written off matches the asset register records. Assess whether a decommissioning provision should have been recognised earlier (IAS 37 — was the obligation present before the formal decommissioning decision?). Review salvage proceeds for reasonableness. Confirm decommissioning does not trigger any lease termination costs for sites being vacated.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Decommissioning programme plan, site-by-site disposal records, fixed asset register write-off entries, salvage proceeds receipts, decommissioning contractor invoices, lease termination assessment for vacated tower sites, and decommissioning cost provision assessment (IAS 37).

title: Asset Under Construction (AUC) — Transfer to PPE
description: Transferring a completed network site from Assets Under Construction to operational PPE.
explanation: Network capex is accumulated in AUC during construction. Once a site is commissioned and live, it transfers to PPE and depreciation begins. The AUC balance is a key metric for tracking capital deployment.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The CWIP/AUC transfer process is triggered by the commissioning and acceptance certificate issued by the network operations team. The capital project in the project management system is flagged as complete, and the fixed asset module generates the CWIP-to-PPE transfer journal. The asset is assigned a unique asset ID, location, and depreciation start date. The AUC balance is monitored monthly by the CFO and capex committee — a persistently high AUC balance indicates capital deployment delays (sites under construction but not yet commissioned). The transfer triggers the start of depreciation from the commissioning date.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the timeliness of CWIP-to-PPE transfers — assets that are commercially operational but still in CWIP are not being depreciated, understating depreciation expense. Test a sample of commissioning certificates against the transfer date in the asset module. Review the AUC ageing report for items that have been in construction for longer than the expected build time — these may be stalled projects requiring write-down assessment. Confirm that depreciation rates assigned at transfer match the approved useful life policy for each asset category.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Commissioning and acceptance certificate per site, capital project completion sign-off, CWIP-to-PPE transfer journal, asset register entry with depreciation start date and useful life, AUC ageing report, stalled project assessment, and capex committee review minutes.

title: Capitalized Borrowing Cost — Network Build (IAS 23)
description: Capitalizing interest on loans specifically used to finance a qualifying network construction project.
explanation: Under IAS 23, borrowing costs directly attributable to a qualifying asset must be capitalized. For telecoms building large network infrastructure over extended periods, this can be a significant amount reducing reported interest expense.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Capitalised borrowing costs are calculated by the treasury and finance team based on either specific borrowings (where the loan was taken out specifically for the qualifying asset) or the weighted average borrowing rate applied to the average AUC balance (for general borrowings). The capitalised amount is posted monthly to the AUC account as a reduction of interest expense. Capitalisation ceases when construction is substantially complete. The calculation is maintained in a treasury model, and the monthly journal is posted as a top-side entry in the ERP. The qualifying period (from commencement of expenditure to substantial completion) is tracked per project.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test whether the qualifying asset criteria are met (substantial period of time to bring to intended use) and whether the construction activities were ongoing during the capitalisation period. Test the capitalisation rate — for specific borrowings, the rate must be the actual borrowing rate; for general borrowings, the weighted average rate must be calculated correctly and include only borrowings outstanding during the period (not new debt raised after the capitalisation period ends). Confirm that capitalisation ceases promptly on commissioning. Test that capitalised borrowing costs are included in the asset's carrying value for impairment testing.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IAS 23 capitalisation policy, qualifying asset identification, specific or general borrowing rate calculation, monthly capitalisation journal with calculation workings, project timeline (commencement and substantial completion dates), suspension periods (if construction was suspended), and borrowings register used for the weighted average rate calculation.

title: Handset Outright Purchase — Walk-In Sale
description: Recording the sale of a handset to a customer who pays full price with no contract.
explanation: An outright sale has a single performance obligation — transfer of the handset. Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously at point of sale. No deferral or bundle allocation is required.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Walk-in handset sales are processed through the retail POS system integrated with the inventory and revenue modules. The sale transaction automatically reduces inventory, posts the COGS, and recognises the sales revenue in the GL simultaneously. Daily POS settlements are reconciled to the bank statement. The retail system tracks sales by model, colour, and storage variant for inventory management. Returns and exchanges within the warranty period are processed as revenue reversals with inventory reinstatement. Outright sales are much simpler from an IFRS 15 perspective than bundle sales.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test POS daily settlements against bank receipts. Confirm inventory reduction at point of sale is correctly reflected in the inventory system. Test the COGS calculation against the weighted average cost or FIFO cost per the inventory valuation method. Review return and exchange transactions — ensure revenue reversals are properly posted and inventory is reinstated at the original cost. Confirm that outright sales are presented separately from bundle handset revenue in management reporting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: POS daily sales report, inventory movement report (model-level), COGS reconciliation to inventory cost, bank settlement confirmation, return and exchange register, inventory valuation method documentation, and outright vs. bundled handset revenue split.

title: Handset Inventory Purchase from Manufacturer
description: Purchasing a batch of smartphones from a manufacturer for retail sale.
explanation: Handset inventory is a significant working capital item for telecoms. Inventory is capitalized at cost including import duties and freight. The mix of handset models and price points directly affects subsidy costs and gross margin.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Handset purchase orders are raised in the procurement module against framework agreements with manufacturers (Apple, Samsung, Huawei). On receipt, goods are counted against the purchase order and the inventory module is updated. The cost includes the manufacturer invoice price, import duties, freight, and insurance (CIF cost). Inventory is valued using the weighted average cost method. The inventory system tracks stock by model, IMEI, and location (warehouse, retail store). Vendor rebates and volume bonuses reduce the cost of inventory and are accrued as receivables against purchases.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors observe or test the physical inventory count at year-end and reconcile to the inventory system. Test the completeness of cost capitalisation — import duties and freight should be included, not expensed. Review vendor rebate receivables for reasonableness and confirm that rebates are deducted from inventory cost (not recognised as revenue unless the contractual conditions are met). Assess slow-moving inventory for write-down to NRV. Test the IMEI register for the existence of high-value handsets.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Purchase orders and vendor invoices, import duty and freight documents, goods receipt note, inventory system stock receipt posting, vendor rebate agreement and accrual, inventory count sheets, aged inventory report, and weighted average cost calculation.

title: Handset Inventory Write-Down — Obsolescence
description: Writing down slow-moving handset models when newer devices make them unsellable at cost.
explanation: Handset technology cycles are short (12-18 months). Models not sold before a new launch may require write-down to NRV. This is a key risk for telecoms carrying large handset inventory.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Inventory write-downs are identified through the inventory ageing and NRV review process, typically performed quarterly. The inventory management system flags models with low sales velocity or upcoming model launches. The commercial and finance teams assess the expected selling price for each model (NRV) against its carrying cost. Where NRV is below cost, the shortfall is written down. The write-down is posted to the inventory write-down expense account. Written-down inventory is often disposed of through special promotions, trade-in deals, or third-party bulk buyers.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the NRV assessment for slow-moving handset models — NRV should be the estimated selling price less estimated costs to sell (including any promotional discounts). Test inventory age against the write-down policy. Confirm that write-downs are applied on a model-by-model basis (not offset across models). Review subsequent selling prices for written-down inventory to assess whether the write-down was adequate. Confirm that write-down reversals (if NRV subsequently exceeds cost) are recognised in accordance with IAS 2 (not exceeding the original write-down amount).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Inventory ageing report by handset model, NRV assessment (market price evidence), write-down calculation and approval, inventory system write-down posting, disposal method for written-down inventory, and subsequent sales price monitoring for reversal assessment.

title: Trade-In Device Received — Inventory Recognition
description: Recording a used device received as a trade-in from a customer upgrading their handset.
explanation: Trade-in devices are recognized at their estimated resale value (NRV). The trade-in value reduces the effective selling price of the new device. The used device is then refurbished and resold.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Trade-in programmes are managed through the retail POS and inventory systems. When a trade-in is accepted, the device is assessed (model, condition, functionality) and a trade-in value is assigned using a pricing guide or automated assessment tool. The used device is received into the 'pre-owned inventory' module at the assessed NRV, with the corresponding credit reducing the new device's selling price. The pre-owned device is then processed by the refurbishment team, which may increase its cost (refurbishment costs are capitalised into inventory). IMEI tracking ensures the second-life cycle is maintained.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the trade-in valuation methodology — NRV estimates must be supportable from the resale market. Test a sample of trade-in devices in inventory against their assessed values and subsequent sale prices. Confirm that refurbishment costs are correctly added to the inventory carrying value. Review IMEI records to confirm all trade-in devices accepted are in the pre-owned inventory system. Assess whether trade-in programmes are generating material volumes that require separate disclosure.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Trade-in assessment tool or pricing guide, trade-in receipts (IMEI, model, condition, assessed value), pre-owned inventory movement report, refurbishment cost tracking, IMEI register for trade-in devices, and subsequent sale price comparison to assessed NRV.

title: Certified Pre-Owned Device Sold
description: Selling a refurbished trade-in device through the telecom's certified pre-owned programme.
explanation: Pre-owned devices generate revenue at a higher margin than new devices since the trade-in cost is low. Revenue and COGS are recognized at the point of sale.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Certified pre-owned sales are processed through the POS or e-commerce system. The COGS is the carrying value of the pre-owned device (trade-in assessed value plus refurbishment costs). Revenue is recognised at point of sale when control passes to the buyer. A limited warranty (typically 3-6 months) on certified pre-owned devices may require a warranty provision under IAS 37 if the operator provides its own warranty rather than simply passing through the manufacturer's. The pre-owned channel may be managed through a dedicated refurbishment partner — the operator's accounting changes if the partner controls the refurbishment and sale process (agent vs. principal).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the COGS is the full carrying value of the pre-owned device (trade-in value + refurbishment costs). Test that warranty provisions for pre-owned devices are recognised where the operator provides its own warranty. Assess the principal/agent classification for third-party refurbishment partners — if the partner controls the pre-owned device before sale, the operator may be an agent (net revenue presentation). Review the NRV of unsold pre-owned inventory for write-down requirements.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Pre-owned inventory movement report (trade-in receipt + refurbishment + sale), COGS calculation per device, POS sales report, warranty provision for pre-owned devices, principal/agent assessment for refurbishment partners, and pre-owned inventory NRV assessment.

title: Device Accessories Sold (Cases, Chargers)
description: Recording the sale of phone accessories at the telecom retail store.
explanation: Accessories are high-margin, low-ticket items sold at the point of handset purchase or separately. Revenue is recognized at point of sale — a single, simple performance obligation.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Accessories inventory is managed separately from handsets in the inventory system, using the same POS integration. Each accessory SKU (stock keeping unit) is assigned a standard cost (or weighted average cost). POS transactions automatically trigger inventory reduction and COGS recognition. Accessories are typically imported and stocked centrally, then distributed to retail stores. Minimum stock levels are configured in the inventory system to trigger automatic replenishment orders. Accessories may also be sold through the online store — e-commerce sales are reconciled to the inventory system.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the accessories physical count at year-end and reconcile to the inventory system. Confirm that accessories returns are processed as revenue reversals with inventory reinstatement. Review the accessories gross margin — a declining margin may indicate shrinkage (theft), pricing errors, or incorrect COGS calculation. Test that accessories purchased bundled with handsets (e.g., earbuds included in box) are correctly included in the handset cost rather than separately inventoried.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Accessories inventory count, POS sales and inventory deduction report, COGS reconciliation to unit cost, accessories purchase orders, shrinkage report, e-commerce accessories sales reconciliation, and accessories gross margin analysis.

title: Warranty Provision on Handsets Sold
description: Accruing a provision for expected warranty claims on handsets sold with a 12-month manufacturer warranty pass-through.
explanation: Where the telecom provides its own warranty (not just passing through the manufacturer's), IAS 37 requires a provision for expected claims based on historical failure rates. The provision is matched to the period of sale.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Where the telecom provides its own warranty (rather than the manufacturer's pass-through), the provision is calculated based on historical warranty claim rates by handset model multiplied by the number of units sold in the period. The provision is recognised in the same period as the related sales. Warranty claims are processed through the customer care system — the cost of repair or replacement is charged against the warranty provision. The provision is reviewed quarterly against the expected remaining warranty exposure. For IFRS 15, an operator-provided warranty that goes beyond quality assurance (e.g., extended coverage) is a separate performance obligation requiring separate revenue allocation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm whether the warranty is the manufacturer's pass-through (no provision required — the cost is borne by the manufacturer) or the operator's own warranty (IAS 37 provision required). Test the provision rate against historical warranty claim data by model. For IFRS 15, assess whether any warranty obligation is a separate performance obligation. Review the adequacy of the provision relative to the outstanding warranty period on sold units. Test warranty claim costs against the provision — high claims vs. provision may indicate under-provisioning.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Warranty terms in the subscriber/device purchase agreement, manufacturer warranty pass-through documentation, historical warranty claim rates by model, provision calculation (units sold × average claim cost × claim rate), warranty claim register, IFRS 15 performance obligation assessment for extended warranties, and provision roll-forward.

title: Interoperator Debt — Aged Receivable Provision
description: Provisioning for overdue interoperator receivables where settlement is uncertain.
explanation: Interoperator receivables can be disputed and age significantly. IFRS 9 ECL provisioning applies to these commercial receivables. Disputed amounts are assessed for probability-weighted recovery.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Interoperator receivables are maintained in the wholesale AR sub-ledger by counterparty and settlement period. The IFRS 9 ECL provision is calculated using a combination of aging analysis (for non-disputed items) and specific assessment (for disputed items). Disputed amounts are individually assessed based on the dispute status, legal advice, and historical settlement patterns with each counterparty. The provision is posted monthly to the bad debt expense account. The interoperator credit committee reviews aged items quarterly and approves provisions and write-offs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the interoperator receivable ageing report and identify items outstanding beyond normal settlement terms (typically 30-60 days). Test disputed items individually — is the dispute documented, and has appropriate escalation occurred? Assess the IFRS 9 ECL provision rate for non-disputed aged items. Review whether long-outstanding interoperator receivables should be written off rather than provisioned. Confirm that interoperator debt collection is actively managed through the bilateral dispute resolution process in the signed interconnect agreements.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Interoperator AR ageing report, dispute log with status and resolution timeline, IFRS 9 ECL provision calculation (aging matrix and specific assessment), interoperator credit committee minutes, bilateral dispute resolution correspondence, and write-off approvals for uncollectable items.

title: Interoperator Settlement — Net Monthly Payment
description: Settling the net monthly interoperator position — paying or receiving the balance after netting termination income against origination costs.
explanation: Operators settle on a net basis monthly. If interconnect payable exceeds receivable, a net cash payment is made. The gross income and expense were already recognized when traffic was carried.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Net settlement payments are processed through the AP module after the monthly reconciliation between the operator and each counterparty is agreed. The net settlement amount (payable minus receivable) is remitted via bank transfer. The payment clears the net payable balance in the GL. Where settlement is on a gross basis (separate payments in each direction), two payment instructions are raised. The settlement is tracked by counterparty in the wholesale billing system. WHT may apply to the gross settlement amount paid to foreign operators (see tel-wht-international-payments).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that the net settlement amount agrees to the signed monthly settlement statement exchanged with each operator. Test that gross income and expense are both fully recognised in the P&L before the net settlement — the settlement itself is purely a balance sheet transaction. Confirm that WHT is correctly applied before the net payment is made. Review any agreed payment deferral arrangements for operators with delayed settlement — ensure the receivable is not netted against the payable in the balance sheet unless the legal right of offset exists.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed monthly settlement statements by counterparty, net settlement calculation, bank payment confirmations, WHT calculation and ISTD remittance, offsetting right assessment (for netting in the balance sheet), and outstanding disputed items register.

title: Transit Traffic Income
description: Earning income for carrying and terminating traffic that originates and terminates on other operators' networks.
explanation: Transit income is earned for routing calls between two other networks. The operator provides the 'middle leg' of an international call. Margins are low but volume can be significant.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Transit traffic is measured by the network mediation system, which captures transit CDRs (calls entering and leaving the network without originating or terminating on it). The transit billing system calculates the income due from the originating operator and the cost owed to the terminating operator, posting both to their respective counterparty accounts. Transit income and transit costs are recognised gross — net presentation is not appropriate as the operator controls the transit routing. Revenue is recognised monthly based on CDR data. Transit rates are typically much lower than termination rates.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that transit income and transit costs are presented gross in the P&L — the operator is a principal in the transit arrangement (it controls the routing and bears the interconnect cost). Test that transit CDRs are reconciled to the amounts invoiced to originating operators. Review transit route profitability — some transit routes may be loss-making if the transit rate received is lower than the termination cost paid on the other side. Confirm that transit traffic is properly excluded from any USO levy calculation if the levy is based on domestic originating traffic only.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Transit CDR report (volume by originating and terminating operator), transit billing statements, transit rate agreement with originating operators, transit cost reconciliation to terminating operators' invoices, transit route profitability analysis, and USO levy calculation exclusion of transit traffic.

title: International Gateway Access Fee
description: Charging operators and ISPs for access to the telecom's international gateway infrastructure.
explanation: Telecoms with international gateway infrastructure charge other operators for accessing it. This infrastructure income is recurring and high-margin as the gateway capex is already sunk.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Gateway access fees are billed monthly per the signed gateway access agreement. The fee may be a fixed monthly charge, a per-Mbps capacity charge, or a per-minute traffic charge depending on the commercial arrangement. Revenue is recognised monthly as gateway access is provided. The gateway infrastructure (international switching equipment, submarine cable landing station) is capitalised as PPE with relatively low incremental cost per additional user, making this a high-margin revenue stream. Usage data from the gateway management system provides the basis for any variable billing.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the fee calculation method matches the signed agreement. Test the IFRS 15 vs. IFRS 16 classification — if access to the gateway is an identified asset that the customer directs the use of, an embedded lease may exist. Assess whether the TRC regulates gateway access fees (in markets with a dominant gateway operator, regulated access is common). Review the gateway access receivable ageing — operators accessing international gateways typically settle promptly given the strategic nature of the service.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Gateway access agreement, IFRS 15/16 classification analysis, gateway usage data (for variable billing), monthly invoice, TRC regulatory framework for gateway access, receivable ageing, and gateway infrastructure asset register.

title: MVNO Wholesale Billing to Hosted Operator
description: Billing a Mobile Virtual Network Operator (MVNO) for network usage at wholesale rates.
explanation: MVNOs lease network capacity from the host operator and resell it under their own brand. The host charges wholesale rates covering voice, data, and SMS usage. This is a low-cost revenue stream as the network cost is already incurred.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: MVNO usage is tracked by the host's network management system and OCS, which differentiates MVNO SIM traffic from its own retail traffic. Monthly usage data (voice minutes, data volumes, SMS counts) is extracted and fed to the wholesale billing system, which applies the MVNO's contracted wholesale rates to generate the monthly invoice. Revenue is recognised monthly as usage is delivered. The MVNO agreement also specifies minimum commitments — where actual usage falls below the minimum, the host recognises the guaranteed minimum revenue regardless. MVNO wholesale revenue is presented separately from retail revenue in management reporting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test MVNO billing completeness by reconciling MVNO SIM traffic from the OCS to the billed usage data. Confirm that minimum commitment revenue is recognised even in periods where actual usage is below the minimum threshold. Review the MVNO receivable ageing — MVNOs typically pay within 30-60 days. Assess the IFRS 15 treatment of any upfront payments from MVNOs (e.g., initial network setup fees) — these may need to be deferred and recognised over the MVNO agreement term.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: MVNO hosting agreement (rates, minimum commitments, settlement terms), OCS traffic report by MVNO SIM prefix, monthly MVNO invoice, minimum commitment revenue recognition, MVNO wholesale receivable ageing, upfront fee deferral assessment, and TRC MVNO licensing confirmation.

title: Technical Staff (Network Engineers) Payroll
description: Recording monthly gross salaries for network engineers, systems administrators, and IT staff.
explanation: Technical staff costs are a significant operating expense capitalized or expensed depending on whether the work relates to network construction (capitalize) or maintenance (expense).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Technical staff payroll is processed through the HR/payroll system with engineers allocated to cost centres by function (network operations, IT, network build). Where engineers work on capital projects (new site builds, 5G rollout), their time is tracked via timesheets and the relevant portion of their salary is capitalised into the AUC (per IAS 16). The timesheet system integrates with the project management system to automatically allocate engineer hours to capital or opex cost centres. Maintenance and operations hours are expensed. The split between capitalised and expensed staff cost is a key area requiring management judgement and consistent policy application.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the capitalisation of internal labour costs — they must be directly attributable to the construction of a specific qualifying asset. Review timesheet completion rates and whether timesheets reflect genuine activity or are filled retrospectively. Test that general engineering training, team management, and non-project activities are not capitalised. The ratio of capitalised to expensed technical staff costs should be consistent with the capex programme intensity — a high capitalisation ratio in a low-capex period warrants scrutiny.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Payroll register by cost centre, timesheet records by engineer, capital project allocation of engineering hours, capitalisation policy for internal labour, capex-to-opex labour split report, and IAS 16 directly attributable cost assessment.

title: Commercial / Sales Staff Payroll
description: Recording monthly salaries for sales, marketing, customer service, and retail channel staff.
explanation: Commercial staff costs are a selling and distribution expense. For retail telecoms, this includes store staff, call centre agents, and field sales teams — typically the second largest cost after network opex.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Commercial staff payroll is processed through the HR/payroll system and posted to selling and distribution cost centres (retail, call centre, field sales, marketing). Commercial staff costs are not capitalised — they are period expenses recognised when incurred. For retail staff working in stores classified as IFRS 16 leases, the staff cost is part of the store's operating cost allocated to the retail channel. Commission-based staff have a base salary component (regular payroll) and a variable commission component (separate payroll run or accrual). The total commercial staff cost is tracked against ARPU and subscriber growth KPIs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that commercial staff costs are not being capitalised as CAC (subscriber acquisition costs) — salaries are not incremental costs to obtain individual contracts under IFRS 15. Test payroll completeness by reconciling headcount in the HR system to payroll records. Review commission structures for any features that may require liability recognition under IAS 19 (e.g., long-service commission bonuses). Confirm that retail store staff costs are allocated to the correct retail channel cost centre.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Payroll register by cost centre, HR headcount report, retail channel cost allocation, commission structure documentation, IFRS 15 CAC classification (salaries excluded), and commercial staff cost vs. revenue KPI analysis.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Telecom Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service benefit for Jordanian telecom employees.
explanation: Large telecoms with hundreds or thousands of employees carry material EOSB liabilities. Monthly provisioning is required under Jordanian labor law to avoid large charges at departure.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: EOSB is calculated by the HR/payroll system monthly for each employee based on their current salary and accumulated years of service. For large telecoms with significant long-service technical workforces, the EOSB liability can be material. Under IAS 19, material EOSB obligations are classified as defined benefit plans — requiring actuarial valuation using the projected unit credit method. The actuarial report is prepared annually and the liability is updated to reflect service cost, interest cost, and actuarial gains and losses. Actuarial gains/losses are recognised in OCI. EOSB payments to departing employees are applied against the provision.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the EOSB provision requires IAS 19 actuarial measurement — if material, a simple monthly accrual understates the liability due to future salary escalation and employee tenure. Engage the client's actuary or an independent actuary to review the valuation. Test the actuarial assumptions (salary growth rate, discount rate, employee attrition rate). Confirm that EOSB is allocated between capitalised amounts (for engineers on capital projects) and expensed amounts. Verify EOSB payments to departing employees are deducted from the provision correctly.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: EOSB provision calculation per employee, IAS 19 actuarial valuation report (if material), actuarial assumptions documentation, OCI actuarial gain/loss posting, EOSB payment records, provision balance reconciliation, and capitalised vs. expensed EOSB split.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — Telecom Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the 14.25% employer SSC contribution for Jordanian telecom employees.
explanation: SSC is calculated on gross salaries. For a large telecom with 400K monthly payroll, the employer SSC is JOD 56,475. It is a mandatory operating cost recognized in the period the salary is earned.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Employer SSC is calculated automatically by the payroll system at 14.25% of each Jordanian employee's gross salary, subject to the SSC salary ceiling. The employer contribution is allocated to cost centres in proportion to the underlying salary cost — network cost centres receive the SSC related to technical staff; commercial cost centres receive the SSC related to commercial staff. The combined employer and employee SSC is remitted to the SSC by the 15th of the following month. For technical staff whose salary is partially capitalised, the related SSC is also partially capitalised into AUC.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile total employer SSC expense to the payroll base multiplied by 14.25%, confirming the ceiling is correctly applied. Confirm timely remittance. For technical staff with capitalised salary, test that the proportionate SSC is also capitalised. Verify that expatriate staff who are not subject to Jordanian SSC are correctly excluded from the SSC calculation. Review any changes to the SSC ceiling during the year and confirm the system configuration was updated promptly.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Payroll SSC calculation report, SSC ceiling schedule, SSC remittance receipts, cost centre allocation of SSC expense, capitalised SSC for technical staff on capital projects, expatriate SSC exemption list, and SSC ceiling update confirmation.

title: Annual Staff Bonus Accrual
description: Accruing the year-end performance bonus for all eligible telecom employees.
explanation: Year-end bonuses are accrued in the performance period and paid in Q1 of the following year. Accrual ensures the expense is matched to the year the performance was delivered.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The bonus accrual is posted as a year-end top-side journal based on management's estimate of the total bonus pool, which is typically determined by the Board compensation committee. The accrual is allocated to cost centres based on each department's headcount and average salary. When bonuses are approved and paid through payroll in Q1, the accrual is reversed and replaced by the actual payroll entries. SSC on bonus payments must also be calculated and provisioned. For listed telecoms, executive bonus disclosures are required in the remuneration report.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the bonus accrual represents a constructive obligation at year-end — has the employee been communicated their bonus entitlement? Test the accrual methodology against the bonus scheme rules. Confirm the accrual is allocated to the correct period (the year of performance, not the year of payment). Review the Board's approval of the bonus pool. Test SSC on the bonus accrual. For listed entities, confirm remuneration disclosures are complete and consistent with the underlying accrual.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bonus scheme rules, Board or compensation committee approval of the bonus pool, accrual calculation by department, SSC calculation on bonus, payroll records for Q1 bonus payment, reversal journal, and remuneration report disclosures for listed entities.

title: Sales Commission — Retail Channel Staff
description: Paying monthly commission to retail store staff based on postpaid activations and handset sales targets.
explanation: Retail commissions are variable selling costs linked to sales performance. They are expensed in the period earned. These are separate from dealer commissions paid to third-party distribution channels.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Internal retail staff commissions are calculated by the retail performance management system based on each store and individual's target attainment (activations, handset sales, plan upgrades). The commission calculation is validated against the CBS activation data before payment. Commissions are processed through the payroll system as variable pay and are subject to income tax withholding and SSC. The expense is allocated to the retail channel cost centre. Unlike dealer commissions, internal staff commissions are not assessed for IFRS 15 CAC capitalisation — they are not incremental costs to obtain individual contracts.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test commission calculations against the performance management system and CBS activation data. Confirm that the commission scheme does not create incentives for mis-selling (e.g., activating postpaid contracts for customers who immediately churn). Review whether internal commission schemes are a form of variable pay subject to IAS 19 short-term employee benefit recognition. Test SSC and income tax treatment of commission payments.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Commission scheme rules, retail performance management system output, CBS activation data cross-reference, payroll commission payment records, SSC and income tax calculation, retail channel cost centre financial statements, and mis-selling risk assessment.

title: Expat Staff Housing Allowance
description: Paying monthly housing allowances for expatriate executive or technical staff.
explanation: Housing allowances for expat staff are part of the employment cost. They are a cash expense disclosed separately in staff cost notes. For senior executives, housing can be a significant component of total compensation.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Expat housing allowances are processed through the payroll system as a separate pay element alongside base salary. The allowance is recorded in the staff expense account allocated to the relevant cost centre (typically technical or executive management). In Jordan, housing allowances paid to expatriates may have different income tax and SSC implications than local staff — the payroll system must be configured to apply the correct tax treatment per work permit and residency status. For senior executives, the total package (salary + housing + school fees + car allowance) is the relevant comparator for IAS 19 remuneration disclosures.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that housing allowances are correctly classified as staff costs (not operating expenses). Test that the tax treatment (income tax and SSC) is appropriate for each expat's residency status. For listed entities, verify that executive housing allowances are included in total remuneration disclosures. Review whether housing provided in-kind (the telecom rents an apartment for the executive) should be assessed under IFRS 16 as a lease.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Employment contract specifying housing allowance entitlement, payroll records, income tax treatment per residency status, SSC exemption documentation (for expats), executive remuneration disclosure, in-kind housing IFRS 16 assessment (if company-rented accommodation), and total cost of expat package analysis.

title: Staff Share Option Scheme — IFRS 2 Expense
description: Recording the monthly IFRS 2 charge for employee share options granted to senior management.
explanation: Under IFRS 2, share options granted to employees are recognized as an expense over the vesting period. The fair value at grant date is spread across the vesting period regardless of subsequent share price movements.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The IFRS 2 charge is calculated by the finance team or a specialist valuer using the Black-Scholes or binomial option pricing model at grant date. The total fair value of options granted is divided by the vesting period (typically 3-4 years) and the monthly expense is posted to the share-based payment expense account with the credit to the share option equity reserve. The calculation model is updated for changes in expected forfeitures (vesting condition adjustments). When options vest and are exercised, the share option reserve is transferred to share capital and share premium. Cash-settled share-based payments have a different accounting treatment (liability, not equity).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review and challenge the option valuation model inputs (share price, exercise price, expected life, volatility, risk-free rate, dividend yield). The volatility assumption for listed telecoms should be based on historical share price data. Test the forfeiture rate assumption — if actual forfeitures differ from estimates, a cumulative catch-up adjustment is required. Confirm the correct vesting period is used and that performance conditions are correctly reflected in the probability of vesting. For unlisted telecoms, market price evidence for the option valuation is more complex and requires additional scrutiny.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Option grant documentation (number of options, exercise price, grant date, vesting conditions), IFRS 2 option valuation model (Black-Scholes or binomial), key model inputs (share price, volatility source, risk-free rate), monthly expense calculation, forfeiture rate and adjustment methodology, share option reserve roll-forward, and exercise and lapse records.

title: Tower Site Rent — Cash Lease Payment
description: Recording the actual monthly cash payment for a tower site lease under IFRS 16.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, the cash lease payment splits between reducing the lease liability (principal) and settling the interest accrual. The P&L impact comes from depreciation of the ROU asset and interest on the lease liability — not the cash payment.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Lease payments are processed through the AP module or direct debit from the bank account. The lease administration system generates the payment split — principal reduction and interest settlement — based on the lease amortisation schedule for each lease. The payment is posted in the ERP with the lease liability (principal) and accrued interest payable (interest) being debited simultaneously. For operators with thousands of tower leases, bulk payment runs are processed with the lease system feeding the payment allocation to the ERP. The cash flow statement presents lease payments in financing activities (principal) and operating/financing activities (interest, per the operator's accounting policy).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the payment split (principal vs. interest) matches the lease amortisation schedule for a sample of tower leases. Test that the accrued interest is fully settled by the payment — no residual balance should remain after a scheduled payment unless it was a partial payment. Confirm that the cash flow statement presents the principal and interest components of lease payments correctly per IAS 7. For variable lease payments (e.g., rent reviews), verify the lease liability has been remeasured and the payment split reflects the revised amortisation schedule.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Lease amortisation schedule (per lease), monthly payment instruction, bank payment confirmation, ERP posting (liability + accrued interest debit, cash credit), cash flow statement lease payment classification, variable rent review remeasurement records, and aggregate lease liability roll-forward.

title: Base Station Energy / Electricity Cost
description: Recording the monthly electricity cost for running base transceiver stations and tower equipment.
explanation: Energy is one of the largest network operating costs for mobile telecoms. Base stations run 24/7 and power consumption is significant. Energy efficiency (solar, battery backup) is a major OPEX reduction initiative in the GCC.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Network energy costs are invoiced by the electricity provider (in Jordan, EDCO/IDECO/JEPCO) monthly per metered connection at each BTS site. The energy invoices are processed through the AP module, coded to the network energy expense account and the relevant network cost centre. Where electricity bills arrive with a one-month lag, a monthly accrual is required based on prior period consumption. Energy cost per site is monitored in the network management system — sites consuming above the benchmark trigger an energy audit. Solar installation costs are capitalised and reduce the energy opex over time.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the monthly energy accrual for unbilled consumption at year-end. Reconcile total energy expense to the number of active BTS sites multiplied by the average energy cost per site — large variances may indicate unrecorded accruals or site-level billing errors. Confirm that solar installation costs are correctly capitalised and that the energy saving is reflected in the opex reduction analysis. Review whether long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) for renewable energy contain embedded leases under IFRS 16.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Electricity invoices by site, energy accrual calculation (for unbilled sites), network energy cost-per-site analysis, solar installation capex records, energy efficiency programme savings tracking, PPA IFRS 16 embedded lease assessment, and annual energy consumption report.

title: Network Operations Centre (NOC) Maintenance
