For shipments near period-end, test the cut-off — COGS and revenue should be recognised in the same period for each shipment. Review the non-cash COGS component (amortisation) to confirm it matches the amortisation schedule. Test that royalties are presented separately from COGS if they are a government impost rather than a production cost.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Production cost model (mining + processing + overhead + amortisation per tonne), concentrate inventory movement schedule, COGS calculation by shipment, non-cash COGS component (UoP amortisation), period-end inventory count or measurement, shipment timing documentation for cut-off testing, and COGS reconciliation to management cost report.

title: Ore Stockpile NRV Write-Down
description: Writing down ore stockpile inventory to net realizable value when metal prices decline below cost.
explanation: Ore stockpile and concentrate inventory are carried at the lower of cost and net realizable value (IAS 2). NRV is the estimated selling price (at current metal prices) less estimated costs of completion (processing for ore, shipping and TC/RC for concentrate) and selling. When metal prices fall sharply, the NRV of ore stockpiles can fall below production cost — requiring a write-down.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: NRV testing is performed at each reporting date for all inventory categories. The NRV calculation for ore stockpile is: (ore grade × metal price × plant recovery) less processing cost to convert to saleable product, less freight and selling costs. For concentrate inventory in transit, NRV is the provisional selling price less remaining TC/RC and freight. The NRV write-down is recognised as a period cost. IAS 2 permits reversal of NRV write-downs in subsequent periods if metal prices recover — the reversal is recognised as a reduction in COGS in the reversal period.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test NRV at period-end using the prevailing metal price and the actual cost structure (not budgeted costs). For ore in a heap leach or stockpile with a long processing time, the NRV calculation must include discounting to reflect the time value of money if the processing period is extended. Confirm that the metal price used for NRV is the spot price at the reporting date (not a management forecast). Test reversal of previous write-downs — reversals cannot exceed the original write-down amount.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Metal price at reporting date (LME closing, iron ore index), ore grade survey, plant recovery rate, processing cost per tonne, NRV calculation by stockpile type, write-down journal and approval, reversal assessment in subsequent periods, and NRV sensitivity analysis to metal price changes.

title: Mine Asset Impairment — CGU Assessment
description: Recognising an impairment of a mining operation's assets when recoverable amount falls below carrying value.
explanation: Mine assets are tested for impairment under IAS 36 when impairment indicators arise — a decline in metal prices, increase in operating costs, reduction in reserve estimates, or changes in regulation. The recoverable amount (higher of value-in-use and fair value less costs to sell) is calculated for the mine as a Cash-Generating Unit (CGU). If the recoverable amount is below the carrying value of mine assets, an impairment is recognised.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Mine impairment testing is performed at the CGU level — typically the individual mine operation. The impairment model (DCF-based value-in-use or fair value less costs to sell based on market comparable transactions) is built in the mine's financial planning tool. Key inputs include: LOM production plan, long-term metal price assumptions, operating cost forecasts, capital expenditure plan, and the appropriate discount rate (WACC). The goodwill allocated to the mine CGU is tested first, then underlying assets pro-rata. Impairment reversal is permitted for mine assets (excluding goodwill) if conditions improve.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors engage valuation specialists for material mining impairments. Key assumptions challenged: long-term metal price (compared to analyst consensus and futures), discount rate (WACC — mining companies carry significant risk premiums), production profile (reserve vs. resource, mining dilution), and operating cost escalation. For FVLCS-based tests, confirm comparable transaction multiples or discounted cash flow values used reflect current market conditions. For value-in-use models, test that the cash flows reflect IAS 36 requirements (Board-approved plans, no speculative enhancements). Review whether the goodwill allocation to mine CGUs is appropriate and has been consistently applied.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: IAS 36 impairment model (DCF or comparable transaction approach), long-term metal price assumptions and source, discount rate (WACC) derivation, LOM production plan and reserve estimate, operating cost and capex forecasts, CGU definition and goodwill allocation, comparable transaction data (if FVLCS approach), sensitivity analysis to key assumptions, Board approval of LOM plan, and independent valuation (for material impairments).

title: Gold Doré Production — Transfer to Bullion Inventory
description: Recording the production of gold doré bars from the carbon-in-leach (CIL) or heap leach circuit.
explanation: Gold miners produce doré bars (impure gold-silver alloy) at site and transport them to refineries for further purification. The doré inventory is valued at production cost — the accumulated mining and processing cost per ounce of gold (and silver) contained. Doré is a significant balance sheet item for gold miners between production and sale at the refinery.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Doré production is tracked in the production module by pour date and bar number. Each bar has an assay certificate showing the gold and silver content (in troy ounces). The cost per ounce is calculated by dividing total production costs (mining + processing + site overhead + amortisation) by the gold-equivalent ounces produced. The doré inventory balance represents bars produced but not yet sold or refined. Doré in transit to the refinery is 'bullion in transit' — maintained in inventory until title passes to the refinery or buyer. Security and insurance coverage are critical for doré in transit.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors physically count or confirm doré bars at site — bars are typically numbered and weighed, with assay certificates confirming gold content. Confirm bars in transit with the logistics provider and insurer. Test the cost per ounce calculation against the period's production cost and ounces produced. Review the valuation of the silver by-product component in the doré — some miners credit silver by-product credits against the cost of gold production (reducing gold COGS). Confirm the doré is not subject to any forward sale that would require mark-to-market accounting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Doré bar register (bar number, weight, assay certificate), period production report (ounces poured), cost per ounce calculation, bullion in transit insurance and logistics documentation, refinery assay certificate (confirming gold/silver content), silver by-product credit treatment, and doré sales contracts (for forward-sold inventory disclosure).

title: Streaming Agreement — Upfront Payment Received
description: Recording the upfront cash payment received from a precious metals streamer in exchange for the right to purchase future production at a fixed price.
explanation: In a streaming agreement, a mining company receives a large upfront payment and commits to sell a fixed percentage of future metal production to the streamer at a below-market fixed price (the 'stream price'). The upfront payment is deferred as a liability and recognised as revenue as metal is delivered to the streamer. The stream is similar to a forward sale obligation — a significant financing instrument in mining.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The streaming liability is recognised at the upfront cash received. As metal is delivered under the stream, the deferred liability is reduced and revenue is recognised. The recognition rate is calculated as: (metal delivered to streamer / total expected deliveries under the stream) × upfront cash payment. Alternatively, IFRS 9 treatment may classify the stream as a financial liability measured at amortised cost (if the delivery obligation is a contractual obligation to deliver a fixed amount of cash or another financial asset). The classification — contract liability under IFRS 15 vs. financial liability under IFRS 9 — requires careful analysis and significantly affects income statement presentation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the streaming agreement is classified as: (a) a contract liability under IFRS 15 (recognised as revenue as performance obligations are satisfied — metal deliveries), (b) a financial liability under IFRS 9 (measured at amortised cost with interest expense), or (c) a derivative (if the delivery is at the company's or streamer's discretion). The classification drives the entire income statement and balance sheet treatment. For IFRS 15 treatment, test the delivery schedule and deferred liability release. For IFRS 9 treatment, test the EIR calculation and amortised cost. Confirm the streaming liability is correctly presented as a non-current liability (or split current/non-current per the expected delivery schedule).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Streaming agreement (delivery obligations, stream price, upfront payment terms), IFRS 9/15/IAS 32 classification analysis, deferred liability recognition schedule (based on delivery profile), metal delivery records to streamer, revenue recognised per delivery, EIR calculation (if IFRS 9 treatment), streaming liability roll-forward, and LOM production plan supporting the delivery profile.

title: Offtake Prepayment — Advance from Smelter
description: Recording an advance payment received from a smelter or trader against future concentrate deliveries.
explanation: Miners sometimes receive advance payments from off-takers (smelters, traders) against future production deliveries. The advance is a financial liability until the concentrate is delivered — at which point it offsets against the concentrate sales receivable. These arrangements are a form of commodity-linked financing common in junior mining where bank debt is difficult to obtain.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The prepayment liability is maintained in the ERP's non-current or current liabilities module depending on the expected delivery timeline. As concentrate is delivered under the off-take agreement, the prepayment liability is applied against the concentrate receivable. Interest may or may not be explicit in the arrangement — if there is a below-market stream price implied by the advance, a financing component must be assessed under IFRS 9/15. The arrangement must be reviewed for embedded derivatives (the off-take price may be linked to metal prices — creating a commodity price derivative embedded in the liability).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the prepayment arrangement contains a significant financing component (IFRS 15 para. 60-65) — if the buyer is providing financing, the cash price equivalent should be determined and the difference allocated to interest expense. Test the embedded derivative assessment — if the advance amount or repayment is linked to future metal prices, an embedded derivative requiring bifurcation under IFRS 9 may exist. Confirm the prepayment liability is being applied correctly as concentrate is delivered. Review the security provided by the miner to the off-taker (typically security over the mine asset or production).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Offtake prepayment agreement (advance amount, delivery obligations, repayment mechanism), IFRS 9/15 classification and significant financing component assessment, embedded derivative analysis, delivery record and prepayment application, security documents, interest calculation (if explicit or implicit), and prepayment liability roll-forward.

title: Corporate Income Tax — Mining Operation
description: Accruing income tax on the mining company's taxable profit from mineral extraction.
explanation: Mining companies face complex tax regimes that often include royalties (which may or may not be income tax deductible), resource rent taxes (profit-based additional taxes triggered when returns exceed a threshold), and ring-fencing (where losses in one mine cannot be offset against profits in another). Significant deferred tax timing differences arise from accelerated capital allowances on mine assets versus UoP amortisation used for accounting.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tax computation for mining operations typically requires specialist tax software (Thomson Reuters ONESOURCE, CorpTax) given the complexity of mining tax regimes. Key mining tax features: (1) accelerated capital allowances (capital expenditure may be immediately deductible for tax, creating a large DTA in development years), (2) royalty deductibility (ad valorem royalties may be deductible; specific royalties vary), (3) resource rent taxes (additional profit-based tax layers), (4) exploration cost deductibility (expensed immediately for tax vs. capitalised for accounting), (5) ring-fencing by mine or by jurisdiction. The ERP must maintain the tax cost base of each mine asset separately from the accounting NBV.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the reconciliation between accounting profit and taxable profit — the large timing differences in mining make this reconciliation complex and a key audit area. Test accelerated capital allowance claims against the capital expenditure register. Assess the resource rent tax calculation — the threshold return and rate are jurisdiction-specific and change with metal prices. Review ring-fencing — confirm losses at one operation cannot be offset against income from another in the tax return. Confirm royalties are correctly included or excluded from the tax calculation per the relevant jurisdiction's mining tax law.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Tax computation (accounting profit to taxable profit reconciliation), capital allowance schedule (tax cost base vs. accounting NBV), resource rent tax calculation, royalty deductibility assessment, deferred tax schedule (timing differences by category), tax loss carryforward schedule (by jurisdiction and ring-fence), mining tax law applicable in each jurisdiction, and tax adviser's opinion on key judgements.

title: Deferred Tax — Accelerated Capital Allowances vs. UoP Amortisation
description: Recording the deferred tax on the timing difference between accelerated tax depreciation and accounting UoP amortisation.
explanation: In many mining jurisdictions, capital expenditure on mine development is immediately deductible for tax (100% deduction in year of expenditure) while for accounting it is capitalised and amortised over the mine life using UoP. This creates a large deferred tax asset in the development years (tax benefit taken upfront; accounting charge spread over mine life). As the mine produces, the DTA unwinds — higher taxable profits relative to accounting profits in production years.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The deferred tax calculation for mining requires maintaining a shadow 'tax asset register' that tracks the tax written-down value of each mining asset separately from the accounting NBV. The DTA = (accounting NBV - tax written-down value) × tax rate. For development-stage operations with 100% capital allowances, the tax WDV reaches zero in the first year of the allowance — the entire accounting NBV represents a DTA. As production proceeds, the DTA unwinds each year as UoP amortisation reduces the accounting NBV while the tax WDV (already zero) remains at zero. The DTA is recognised only to the extent it is probable that future taxable profits will be available for utilisation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the DTA recoverability assessment — for mines with long development periods or mines in lower-grade commodity environments, the probability of recovering large DTAs against future taxable profits requires careful analysis. Test the LOM financial model's projected taxable profits against the DTA balance. Confirm that the shadow tax asset register correctly reflects all capital allowances taken in prior periods. Review the ring-fencing constraint — if the DTA can only be utilised against future profits from the same mine (due to ring-fencing), the recovery timeline may extend significantly.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Shadow tax asset register (tax WDV vs. accounting NBV per asset), capital allowance calculations per jurisdiction, DTA recoverability assessment (LOM projected taxable profits), ring-fencing analysis, deferred tax roll-forward, effective tax rate reconciliation, and tax adviser confirmation of applicable capital allowance rates and deductibility rules.

title: Joint Venture Mine — Proportionate Share Accounting
description: Recording the owner's proportionate share of a joint operation mine's assets, liabilities, revenues, and costs.
explanation: Many major mines are operated as joint ventures or unincorporated joint operations (UJOs). Under IFRS 11, a joint operation — where each party has rights to the assets and obligations for the liabilities — is accounted for by recognising the owner's proportionate share of the JV's assets, liabilities, revenues, and costs in its own financial statements. This differs from a joint venture (equity method) where only the net investment is recognised.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Joint operation accounting is maintained by the operator, who provides each participant with a monthly management statement showing their proportionate share of costs, production, and capital. Each participant records these proportionate amounts in their own ERP as if they directly owned that share of the mine. The operator's management statements must comply with the joint operating agreement (JOA) and be audited separately. For large multi-billion dollar mines (BHP-Rio JVs, Escondida, Olympic Dam), the proportionate share can be a major component of each participant's financial statements.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the IFRS 11 classification — is the mine a joint operation (proportionate share accounting) or a joint venture (equity method)? The key test is whether each party has direct rights to the assets and obligations for the liabilities under the contractual arrangement. Test the proportionate share calculation against the JOA ownership percentages. Confirm that the operator's monthly management statements are complete and accurately reflect the mine's activity. Review whether any changes in ownership percentage (farm-ins, farm-outs) have been correctly accounted for.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Joint operating agreement (JOA — ownership percentages, operator responsibilities), IFRS 11 classification analysis, operator monthly management statements, proportionate share calculation, audit of operator's financial statements, JOA amendment history, and farm-in/farm-out transaction accounting (if ownership changed during the period).

title: Metal Price Hedging — Forward Sale Contract
description: Recording the fair value of gold or copper forward contracts used to hedge future production revenue.
explanation: Mining companies use metal forward contracts to hedge the price they receive for future production. When hedging under IFRS 9 cash flow hedge accounting, fair value changes on the derivative go to OCI (not P&L) and are recycled to revenue when the hedged production is sold. An increase in the spot price creates a loss on the forward (liability position), while an unhedged producer would benefit from the higher price — the hedge 'locks in' the earlier lower price.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Metal hedge derivatives are managed in the treasury management system (TMS — Openlink, Finastra, or equivalent commodity risk system). At each reporting date, the forward contracts are marked to market using LME or gold fix forward prices. For a short-side hedge (the miner has sold forward), a rising metal price creates a mark-to-market loss on the hedge (the miner has locked in a lower selling price). The effective portion goes to OCI; any ineffectiveness goes to P&L. When production is delivered into the hedge, the OCI balance is recycled to reduce (or increase) revenue. Mining companies often disclose the average hedge price vs. spot price to show the opportunity cost or benefit of hedging.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the fair value of metal derivatives against independent commodity price data (LME forward curves, gold forward rates). Confirm IFRS 9 hedge designation documentation — the relationship between the hedge instrument and hedged item (future production) must be formally documented at inception. Test hedge effectiveness — for a straightforward commodity forward, effectiveness should be high. Confirm the hedged production volumes are consistent with the mine's production plan. Review whether any hedge positions have been unwound or restructured (creating gains/losses that may be deferred in OCI).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Hedge derivative contract confirmations, IFRS 9 hedge designation documentation, LME or gold forward price at reporting date, mark-to-market valuation, hedge effectiveness testing, OCI roll-forward (fair value changes - recycled to revenue = closing balance), production plan supporting hedged volumes, and hedge position disclosure (volume hedged, average hedge price, period of delivery).

title: Reclamation Fund — Mandatory Regulatory Contribution
description: Recording mandatory cash contributions to a government-administered mine reclamation fund.
explanation: Many jurisdictions require miners to contribute cash to a government-held mine reclamation fund or post security bonds. These contributions are recorded as restricted financial assets — they cannot be used for operations. The restricted asset offsets part of the rehabilitation provision liability (but does not eliminate the accounting provision, which is measured at the present value of the full rehabilitation cost).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Reclamation fund contributions are tracked in the restricted assets section of the ERP balance sheet. The fund balance earns interest, which is credited to the fund (reducing the company's future contribution requirement). The fund is not netted against the rehabilitation provision on the balance sheet — both the full provision (gross liability) and the fund (separate asset) are presented. IFRIC 5 governs the accounting for interests in decommissioning, restoration, and rehabilitation funds — the right to receive a reimbursement from the fund is recognised separately from the liability.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors obtain direct confirmation of the reclamation fund balance from the government custodian. Confirm that the fund is classified as a restricted asset (not available for operations). Test interest earned on the fund is credited to the fund (and may reduce future contribution requirements). Confirm that the accounting rehabilitation provision (PV of total estimated closure cost) is not reduced by the fund balance — they are presented gross. Review whether the fund balance plus expected future contributions are sufficient to cover the full rehabilitation liability — a deficiency creates additional future contribution obligations.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Reclamation fund government statement, fund contribution schedule per regulation, fund balance confirmation from the government custodian, IFRIC 5 accounting treatment, rehabilitation provision balance (separate, not netted), interest earned on fund, fund sufficiency assessment (fund vs. full rehabilitation liability), and regulatory correspondence on contribution requirements.

title: Royalty Income — Mineral Rights Lessor
description: Recording royalty income earned by a landowner or mineral rights holder when a miner extracts minerals from their property.
explanation: Entities that own mineral rights or surface rights over mineralized land earn royalties from mining companies extracting minerals under licence. Royalty income is recognized as the mineral is extracted (usage-based) — the IFRS 15 sales-based royalty exception applies. The royalty is calculated based on reports submitted by the mining company showing production volumes and metal prices.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Royalty income receivables are tracked against the production reports received from the mining operator. The royalty is recognized when the underlying mineral is produced or sold (usage-based), not when cash is received. For royalties based on revenue (NSR — Net Smelter Return royalties), the income equals the miner's net revenue from that mineral times the royalty percentage. The royalty receivable is an IFRS 9 financial asset at amortised cost. For publicly listed royalty companies (Franco-Nevada, Royal Gold, Wheaton Precious Metals), royalty income is the primary revenue stream — the portfolio of royalties is the core asset.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors obtain production and revenue reports from the mine operator and independently calculate the royalty due. For NSR royalties, test the operator's net smelter return calculation — the deductions from gross revenue (TC/RC, transport, penalties) reduce the NSR base and therefore the royalty. Confirm the royalty rate and base per the royalty deed. Review the creditworthiness of the mining operator — royalty receivables have counterparty risk. For royalties with minimum payments or advance royalties, assess whether the minimum payment creates a liability.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Royalty deed (royalty percentage, base, payment terms), mine operator's production and revenue report, royalty calculation, royalty receivable reconciliation, advance royalty or minimum payment terms, operator credit assessment (IFRS 9 ECL), and royalty audit rights (for verification of operator's reports).

title: Tailings Storage Facility — Capital Construction
description: Capitalising the construction cost of a tailings storage facility (TSF) for processing waste.
explanation: Tailings storage facilities are critical and often controversial infrastructure for processing residue. The TSF is capitalised as a mine development asset and amortised over its operational life (UoP method aligned with the mine's production life). TSF failures have catastrophic environmental consequences (Brumadinho, Samarco) — the regulatory and liability implications are enormous. TSF design, construction, and monitoring costs are all capitalised as part of the TSF asset.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: TSF construction is tracked as a major capital project in the ERP project module, typically spanning multiple years. The initial construction, annual heightening (as the TSF fills with tailings), and monitoring infrastructure are all capitalised. The TSF is a single asset but with stages — each heightening campaign adds to the asset at its incremental cost. The TSF asset is amortised using UoP based on the tonnes of tailings it will accommodate over its life, which aligns with the mine's production life. The rehabilitation provision includes TSF closure costs (capping, revegetation, long-term water monitoring).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the TSF is classified as a mine asset (PPE, not investment property). Test the capitalisation of heightening costs — each heightening extends the TSF's capacity and is capitalised. Confirm the TSF meets current regulatory standards — post-Brumadinho, TSF design standards have tightened significantly (GISTM — Global Industry Standard on Tailings Management). Assess whether any TSF stability issues (seismic risk, water saturation) represent impairment indicators. Review the rehabilitation provision for TSF closure — long-term water treatment obligations can persist for decades after mine closure.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: TSF design and construction permits, geotechnical stability report (independent), capital project cost tracking, TSF capacity calculation and UoP amortisation schedule, annual heightening capital programme, GISTM compliance assessment, TSF monitoring programme, rehabilitation provision for TSF closure (including long-term water treatment), and TSF emergency action plan.

title: Ongoing Water Treatment and Environmental Monitoring
description: Recording the ongoing cost of water treatment and environmental monitoring at an operating mine.
explanation: Operating mines incur ongoing environmental costs — acid mine drainage treatment, water quality monitoring, groundwater management, and dust suppression. These are operating costs (not capital) and are expensed as incurred. They are a direct production cost that should be included in the mine's cost per tonne calculation. Post-closure water treatment costs are part of the rehabilitation provision.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Environmental operating costs are tracked in a dedicated environmental cost centre within the mine's operating cost structure. They are allocated to the mine's production cost per tonne — reducing the mine's operating margin. For operations in water-stressed regions (increasingly common with climate change), water management costs are growing. Post-closure water treatment commitments (perpetual treatment of acid mine drainage) must be reflected in the rehabilitation provision — the discounted present value of perpetual treatment costs is a significant and growing rehabilitation liability.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm that ongoing environmental costs are expensed (not capitalised as mine assets). Test that the rehabilitation provision includes the present value of post-closure water treatment commitments — the perpetuity value of ongoing water treatment is a complex estimate requiring specialist input. Review regulatory compliance — environmental monitoring reports submitted to the regulator identify any deficiencies creating potential fines or remediation obligations. Assess whether any environmental incidents during the period create additional provision requirements (spills, exceedances of permitted discharge limits).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Environmental management plan, water treatment cost invoices, environmental monitoring reports (submitted to the regulatory authority), water quality data, post-closure water treatment provision calculation (including perpetuity estimate for acid mine drainage), environmental regulatory correspondence, incident register (spills, exceedances), and rehabilitation provision update for post-closure treatment.

title: Smelter Treatment and Refining Charges (TC/RC)
description: Recording the treatment and refining charges payable to the smelter as a deduction from mineral revenue.
explanation: Concentrate producers pay smelters a treatment charge (TC — per dry metric tonne of concentrate) and refining charge (RC — per pound of metal refined) to process their concentrate into refined metal. TC/RC are deducted from the concentrate revenue — they are not a separate expense but a reduction in the selling price. TC/RC are negotiated annually between miners and smelters, and are a key commercial term in concentrate off-take agreements.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: TC/RC are calculated and deducted from the concentrate invoice in the ERP billing module. The off-take agreement specifies the TC (in US$/dmt of concentrate) and RC (in US cents per pound of payable metal). These are deducted from the gross metal value to arrive at the net payable amount. Concentrate with high impurity levels (arsenic, fluorine, bismuth) may attract additional penalties that are deducted similarly. TC/RC are presented as a deduction from revenue (not a separate line item in cost of sales) — this is an important presentation choice affecting gross margin metrics.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test TC/RC deductions against the signed off-take agreement for each shipment. Confirm that TC/RC rates applied match the current benchmark or contractual rates. Review impurity penalty deductions for each shipment — confirm penalty triggers and rates match the contract. Test whether TC/RC is presented as a revenue deduction (appropriate, as it is part of the pricing mechanism) or as cost of sales (potentially misleading). For annual TC/RC benchmark negotiations, assess whether the contracted rates are at market benchmark levels.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Off-take agreement (TC/RC schedule, penalty provisions), benchmark TC/RC rate publications (CRU, Wood Mackenzie), shipment invoice with TC/RC deduction, concentrate assay certificate (for penalty calculation), TC/RC presentation policy (revenue deduction vs. cost of sales), and annual TC/RC negotiation documentation.

title: Concentrate Freight and Marine Insurance
description: Recording shipping and insurance costs on concentrate transport from mine to smelter.

entries.account: Freight and Shipping Expense (or Revenue Deduction)

explanation: Concentrate is transported from the mine to the smelter by truck, rail, and ship. Freight costs may be borne by the miner (FOB shipping point) or the smelter (delivered pricing). Marine insurance protects the concentrate in transit against loss. Under FOB terms, freight is an expense; under CIF terms, it is included in the delivered price. The treatment affects revenue measurement.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Freight costs are captured in the ERP based on shipping invoices from freight forwarders and shipping companies. Under FOB pricing, the miner recognises revenue at the port of loading and freight is a separate operating expense. Under CIF pricing, revenue is higher (includes freight) but freight cost is an offsetting expense — the net is the same. Marine insurance premiums are paid for each shipment — a prepaid cost recognised as the vessel is in transit. The shipping terms (FOB, CIF, CFR) determine the revenue recognition point and freight expense treatment.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the shipping terms in the off-take agreement and ensure the revenue recognition point matches (FOB = loading port, CIF = destination). Test that freight is an expense under FOB terms (not included in revenue). Confirm marine insurance provides coverage for the full value of concentrate in transit. Review concentrate in transit at period-end — the quantity and value of concentrate on vessels are significant assets that must be confirmed through shipping documents.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Off-take agreement (shipping terms — FOB or CIF), bill of lading per shipment, freight invoices, marine insurance certificates, concentrate in transit schedule at period-end, revenue recognition policy (loading port vs. destination), and shipping company performance data (delivery confirmation).

title: Resource Rent Tax (Profits-Based Additional Tax)
description: Accruing a resource rent tax or super profits tax triggered when mining returns exceed a threshold rate.
explanation: Several mining jurisdictions impose resource rent taxes (Australia's MRRT, Papua New Guinea's MRAT, various African regimes) triggered when the rate of return on mining investment exceeds a specified threshold (e.g., the long-term bond rate plus 7%). These are profit-based levies in addition to corporate income tax. Under IAS 12, resource rent taxes may be income taxes (if they are a tax on income) or operating levies (requiring IAS 37 treatment) — the distinction affects deferred tax accounting.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Resource rent tax calculations are typically performed outside the core ERP in specialist tax models that apply the complex uplift, threshold, and credit mechanisms specific to each jurisdiction's legislation. The result is fed into the ERP as a provision. The key judgment is whether the RRT constitutes an income tax for IAS 12 purposes — if it does, deferred tax must be calculated. The tax base of mining assets under the RRT regime (which may use different starting values and allowance rates than the corporate income tax regime) creates additional deferred tax complexity.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors engage specialist mining tax advisers for resource rent tax assessments. The IAS 12 vs. IAS 37 classification is the primary accounting judgment — income taxes require deferred tax accounting; non-income levies do not. Test the RRT threshold calculation — the 'allowance' mechanism (uplift on project expenditure) reduces the taxable amount. Confirm that the project expenditure used in the allowance calculation matches the capital expenditure records. Review the RRT in the context of the effective tax rate — high combined royalty + income tax + RRT burdens can make operations marginal.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Resource rent tax legislation and regulations, RRT calculation model (project expenditure, uplift allowances, threshold return, taxable profit), IAS 12 vs. IAS 37 classification analysis, deferred RRT calculation (if IAS 12 applies), RRT payment schedule, specialist tax adviser's opinion, and effective total tax rate reconciliation (royalty + CIT + RRT as a percentage of pre-tax income).

title: Sustaining Capital Expenditure vs. Operating Cost
description: Recording sustaining capital expenditure that maintains the mine's productive capacity at the existing level.
explanation: Sustaining capital expenditure (sustaining capex) replaces worn equipment, maintains infrastructure, and sustains production at existing levels — as distinct from growth capex which increases capacity. Sustaining capex is capitalised and depreciated over the equipment's useful life. The World Gold Council's AISC (All-In Sustaining Cost) metric specifically includes sustaining capex — it has become the industry's primary cost reporting standard.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Sustaining capex is classified separately from growth capex in the ERP project management module and capital budget. The capital vs. operating expenditure boundary is a significant judgment area in mining: equipment replacement above the capitalization threshold is sustaining capex; consumable replacement (drill bits, wear parts, tyres on haul trucks) is operating expenditure. Some operators use a 'betterment' test — only costs that extend the equipment's life or improve its capability are capitalised; like-for-like replacement may be expensed depending on policy. AISC reporting requires disclosure of sustaining capex as a component of the cost metric.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the capital vs. operating classification of maintenance and replacement expenditures. For haul truck tyre replacement (a significant cost in open pit mining), the treatment varies — some classify as sustaining capex (capitalise and amortise), others as operating consumable (expense). Confirm the company's capitalisation policy is consistently applied. Review AISC disclosure — sustaining capex included in AISC should agree to the capital expenditure schedule. Test that growth capex (expanding the mine) is correctly excluded from AISC.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Capital vs. operating expenditure policy, sustaining capex project register, equipment replacement records, AISC calculation reconciliation, sustaining vs. growth capex split disclosure, component replacement accounting analysis (IAS 16), haul truck tyre treatment documentation, and capital budget allocation by category.

title: All-In Sustaining Cost (AISC) — Non-GAAP Disclosure
description: Calculating and disclosing the World Gold Council AISC metric for a gold mining operation.
explanation: AISC (All-In Sustaining Cost) is a non-GAAP industry metric developed by the World Gold Council representing the full cost of maintaining a mine in production, excluding growth capex and corporate costs. AISC = mining costs + processing costs + G&A + royalties + sustaining capex + mine closure costs per ounce of gold produced. It is the primary cost benchmark used by investors and analysts to compare gold miner efficiency.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: AISC is calculated from the ERP's cost data but presented as a non-GAAP supplementary metric. The reconciliation from reported GAAP cost of sales to AISC bridges: starting from GAAP COGS (which includes depreciation/amortisation), removing non-cash items (D&A), adding sustaining capex (cash capital expenditure), adding royalties (if not in COGS), adding corporate-level G&A attributable to the mine, and dividing by ounces sold (or produced, depending on disclosure policy). The World Gold Council's 2013 guidance and subsequent updates define the components. Each component is sourced from the ERP cost centres.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors do not audit non-GAAP metrics but review them for consistency with GAAP figures and internal consistency. The primary risk is that AISC is used to misrepresent the mine's cost profile — overstating production, understating royalties, or excluding sustaining capex from the AISC calculation. Confirm the AISC-to-GAAP reconciliation is disclosed with equal or greater prominence than the non-GAAP metric itself. Test that the components are sourced consistently from the same cost data used in GAAP reporting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: AISC calculation workings (by component), GAAP-to-AISC reconciliation, World Gold Council AISC guidance compliance, production (ounces) used as the denominator, sustaining capex schedule (AISC component), royalty amount included in AISC, non-GAAP disclosure policy and prominence guidelines, and audit committee approval of AISC disclosure.

title: EOSB Provision — Mining Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing end-of-service benefits for Jordanian mining company employees.
explanation: Jordanian mining companies (Jordan Phosphate Mines Company — JPMC, Arab Potash Company — APC, and other extractive operators) must accrue EOSB for all Jordanian staff under the Labour Law. For mining operations with large blue-collar workforces (miners, operators, maintenance technicians), the aggregate EOSB can be very significant. The EOSB is allocated to production cost centres (mine operations, processing plant) and overhead.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: EOSB is calculated monthly per employee and allocated to the mine's production cost centre (thus included in the cost per tonne calculation) for operational staff, and to overhead for management and administrative staff. For mines with long-service workforce cohorts (many Jordanian mining operations have multi-generational workforces), IAS 19 actuarial valuation is required. The actuarial assumptions for mining workforces include higher accident and disability rates, affecting both the attrition assumption and the liability pattern. EOSB for production staff flows through COGS as part of the mine's operating cost.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test the allocation of EOSB to production cost centres — EOSB for miners and operators should be included in the cost per tonne (affecting inventory valuation and COGS), not just in overhead. Confirm the IAS 19 actuarial assumptions are appropriate for mining workforces (higher injury rates, specific salary escalation patterns in unionised mining environments). Test the provision roll-forward and confirm departures are deducted correctly. Review whether any early retirement programs or voluntary separation schemes create a restructuring obligation under IAS 37.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: EOSB provision schedule per employee and cost centre, IAS 19 actuarial valuation, actuarial assumptions (discount rate, salary growth, attrition, occupational injury rate), EOSB inclusion in cost per tonne calculation, provision roll-forward, early retirement scheme assessment (if applicable), and Jordanian Labour Law compliance.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — Mining Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the 14.25% employer SSC contribution for Jordanian mining company employees.
explanation: SSC applies to all Jordanian employees in mining operations. For blue-collar mining and processing staff, the SSC is calculated on basic wages plus any shift allowances. The SSC is allocated to the same cost centres as the underlying wages — mine operations staff SSC is included in the cost per tonne of ore produced, making it a production cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: SSC for mining operations is calculated on the full gross compensation including shift premiums, hazard allowances, and overtime. For unionised mining workforces, the wage base can be complex. SSC is allocated to production cost centres (mine operations, processing, maintenance) for blue-collar staff and to overhead for management. As a production cost, SSC is part of the cost per tonne and flows into inventory valuation. The SSC ceiling applies per employee — for highly paid mining engineers and managers, the ceiling limits the SSC base.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile SSC expense to payroll multiplied by 14.25% with the ceiling applied. Confirm SSC is allocated to production cost centres for operational staff (affecting COGS and inventory valuation). For expatriate mining engineers and specialists on work permits, confirm the SSC exemption or bilateral agreement treatment. Test timely remittance to the SSC — late payment in mining attracts penalties.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Payroll register including shift allowances and overtime, SSC ceiling application per employee, SSC remittance receipts, production vs. overhead SSC allocation, expatriate SSC treatment documentation, cost per tonne SSC component, and SSC registration for all eligible employees.

title: GST Treatment — Mineral Exports (Jordan)
description: Recording the zero-rated GST treatment of mineral concentrate exports from Jordan.
explanation: Mineral exports from Jordan (phosphate, potash, other minerals) are typically zero-rated for GST purposes — the miner charges 0% GST on exports and can recover input GST paid on domestic purchases (equipment, services). This is distinct from the GST-exempt classification — zero-rating preserves the input tax recovery right. The ISTD's rules on export zero-rating for mineral products must be carefully applied.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The ERP billing system applies the zero-rating GST code to export mineral sales invoices. The export documentation (bill of lading, customs export declaration) must be retained to support the zero-rating claim. Input GST on domestic purchases (local contractor services, local equipment, maintenance parts) is recovered in the monthly GST return — the zero-rated export status makes the mine a fully taxable business for input GST recovery purposes (unlike exempt supplies, which restrict input GST recovery). The net effect is favourable — no output GST on exports, full input GST recovery on domestic inputs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the zero-rating treatment is supported by export documentation. Test that input GST claims on domestic purchases are valid — any purchases used for non-business purposes are not claimable. Confirm the GST return correctly claims input GST. For domestic mineral sales (if any), confirm the correct output GST rate is applied. Review whether the ISTD has audited the zero-rating claims and whether any assessments are outstanding.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Export mineral sales invoices (zero-rated GST), customs export declarations and bills of lading, ISTD GST return filings, input GST recovery on domestic purchases, zero-rating eligibility confirmation, ISTD audit correspondence, and GST registration certificate confirming export business status.

title: Import Duty Exemption — Mining Equipment
description: Recording the import of mining equipment benefiting from a duty exemption under a mining investment agreement.
explanation: Mining companies in Jordan and other MENA jurisdictions often negotiate duty exemptions for imported capital equipment under their mining concession agreements or investment promotion laws. Capital equipment (haul trucks, crushers, processing equipment) exempt from import duty is capitalised at the ex-duty cost. If an exemption is not available, customs duties are capitalised as part of the asset cost.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Duty-exempt equipment imports are processed through customs with the exemption certificate from the Ministry of Investment or Mining Authority. The equipment is capitalised in the ERP at the CIF cost (no customs duty added since it is exempt). The exemption certificate is maintained in the asset file. If duty exemption is partial (some items exempt, others dutiable), the duty on dutiable items is capitalised into those items' cost. Duty exemption availability often depends on the equipment being listed in the mining concession agreement's approved equipment schedule.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the duty exemption is properly authorised by the relevant ministry — each exempt item should have a specific exemption approval. Test that the equipment is used for mining purposes within the concession area (conditions for the exemption typically require this). Confirm that duty-free equipment not ultimately used for the approved purpose may require duty payment — creating a contingent liability. Review whether any exemption certificates have expired or are at risk of cancellation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Duty exemption certificate (per equipment item or batch), mining concession agreement (equipment schedule), customs import declaration showing zero duty, ministry approval letter, equipment description and quantity, usage confirmation (equipment deployed at the mine site), and exemption expiry date monitoring.

title: Capitalization of Renewable Asset Construction (WIP)
description: Recording the costs incurred during the construction phase of a renewable energy asset, such as a solar farm or wind turbine.
explanation: During the construction of renewable energy facilities, direct costs (materials, EPC contractor invoices, direct labor) are capitalized into a Construction in Progress (CIP) account. These assets are not depreciated until they reach Commercial Operation Date (COD) and are ready for their intended use.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tracked via project accounting modules within the ERP. Each solar farm or wind project should have a distinct WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) code to track budget vs. actuals.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will vouch additions to CIP to EPC contracts and invoices. They will heavily scrutinize the capitalization of internal costs (e.g., project management time) to ensure they directly relate to bringing the asset to its location and condition.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: EPC contracts, contractor progress billings, timesheets for capitalized internal labor, and capitalization policy.

title: Capitalization of Borrowing Costs (Green Project Finance)
description: Capitalizing interest expense incurred on project financing during the active construction phase of a qualifying renewable asset.
explanation: Under IAS 23 and ASC 835-20, borrowing costs directly attributable to the acquisition or construction of a qualifying asset (like a wind farm taking substantial time to build) must be capitalized as part of the asset's cost, rather than expensed.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Usually calculated outside the core ERP in a spreadsheet or a specialized debt management system, then entered via manual journal. Capitalization must cease when the asset reaches COD.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will recalculate the capitalization rate (weighted average cost of borrowings or specific project debt rate) and ensure interest capitalization stopped exactly at the Commercial Operation Date.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Project finance agreements, interest calculations, COD certificates, and proof of active construction during the period.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) - Decommissioning
description: Initial recognition of the liability to dismantle and remove renewable energy equipment (e.g., wind turbines, solar panels) and restore the land at the end of the lease/useful life.
explanation: Renewable energy operators are typically legally bound to decommission assets and restore the site. The present value of these estimated future costs is capitalized as part of the asset's cost and simultaneously recognized as a liability (IAS 37 / ASC 410-20).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The ARO asset is depreciated over the useful life of the plant, while the ARO liability is accreted (increased) over time through an interest/accretion charge until it matches the estimated future cash outflow.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Significant estimates are involved: inflation rates, discount rates, and estimated future contractor costs. Auditors usually require a third-party decommissioning study to validate the cost estimates.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Land lease agreements (restoration clauses), third-party engineering decommissioning cost estimates, discount rate calculations, and management's memo on assumptions.

title: ARO Accretion Expense
description: Recording the periodic accretion expense (interest) to increase the ARO liability to its future expected payout amount.
explanation: Because the ARO was recorded at present value, the liability must grow over time until the decommissioning date. This growth is recorded as accretion expense, representing the time value of money.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up as a recurring automated journal entry in the ERP based on the ARO amortization schedule established at asset inception.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Verification that the accretion rate matches the discount rate used at initial recognition, and that accretion is calculated correctly over the period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: ARO roll-forward schedule, original discount rate documentation.

title: Reclassification from CIP to Operating Assets (COD)
description: Moving the accumulated costs of a renewable asset from Construction in Progress to active Property, Plant & Equipment upon reaching Commercial Operation Date.
explanation: Once the asset is fully constructed, connected to the grid, and commercially viable, it is transferred out of WIP. Depreciation commences on this date.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Triggers the capitalization of the final asset in the Fixed Asset Register (FAR). Depreciation parameters (useful life, salvage value) are finalized and activated.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will closely review the COD certificate from the utility/grid operator to ensure depreciation started in the correct accounting period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Interconnection agreement, COD certificate from independent engineer, final commissioning tests.

title: Virtual Power Purchase Agreement (VPPA) - Unrealized Gain
description: Recording the mark-to-market (MtM) unrealized gain on a VPPA derivative contract when forward market electricity prices rise above the contract's fixed strike price.

entries.account: Unrealized Gain on Derivatives (P&L or OCI)

explanation: A VPPA is typically a financial Contract for Differences (CfD). Since no physical power is delivered, it fails the 'own-use' exemption and must be accounted for as a derivative at fair value (IFRS 9 / ASC 815). If market prices rise, the contract is "in the money" for the off-taker.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: VPPA derivatives are complex and usually tracked in specialized Treasury Management Systems (TMS) or external valuation reports, with summary entries booked to the ERP at month/quarter-end.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Valuation relies on highly subjective forward price curves for electricity nodes. Auditors will engage their internal valuation specialists to independently model the VPPA fair value.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: VPPA contract, forward electricity pricing curves (e.g., from PJM, ERCOT), third-party valuation reports, and hedge accounting documentation (if applying cash flow hedge accounting).

title: VPPA Monthly Settlement - Cash Received
description: Recording the monthly financial settlement of a VPPA when the floating market price exceeds the fixed strike price, resulting in a payment from the project to the corporate buyer.
explanation: In a CfD, if the variable market electricity price > fixed contract price, the renewable energy project pays the corporate buyer the difference. This acts as a financial hedge against the buyer's physical electricity costs.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Recorded in the cash receipts module. If hedge accounting is applied, the realized gain may be mapped to offset utility expenses rather than showing as separate revenue.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Reconciliation of the settlement statement from the project developer to the cash received and the original VPPA pricing terms.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Monthly settlement statement from the developer, bank statement, VPPA contract.

title: Renewable Energy Certificates (REC) - Generation
description: Recognizing RECs generated by a renewable energy facility upon producing megawatt-hours (MWh) of clean electricity.
explanation: When a facility generates power, it is issued RECs (e.g., 1 REC per 1 MWh). These are typically recognized as inventory (held for sale) or intangible assets (held for compliance) at fair value, representing an additional revenue stream separate from physical electricity sales.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: RECs are tracked in a specialized registry (e.g., WREGIS, PJM-GATS). The ERP inventory module must track REC vintages (year of generation) as prices vary by vintage.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will reconcile the MWh produced via meter data to the RECs minted in the registry, and test the fair value applied at initial recognition.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Generation meter data, registry minting reports, market pricing data for RECs.

title: Sale of Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs)
description: Recording the sale of generated RECs to a third party (e.g., a utility needing to meet Renewable Portfolio Standards).
explanation: When RECs are sold independently of electricity (unbundled), revenue is recognized upon the transfer of control (usually when RECs are transferred in the tracking registry). The carrying value of the RECs is expensed as COGS.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Requires a standard sales order and fulfillment process in the ERP. The 'shipment' is the transfer of the digital certificate in the registry.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Verification of the transfer of control via the tracking registry (proof of delivery). Testing of the profit margin to ensure inventory was appropriately costed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: REC purchase and sale agreement (REC PSA), registry transfer confirmation, sales invoice.

title: Purchase of Voluntary Carbon Offsets (VERs)
description: A corporation purchasing Verified Emission Reductions (VERs) to offset its Scope 1, 2, or 3 emissions to meet voluntary Net Zero targets.

entries.account: Prepaid Expenses / Intangible Assets - Carbon Credits

explanation: Voluntary carbon credits purchased to offset future emissions are generally capitalized as an intangible asset or prepaid expense until they are officially retired/surrendered against the company's emissions.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tracked as an intangible asset. The company must monitor the vintage and project type, as offset quality is increasingly scrutinized.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Confirmation of existence through a recognized registry (e.g., Verra, Gold Standard). Assessment of impairment if the market value of the offsets drops significantly.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Purchase agreement, registry holding statement, proof of payment.

title: Retirement of Carbon Offsets
description: Recognizing the expense when carbon offsets are officially retired in a registry to claim the environmental benefit (neutralize emissions).

entries.account: Prepaid Expenses / Intangible Assets - Carbon Credits

explanation: A carbon credit has no further value once retired; the environmental benefit has been consumed. The capitalized cost is moved to the income statement as an operating expense.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: A manual journal entry triggered by the operational ESG team providing proof of retirement. Often recognized at year-end alongside the final GHG footprint calculation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will verify the retirement certificate to ensure the company has the right to make 'carbon neutral' claims and prevent greenwashing.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Retirement certificate from Verra/Gold Standard showing the specific serial numbers permanently retired on behalf of the entity.

title: Issuance of a Green Bond
description: Issuing a green bond where the proceeds are strictly ring-fenced for financing eligible environmental projects.
explanation: Accounted for as standard debt under IFRS 9 / ASC 470, but proceeds are typically held in a restricted cash account. They can only be drawn down to fund projects that meet the criteria defined in the company's Green Bond Framework.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The bank account receiving the funds must be tagged as restricted. Treasury modules will track the amortization of bond issuance costs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Strict auditing of the 'Use of Proceeds'. If funds are used for non-green purposes, the company breaches the bond covenant, which could trigger immediate repayment and severe reputational damage.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bond indenture, Green Bond Framework, Second Party Opinion (SPO) from an ESG rating agency, bank statements.

title: Green Bond - Deployment of Proceeds
description: Transferring funds from the restricted green bond account to the general operating account to reimburse the company for eligible green project expenditures.
explanation: As the company incurs CAPEX on eligible green projects (e.g., building a solar farm), it draws down the restricted cash to replenish its general funds.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Requires a strict mapping between the CAPEX WBS codes and the restricted cash drawdown to ensure perfect traceability for annual Green Bond reporting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Vouching the transfer to the specific invoices of the eligible green project. Auditors verify that the project aligns with the pre-approved Green Bond Framework.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Internal funds transfer requests, eligible project invoices, management attestation of eligibility.

title: Sustainability-Linked Loan (SLL) - Interest Rate Penalty
description: Recording increased interest expense due to a margin step-up on an SLL because the company failed to meet its predetermined ESG KPIs (e.g., diversity targets or GHG reduction).
explanation: In an SLL, the interest rate fluctuates based on the borrower's ESG performance. If a KPI is missed, a penalty (margin step-up) is applied. This is treated as an adjustment to the effective interest rate of the loan.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The loan master data in the Treasury module must be updated with the new interest rate effective from the KPI testing date.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will review the annual ESG KPI audit report (usually verified by a third party) and ensure the interest rate calculation correctly incorporates the step-up/step-down provisions of the credit agreement.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Credit agreement, annual ESG performance report, third-party assurance statement on KPIs, bank interest statement.

title: Receipt of Government Grant for Green Tech
description: Receiving a cash grant from the government (e.g., Inflation Reduction Act incentives) to fund the purchase of renewable energy equipment.
explanation: Under IAS 20, grants related to assets are initially recognized as deferred income (or deducted from the asset's carrying amount). They are not recognized as immediate income to ensure they are matched against the depreciation of the asset they funded.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up a deferred revenue schedule in the ERP that mirrors the depreciation schedule of the associated green asset.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Ensuring the company has met all the conditions attached to the grant (e.g., domestic content requirements, prevailing wage requirements). If conditions aren't met, the grant may be repayable.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Grant award letter, proof of cash receipt, documentation proving compliance with grant stipulations.

title: Amortization of Government Grant
description: Recognizing a portion of the deferred green tech government grant as income over the useful life of the funded asset.
explanation: The deferred grant is systematically recognized in profit or loss over the periods in which the entity recognizes as expenses the related costs (depreciation) which the grants are intended to compensate.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Automated monthly entry tied to the Fixed Asset depreciation run. If the asset is impaired or disposed of, the remaining deferred grant is immediately recognized.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Matching the amortization rate of the grant to the depreciation rate of the underlying asset.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Amortization schedule, Fixed Asset Register (FAR).

title: Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) - Grid Arbitrage Revenue
description: Recording revenue generated by a battery storage system discharging (selling) electricity back to the grid during peak pricing hours.
explanation: BESS facilities buy power when it is cheap and sell it when it is expensive. The sale of discharged power is recognized as revenue under IFRS 15 based on the MWh delivered to the grid.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Revenue is usually recorded net of 'charging costs' (Cost of Sales) in management reporting, though gross presentation is required for statutory accounts.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Reconciliation of the Independent System Operator (ISO) settlement statements to the physical meter data (discharge MWh).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: ISO settlement statements, meter data, nodal pricing historical data.

title: BESS - Grid Charging Cost
description: Recording the cost of electricity purchased from the grid to charge a Battery Energy Storage System during off-peak hours.

entries.account: Cost of Goods Sold - Grid Electricity

explanation: The cost to charge the battery is effectively the inventory cost of the power. However, because power cannot be easily stored long-term and is constantly cycled, it is typically expensed directly to COGS as the battery charges and discharges in the same month.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Depending on the grid operator, charging costs and discharging revenues might be settled net. However, accounting standards usually require gross presentation in the GL.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Verification of negative locational marginal pricing (LMP). In some cases, the battery is paid to charge (negative pricing), which is treated as revenue rather than a cost reduction.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Utility invoices, meter charging data.

title: Impairment of Fossil Fuel Asset (Stranded Asset)
description: Recognizing an impairment loss on a legacy coal or gas plant due to new environmental regulations or carbon taxes that make the asset economically unviable.
explanation: Under IAS 36 / ASC 360, if the carrying amount of an asset exceeds its recoverable amount (due to a transition risk like carbon pricing), the asset is impaired. ESG factors are a major trigger for impairment testing in heavy industries.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Reduces the net book value of the asset in the Fixed Asset Register, leading to lower depreciation charges in future periods.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will intensely scrutinize the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model used to determine the Value in Use. They will check if future carbon tax assumptions and early decommissioning dates align with the company's stated Net Zero targets.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Impairment memo, DCF valuation model, future carbon pricing curves, management's strategic transition plan.

title: Provision for Environmental Clean-up
description: Accruing a liability for the legal or constructive obligation to remediate environmental contamination (e.g., a chemical spill or legacy soil pollution).
explanation: If a company has contaminated land and has a present obligation to clean it up (due to laws or published company policy), it must recognize a provision for the best estimate of the remediation costs (IAS 37 / ASC 410-30).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tracked as a long-term liability. When actual clean-up costs are incurred, they are debited against this provision, not expensed again.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Assessment of whether the obligation is probable and measurable. Auditors will rely heavily on legal counsel letters and environmental engineering estimates.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Environmental impact assessment, legal correspondence, engineering cost estimates, regulatory notices.

title: Accrual of Carbon Emission Liability (Cap-and-Trade)
description: Accruing a liability for greenhouse gas emissions generated during the period under a compliance Cap-and-Trade system (e.g., EU ETS).
explanation: As a facility emits CO2, it incurs an obligation to surrender carbon allowances at the end of the compliance period. The liability is typically accrued monthly based on actual emissions at the current market price of allowances.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: A monthly manual accrual. At year-end, the liability is settled by surrendering government-granted allowances or purchased allowances.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Reconciliation of the accrued liability to the actual emissions reported by the Continuous Emissions Monitoring System (CEMS) and verified by a third-party GHG auditor.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Monthly emissions reports, market pricing data for EUAs (or local equivalent), third-party emission verification report.

title: ESG & Sustainability Reporting Costs
description: Recording fees paid to consultants and auditors for preparing and assuring the annual ESG / Sustainability Report.
explanation: Costs related to compiling carbon footprints, drafting sustainability reports (e.g., CSRD, SASB, TCFD), and obtaining third-party limited assurance are treated as period operating expenses (OPEX).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Coded to the Sustainability or Corporate Affairs cost center. Cannot be capitalized as they do not generate future economic benefits.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Standard expense vouching. Ensure proper cut-off so expenses are recorded in the year the service was provided.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Consulting contracts, assurance engagement letters, invoices.

title: Purchase of Biomass Fuel Inventory
description: Purchasing organic matter (wood pellets, agricultural waste) to be combusted for renewable energy generation.
explanation: Unlike wind or solar, biomass requires physical fuel. The purchased biomass is recorded as raw material inventory and expensed to COGS as it is consumed in the boiler.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Requires robust inventory management (weighbridges, moisture content adjustments). Biomass quality degrades over time, requiring potential inventory write-downs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will observe the physical inventory count of stockpiles. Because measuring piles of woodchips is imprecise, they rely on aerial drone surveys or geometric volume calculations.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Weighbridge tickets, supplier invoices, inventory count sheets, moisture lab reports.

title: Solar Equipment Lease setup (Lessee - IFRS 16)
description: Initial recognition of a Right-of-Use (ROU) asset and lease liability for a corporate building installing leased rooftop solar panels.
explanation: Under IFRS 16 / ASC 842, long-term leases of solar equipment (where the lessee consumes the power and controls the asset) must be capitalized on the balance sheet at the present value of future lease payments.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Managed within the ERP Lease Accounting module, which handles the PV calculation and generates monthly depreciation and interest amortization entries.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will check the determination of the incremental borrowing rate used to discount the liability and verify that non-lease components (e.g., maintenance) are appropriately separated if elected.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Solar lease agreement, incremental borrowing rate memo, lease amortization schedule.

title: Community Investment Grant (The 'S' in ESG)
description: Recording a cash donation or grant made to a local community affected by the company's operations, as part of the company's social responsibility initiatives.
explanation: Voluntary contributions to community development, indigenous relations, or local education are recognized immediately as an expense. This supports the social pillar of the company's ESG profile.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tracked in a specific GL account for CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility) to facilitate easy aggregation for the annual Sustainability Report.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Compliance checks against the company's Anti-Bribery and Corruption (ABC) policies (e.g., FCPA, UK Bribery Act) to ensure donations are legitimate and not disguised political contributions.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Grant application from the NGO/community, internal approval matrix, proof of payment, anti-corruption clearance.

title: Tax Equity Partnership - Initial Funding
description: Recording the initial cash contribution from a tax equity investor (e.g., a bank) into a renewable energy project partnership (YieldCo or flip structure).

entries.account: Non-Controlling Interest - Tax Equity / Tax Equity Liability

explanation: In US renewable finance, tax equity investors provide capital in exchange for the majority of early tax benefits (ITCs/PTCs and MACRS depreciation). Depending on the structure (partnership flip vs. sale-leaseback), this is recorded either as a liability or as Non-Controlling Interest (NCI) within equity.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Requires complex consolidation setups in the ERP. Project SPVs (Special Purpose Vehicles) are tracked separately, and the tax equity slice is eliminated or broken out during consolidation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will review the LLC Operating Agreement to determine if the contribution should be classed as debt (liability) or equity under ASC 810 / ASC 480 based on redemption rights and liquidation preferences.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LLC Operating Agreement, Equity Capital Contribution Notice, Bank Statements.

title: Tax Equity - HLBV Income/Loss Allocation
description: Allocating project income or loss to the tax equity investor using the Hypothetical Liquidation at Book Value (HLBV) method.
explanation: Because tax equity partnerships do not allocate economics based on strict ownership percentages (they 'flip' over time), standard equity accounting fails. HLBV calculates how much the investor would receive if the partnership liquidated at book value at the end of the period minus the beginning of the period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: HLBV calculations are notoriously complex and run in dedicated financial models outside the ERP. The resulting delta is booked via manual journal entry at period-end.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Heavy audit scrutiny on the HLBV model. Auditors recalculate the hypothetical liquidation waterfall based on the exact distribution tiers defined in the LLC agreement.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: HLBV tracking model, period-end project balance sheet, partnership agreement waterfall provisions.

title: Investment Tax Credit (ITC) - Initial Recognition (Deferral Method)
description: Recognizing the federal Investment Tax Credit (ITC) upon a solar project reaching Commercial Operation Date (COD).
explanation: Under the deferral method (ASC 740), the ITC is not recognized immediately in the income statement. It directly reduces taxes payable (or creates a refund) but establishes a deferred liability that is amortized to income over the useful life of the solar asset.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The Deferred ITC is mapped to amortize automatically on the exact same schedule (usually 30-35 years) as the underlying Fixed Asset.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Verification of the 'Eligible Basis'—auditors heavily test which construction costs qualify for the ITC percentage. Inclusion of non-eligible costs (e.g., transmission lines outside the project) can trigger IRS recapture.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Cost segregation study (to determine eligible basis), placed-in-service documentation, tax returns.

title: Production Tax Credit (PTC) - Generation
description: Recognizing Production Tax Credits earned based on the actual MWh of electricity generated by a wind farm.
explanation: Unlike the ITC which is upfront, PTCs are earned over the first 10 years of a wind project's life based on metered generation. The credits flow directly to the P&L in the period the electricity is generated, typically reducing the income tax provision.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Calculated monthly based on the generation meter. Ensure the ERP tracks the 10-year expiration window for PTC eligibility per site.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Reconciliation of the PTC calculation to the physical generation meter data and grid settlement statements.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Generation meter data, independent engineering reports confirming PTC eligibility, tax provision workpapers.

title: Capitalization of Green Hydrogen Electrolyzer
description: Recording the final installation and commissioning costs of an industrial electrolyzer used to split water into green hydrogen using renewable power.
