explanation: Standard fixed asset capitalization, but critical for establishing the basis for green energy tax credits (like the 45V credit in the US). Includes equipment, specialized safety valves, and high-pressure storage tanks.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The electrolyzer plant usually requires separate componentization in the Fixed Asset Register. The stack (which degrades over 5-7 years) must be depreciated separately from the balance of plant (20+ years).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Reviewing the componentization schedule to ensure the highly degradable electrolyzer cells are not amortized over too long a period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: OEM equipment invoices, commissioning reports, componentization memo.

title: Green Hydrogen - Inventory Cost Allocation
description: Allocating the direct costs (renewable power, purified water) and overhead (electrolyzer depreciation) into the cost of green hydrogen inventory.
explanation: Green hydrogen is an inventoried manufactured good. Under standard absorption costing (IAS 2 / ASC 330), direct materials (water), direct energy (power), and a portion of fixed manufacturing overhead (depreciation of the facility) must be capitalized into the inventory cost until sold.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tracked in the ERP manufacturing/process costing module. Requires a standard routing that consumes MWh of electricity and liters of water per kg of hydrogen produced.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Testing the overhead allocation rate. Auditors ensure that power used for non-production purposes (facility lighting) is expensed, while direct production power is capitalized to inventory.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bill of Materials (BOM) for hydrogen, meter readings for power/water, standard costing models.

title: Carbon Capture (CCUS) - 45Q Tax Credit Accrual
description: Recognizing tax credits generated by permanently capturing and geologically sequestering industrial CO2 emissions.
explanation: In the US, Section 45Q provides a per-ton tax credit for carbon oxide captured and safely stored. This acts similarly to a PTC and is recorded as the metric tons are injected into Class VI wells.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Accrual based on the telemetry data from the injection well flow meters. Subject to strict EPA MRV (Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification) plans.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: High risk of recapture if CO2 leaks. Auditors verify the volumes injected against EPA submittals and check for any recorded surface leakage events.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: EPA MRV reporting, injection well flow meter logs, lifecycle GHG analysis.

title: EV Fleet - Accelerated Battery Impairment/Depreciation
description: Recording an accelerated depreciation charge or impairment on a commercial EV fleet due to faster-than-expected battery degradation (e.g., heavy fast-charging use).
explanation: EV batteries degrade based on charging cycles and thermal stress, not just time. If telematics show a fleet's battery health (State of Health - SoH) has degraded below the expected useful life curve, an adjustment to accumulated depreciation is required.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: If fleet batteries are componentized from the chassis, this entry adjusts the battery component. ERP fixed asset modules may require a change in useful life parameters going forward.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will pull telematics reports (e.g., Geotab) to verify the SoH of the batteries and validate management's assumptions on remaining useful life.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fleet telematics reports showing battery SoH, revised depreciation schedule, management memo on asset life.

title: Physical PPA - Power Delivery (NPNS Exception)
description: Recording the sale of electricity under a long-term physical Power Purchase Agreement that qualifies for the Normal Purchase Normal Sale (NPNS) scope exception.
explanation: While long-term fixed-price contracts usually meet the definition of a derivative, physical power delivery contracts that the entity fully intends to fulfill (deliver physical electrons) qualify for the NPNS exception. Therefore, they avoid complex mark-to-market derivative accounting and are treated as normal revenue under IFRS 15 / ASC 606 upon delivery.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Standard Accounts Receivable billing process triggered by monthly meter reads.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Verification that the contract actually qualifies for NPNS (e.g., no net settlement clauses, entity has the physical capacity to deliver). If it fails NPNS, the entire contract must be marked-to-market.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Physical PPA contract, NPNS election memo, meter delivery data.

title: Firming and Shaping Costs (Renewable Intermittency)
description: Paying a fee to a trading desk to 'firm and shape' intermittent solar/wind power into a continuous, flat baseload block of power for a 24/7 corporate facility.
explanation: Because a corporate buyer needs power at night when solar isn't producing, they pay a proxy trading firm to absorb the intermittent renewable energy and supply steady grid power. The cost of this service is expensed as an energy procurement overhead.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Recorded as part of the overall utility/energy cost center. Delineated from the actual raw energy cost for management reporting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Vouching to the physical sleeving or firming agreement with the energy trader.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Firming/Sleeving agreement, monthly trader settlement statements.

title: Mark-to-Market of Embedded Derivative in a PPA
description: Recording the unrealized loss on an embedded derivative separated from a host power contract (e.g., a PPA with pricing tied to an unrelated commodity index like natural gas).
explanation: If a contract contains pricing features not clearly and closely related to the host contract (like electricity priced off a gas index), the embedded feature must be bifurcated (separated) and accounted for as a standalone derivative at fair value.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Bifurcated derivatives are tracked via specialized Treasury/Risk management systems. The entry is booked back to the ERP at month-end.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors rigorously review all long-term supply contracts for embedded derivatives. Valuation models for these are heavily scrutinized by internal quant teams.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: PPA contract, bifurcation analysis memo, complex valuation model (e.g., Monte Carlo simulation).

title: Afforestation (IAS 41) - Fair Value Gain on Timber
description: Recognizing the annual biological growth of a forest planted explicitly for carbon sequestration and eventual sustainable harvest.

entries.account: Fair Value Gain on Biological Assets (P&L)

explanation: Under IAS 41 (Agriculture), living plants (biological assets) are measured at fair value less costs to sell. As trees grow over the year, their increased mass and market value are recognized immediately in the P&L as an unrealized gain.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Tracked in specialized forestry modules or external spreadsheets. The asset is non-current but distinct from standard PPE.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Highly specialized audit area. Relies on third-party 'cruising' reports (sample tree measurements by foresters) and complex discounted cash flow models based on future timber prices.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Forestry cruise reports, DCF valuation model, timber market pricing curves.

title: Capitalization of Silviculture Costs
description: Capitalizing the costs of maintaining a newly planted forest (e.g., weed control, fertilization, thinning) prior to maturity.
explanation: Direct costs incurred to grow and maintain biological assets before they reach maturity or are harvested are capitalized into the carrying amount of the biological asset.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Similar to a WIP process. Costs are collected in a cost center and settled to the Biological Asset account at period end.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Distinguishing between capitalization (costs that enhance growth) and standard maintenance expense (costs that just maintain current state, though forestry often capitalizes most early-stage costs).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Contractor invoices for spraying/thinning, forestry management plan.

title: Waste-to-Energy - Tipping Fee Revenue
description: Recognizing revenue for accepting municipal solid waste at a biomass/incinerator facility, prior to generating power from it.
explanation: Waste-to-Energy (WtE) plants have two distinct revenue streams: selling the generated electricity and charging municipalities a 'tipping fee' to accept their garbage. This records the service of waste disposal.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Triggered by the weighbridge integration with the ERP. As a garbage truck is weighed in and out, the net tonnage generates a sales invoice.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Reconciliation of the weighbridge software logs to the invoiced tonnage. Checking for proper cutoff at month-end.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Weighbridge tickets, municipal waste contracts, sales register.

title: Circular Economy - Solar Panel Scrap Revenue
description: Recording revenue from selling end-of-life decommissioned solar panels to a specialized recycling firm that recovers silver, copper, and silicon.
explanation: As part of circular economy initiatives, renewable components are recycled rather than landfilled. The sale of these materials generates scrap revenue, separate from the core energy business.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Recorded as miscellaneous or 'Other Income'. The actual decommissioning of the panels would separately trigger the utilization of the ARO (Asset Retirement Obligation) liability.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Ensuring the scrapped assets were properly written off the Fixed Asset Register. Verifying the physical transfer of the hazardous e-waste to licensed facilities.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Scrap sale invoice, bill of lading, certificate of destruction/recycling from the vendor.

title: Joint Venture - Equity Method Investment (Wind/Solar)
description: Recognizing the initial investment and the subsequent proportional share of net income from a jointly controlled renewable energy project.

entries.account: Equity in Earnings of Joint Venture

explanation: Because renewable projects are incredibly capital intensive, developers often form Joint Ventures (JVs) to share risk. Under IFRS 11 / ASC 323, if an entity has joint control over the net assets, it must use the Equity Method. The investment is recorded at cost on the balance sheet and increases/decreases based on the investor's share of the JV's bottom-line net income.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The JV operates its own independent ERP and ledger. At month-end, the parent company books a manual top-level journal entry to record its percentage share of the JV's final net income.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will meticulously review the JV operating agreement. If the parent company actually has operational control (e.g., they make all the key decisions despite owning only 50%), they must fully consolidate the asset rather than using the one-line equity method.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: JV Agreement, JV audited financial statements, memo on control and consolidation.

title: Feed-in Tariff (FiT) Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue from selling renewable electricity to the grid at a government-mandated, above-market fixed premium rate.
explanation: Feed-in Tariffs (FiTs) are policy mechanisms designed to accelerate green investment by offering long-term contracts (15-20 years) to renewable producers. The price paid is usually higher than the standard wholesale market price. Revenue is recognized under IFRS 15 as MWh are delivered.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Standard Accounts Receivable process. However, some utilities break the payment into two lines: the base market price and the FiT premium subsidy. Both generally roll up into total revenue.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Verification of the specific FiT tier the asset qualified for (older assets usually have much higher FiT rates). Auditors will check the expiration date of the FiT contract, as a drop-off to standard market rates can trigger an impairment test.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: FiT contract/award letter from the government or utility, monthly delivery meter data.

title: Microgrid - Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Energy Trading Revenue
description: Recording the sale of excess solar energy from one corporate facility directly to an adjacent facility operating on the same localized, decentralized microgrid.
explanation: In decentralized microgrids, localized entities can trade power behind the main utility meter. When Facility A's solar panels overproduce, they sell the electrons directly to Facility B. Revenue is recognized based on the transfer of power measured by local smart meters, bypassing the traditional utility.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: This is a rapidly evolving area. Billing is often managed outside the ERP via distributed ledgers (blockchain) or IoT smart contract platforms, which then push summarized monthly billing data into the ERP via API.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will focus heavily on IT General Controls (ITGC) for the smart meters and the blockchain/IoT platform calculating the trades to ensure billing accuracy and prevent tampering.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Microgrid participation agreement, smart meter logs, distributed ledger transaction reports.

title: Capital Call - LP Funding Received
description: Recording the receipt of called capital from Limited Partners (LPs) in response to a capital call notice issued by the General Partner (GP) to fund a new portfolio investment or fund expenses.
explanation: When the GP issues a capital call notice, a receivable is established against each LP's unfunded commitment. Upon receipt of wire transfers from LPs, the receivable is extinguished and cash is recognized. The capital is recorded as a contribution to partners' capital, not revenue.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Most fund accounting platforms (Allvue, Investran, Geneva) maintain a capital account ledger per LP. The call amount is allocated pro-rata to each LP's capital account based on their committed percentage. Ensure the call notice date and funding due date are tracked separately for default interest calculations.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will reconcile LP capital account statements to the subscription agreements and the LP register. Unfunded commitment schedules will be tested for accuracy against the LPA (Limited Partnership Agreement).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Capital call notice, LP subscription agreements, wire transfer confirmations, LP commitment schedule showing funded vs. unfunded commitments.

title: Capital Call - Deployment into Portfolio Investment
description: Recording the deployment of called capital into a new or follow-on portfolio company investment (equity purchase or loan), funded directly from LP capital call proceeds.

entries.account: Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Company A

explanation: The total invested cost is allocated between the investment itself and any directly attributable transaction costs (legal, due diligence, advisory fees) that are capitalized as part of the cost basis under ASC 946. Expensing vs. capitalizing deal costs depends on the fund's accounting policy and whether it qualifies as an Investment Company under ASC 946.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Each portfolio investment should be set up as a distinct position/security in the fund's portfolio accounting system with its own cost lot. Track the legal entity structure (e.g., direct equity, blocker corp, debt instrument) as it drives subsequent fair value and income treatment.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will verify the investment purchase price against executed transaction documents (SPA, closing statement). Deal cost capitalization policy will be scrutinized and must be consistent with prior periods.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Executed Stock Purchase Agreement (SPA) or subscription agreement, closing statement, wire transfer confirmation, board approval minutes, deal cost invoices.

title: Management Fee - Accrual and GP Offset Against Capital Call
description: Accruing the periodic management fee owed to the GP and applying the standard 80% (or LPA-specified percentage) monitoring/transaction fee offset to reduce the management fee charged to LPs.
explanation: The gross management fee (typically 1.5%–2.0% of committed or invested capital p.a.) is reduced by the LPA-mandated offset percentage of any monitoring fees, transaction fees, or board fees received by the GP from portfolio companies. The net amount is what LPs effectively bear. This offset is a critical LP protection mechanism.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Fund accounting systems should track gross management fee, offset income received by the GP, and the net fee charged to the fund as separate line items for LP reporting transparency. The fee basis (committed vs. invested capital) typically shifts at the end of the investment period per the LPA.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will recalculate the management fee from scratch using the LPA fee provisions, verify the offset amounts against GP-provided schedules of portfolio company fees received, and confirm the effective net fee rate to LPs.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LPA fee provisions, management fee calculation schedule, GP certificate of portfolio company fees received (monitoring, transaction, board fees), LP capital account statements.

title: Portfolio Investment - Fair Value Mark-to-Market (Unrealized Gain)
description: Recording the quarterly or annual fair value adjustment on a private equity portfolio company investment under ASC 820, resulting in an unrealized gain flowing through the fund's income statement.

entries.account: Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Company B
Net Change in Unrealized Appreciation - Investments

explanation: Under ASC 946 (Investment Companies) and ASC 820, private equity funds carry portfolio investments at fair value with all unrealized gains and losses flowing through the income statement each period. Fair value is typically determined using a combination of market multiples (EV/EBITDA), recent transaction comparables, discounted cash flow (DCF), or the price of a recent round of financing.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The fair value adjustment is recorded as a mark in the portfolio accounting system. The cost basis does not change — only the fair value layer is updated. NAV per unit/share calculations must reflect the updated fair value for LP reporting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Fair value estimates for illiquid private investments are one of the highest-risk audit areas. Auditors (often using a valuation specialist) will independently validate the valuation methodology, comparable company multiples, financial projections, and DLOM (Discount for Lack of Marketability) applied. Expect significant back-and-forth on Level 3 inputs.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Valuation committee memo and sign-off, Board-approved valuation policy, comparable company analysis, portfolio company financial statements (trailing LTM), DCF model, third-party valuation report (if obtained).

title: Portfolio Investment - Fair Value Mark-to-Market (Unrealized Loss / Write-Down)
description: Recording a downward fair value adjustment on a portfolio investment reflecting deteriorating company performance, market multiple compression, or credit distress.

entries.account: Net Change in Unrealized Depreciation - Investments
Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Company C

explanation: When a portfolio company's performance deteriorates (revenue decline, covenant breach, market multiple compression), the fund must write down the carrying fair value. For investment companies under ASC 946, this flows directly through the Statement of Operations as a realized or unrealized loss — there is no separate OCI treatment. A full write-down to zero represents a realized loss upon disposition.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Systems must distinguish between 'unrealized depreciation' (still held) and 'realized loss' (disposed/written off). The NAV impact must be allocated to LP capital accounts, reducing each LP's ending capital balance proportionally.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Management's impairment triggers and the timeliness of write-downs are heavily scrutinized. Auditors look for evidence that GPs are not delaying write-downs on underperforming assets to inflate NAV and management fees. Portfolio company covenant compliance certificates and lender communications are key evidence.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Updated valuation model, portfolio company management accounts, lender/creditor correspondence, valuation committee minutes documenting the write-down rationale and approval.

title: GP/LP Distribution - Return of Capital (Pre-Carried Interest)
description: Recording a distribution to LPs representing a return of invested capital from a portfolio company exit or recapitalization proceeds, before any carried interest or performance fee threshold has been met.
explanation: Pre-hurdle distributions are purely a return of contributed capital and reduce each LP's capital account balance. No income or gain is recognized until the LP has received back all contributed capital (and preferred return, if applicable per the LPA). The distribution waterfall sequence must be checked before processing: (1) Return of Capital → (2) Preferred Return → (3) GP Catch-Up → (4) Carried Interest split.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Fund accounting systems must run the full waterfall calculation engine before posting any distribution. Each LP's capital account is debited by their pro-rata share. Generate LP distribution notices showing cumulative contributions, prior distributions, and closing capital account balance.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will recompute the waterfall calculation from scratch against the LPA provisions. They verify that no carried interest was paid before the preferred return hurdle was cleared (clawback risk).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Exit proceeds wire confirmation, distribution waterfall calculation workbook, LPA waterfall provisions, LP distribution notices, updated LP capital account statements.

title: GP/LP Distribution - Carried Interest (Performance Fee) via Waterfall
description: Recording the GP's carried interest distribution after LPs have received their full capital return and preferred return (hurdle rate), representing the GP's 20% (or LPA-specified) profit share on investment gains.

entries.account: GP Capital Account / Carried Interest Payable (20%)

explanation: Once the preferred return hurdle (typically 8% IRR) is satisfied, the GP 'catch-up' provision (if in the LPA) allows the GP to receive 100% of distributions until it has received its full carried interest percentage on all profits. After catch-up, the remaining profit is split 80/20 (LP/GP). The carried interest is a profits interest and is treated as a capital gain for tax purposes in most structures — not ordinary income.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Carried interest distributions to the GP reduce the GP's capital account in the fund's books. If the carried interest is held in a separate carry vehicle (common in institutional fund structures), the payment flows to that entity rather than the GP management company directly.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: The waterfall calculation is the #1 audit focus item in PE fund audits. Auditors will recalculate the entire fund-level IRR (or deal-by-deal, depending on the LPA), verify the hurdle has been cleared, recompute the catch-up, and trace the final split to the LPA.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Full waterfall calculation model (fund-level and/or deal-by-deal), IRR calculation with cash flow schedule, LPA waterfall and carried interest provisions, prior distribution history by LP, GP distribution notice.

title: GP Clawback Obligation - Accrual of Contingent Liability
description: Recognizing a contingent clawback liability for the GP when early carried interest distributions may need to be returned to LPs due to subsequent underperforming investments causing the overall fund IRR to fall below the preferred return hurdle.

entries.account: Clawback Expense / Reduction in Carried Interest Income

explanation: A clawback obligation arises when a GP has received carried interest on early profitable exits, but subsequent losses in the portfolio cause the fund's overall return to fall below the preferred return hurdle on a blended basis. The GP must return the excess carry to LPs. Many LPAs require the GP to hold carried interest in an escrow account to fund potential clawback obligations.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Clawback calculations are typically run at each quarter-end and year-end using the 'as-if liquidation' method — modeling the fund as if all remaining investments were liquidated at current fair value and computing whether the GP would owe money back. This is a complex iterative calculation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will assess the probability and measurement of the clawback liability under ASC 450 (Contingencies). If probable and estimable, it must be accrued. The as-if liquidation model inputs (fair values) are subject to the same scrutiny as the fair value marks.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Clawback calculation model (as-if liquidation basis), prior carried interest distribution history, current portfolio fair value marks, LPA clawback provisions and escrow requirements, legal opinion if amount is disputed.

title: Subscription Credit Facility - Drawdown to Fund Investment
description: Recording a drawdown on the fund's subscription credit facility (a short-term credit line secured by LP unfunded commitments) used to fund a portfolio investment before a formal capital call is issued to LPs.

entries.account: Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Company D

explanation: Subscription lines (or 'capital call lines') allow GPs to fund investments quickly without waiting for LP capital calls (which can take 10 business days). The credit line is secured by LP unfunded commitments. The drawdown is recognized as a liability of the fund. A subsequent capital call is issued to repay the facility, at which point the liability is extinguished and replaced with LP capital contributions.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The credit facility balance must be included in the fund's NAV calculation as a liability. Heavy use of subscription lines can inflate IRR metrics by compressing the time period between LP capital calls and distributions — a practice increasingly scrutinized by LPs and regulators. Track the facility balance, maturity date, and interest rate.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the credit facility is appropriately classified as short-term or long-term and whether the interest expense is properly accrued and allocated to LP capital accounts. LP advisory board consent may be required per the LPA for facilities above certain thresholds.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Credit agreement with the lending bank, borrowing base certificate, facility drawdown notice, collateral schedule (LP unfunded commitments pledged), LPAC consent (if required by LPA).

title: Portfolio Company Exit - Realized Gain Recognition
description: Recording the full disposal of a portfolio company investment (via M&A sale, IPO secondary, or recapitalization), recognizing the realized gain as the difference between total exit proceeds and the investment's cost basis.

entries.account: Net Unrealized Appreciation Reversal - Portfolio Company E
Investments at Cost Basis - Portfolio Company E

explanation: Upon full exit, any previously recorded unrealized appreciation on the position is reversed, the cost basis is removed, and the total economic gain (proceeds minus original cost) is recognized as a realized gain. Under ASC 946, realized and unrealized gains/losses are presented separately in the Statement of Operations. Escrow holdbacks from M&A deals should be recorded as a receivable and released upon satisfaction of conditions.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The gain/loss calculation must be done on a lot-by-lot basis (FIFO, LIFO, or specific lot identification per fund policy). Rollover equity retained in the buyer's entity must be fair valued and recorded as a new investment position rather than proceeds.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will trace exit proceeds to the closing statement and wire confirmations, recompute the realized gain from the original cost basis lots, and assess proper treatment of transaction expenses, escrow arrangements, and any retained equity rollover.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Signed purchase and sale agreement, final closing statement, wire transfer confirmation, escrow agreement and holdback schedule, original investment cost basis lot schedule, rollover equity valuation (if applicable).

title: Fund Formation - Organizational Costs Capitalized
description: Recording legal, registration, and structuring costs incurred during the formation of a new private equity fund prior to the first closing.
explanation: Costs incurred to organize the fund (LP agreement drafting, regulatory filings, placement agent structuring fees) are capitalized as organizational costs. Under ASC 946, investment companies typically expense these at fund inception or amortize over a short period (commonly 12–60 months). The LPA will specify whether these are borne by the fund or the GP management company.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up a deferred organizational cost asset account. Configure amortization schedule based on fund policy — most PE funds use 60-month straight-line amortization from the first closing date. Track separately from deal costs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors will assess whether organizational costs meet the capitalization threshold and whether the amortization period is reasonable and consistently applied. Costs that primarily benefit the GP (not the fund) should not be charged to the fund.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Invoices from legal counsel and formation agents, LPA provision on organizational cost allocation, fund formation timeline, amortization schedule.

title: Fund Formation - Organizational Costs Amortization (Monthly)
description: Recording the monthly amortization of capitalized fund organizational costs over the elected amortization period.
explanation: The monthly amortization charge ($450,000 / 60 months = $7,500/month) reduces the net carrying value of organizational costs on the balance sheet. This expense is allocated to all LP capital accounts pro-rata each period, reducing NAV per unit.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Automate the monthly journal entry in the fund accounting system. Ensure the expense is included in the management expense ratio (MER) disclosure to LPs in the quarterly reports.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the amortization calculation ties to the original cost basis and elected period. Fully amortized balances should be written off.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Original capitalization entry, amortization schedule, fund policy document on organizational cost treatment.

title: Placement Agent Fee - Fund Raise Commission
description: Recording the placement agent fee paid to third-party capital raisers upon closing of LP commitments, typically 1%–2% of committed capital raised.
explanation: Placement agent fees are paid by the fund or the GP (per LPA terms) to registered broker-dealers who source LP commitments. When borne by the fund, these are an expense that reduces LP NAV. Some LPAs allow placement fees to offset management fees. SEC regulations require placement agents to be registered broker-dealers under Dodd-Frank.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Classify separately from organizational costs. If the LPA specifies that placement fees offset management fees, record the offset at the time of fee billing. Track the total placement fees paid per LP introduced for regulatory disclosure.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the placement agent is a registered broker-dealer (FINRA/SEC registered) to assess regulatory compliance. The fee calculation will be rechecked against the placement agreement and LP commitment amounts.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Placement agent agreement, FINRA registration confirmation, closing certificate of LP commitments, fee calculation schedule, wire transfer confirmation.

title: First Closing - LP Commitment Register & Equalization
description: Recording the equalization payment required from LPs joining at a subsequent closing to place them on equal economic footing with LPs from the first closing, including interest on prior capital calls.
explanation: LPs joining at a second or final closing must pay their pro-rata share of all prior capital calls plus an equalization interest charge (typically 6%–8% p.a. on missed calls). The interest component compensates existing LPs for the opportunity cost of having deployed capital while the late closer was not yet in the fund.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Equalization calculations must be run for each late-closer LP individually based on the specific calls they missed and the days elapsed. The interest received is typically reallocated to existing LP capital accounts, not retained as fund income.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the equalization calculation for each subsequent-closing LP, confirm the interest rate matches the LPA, and ensure the reallocation to existing LPs is correctly processed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Subscription agreement for subsequent-closing LP, equalization calculation workbook, LPA provisions on subsequent closings, wire transfer confirmation.

title: LP Default on Capital Call - Defaulting LP Penalty
description: Recording the accounting treatment when an LP fails to fund a capital call by the due date, triggering default provisions under the LPA including penalty interest and potential forfeiture of LP interest.
explanation: When an LP defaults, the receivable remains on the books but is reclassified to a default receivable. The GP typically has remedies including: charging default interest (10%–15% p.a.), reducing the defaulting LP's interest, selling the LP interest to a third party at a discount, or in extreme cases forfeiting the LP's interest. The non-defaulting LPs may be required to fund the shortfall via an additional call.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Flag the LP as 'defaulting' in the capital account ledger. Cease allocating fund income to the defaulting LP if the LPA permits this. Track the accrual of default interest separately. Document all GP cure notices and legal correspondence.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the collectibility of the defaulting LP receivable and whether an allowance is required. Legal opinions on the enforceability of default provisions will be reviewed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Capital call notice, LP default notice, LPA default provisions, GP legal counsel opinion, correspondence with defaulting LP, cure period timeline.

title: LP Default - Non-Defaulting LP Shortfall Funding
description: Recording the additional capital call issued to non-defaulting LPs to cover the shortfall created by a defaulting LP, as permitted under the LPA.
explanation: Non-defaulting LPs are called upon to fund the defaulting LP's share pro-rata to their existing commitments. This increases the non-defaulting LPs' funded contributions and reduces their remaining unfunded commitment. The additional call amount is tracked separately for purposes of calculating the defaulting LP's penalty/forfeiture.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Issue revised capital call notices to non-defaulting LPs. Update the LP register to reflect increased funded contributions and decreased unfunded commitments for each non-defaulting LP. Cross-reference to the original defaulting LP receivable.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the shortfall funding is authorized by the LPA, verify the pro-rata allocation to non-defaulting LPs, and assess the impact on the defaulting LP's economic interest.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Amended capital call notice, LPA default and shortfall provisions, updated LP commitment schedule, wire confirmations from non-defaulting LPs.

title: Recycled Capital - Reinvestment of Returned Proceeds
description: Recording the reinvestment of early exit proceeds or dividend recap proceeds back into new portfolio investments within the investment period, as permitted by the LPA recycling provision.

entries.account: Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Company F (New)

explanation: Many LPAs allow the GP to recycle returned capital (from exits, dividends, or recaps) within the investment period rather than distributing it to LPs. Recycled capital does not count against the fund's investable capital limit, allowing the GP to effectively invest more than the total committed capital. The LPA typically caps recycling at a percentage of committed capital (e.g., 120%).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a recycled capital tracker showing amounts recycled, dates, source transactions, and deployment transactions. This is critical for LP reporting and verifying the fund has not exceeded the LPA's recycling cap.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors recalculate total deployed capital including recycled amounts to ensure the LPA cap is not breached. Management fee calculations may be affected if recycled capital changes the invested capital base.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LPA recycling provisions, recycled capital calculation schedule, source exit/recap closing documents, new investment deployment documents.

title: Broken Deal Costs - Failed Transaction Expense
description: Expensing due diligence, legal, and advisory costs incurred on a potential portfolio investment that did not close (broken deal / aborted transaction).
explanation: When a potential acquisition falls through, all costs incurred (legal due diligence, financial due diligence, management consulting, travel) must be immediately expensed — they cannot be capitalized since no asset was acquired. The LPA specifies whether broken deal costs are borne by the fund (reducing LP NAV) or by the GP management company.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a deal pipeline project code for each potential investment. If the deal closes, accumulated costs are capitalized to the investment. If broken, costs are immediately expensed and the project code is closed. Some GPs recover broken deal costs from deal fees on future successful transactions.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that costs allocated to the fund versus the GP management company are consistent with the LPA. Any cost-sharing arrangements between funds must be clearly documented.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LOI or NDA showing the transaction was pursued, invoices for DD costs, board/IC memo documenting the decision to abort the transaction, LPA broken deal cost provisions.

title: Fund Operating Expenses - Audit, Legal & Fund Administration
description: Recording recurring fund-level operating expenses including annual audit fees, fund counsel legal fees, fund administrator fees, and D&O insurance premiums.
explanation: Fund-level expenses (distinct from portfolio company expenses) reduce LP NAV and are typically outlined as fund-borne costs in the LPA. These are separate from the management fee and must be disclosed in LP quarterly reports. GP overhead costs (salaries, office rent) are NOT fund expenses — they are covered by the management fee.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Create a fund expense budget at the start of each year. Accrue monthly estimates and true-up quarterly when invoices are received. Allocate all expenses to LP capital accounts in the quarterly NAV calculation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that fund expenses are LPA-permitted fund costs and not GP overhead misallocated to the fund. Related-party transactions (e.g., fund administrator affiliated with the GP) require enhanced disclosure.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LPA permitted fund expense list, invoices from service providers, D&O insurance policy, fund administrator agreement, engagement letters.

title: Preferred Return (Hurdle Rate) - Quarterly Accrual
description: Accruing the LP preferred return (typically 8% per annum) on contributed and uncalled capital for purposes of tracking the hurdle threshold that must be cleared before the GP earns carried interest.

entries.account: Preferred Return Payable to LPs (Waterfall Tracker)

explanation: The preferred return (or 'hurdle rate') is not a cash payment obligation — it is an economic threshold that LPs must receive before the GP participates in profits via carried interest. The accrual is maintained in a sub-ledger waterfall tracker and is used in the distribution waterfall calculation. It compounds annually (or as specified in the LPA) on each LP's contributed capital from the date each capital call was funded.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain the preferred return calculation in the waterfall model, not as a balance sheet liability. Track independently for each LP and each capital call tranche (since different tranches have different funding dates and thus different accrual start dates). Compound per LPA specifications (annually, daily, or simple interest).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors recompute the preferred return accrual from scratch, verifying the starting date of each tranche, the compounding method, and the rate. Errors in preferred return calculations are among the most common PE fund audit findings.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LPA preferred return provisions, funded capital call history by LP with dates, preferred return calculation model, waterfall tracker.

title: Management Fee - Step-Down After Investment Period Ends
description: Recording the reduction in management fee basis when the fund transitions from the investment period (fee on committed capital) to the post-investment period (fee on invested/cost basis of remaining portfolio).
explanation: The LPA typically provides for a step-down in the management fee basis after the investment period ends (usually Year 5–6 of a 10-year fund). During the investment period, fees are typically 2% of committed capital. Post-investment period, fees step down to 1.5%–2% of invested capital (cost basis of remaining portfolio), which declines as investments are exited. This entry reflects the lower fee under the new basis.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Update the management fee calculation parameters in the fund accounting system at the end of the investment period. The fee basis now references the net invested capital schedule (cost basis minus write-downs and realized exits). Set up automated recalculation quarterly as the portfolio changes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the investment period end date (per LPA) and confirm the fee basis has correctly shifted. Overcharging management fees post-investment period is a common SEC enforcement area for PE GPs.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LPA investment period definition and fee step-down provisions, investment period end date determination memo, invested capital schedule (cost basis of remaining portfolio), fee calculation workbook.

title: Management Fee Waiver - GP Co-Investment Reduction
description: Recording the reduction of management fees when the GP elects to waive fees in exchange for a profits interest or co-investment right, a common tax-efficient structuring technique.

entries.account: Management Fee Waiver - GP Profits Interest

explanation: A management fee waiver allows the GP to forgo ordinary income (management fees) in exchange for an increased profits interest in the fund. This is a tax planning technique — the GP substitutes ordinary income (fully taxable) for capital gains treatment on the waived amount (taxed at lower capital gains rates). The waiver must be structured as a 'disguised sale' safe harbor to avoid IRS recharacterization.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Document the fee waiver election in writing prior to the period to which it relates (IRS requirement). The waived amount is tracked as an additional profits interest allocation in the fund's tax capital accounts. Coordinate with the fund's tax advisors on proper K-1 reporting.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: The IRS has scrutinized management fee waiver structures heavily. Auditors will confirm the waiver was properly elected in advance, the arrangement meets the requirements of IRS Notice 2005-43, and K-1 allocations are consistent with the waiver.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Fee waiver election document (signed prior to period), LPA amendment or side letter authorizing the waiver, tax advisor opinion, K-1 allocation schedule.

title: Subscription Credit Facility - Repayment via LP Capital Call
description: Recording the repayment of the subscription line of credit using proceeds from the LP capital call that was issued to fund the facility repayment.
explanation: When the capital call proceeds are received from LPs, the subscription line is repaid in full including accrued interest. The interest cost is typically allocated to LP capital accounts as a fund expense. The net effect is that LPs fund the investment at a slight delay with a small interest cost, but the GP benefits from faster deal execution.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track the interest accrual on the facility daily based on the outstanding balance and the credit agreement rate (typically SOFR + spread). Ensure the interest expense is included in the fund's expense ratio disclosure to LPs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the facility balance per the bank statement, recompute interest expense, and verify the facility is within the borrowing base limits (typically 25%–50% of unfunded LP commitments as collateral).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bank credit agreement, borrowing base certificate, wire transfer for repayment, interest accrual calculation, LP capital call notice (matching repayment amount).

title: NAV-Based Credit Facility - Drawdown for LP Liquidity
description: Recording a drawdown on a NAV-based credit facility (secured against the fair value of the fund's portfolio rather than unfunded LP commitments) used to provide LP liquidity or fund follow-on investments in a mature fund.

entries.account: NAV Credit Facility Payable - Senior Lender

explanation: NAV facilities are used by mature PE funds (typically post-investment period) where unfunded LP commitments are exhausted. The facility is secured by the fund's portfolio investment fair values (typically lenders advance 15%–25% of portfolio NAV). These are more expensive than subscription lines and are used for follow-on investments, LP liquidity needs, or bridging to exits.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The NAV facility is a senior liability of the fund that takes priority over LP interests in a wind-down scenario. Ensure the facility is clearly disclosed in LP quarterly reports. The lender will require quarterly portfolio NAV certification and may include NAV-based maintenance covenants.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the fair value of the pledged portfolio assets supporting the facility. Covenant compliance certificates must be tested. The facility significantly changes the fund's risk profile and LP distributions waterfall.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: NAV credit agreement, portfolio NAV certification letter, lender borrowing base calculation, LP advisory committee consent, quarterly covenant compliance certificate.

title: GP Management Company - Management Fee Revenue Recognition
description: Recording management fee revenue at the GP management company level (separate entity from the fund), when the management company receives the net management fee from the fund it manages.
explanation: This entry is recorded in the GP management company's books (e.g., ABC Capital Management LLC), not in the fund's books. The management company recognizes the net management fee (after offsets) as revenue under ASC 606. This revenue is used to pay GP staff salaries, office rent, and other overhead. The management company and the fund are separate legal entities with separate financial statements.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain completely separate accounting systems or entities for the GP management company and each fund. Intercompany transactions must be properly eliminated if consolidated reporting is prepared. Management company revenues are subject to ordinary income tax rates.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors of the management company (if separately audited) verify fee revenue agrees to the fund's expense records. Related-party disclosures are required. Any expenses of the management company improperly pushed to the fund will be identified.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Management agreement between the fund and the GP management company, fee calculation schedule, wire transfer from fund to management company.

title: Co-Investment - LP Direct Participation Alongside Fund
description: Recording the fund's investment in a portfolio company where LPs are also investing directly alongside the fund (co-investment), and the fund books only its own allocated portion.

entries.account: Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Company G (Fund Share)

explanation: In a co-investment, the fund invests its allocated portion while select LPs invest additional capital directly into the same portfolio company via a separate co-investment vehicle (typically at reduced or zero management fee and carry). The fund books only its own share — the LP co-investment vehicle is a separate entity not consolidated into the fund's financial statements.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up the co-investment vehicle as a separate fund/entity in the portfolio accounting system. The fund and co-invest vehicle hold securities in the same portfolio company but their financials are reported separately. Ensure pro-rata fair value marks are applied consistently across both vehicles.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the allocation of the investment between the fund and co-invest vehicle is fair and consistent with the LPA co-investment rights provisions. Any preferential allocation to favored LPs requires disclosure.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Co-investment side letter or LPA provisions, allocation memo from investment committee, co-invest vehicle subscription documents, portfolio company cap table showing fund vs. co-invest vehicle ownership.

title: Co-Investment Vehicle - No Fee / No Carry Structure Setup
description: Recording the initial LP capital contribution into a standalone co-investment special purpose vehicle (SPV) that charges zero management fee and zero carried interest, investing alongside the main fund.
explanation: Co-invest SPVs are single-purpose entities formed for one specific investment. LPs who receive co-invest rights (typically larger LPs as a preferred economic concession) contribute capital directly. Since no ongoing management is required, these vehicles charge no management fee or carry, providing LPs with lower-cost exposure to specific deals.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Each co-invest SPV requires its own set of books and annual financial statements. GP carries administrative burden for these vehicles. Ensure proper state registration of each SPV (typically Delaware LLC or LP).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: If audited, the SPV audit is straightforward — single investment, single set of LPs. The main risk is valuation consistency between the SPV and the main fund holding the same portfolio company.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SPV operating agreement, LP subscription documents, wire transfer confirmation, investment committee allocation memo.

title: Side Pocket - Segregation of Illiquid / Special Asset
description: Recording the transfer of a portfolio investment into a side pocket structure to ring-fence an illiquid or hard-to-value asset from the main fund's liquid portfolio, preventing unfair dilution for incoming and exiting investors.

entries.account: Side Pocket Investment - Portfolio Company H
Investments at Fair Value - Main Portfolio

explanation: Side pockets are more common in hedge funds but used in some PE structures when a specific investment becomes illiquid, enters distress, or involves a restructuring that makes fair valuation difficult. The asset is moved to a separate sub-account (side pocket) with restricted liquidity. Only LPs invested at the time of the side pocket designation participate in that investment's economics.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Create a separate series or sub-ledger for the side pocket in the fund accounting system. Existing LP interests are bifurcated into main fund interests and side pocket interests. New investors do not participate in the side pocket. All future income, expenses, and gains/losses are tracked separately.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors scrutinize the valuation of side pocket assets heavily (by definition they are hard to value). The fairness of the side pocket designation (which LPs are included/excluded) and the methodology used for valuation require detailed documentation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Investment committee memo authorizing side pocket designation, LPA side pocket provisions or investor consent, valuation policy for the specific asset, LP notification of side pocket creation.

title: LP Interest Transfer (Secondary) - Seller's Accounting
description: Recording the sale of an LP's interest in the fund to a secondary buyer, from the perspective of the fund recognizing the transfer and updating the LP register.
explanation: When an LP sells its fund interest in the secondary market, the fund itself does not receive or pay cash — it simply updates its LP register. The fund records the derecognition of the seller's capital account and the recognition of the buyer's capital account at the same carrying value. Any gain or loss is realized by the selling LP on their own books, not in the fund.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The fund's administrator processes the transfer upon receipt of GP consent (required in most LPAs), executed transfer agreement, and AML/KYC completion for the incoming LP. The unfunded commitment transfers to the new LP at the same amount.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify GP consent was obtained per the LPA, the transfer was properly documented, and the incoming LP completed all AML/KYC requirements. The unfunded commitment transfer must be confirmed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LP transfer agreement, GP consent letter, AML/KYC documentation for incoming LP, updated LP register, unfunded commitment transfer confirmation.

title: GP-Led Secondary Restructuring - Continuation Fund
description: Recording the transfer of select portfolio assets from an existing fund into a new continuation vehicle (GP-led secondary), where existing LPs can cash out or roll into the new vehicle.

entries.account: Investment in Continuation Vehicle - Portfolio Co. I
Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Co. I (Old Fund)

explanation: GP-led secondaries allow a GP to extend the life of high-performing assets beyond the main fund's term. The asset is transferred at independently determined fair value to a new continuation vehicle. Existing LPs can elect to receive cash (paid by secondary investors buying into the continuation vehicle) or roll their interest into the new vehicle. A fairness opinion from an independent advisor is market standard.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The continuation vehicle is a new legal entity requiring its own fund setup, including GP entity, LPA, and fund accounting system. The old fund derecognizes the asset at fair value (which should match the consideration received from the continuation vehicle). No gain/loss arises if transferred at NAV.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: GP-led secondaries are heavily scrutinized for conflicts of interest. Auditors (and LPs/LPAC) will review the independent valuation/fairness opinion, the process for ensuring LPs received fair value, and SEC compliance (these transactions are now subject to enhanced SEC disclosure requirements).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Independent fairness opinion, LPAC consent, LP election forms (cash out vs. roll), continuation vehicle LPA, transfer agreement at agreed valuation, SEC Form ADV disclosures.

title: Direct Lending - Unitranche Loan Investment (BDC / Credit Fund)
description: Recording the origination of a unitranche senior secured loan to a middle-market portfolio company by a credit-focused PE fund or Business Development Company (BDC).

entries.account: Loan Investment - Unitranche Senior Secured (Portfolio Co. J)

explanation: Direct lending funds originate secured loans to portfolio companies. The loan is recorded at face value net of any original issue discount (OID) or upfront fees paid. OID is amortized over the loan term using the effective interest method, increasing the loan's carrying value toward par and recognizing additional interest income. Under ASC 946, BDCs and credit funds carry loans at fair value each period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up each loan as a separate security position with amortization schedules for OID and fees. Track the effective interest rate, payment schedule, covenants, and PIK toggle provisions. Fair value marks will deviate from amortized cost if credit spreads change.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors validate the loan terms against the executed credit agreement, verify OID amortization methodology, and assess the fair value measurement (particularly for Level 3 illiquid loans). Non-accrual policy must be documented and consistently applied.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Executed credit agreement, promissory note, OID/fee schedule, effective interest rate calculation, lien search confirmation, portfolio company financial statements.

title: PIK Interest - Payment-in-Kind Interest Income Accrual
description: Accruing PIK (Payment-in-Kind) interest on a portfolio company loan where interest is added to the principal balance rather than paid in cash, increasing the loan's face amount.

entries.account: Loan Investment - PIK Interest Capitalized (Portfolio Co. K)

explanation: PIK loans allow cash-constrained borrowers to accrue interest rather than pay it currently. The interest compounds by increasing the loan principal. For the fund, PIK interest is recognized as income even though no cash is received. This creates a non-cash income component that must be disclosed separately to LPs, as it inflates net income without corresponding cash flows available for distribution.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track PIK principal separately from cash-pay principal in the loan ledger. PIK interest accruals increase the cost basis of the loan. When the loan is repaid or sold, the full PIK principal plus cash principal is received. Fair value marks must reflect the full PIK-accreted principal.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify PIK elections (some loans have toggle provisions between cash and PIK) are documented per the credit agreement. Non-cash income must be clearly labeled in the financial statements and LP reports.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Credit agreement PIK provisions, PIK election notices, PIK interest calculation schedule, amortization table showing PIK accreted principal.

title: Mezzanine Debt Investment - Warrant Coverage Bifurcation
description: Recording a mezzanine loan investment where the fund receives equity warrants as additional compensation (kicker), requiring bifurcation of the proceeds between the debt and warrant components.

entries.account: Mezzanine Loan Investment - Portfolio Co. L
Warrant Investment - Portfolio Co. L (Equity Kicker)

explanation: Mezzanine loans typically include warrant coverage (the right to purchase equity in the portfolio company) as compensation for the higher risk/junior position. The total investment proceeds are bifurcated between the loan (fair value at date of investment) and the warrant (residual value). The warrant is recorded as a separate equity investment and marked to fair value each period independently.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain the loan and warrant as two separate positions in the portfolio system, even if issued together. The loan OID created by the warrant bifurcation is amortized into interest income over the loan term using the effective interest method. The warrant is revalued using Black-Scholes or other option pricing models.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors challenge the bifurcation methodology — particularly the fair value assigned to the warrants at origination. If the warrant value is understated, the effective yield on the loan is understated. Option pricing model inputs (volatility, risk-free rate) are subject to scrutiny.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Credit agreement with warrant coverage terms, warrant agreement, bifurcation valuation model, effective interest rate calculation including OID from warrant bifurcation.

title: Preferred Equity Investment - Accrued PIK Dividends
description: Recording the quarterly accrual of cumulative PIK dividends on a preferred equity investment in a portfolio company, increasing the liquidation preference and recorded investment value.

entries.account: Preferred Equity Investment - Portfolio Co. M (PIK Dividend)

explanation: Cumulative preferred equity typically accrues dividends at a fixed rate (e.g., 8%–12% p.a.) that compounds on the liquidation preference if not paid in cash (PIK). This increases the fund's carrying value of the preferred investment and creates non-cash income. Upon exit, the full accreted liquidation preference (original investment plus cumulative PIK dividends) is received before common equity participates.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track the liquidation preference separately from the original investment cost. The liquidation preference schedule should be maintained and updated each quarter. Fair value of the preferred is based on the higher of liquidation preference value or conversion/exit value.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the dividend accrual rate and compounding frequency against the preferred stock certificate or investment agreement. The fair value of preferred equity (particularly in stressed scenarios where the portfolio company may not have sufficient equity value to cover the liquidation preference) requires careful assessment.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Preferred stock certificate or investment agreement, dividend accrual calculation schedule, liquidation preference waterfall analysis, fair value assessment memo.

title: Dividend Recapitalization - Proceeds Received by Fund
description: Recording the receipt of a special dividend from a portfolio company funded by new third-party debt at the portfolio company level (dividend recapitalization), returning capital to the fund without a full exit.

entries.account: Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Co. N (Cost Basis Reduction)

explanation: A dividend recap allows the fund to receive cash from a portfolio company by having the company borrow debt and pay a special dividend. For the fund, this is accounted for as a return of capital (up to cost basis) with any excess recognized as realized gain. The fund retains its equity stake post-recap. The portfolio company's fair value post-recap is lower due to the new debt on its balance sheet.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Update the cost basis of the investment to reflect the return of capital. The subsequent fair value mark must reflect the portfolio company's post-recap balance sheet (higher leverage = lower equity value, partially offset by cash received). Track the original cost basis for DPI (Distributions to Paid-In) calculation.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the classification of recap proceeds (return of capital vs. dividend income). The post-recap fair value mark is scrutinized to ensure the increased leverage and interest burden are properly reflected. Tax withholding may apply if the portfolio company is structured internationally.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Portfolio company board resolution declaring special dividend, new credit agreement for the portfolio company debt, wire transfer confirmation, post-recap valuation analysis, cost basis schedule.

title: Blocker Corporation - Setup for Tax-Exempt and Foreign LPs
description: Recording the capitalization of a blocker corporation (C-Corp) interposed between the fund and a portfolio company to shield tax-exempt LPs (pension funds, endowments) and foreign LPs from UBTI and FDAP income.

entries.account: Investment in Blocker Corporation - Portfolio Co. O

explanation: Tax-exempt LPs (universities, pension funds) are subject to UBTI (Unrelated Business Taxable Income) if they invest directly in operating businesses through pass-through entities. Foreign LPs face FDAP withholding and ECI (Effectively Connected Income) risks. A blocker C-Corp absorbs the UBTI/ECI at the corporate level, pays corporate tax, and pays dividends to the fund — converting operating income into dividend income for the LP, which is not UBTI.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The blocker corporation is a separate legal entity with its own tax return (Form 1120) and books. The fund's investment in the blocker is a single equity investment line item. The blocker's own portfolio investment is on the blocker's books. Consolidation into the fund's financials depends on whether the fund is deemed to control the blocker.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the blocker's tax position — corporate tax accruals at the blocker level reduce the net return to LPs that use it. The economic cost of the blocker (corporate taxes) vs. the benefit (UBTI protection) must be documented. IRS scrutiny of blocker structures is significant.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Blocker corporation formation documents (Delaware C-Corp), corporate resolution, subscription of shares, tax advisor memo on UBTI/FDAP analysis, blocker's separate financial statements and tax returns.

title: Blocker Corporation - Corporate Income Tax Accrual
description: Recording the corporate income tax accrual at the blocker corporation level on operating income (interest, dividends, capital gains) earned from the portfolio investment held by the blocker.
explanation: The blocker C-Corp is a taxable entity and pays federal corporate income tax (21%) plus applicable state taxes on its net income. This tax drag reduces the net return passed back to the fund and ultimately to LPs who invested through the blocker. The trade-off is the elimination of UBTI risk for tax-exempt LPs, who are often willing to accept the tax drag as the cost of protection.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The blocker files Form 1120 annually. Estimated quarterly tax payments (Form 1120-W) are required to avoid underpayment penalties. The blocker's income and deductions are separate from the fund's K-1 schedule — LPs investing through the blocker receive dividend income, not a K-1.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors review the blocker's tax provision calculation. ASC 740 (income taxes) applies at the blocker level. Uncertain tax positions (UTPs) must be assessed. State tax nexus analysis is required if the blocker has operations or portfolio investments in multiple states.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Blocker Form 1120 (tax return), tax provision workpapers, estimated tax payment receipts, state nexus analysis, ASC 740 uncertain tax position documentation.

title: Foreign LP Withholding Tax - FIRPTA / FDAP Distribution
description: Recording the withholding of US federal tax on distributions to foreign LPs under FIRPTA (Foreign Investment in Real Property Tax Act) or FDAP (Fixed, Determinable, Annual, Periodic income) rules.

entries.account: LP Capital Account - Foreign LP Distribution

explanation: US partnerships (including PE funds) are required to withhold tax on distributions to foreign LPs. For effectively connected income (ECI), withholding is at the highest applicable tax rate (37% for individuals, 21% for corporations). FIRPTA applies to gains from disposition of US real property interests. Tax treaties may reduce the withholding rate. The withheld amount is remitted to the IRS via Form 8813.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track foreign LP withholding positions by LP and by income character (ECI, FDAP, capital gain). The fund must file Form 8805 for each foreign LP and Form 8804 for the partnership as a whole. Withholding certificates (Form W-8BEN-E) from foreign LPs determine the applicable treaty rates.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify withholding calculations and tax remittances. Failure to withhold results in the fund being liable for the tax plus penalties. Foreign LP immigration status and treaty eligibility documentation must be current.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: W-8BEN-E from each foreign LP, withholding calculation by income character, Form 8813 (quarterly remittance), Form 8804 and 8805 (annual filings), tax treaty analysis memo.

title: Fund of Funds - Investment in Underlying PE Fund
description: Recording an investment made by a fund of funds (FoF) vehicle into an underlying PE fund as a Limited Partner, with look-through treatment for financial reporting.

entries.account: Investment in Underlying Fund - XYZ PE Fund III (LP Interest)

explanation: A fund of funds invests capital commitments into multiple underlying PE funds, providing LP diversification. The FoF records its LP interest in each underlying fund at cost initially, then adjusts to fair value each period based on the underlying fund's NAV (LP's proportionate share of the underlying fund's net assets). Under ASC 946, the practical expedient (NAV as fair value) is typically used for these Level 3 LP interests.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a position record for each underlying fund LP interest. Fair value is updated based on the underlying fund's quarterly NAV reports (with a quarter lag if reports are not timely). The FoF must also track unfunded commitments to underlying funds as contingent obligations.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the NAV practical expedient is applicable and whether the underlying funds' NAVs are audited or unaudited. Significant differences between the FoF's NAV reports and the underlying fund's audited financials must be investigated.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: LP subscription agreement to underlying fund, underlying fund quarterly NAV report, commitment schedule (funded vs. unfunded by underlying fund), fair value assessment workpapers.

title: Fund of Funds - Double Fee Layer Accrual and Disclosure
description: Recording the FoF-level management fee and carried interest in addition to the underlying fund fees, representing the 'double fee' layer borne by FoF investors.

entries.account: FoF Management Fee Expense (0.5% on NAV)
Underlying Fund Fees (Embedded in NAV - Memo)

explanation: FoF investors bear two layers of fees: (1) the underlying funds' management fees (typically 1.5%–2%) and carry (20%), embedded in the underlying fund's NAV, and (2) the FoF-level fees (typically 0.5%–1% management fee and 5%–10% carry). The total fee load is a key disclosure requirement. LPs must understand that the FoF fees are in addition to — not instead of — the underlying fund fees.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The underlying fund fees are NOT recorded as separate line items in the FoF's books — they are captured in the declining NAV of the LP interests. The FoF only records its own management fee and carry. Disclose the total fee load (both layers) in LP reports and DDQs.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors and investors scrutinize the total fee disclosure. SEC guidance requires FoFs to disclose the full economic cost to investors including embedded underlying fund fees. Undisclosed fee layers are a regulatory risk.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Underlying fund fee schedules and LPAs, FoF management agreement, total fee disclosure in offering documents and LP reports, fee load calculation showing both layers.

title: Distressed Debt Investment - Purchase at Discount to Par
description: Recording the purchase of a distressed portfolio company's debt securities at a significant discount to face value (e.g., 40 cents on the dollar) with intent to either recover par through restructuring or convert to equity.

entries.account: Distressed Debt Investment - Portfolio Co. P (Senior Secured)

explanation: Distressed debt is purchased at a market price well below face value ($10M face at 40 cents = $4M cost). The fund records the investment at actual cost paid (purchase price), not face value. If the fund recovers more than cost (through restructuring, par paydown, or debt-to-equity conversion), the excess is a realized gain. The potential return is the difference between par ($10M) and cost ($4M) = $6M gain.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track face value, cost basis, and fair value as three separate data points for each distressed position. Fair value fluctuates with market prices (if traded) or valuation model outputs. If the fund accumulates enough debt to control the restructuring (fulcrum security), the investment thesis changes significantly.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess fair value for distressed debt — these are typically Level 2 or Level 3 instruments. Credit deterioration triggers and non-accrual assessment are key areas. If the fund is converting debt to equity, the accounting for the conversion (gain/loss recognition vs. cost carryover) requires careful analysis.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Trade confirmations for debt purchase, face value and CUSIP/security identifier, credit agreement for the underlying debt instrument, restructuring timeline and expected outcomes.

title: Distressed - Debt-to-Equity Conversion in Restructuring
description: Recording the conversion of distressed debt holdings into equity of the reorganized portfolio company pursuant to a Chapter 11 plan of reorganization or out-of-court restructuring.

entries.account: Equity Investment - Reorganized Portfolio Co. P (Post-Reorg)
Distressed Debt Investment - Portfolio Co. P (Pre-Reorg)

explanation: When distressed debt converts to equity in a reorganization, the fund derecognizes the debt investment at cost and recognizes the new equity at its initial fair value (determined by the confirmed reorganization plan or independent valuation of the reorganized entity). The difference between the new equity's fair value and the original debt cost is recognized as a realized gain or loss.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The new equity position is set up as a fresh investment with a new cost basis equal to its fair value at conversion. All prior unrealized appreciation/depreciation on the debt is reversed and replaced with the realized gain/loss. Tax treatment of the gain may differ from book treatment — consult tax advisors on the specific restructuring mechanics.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors require an independent valuation of the reorganized equity at the conversion date. The fair value determination must be well-supported given the inherent uncertainty of newly reorganized companies. Court-confirmed plan of reorganization is key supporting documentation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Confirmed plan of reorganization (Chapter 11) or restructuring support agreement (out-of-court), new equity issuance documents, independent valuation of reorganized equity, tax advisor memo on gain/loss character.

title: Portfolio Company - Full Write-Off (Total Loss)
description: Recording the complete write-off of a portfolio company investment to zero upon bankruptcy filing, liquidation, or determination that the investment has no remaining recoverable value.

entries.account: Net Realized Loss on Investments - Write-Off
Net Unrealized Depreciation Reversal - Portfolio Co. Q
Investments at Fair Value - Portfolio Co. Q

explanation: When a portfolio company is determined to have zero recoverable value, the investment is written off entirely. Previously recorded unrealized depreciation is reversed (offsetting the realized loss) so that the net economic loss to the fund equals the original cost basis. The total realized loss equals the original cost ($5.5M), while the unrealized reversal and the remaining carrying value ($4M) combine to extinguish the asset.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Close the position in the portfolio system. The write-off triggers a realized loss that flows into the fund's income statement and reduces LP capital accounts. Inform LPs in the quarterly report of the write-off. The realized loss may be recyclable for tax loss harvesting purposes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the basis for the write-off determination — specifically whether the write-off event (bankruptcy, liquidation, board decision) has actually occurred and is documented. Early write-offs of struggling but not-yet-bankrupt companies require strong evidence of zero recovery expectation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Bankruptcy filing (if applicable), liquidation notice, board of directors write-off resolution, legal opinion confirming no recovery prospects, GP IC memo documenting the decision.

title: Currency Hedging - FX Forward to Hedge EUR-Denominated Investment
description: Recording a forward foreign exchange contract entered into by the fund to hedge the USD/EUR currency risk on a European portfolio company investment denominated in Euros.

entries.account: FX Forward Contract - Fair Value Asset
Unrealized Gain on FX Hedge - P&L

explanation: When the fund holds a EUR-denominated investment, USD strengthening erodes the USD value of returns. An FX forward contract locks in a future exchange rate, converting the EUR exit proceeds into a known USD amount. Under ASC 815, the forward contract is marked to fair value each period.
