description: Recording the franchisor's reacquisition of a franchise location from a franchisee (either by purchase or termination), recognizing the reacquired franchise rights as an intangible asset.
explanation: When a franchisor repurchases a franchise location (either to operate as a corporate store or to resell to a new franchisee), the acquisition is recorded at purchase price. The cost is allocated among identifiable assets acquired (equipment, inventory) and reacquired franchise rights (the intangible representing the right to operate and re-grant the franchise in that location). The reacquired franchise right is amortized over the remaining term of the original franchise agreement reacquired.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Perform a purchase price allocation (PPA) at the acquisition date. The reacquired franchise right is amortized over the remaining original franchise term (ASC 805 for business combinations, ASC 350 for asset acquisitions). If the location is subsequently refranchised, the reacquired right is derecognized.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the purchase price allocation methodology and challenge the value assigned to reacquired franchise rights vs. tangible assets. If the acquisition constitutes a business combination under ASC 805, full goodwill accounting applies.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchise purchase agreement, purchase price allocation, independent appraisal of equipment and inventory, franchise agreement showing remaining term, board approval for reacquisition.

title: Franchisor - Corporate-Owned Store Revenue Recognition
description: Recording revenue from franchisor-operated corporate stores, recognized at point of sale (for retail/food service) and presented separately from franchise royalty revenue in the income statement.
explanation: Franchisors that operate company-owned stores recognize point-of-sale revenue from those stores. This revenue is presented separately from franchise royalty and fee revenue in the income statement, per SEC Regulation S-X requirements for public franchisors. Company-owned store margins are typically lower than franchise economics (since the franchisor bears all operating costs), but provide operational credibility and innovation testing grounds.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Separate company-owned store financials from the franchisor's franchise operations. Each company store should have its own P&L with full operating costs (COGS, labor, occupancy, marketing) deducted. The segment or management reporting should clearly distinguish company-operated from franchised units.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify revenue recognition at point of sale per ASC 606. For food service, cash overages/shortages must be reconciled daily. For retail, gift card breakage and loyalty redemptions require separate accounting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: POS daily sales reports, cash register reconciliations, credit card settlement reports, sales tax filings by location, food cost reports (for restaurant franchisors).

title: Franchisor - Refranchising (Corporate Store Sold to New Franchisee)
description: Recording the sale of a company-operated store to a franchisee (refranchising), recognizing the gain or loss on disposal of assets and deferral of any initial franchise fee received.

entries.account: Deferred Revenue - Initial Franchise Fee (New Franchisee)

explanation: Refranchising is a strategic initiative where a franchisor sells company-owned stores to franchisees, converting the economics from company-operated (lower margin, higher capital) to franchised (asset-light, royalty-based). The gain is the difference between proceeds and net book value of tangible assets disposed, less any transaction costs. The initial franchise fee component is separately deferred per ASC 606.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track refranchising transactions separately from normal franchise fee revenue for investor reporting and segment analysis. Public franchisors (McDonald's, Yum! Brands) report refranchising gains/losses as a separate line item. The gain creates a temporary boost to earnings that is not recurring.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the net book value of disposed assets, the purchase price allocation between assets and franchise fee, and the appropriate deferral of the franchise fee component. Gains from refranchising are carefully scrutinized for proper timing of recognition.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Purchase and sale agreement, purchase price allocation, fixed asset disposal reports, lease assignment or new lease agreement, franchise agreement for the new franchisee, closing statement.

title: Franchisor - Master Franchise Agreement Fee (Sub-Franchising Rights)
description: Recording the master franchise fee received from a master franchisee who is granted the right to sub-franchise the brand within a defined territory (country or large region), including rights to recruit and support sub-franchisees.
explanation: Master franchise agreements grant a master franchisee the right to develop and sub-franchise an entire territory. The master fee reflects the value of territorial exclusivity and sub-franchising rights. Under ASC 606, the master fee is recognized over the term of the performance obligations (which may span many years as the territory is developed). The master franchisee will then collect fees from their sub-franchisees, retaining a portion and remitting the balance to the franchisor.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Master franchise fees are typically recognized over the initial development period or the full master franchise term. Set up a deferred revenue schedule with recognition triggers tied to performance milestones (territory development progress) or straight-line over the term.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors challenge the performance obligation identification in master franchise agreements — the rights granted are complex and long-duration. The standalone selling price of territorial exclusivity vs. support services must be assessed separately.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Master franchise agreement, development schedule for the territory, performance obligation analysis, standalone selling price determination, deferred revenue recognition schedule.

title: Franchisor - Master Franchisee Royalty Split Receipt
description: Recording the franchisor's share of royalties collected by the master franchisee from sub-franchisees, remitted to the franchisor per the royalty split specified in the master franchise agreement.
explanation: In a master franchise arrangement, the total royalty from sub-franchisees is typically split between the master franchisee (who manages the sub-franchisees) and the head franchisor. For example, if sub-franchisees pay 6% royalty, the master franchisee might retain 3% and remit 3% to the head franchisor. The franchisor recognizes only its share of the royalty revenue as sub-franchisees make sales. The master franchisee is both a licensee (of the franchisor) and a licensor (to sub-franchisees).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: The franchisor's revenue equals only its contractual share of sub-franchisee sales. Monthly royalty reports from the master franchisee should be reviewed against sub-franchisee sales data. Where possible, integrate sub-franchisee POS data for independent verification.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: The franchisor cannot independently verify sub-franchisee sales without access to the master franchisee's records. Audit rights over the master franchisee's records (including sub-franchisee reports) are critical. Royalty fraud at the master franchisee level is the highest risk.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Master franchise agreement royalty split provisions, master franchisee monthly royalty reports, sub-franchisee sales summary from master franchisee, payment confirmation, master franchisee audit rights provision.

title: International Master Franchise - Foreign Currency Royalty Translation
description: Recording royalties received from an international master franchisee in a foreign currency and translating to USD functional currency, recognizing any foreign exchange gain or loss.

entries.account: Foreign Exchange Gain on Royalty Collection

explanation: When a US-based franchisor receives royalties from an international master franchisee in a foreign currency (e.g., EUR, GBP, CAD), the receivable is initially recorded at the exchange rate when revenue is recognized. Upon collection, the actual USD received may differ from the recorded receivable due to exchange rate movements. The difference is recognized as a foreign exchange gain or loss in the period of collection.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Configure multi-currency receivable tracking in the ERP. Revalue foreign currency receivables at each period-end spot rate, with unrealized FX gains/losses recognized. Consider FX forward contracts to hedge significant international royalty streams. Report FX impact on royalty revenue separately for investor clarity.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify period-end FX revaluation of foreign currency receivables and the translation rates used. Significant FX exposures should be disclosed in financial statement footnotes. Hedging designations (if any) require ASC 815 documentation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Master franchise agreement currency provisions, royalty billing in local currency, FX rate at billing date and collection date, FX revaluation calculation, bank statement showing USD received.

title: Master Franchisee - Royalty Collection from Sub-Franchisee & Remittance Split
description: Recording the master franchisee's collection of royalties from sub-franchisees and the split between the master franchisee's retained portion and the amount remitted to the head franchisor.
explanation: The master franchisee recognizes only its retained share of sub-franchisee royalties as revenue. The portion owed to the head franchisor is immediately recorded as a payable upon collection. Total sub-franchisee royalties collected (6%) are split 50/50: $85,000 retained by master (3%) and $85,000 payable to head franchisor (3%). The master franchisee's income statement only shows its net retained royalty share.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up a payable account specifically for head franchisor remittances. Remit per the schedule in the master franchise agreement (typically monthly). Maintain detailed records of all sub-franchisee royalties collected for head franchisor audit rights purposes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors of the master franchisee verify the royalty split calculation and that the head franchisor payable is remitted on time. Late remittances typically accrue interest per the master franchise agreement.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Master franchise agreement royalty split provisions, sub-franchisee royalty reports, master franchisee royalty remittance schedule, payment confirmation to head franchisor.

title: Franchisor - Franchisee Financing Assistance (Note Receivable)
description: Recording a loan extended by the franchisor to a new franchisee to assist with initial franchise fee or build-out financing, when the franchisee cannot obtain traditional bank financing.
explanation: Some franchisors offer financing programs to attract franchisees who have insufficient capital or cannot qualify for SBA or traditional bank loans. The loan is recorded as a note receivable and bears interest (at or above AFR to avoid imputed interest issues). Monthly principal and interest payments reduce the balance. These loans create credit risk for the franchisor and must be separately disclosed in the FDD. If the note is for a portion of the initial franchise fee, ASC 606 revenue recognition is affected.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a loan sub-ledger for each franchisee note receivable with amortization schedules. Apply the effective interest method if the note bears a below-market rate (imputed interest under ASC 835). Monitor payment performance and establish an allowance for doubtful notes based on aging and franchisee financial condition.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess the collectibility of franchisee notes receivable and the adequacy of the allowance. If the note is in lieu of cash for the initial franchise fee, the timing of revenue recognition for the fee must be assessed — contingent collection risk affects when revenue is recognized.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Promissory note, loan terms (rate, term, security), FDD Item 10 financing disclosure, amortization schedule, payment history, credit analysis of franchisee borrower.

title: Franchisor - Franchisee Note Receivable Impairment Allowance
description: Recording an allowance for credit losses on a franchisee note receivable when the franchisee shows financial distress indicators (late payments, declining sales, covenant breaches).

entries.account: Provision for Credit Losses - Franchisee Note
Allowance for Credit Losses - Franchisee Notes Receivable

explanation: Under ASC 326 (CECL — Current Expected Credit Losses), franchisors must estimate expected credit losses on notes receivable from franchisees over the life of the instrument. Indicators of increased credit risk include: chronic late royalty payments, declining same-store sales, lease payment arrears, or the franchisee seeking concessions. The allowance is updated quarterly based on the latest franchisee financial information.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Implement CECL-compliant expected loss modeling for the franchisee loan portfolio. Key inputs: historical default rates by franchise maturity year, current franchisee financial metrics, and forward-looking economic conditions. For small portfolios, a qualitative approach with individual assessment may be appropriate.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: CECL adoption requires auditors to assess the reasonableness of expected loss estimates. The franchisor's qualitative and quantitative inputs, model documentation, and historical calibration are all reviewed. Under-reserving against struggling franchisees is a common audit finding.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchisee financial statements, same-store sales trend, payment history, CECL model documentation and inputs, credit risk assessment by franchisee, historical default rate data.

title: Franchisor - Encroachment Settlement Payment to Franchisee
description: Recording a settlement payment made by the franchisor to a franchisee for opening a new company store or granting a new franchise within the franchisee's protected territory (encroachment claim).
explanation: Encroachment occurs when a franchisor permits a competing franchise unit or company store to operate within an existing franchisee's territory (whether formally protected or based on reasonable expectation). Settlements compensate the franchisee for lost revenue and market share. These disputes are among the most common litigation matters in franchise law. The settlement is a period expense, often disclosed as a legal settlement loss.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Record as a legal settlement expense in the period the settlement is reached. Prior to settlement, maintain a contingent liability accrual under ASC 450 if loss is probable and estimable. Disclose material encroachment disputes in financial statement footnotes even before settlement.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors inquire about all pending franchise litigation including encroachment claims. Material unaccrued claims create a risk of understatement of liabilities. Legal confirmations from franchise counsel are obtained during the audit to identify all pending matters.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Settlement agreement, release of claims, legal counsel confirmation of settlement, disclosure in financial statement notes, prior litigation reserve documentation.

title: Franchisor - Encroachment Claim Contingent Liability Accrual
description: Accruing a contingent liability for a pending franchisee encroachment claim when loss is probable and the amount is reasonably estimable under ASC 450.
explanation: When franchise counsel advises that an encroachment claim is probable of loss and the estimated settlement range is $35,000–$55,000, the minimum of the range ($45,000, or the best estimate if determinable) is accrued under ASC 450. This accrual is recorded even though litigation is ongoing. The franchisor discloses the nature of the claim and the accrued amount (or a range if more appropriate) in the financial statement footnotes.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a litigation reserve sub-ledger by claim. Legal counsel should provide quarterly updates on the status and estimated range of loss for each material claim. The accrual is updated as new information becomes available.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Litigation contingency assessment is a required audit procedure. Auditors send legal confirmation letters to all franchise counsel requesting disclosure of all pending matters and their probability of loss assessment. Under-accrued contingencies are a material misstatement risk.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Legal counsel's assessment of loss probability and estimated range, ASC 450 evaluation memo, legal confirmation letter response, litigation reserve schedule by claim.

title: Franchisee - Gift Card Sale (Deferred Revenue)
description: Recording the sale of a franchise gift card at the franchisee location, deferred as a liability until the card is redeemed for goods or services.
explanation: Gift card sales do not generate immediate revenue — the obligation to provide future goods or services is a liability (deferred revenue) until the card is redeemed. Under ASC 606, revenue is recognized when the customer redeems the card (performance obligation satisfied). In most franchise systems, gift cards are managed at the franchisor level (enterprise-wide cards) or the local franchisee level. Breakage (cards that will never be redeemed) is recognized as revenue based on estimated redemption patterns.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: If the franchisor manages gift cards centrally, the franchisee may remit the proceeds to the franchisor and not record the deferred revenue. If local, maintain a gift card liability balance in the POS system and general ledger. Ensure gift card balances are included in royalty-base gross sales only upon redemption (not when sold).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that gift card sales are excluded from royalty-base gross sales until redeemed. For franchisors with centrally managed gift cards, the unredeeemed balance must be properly reflected as a liability. Breakage estimation methodology must be reasonable and consistently applied.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Gift card program agreement (franchisor vs. franchisee-managed), POS gift card balance reports, breakage estimation methodology, redemption history data.

title: Franchisee - Gift Card Redemption Revenue Recognition
description: Recording revenue when a customer redeems a franchise gift card, reducing the gift card liability and recognizing the corresponding revenue.
explanation: Upon redemption, the gift card liability is reduced and revenue is recognized for the amount of the redemption. Partial redemptions leave the remaining balance on the card as a liability. If the gift card system is centrally managed by the franchisor, the franchisor remits the redemption value to the franchisee upon redemption at the franchisee's location, and both parties must account for the settlement between them.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: POS systems should automatically record gift card redemptions. Ensure daily reconciliation of gift card redemptions against the liability sub-ledger. For centrally managed systems, reconcile the intercompany settlement between the franchisor and franchisee for redemptions.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors test gift card redemption recording by tracing a sample of POS redemption transactions to the liability reduction. The royalty base (gross sales) must include redemptions — franchisees sometimes exclude gift card redemptions from reported gross sales to reduce royalties.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: POS redemption reports, gift card liability roll-forward, intercompany settlement records (if central system), royalty calculation confirming redemptions are included in gross sales base.

title: Franchisor - Gift Card Breakage Revenue (Unredeemed Balances)
description: Recognizing breakage revenue on gift card balances that are estimated to be permanently unredeemed (the 'breakage' portion), based on historical redemption patterns.
explanation: Based on historical data, a predictable percentage of gift card balances will never be redeemed ('breakage'). Under ASC 606, breakage is recognized as revenue in proportion to the actual redemption pattern (the 'proportional method') if the entity expects to be entitled to the breakage. The breakage rate is estimated from historical redemption curves. Some states require unclaimed property (escheatment) of unredeemed gift card balances after a specified dormancy period.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Calculate breakage quarterly or annually using the redemption history by card issuance cohort. States with no-breakage laws (prohibiting retention of unredeemed balances) require escheatment remittance. Track gift card issuance by state to manage escheatment obligations.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors challenge the breakage estimation methodology and rate. Overstated breakage accelerates revenue recognition. The estimation must be supported by historical data covering multiple cohorts. State escheatment law compliance is also reviewed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Historical redemption curve data by issuance cohort, breakage rate calculation, state escheatment compliance schedule, unclaimed property filings (if applicable).

title: Franchisor - Loyalty Program Points Issued (Deferred Revenue)
description: Recording the deferral of a portion of sales revenue when customers earn loyalty points, representing the stand-alone selling price of the future reward obligation.

entries.account: Sales Revenue (Net - Adjusted for Points Deferral)

explanation: Under ASC 606, loyalty points issued to customers are a separate performance obligation. The transaction price is allocated between the goods/services delivered and the loyalty points earned, based on standalone selling prices. The portion allocated to points is deferred until the points are redeemed. For a $125,000 weekly sales cycle where 4.16% of revenue is attributable to points at standalone value, $5,200 is deferred.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Loyalty program accounting requires integrating the loyalty platform (e.g., Punchh, Paytronix, Spendgo) with the accounting system. The deferred revenue balance must be updated weekly for new points issued, redemptions, and expired points. This is one of the most operationally complex ASC 606 applications for franchise systems.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors challenge the standalone selling price (SSP) of loyalty points — typically derived from the expected redemption value adjusted for estimated breakage. The breakage assumption materially affects the SSP. Auditors also verify the total deferred loyalty liability is reasonable relative to outstanding point balances.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Loyalty program terms and conditions, SSP determination methodology, points outstanding report, redemption history, breakage estimation, loyalty platform data reconciliation to general ledger.

title: Franchisor - Loyalty Points Redemption Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing the deferred loyalty revenue when customers redeem loyalty points for free or discounted products at franchise locations.
explanation: When loyalty points are redeemed, the corresponding deferred revenue is released to revenue. The revenue recognized equals the SSP previously deferred for those specific points. If customers redeem points for free items, the revenue recognized upon redemption offsets the COGS of the free item provided. The net effect is that the profitability of the 'free' item to the franchisee is covered by the previously deferred revenue.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: POS redemption events trigger the deferred revenue release. Reconcile loyalty redemptions between the loyalty platform and the accounting system weekly. For franchise systems where loyalty is managed centrally by the franchisor, intercompany settlements with individual franchisees for redemptions at their locations must be tracked.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that loyalty redemption entries correspond to actual customer redemption events in the loyalty platform. The intercompany settlement between franchisor and franchisees for centrally-managed loyalty programs is a common complexity reviewed in franchise audits.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Loyalty platform redemption reports, deferred loyalty revenue roll-forward, intercompany settlement records, POS redemption transaction data.

title: Franchisor - Franchise System Goodwill (Acquisition of Another Franchisor)
description: Recording goodwill arising from the acquisition of another franchise system (brand), where the purchase price exceeds the fair value of identifiable net assets including franchise agreement intangibles.
explanation: When one franchisor acquires another franchise brand, the purchase price allocation under ASC 805 includes: tangible assets, identifiable intangibles (franchise agreement relationships, favorable lease intangibles, developed technology), and residual goodwill. The trade name/brand is typically the largest intangible and is usually indefinite-lived (not amortized, tested for impairment annually). Franchise agreement relationships are finite-lived and amortized over the weighted average remaining agreement term.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Engage a qualified valuation firm for purchase price allocation. Set up each intangible with the appropriate useful life and amortization method. Goodwill is assigned to the reporting unit (the acquired brand) for annual impairment testing purposes. Franchise agreement relationships require a weighted average remaining term calculation across the entire franchisee network.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Franchise brand acquisitions receive intensive audit scrutiny on purchase price allocation. The value allocated to goodwill vs. amortizable intangibles significantly impacts future earnings (more goodwill = less amortization = higher reported income). Auditors use a valuation specialist to independently assess the PPA.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Acquisition agreement, purchase price allocation by qualified valuation firm, brand income approach valuation, franchise agreement database for remaining term calculation, goodwill impairment testing documentation, indefinite-lived intangible assessment.

title: Franchisor - Franchise Brand Goodwill Impairment
description: Recording a goodwill impairment charge when the carrying value of the franchise brand reporting unit (including goodwill) exceeds its fair value, triggered by declining royalties, brand deterioration, or macroeconomic factors.
explanation: Annual goodwill impairment testing (ASC 350) compares the reporting unit's carrying value (including goodwill) to its estimated fair value (typically a DCF of royalty projections plus a control premium). If the reporting unit's fair value is less than its carrying value, the difference is the impairment charge, limited to the goodwill balance. Triggering events (significant decline in same-store sales, brand scandals, accelerated unit closures) may require interim impairment testing.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Perform annual goodwill impairment testing at the reporting unit level. The key assumption in a franchise brand DCF is the royalty income projection, which depends on unit count growth, same-store sales performance, and royalty rate assumptions. A qualitative assessment (Step 0) may allow bypassing quantitative testing if it is 'more likely than not' that fair value exceeds carrying value.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Goodwill impairment is one of the most judgmental accounting estimates and receives intensive audit scrutiny. Auditors use valuation specialists to independently assess the reporting unit's fair value. Revenue and EBITDA multiples from comparable franchise transactions are used to corroborate DCF results.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Annual impairment test workpapers, DCF model with assumption support, comparable transaction multiples, qualitative assessment memo (if Step 0 approach used), board approval of impairment charge, prior year carrying value reconciliation.

title: Franchisor - FDD Preparation and State Registration Costs
description: Recording the legal, consulting, and filing costs associated with preparing, updating, and registering the Franchise Disclosure Document (FDD) in federal and state registration jurisdictions.
explanation: The FDD must be updated annually and registered in 14 registration states before the franchisor can offer or sell franchises in those states. Annual FDD preparation costs include franchise attorney fees, audit fees for required financial statements, and state registration fees. These are period operating costs expensed as incurred. The FDD must be provided to prospective franchisees at least 14 calendar days before they sign any agreement or pay any money.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: FDD costs are period expenses. Track FDD update and registration status by state in a franchise compliance calendar. The FDD must be updated within 120 days of fiscal year-end (for financial statement updates) and whenever there are material changes in the 23 disclosure items during the year.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors note that the FDD requires audited financial statements — the franchise audit is essentially a trigger for this disclosure requirement. Auditors may also be asked to provide comfort letters or consent to the use of their audit report in the FDD.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: FDD (current annual version), state registration certificates for all 14 registration states, filing fee receipts, franchise counsel invoices, FDD update timeline documentation.

title: Franchisee - Percentage Rent (Variable Lease Payment Based on Sales)
description: Recording the variable percentage rent component of a franchise location lease where rent increases above a base amount when sales exceed a specified threshold.
explanation: Many franchise locations (especially in shopping malls and strip centers) have leases with a base rent plus percentage rent (e.g., 6% of gross sales exceeding a 'natural breakpoint'). The natural breakpoint = Base Rent / Percentage Rate. Sales above the breakpoint trigger percentage rent. Under ASC 842, variable lease payments based on a lessee's sales are not included in the ROU asset/lease liability calculation — they are expensed in the period when the sales triggering them occur.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track monthly sales against the lease breakpoint. Calculate percentage rent due each month and remit per the lease terms (typically quarterly or annually). Under ASC 842, percentage rent is a variable payment NOT included in the right-of-use asset calculation — only fixed and in-substance-fixed payments are included.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the breakpoint calculation and the exclusion of percentage rent from the ROU asset and lease liability. Percentage rent reported to the landlord must be consistent with sales reported to the franchisor for royalty purposes — discrepancies are a red flag for sales underreporting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Lease agreement with percentage rent clause, natural breakpoint calculation, monthly sales-to-breakpoint comparison, percentage rent calculation and payment schedule, landlord certificate (for annual reconciliation).

title: Franchisee (Food Service) - Food Cost / COGS Weekly Recognition
description: Recording the weekly cost of goods sold (food cost) for a food service franchise, calculated based on beginning inventory plus purchases minus ending inventory, targeted at a franchise-mandated food cost percentage.

entries.account: Food Cost / Cost of Goods Sold

explanation: Food cost COGS = Beginning Food Inventory + Purchases - Ending Food Inventory = $21,875 for the week. At a 35% food cost target on $62,500 weekly sales, this is on target. Franchisors monitor franchisee food cost percentages closely (often requiring weekly reporting) because high food cost indicates waste, theft, portioning errors, or purchasing from unapproved suppliers. Chronic deviation from the system average is an audit trigger for the franchisor.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Conduct weekly physical inventory counts for food service franchises. Many franchise systems use integrated inventory management software (Compeat, Restaurant365, MarketMan) that tracks theoretical vs. actual food cost automatically. Variance analysis between theoretical (based on recipes and sales mix) and actual food cost is a critical operations metric.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: For franchisee financial statement audits, food cost is typically the highest-risk expense line item (cash-intensive business, susceptibility to shrink and theft). Auditors test inventory counts, reconcile purchases to invoices, and compare food cost % to system averages. Significant below-average food cost may indicate underreported sales.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Weekly inventory count sheets, purchase invoices from approved suppliers, food cost calculation workpapers, theoretical vs. actual food cost variance report, POS sales mix report.

title: Franchisee (Food Service) - Labor Cost Weekly Accrual
description: Recording the weekly labor cost accrual for a food service franchise location, including hourly crew wages, manager salaries, payroll taxes, and benefits.
explanation: Labor is typically the second-largest cost for food service franchisees after occupancy. At a 34% labor cost target on $62,500 weekly sales = $21,250 target. Including payroll taxes brings total labor burden to ~36.3%. Franchisors track labor cost % (labor/gross sales) as a key performance indicator and provide labor optimization guidance. Overtime management is critical — unexpected overtime spikes significantly impact profitability.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Use a labor management system (HotSchedules, 7shifts, Deputy) integrated with POS and payroll to track actual vs. scheduled hours and labor cost in real-time. Weekly payroll accruals must account for mid-week pay periods and include the unpaid portion of the current week.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: For franchisee financial statement audits, wage and hour compliance (minimum wage, overtime, break requirements) is assessed. Auditors also verify the consistency between payroll records and tax filings (W-2s, 941s). Significant labor cost below the system average may indicate cash wage payments (not reported on payroll).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Weekly payroll registers, timekeeping records, manager salary agreements, payroll tax calculation (Form 941), unemployment tax filings, labor management system reports.

title: Franchisor - Same-Store Sales (Comp Sales) Tracking & Disclosure
description: Recording the reclassification of a franchise location into the comparable store base after 13 months of operation, and the accounting considerations for same-store sales reporting to investors.

entries.account: Same-Store Sales Base (Royalty Revenue - Comp Units)

explanation: Same-store sales (SSS) or comparable store sales is the most important operating metric for franchise systems. A unit enters the comparable base after a defined period (typically 13 months or the 14th month of operation). Public franchisors are required to disclose SSS trends (SEC guidance since 2018). Royalty revenue growth from comp store sales improvement is organic — additional royalty from new unit additions is a separate growth component.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a unit classification database distinguishing comparable and non-comparable units. Automate the transition of units into the comp base at the appropriate anniversary month. SSS is typically reported as a % change vs. prior year comparable period. Temporary closures may require a unit to be removed from the comp base.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Public franchisors' SSS disclosure is reviewed by SEC staff for consistency and completeness. The definition of 'comparable' must be clearly disclosed and consistently applied. Cherry-picking inclusion/exclusion of units to inflate SSS metrics is an SEC enforcement risk.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Unit opening date database, comp base definition policy, SSS calculation by unit, total system sales summary, SEC disclosure of SSS methodology.

title: Franchisee - Unit Closure & Asset Write-Off
description: Recording the write-off of remaining net book value of leasehold improvements, equipment, and franchise rights when a franchisee closes a location before the end of the franchise term.

entries.account: Loss on Unit Closure - Leasehold Improvements
Loss on Closure - Franchise Rights (Net Book Value)

explanation: When a franchise location closes, all remaining asset net book values are written off as a loss on closure. The write-off includes leasehold improvements (which cannot typically be removed), equipment (which may have salvage value), and any unamortized franchise rights. If the closure triggers an early lease termination, the ROU asset and lease liability must also be derecognized, with any difference recognized as a gain or loss on lease termination.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Process asset retirements for all fixed assets at the location. Obtain salvage value estimates for any equipment that can be sold or relocated. The ROU asset and lease liability derecognition upon early termination requires a separate calculation — the termination penalty fee is recognized as a loss in the period of termination.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors examine unit closure decisions for evidence of impairment indicators that should have been recognized earlier (ASC 360 long-lived asset impairment). A sudden write-off without prior impairment testing may indicate delayed recognition.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Board or management decision to close, notice to franchisor, asset disposal records, equipment salvage appraisals, early lease termination agreement, final inventory count, ROU asset derecognition calculation.

title: Franchisee - ROU Asset & Lease Liability Derecognition on Unit Closure
description: Recording the derecognition of the right-of-use asset and lease liability upon early lease termination when a franchise location closes, with recognition of any lease termination penalty.
explanation: When a lease is terminated early, the lessee derecognizes the ROU asset and lease liability. Any difference between the derecognized ROU asset net book value and the derecognized lease liability (plus any termination penalty paid to the landlord) results in a gain or loss on lease termination. A gain arises when the lease liability exceeds the ROU asset net book value (common later in the lease term as the liability amortizes faster than the ROU asset in operating leases).

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Calculate the ROU asset net book value and remaining lease liability at termination date using the lease accounting system. The termination penalty (if paid separately) is a period expense. For operating leases, the ROU asset amortization pattern differs from the liability reduction pattern, creating timing differences.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the termination date, the final lease liability and ROU asset balances, and any termination fees paid. Early terminations that result in unexpectedly large gains may indicate the original lease accounting was incorrect (overstated liability).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Lease termination agreement, landlord confirmation of early termination, lease accounting system reports showing final balances, termination penalty payment confirmation, gain/loss calculation.

title: Franchisee - Long-Lived Asset Impairment (Underperforming Location)
description: Recording an impairment charge on leasehold improvements and other long-lived assets at a franchise location whose projected undiscounted cash flows no longer exceed the carrying value of the asset group.
explanation: Under ASC 360, long-lived assets are tested for impairment when triggering events occur (significant underperformance vs. projections, planned closure, negative same-store sales trend). Step 1: Compare the sum of undiscounted future cash flows to the asset group carrying value. If carrying value exceeds undiscounted cash flows, Step 2 applies: write down to fair value (typically based on a discounted cash flow or cost approach). The impairment reduces the carrying value prospectively — not recorded as a change in estimate.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: For multi-unit franchisee operators, review each location's operating performance quarterly for impairment triggers. The 'asset group' for impairment testing typically includes all assets at the specific location level (leasehold improvements, equipment, ROU asset). Franchise rights may be tested separately as an intangible asset.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether impairment triggering events were present at earlier periods (not just at the reporting date). For locations with persistent negative cash flows, auditors will challenge whether impairment should have been recognized sooner. The undiscounted cash flow projection is a key audit estimate.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Location-level financial performance history, management's cash flow projections (3–5 year), impairment testing model (undiscounted cash flows vs. carrying value), fair value determination, triggering event identification memo.

title: Franchisee - Franchisor-Mandated Remodel / Re-Image Program
description: Recording the capitalization of costs for a franchisor-mandated renovation program (re-image, remodel, or refresh) required as a condition of franchise renewal or ongoing compliance.

entries.account: Loss on Retirement - Old LHI (Not Fully Depreciated)

explanation: Franchise systems typically require periodic remodels (every 8–12 years) to maintain brand standards. New remodel costs are capitalized if they extend the useful life or add new capabilities. Existing leasehold improvements being replaced are retired (cost and accumulated depreciation removed). If not fully depreciated, the net book value of retired assets is a loss. The new LHI are depreciated over the shorter of useful life or remaining lease term.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Create a remodel project code to accumulate all costs. Upon completion, capitalize qualifying costs and expense any items that don't extend useful life (painting, minor repairs). Retire replaced assets promptly — many franchisees delay asset retirements, overstating asset balances.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors examine remodel capitalization to ensure routine maintenance is not being capitalized. The retirement of old assets (and any related loss) must be properly recorded — capitalization of new assets without retiring old ones overstates total assets. Lease term reassessment may be required after a remodel if it suggests the franchisee intends to continue operating.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchisor remodel specifications and approval, contractor invoices, capitalization vs. expense analysis, asset retirement documentation for replaced items, updated depreciation schedule.

title: Franchisor - Area Representative Agreement Fee
description: Recording the fee received from an Area Representative (AR) who is granted the right to recruit, support, and supervise franchisees within a defined territory in exchange for a share of royalties and franchise fees.
explanation: Area Representatives (different from master franchisees — ARs do not sub-franchise; they recruit and support franchisees on behalf of the franchisor) pay an upfront fee for territorial rights. ARs earn a percentage of ongoing royalties and initial fees for franchisees they develop in their territory. The AR fee is recognized under ASC 606 over the AR's service period as the territorial development and franchisee support obligations are met.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track area representative territories and the franchisees assigned to each AR. Revenue recognition from AR fees should align with the AR's ongoing service performance (franchisee support). Royalty splits to ARs are an ongoing operating expense (commission) for the franchisor.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the AR arrangement creates a separate performance obligation beyond the territorial license grant. If the AR provides ongoing franchisee support services over the term, revenue should be deferred and recognized over the AR's service period.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Area representative agreement, territory map, franchisee assignment to AR, development schedule, royalty split calculation for AR commissions.

title: Franchisor - Area Representative Commission on Royalties (Ongoing)
description: Recording the ongoing royalty commission paid to area representatives as their contractual share of royalties collected from franchisees within their territory.
explanation: ARs typically receive 50% of the royalties and initial fees collected from franchisees in their territory (split varies by system). For a territory generating $45,000/month in royalties, the AR receives $22,500 and the franchisor retains $22,500. This commission is an ongoing operating expense that significantly impacts the franchisor's effective royalty yield. The franchisor's net royalty income from AR territories is substantially lower than from directly managed territories.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Calculate AR commissions from the royalty billing system. Commissions should be recorded in the same period as the royalties from which they are derived. Maintain a commission sub-ledger by AR territory. Deduct commissions from the net royalty revenue line in management reporting to show the franchisor's 'net net' royalty yield.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify AR commission calculations against the AR agreement and test commissions to royalty collections. Overpaid commissions are a recurring issue where royalties are collected but commissions are calculated on a broader base than the agreement specifies.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Area representative agreement (commission rate and base), royalty collection report by AR territory, commission calculation, payment confirmation to AR.

title: Multi-Unit Franchisee - Intercompany Eliminations (Common Ownership)
description: Recording intercompany eliminations for a multi-unit franchisee who operates multiple franchise locations through separate LLC entities under common ownership, when preparing consolidated financial statements.

entries.account: Intercompany Management Fee Income (Parent Entity - Eliminated)
Intercompany Management Fee Expense (Operating Entity - Eliminated)

explanation: Many multi-unit franchisee operators use a management company structure where a parent management company charges each operating LLC a management fee for centralized services (accounting, HR, purchasing). When consolidated financial statements are prepared, these intercompany management fees are eliminated. Lenders often require consolidated financial statements from multi-unit operators, and intercompany transactions can obscure true unit-level profitability if not eliminated.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up intercompany accounts in each entity's chart of accounts. Use a consolidation module or spreadsheet to eliminate all intercompany transactions (management fees, loans, shared expenses). Ensure the elimination entries tie out — the parent's income equals the subsidiaries' expense in total.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors examine multi-unit operators' intercompany transactions carefully. Management fees from operating units to a parent entity that are not arm's-length can be used to shift profits and mislead lenders. Document the basis for any intercompany charges (time-based, cost-plus, flat fee).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Intercompany management agreement, cost allocation basis, intercompany receivables/payables reconciliation, consolidation worksheet showing entity-by-entity and eliminated amounts.

title: Franchisee - SBA 7(a) Loan for Franchise Startup Financing
description: Recording the proceeds from an SBA 7(a) loan used by a new franchisee to fund the initial franchise fee, leasehold improvement build-out, and working capital for the first year of operations.
explanation: SBA 7(a) loans (up to $5M, guaranteed by SBA up to 85%) are the most common financing vehicle for new franchisees. The SBA maintains a Franchise Registry of approved franchise systems — SBA-approved systems have expedited loan processing. The proceeds fund: initial franchise fee ($50K), build-out ($200K), equipment ($75K), and working capital ($25K). Loan proceeds are classified as current and long-term based on the amortization schedule.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Record the loan proceeds and classify current vs. long-term portions. Set up an amortization schedule (SBA 7(a) loans have terms up to 25 years for real estate; 10 years for equipment and working capital). Book origination fees as debt issuance costs, not separate expenses, and amortize over the loan term.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the loan proceeds are recorded at the correct amount net of origination fees. Debt covenant compliance (if any) must be assessed. The SBA guarantee does not eliminate the financial reporting requirement for proper debt classification.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: SBA loan agreement, amortization schedule, UCC filing (collateral), SBA authorization and note, origination fee schedule, current/long-term debt classification calculation.

title: Franchisee - Working Capital Line of Credit (Revolving Credit)
description: Recording draws and repayments on a revolving line of credit used by a franchisee for seasonal working capital needs and short-term cash flow management.
explanation: Food service and retail franchisees often need short-term working capital to manage seasonal fluctuations, inventory buildups (pre-holiday), or gap-fill before royalty cycle timing. A revolving line of credit provides flexible access to funds. Unlike SBA term loans, draws and repayments can occur multiple times. The line is classified as current if repayable within 12 months. Interest is accrued on the outstanding daily balance.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track the revolving line balance daily if interest accrues on daily average balance. Classify as current liability. Disclose the credit limit, outstanding balance, and maturity date in financial statement footnotes. Many franchise lenders include cross-default provisions with other franchise entity debt.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors confirm the line of credit balance with the lender directly (bank confirmation). Covenant compliance (minimum debt service coverage, minimum net worth) must be tested. Lines classified as current that have recurring outstanding balances for more than a year may need reclassification analysis.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Credit agreement with the bank, draw and repayment history, bank statements confirming line balance, interest accrual calculation, covenant compliance certificate.

title: Franchisor - Royalty-Free Period Incentive for New Franchisee
description: Recording the accounting treatment when a franchisor grants a new franchisee a royalty-free period (e.g., first 3 months waived) as a new franchise development incentive.

entries.account: Royalty Concession / Franchise Development Incentive Expense

explanation: Royalty waivers granted to incentivize new franchise development or attract franchisees to underperforming markets are a variable consideration under ASC 606. The franchisor estimates the total transaction price including the effect of royalty waivers over the contract term. If the waiver is a fixed concession (e.g., first 3 months), it reduces the expected royalty revenue for the period. The waiver is effectively an incentive expense reducing total royalty revenue recognized.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track all franchise development incentives by franchisee agreement. The royalty waiver reduces the net royalty revenue recognized during the waiver period. Disclose the existence and magnitude of incentive programs in the franchisor's financial statements and FDD (Item 11 / Item 19 considerations).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that royalty waivers are properly reflected in royalty revenue (not overstating royalties for the waiver period). If waivers are significant, the variable consideration constraint under ASC 606 must be assessed — it may require deferral of revenue to offset the estimated future waiver.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchise agreement development incentive addendum, royalty waiver schedule, development incentive program policy, disclosure in FDD.

title: Franchisor - Sale of Entire Franchise System / Brand to Strategic Acquirer
description: Recording the gain on sale of an entire franchise system (brand, franchise agreements, IP, and associated assets) to a strategic or financial acquirer.

entries.account: Gain on Sale of Franchise System

explanation: The sale of an entire franchise brand is a business disposition. All assets of the franchise system are derecognized at their carrying values. The gain is the difference between net proceeds (after transaction costs) and the total net book value of all derecognized assets including goodwill, intangibles, and net tangible assets. Transaction costs (investment banking fees, legal fees, management bonuses triggered by change of control) reduce the gain.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Coordinate with legal counsel and investment bankers on the exact closing date and final purchase price (including any earnout or working capital adjustments). Derecognize all assets in the fiscal period when control transfers. Management bonuses triggered by the transaction are deducted from the gain calculation for book purposes.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the closing date for proper period recognition, the completeness of derecognized assets (including contingent assets not on the balance sheet), transaction cost classification, and any earn-out provisions that may defer a portion of the gain.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Purchase and sale agreement, final closing statement, list of all transferred assets, management fee and investment banking fee invoices, earn-out provisions (if any), board approval, regulatory approval (Hart-Scott-Rodino if required).

title: Franchisor - Incremental Costs of Obtaining Franchise Contract (ASC 606-10-340)
description: Capitalizing the incremental costs (franchise sales commissions) paid to the franchisor's internal franchise development team upon signing a new franchise agreement, amortized over the franchise agreement term.

entries.account: Contract Acquisition Costs - Franchise Sales Commission (Asset)

explanation: Under ASC 606-10-340, incremental costs of obtaining a contract (costs the entity would not have incurred but for obtaining the contract) must be capitalized and amortized consistently with the recognition of the related revenue — over the franchise agreement term. For a franchise sales representative who earns a $12,000 commission upon signing a 10-year franchise agreement, the commission is capitalized and amortized at $1,200/year. The practical expedient allows immediate expensing if the amortization period would be one year or less.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up a contract acquisition cost asset account by franchise agreement. Amortize over the initial term (not including renewals unless renewal is highly probable). If the franchise agreement is terminated early, the remaining unamortized balance is expensed immediately. Track by franchisee for accurate amortization management.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that only incremental costs (primarily commissions) are capitalized — not direct costs such as legal fees (which are expensed). The amortization period must match the revenue recognition period for the related franchise agreement. Premature expensing of these costs is also a finding (should be capitalized, not expensed).
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchise development commission plan, signed franchise agreement, commission calculation tied to specific agreement, amortization schedule, distinction of incremental vs. non-incremental costs.

title: Franchisor - Contract Acquisition Cost Amortization (Monthly)
description: Recording the monthly amortization of capitalized franchise sales commissions (contract acquisition costs) over the term of the related franchise agreement.
explanation: Monthly amortization = $12,000 / 120 months (10-year term) = $100/month. This is recognized as a selling cost on the franchisor's income statement. For a large franchise system with hundreds of new agreements per year, the total capitalized commission asset can be significant. The amortization pattern mirrors the royalty revenue recognition pattern — both are spread over the franchise agreement term.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Automate monthly amortization in the ERP. Generate an aging report of capitalized contract costs by agreement to identify early terminations requiring accelerated amortization. At year-end, the total amortization must be reconciled to the income statement commission expense line.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors reconcile the contract acquisition cost roll-forward (beginning balance + capitalization - amortization - early termination write-offs = ending balance). The balance should be proportional to the volume of new franchise agreements executed.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Contract acquisition cost roll-forward schedule, new franchise agreement commission calculations, amortization schedule, early termination write-off analysis.

title: Franchisor - Franchisee Incentive Payment (Contra Revenue)
description: Recording a cash payment or royalty credit provided to a franchisee as a development incentive, performance reward, or settlement concession — classified as contra revenue rather than expense.

entries.account: Cash / Royalty Credit Payable - Franchisee Z

explanation: Under ASC 606, payments made by a vendor (franchisor) to a customer (franchisee) are classified as contra revenue (reduction of revenue) unless the payment is in exchange for a distinct good or service provided by the franchisee to the franchisor. Development incentives, royalty holidays, and performance bonuses paid to franchisees are contra revenue — they reduce the net royalty and fee revenue reported, not as a separate expense. This is commonly misclassified as a marketing or G&A expense.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Set up a dedicated contra-revenue account for franchisee incentive payments, clearly labeled as a reduction of revenue. This is critical for proper presentation of gross vs. net revenue. Public franchisors report this as a reduction of total revenues in the income statement, not below the revenue line.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Misclassification of franchisee incentive payments as marketing or administrative expense overstates both revenues and expenses. Auditors assess the nature of each significant franchisee payment to determine proper classification. SEC staff have commented on improper gross-up of revenues for incentive payments.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchisee incentive program documentation, specific payment authorization, basis for classification (contra revenue vs. expense — is the franchisee providing a distinct service in exchange?).

title: Franchisor - Product Sales Revenue (Franchisor as Supplier to Franchisees)
description: Recording product sales revenue when the franchisor acts as the primary product supplier to franchisees, selling proprietary products directly to the franchise network and recognizing revenue upon delivery.
explanation: Some franchise systems (particularly food service brands and specialty retail) require franchisees to purchase proprietary products exclusively from the franchisor's supply chain operation. The franchisor recognizes product sales revenue (gross) when products are delivered to franchisees. This revenue stream can dwarf royalty revenue in product-heavy systems (e.g., some fast casual brands where the franchisor supplies the core protein/ingredients). Gross vs. net presentation depends on whether the franchisor controls the goods before delivery.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Product sales to franchisees must be presented as a separate revenue line from royalties and fees. COGS (cost of products sold to franchisees) is also separately presented. The gross margin on product sales is a key profitability metric for supply-chain-centric franchise systems. Ensure intercompany eliminations if the supply chain is through a related-party entity.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: The gross vs. net revenue presentation is a significant audit judgment. If the franchisor merely arranges for supplier delivery without controlling the goods, net presentation (commission basis) applies. Auditors apply the principal/agent framework (ASC 606-10-55-36 through 55-40) to determine the correct presentation.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Supply chain agreement with franchisees, product pricing schedule, delivery confirmations, COGS calculation, principal/agent analysis documentation, related-party disclosure if supply chain entity is affiliated.

title: Franchisor - Cost of Goods Sold (Franchise Supply Chain / Product Sales)
description: Recording the cost of goods sold associated with products sold by the franchisor to franchisees through the franchisor's supply chain operation.

entries.account: Cost of Products Sold - Franchise Network Supply

explanation: The COGS associated with franchisor product sales to the network represents the cost of manufacturing or procuring the products (70% cost ratio = $2.24M on $3.2M product sales, 30% gross margin). This gross margin on product supply represents the franchisor's supply chain profit. Combined with royalties, the total effective 'take rate' from franchisees includes both royalty % and the margin embedded in required product purchases.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain a separate COGS line for franchise network product supply, distinct from any company-operated store COGS. Inventory should be physically verified quarterly. The gross margin on product supply is often not disclosed separately in the FDD — but it represents real economic cost to franchisees that reduces their unit economics.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify inventory valuation at the lower of cost or net realizable value (ASC 330). COGS must reconcile to the inventory roll-forward (beginning inventory + purchases - ending inventory = COGS). Transfer pricing between the franchisor and any related-party supply chain entity is a key audit focus.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Inventory purchase invoices, physical count documentation, COGS calculation (inventory roll-forward), product pricing to franchisees, gross margin analysis, supply chain entity related-party disclosure.

title: Franchisor - Sales-Based Royalty Variable Consideration Constraint
description: Applying the variable consideration constraint under ASC 606 to limit royalty revenue recognition when there is significant uncertainty about the total royalties to be earned from a new or underperforming franchisee.

entries.account: Royalty Revenue (Constrained - Probable Not to Reverse)

explanation: For new franchisees in ramp-up or financially distressed franchisees with a history of royalty non-payment, the variable consideration constraint may require limiting royalty revenue recognition to amounts 'highly probable not to be significantly reversed.' If a franchisee has $50,000 in billed royalties but has a history of non-payment, only the amount expected to be collected without risk of reversal ($28,000 in this case) would be recognized. The unconstrained amount is recognized only when the uncertainty resolves.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Apply the constraint at the individual franchisee level for franchisees with payment history issues. The constraint determination requires judgment based on the franchisee's payment history, current financial condition, and the franchisor's legal remedies (security deposits, personal guarantees). Document the constraint assessment for each impacted franchisee.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors assess whether the variable consideration constraint is being applied consistently and to the right set of franchisees. Overoptimistic royalty revenue recognition without applying the constraint for financially weak franchisees overstates revenue. The constraint and the allowance for doubtful accounts serve different but related purposes.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Franchisee payment history (12-24 months), current financial condition assessment, constraint determination memo by franchisee, reconciliation of billed royalties to recognized royalties for constrained accounts.

title: Franchisor - FDD Audited Financial Statement Requirement (Item 21)
description: Recording audit fees and financial statement preparation costs required to comply with FDD Item 21, which mandates audited financial statements as part of the annual Franchise Disclosure Document.
explanation: FDD Item 21 requires the franchisor to include audited financial statements (balance sheet, income statement, cash flow statement) for the most recent three fiscal years. This is a mandatory annual requirement under the FTC Franchise Rule. For franchisors operating in registration states (CA, IL, NY, MD, etc.), the FDD (with audited financials) must be registered and approved before franchises can be offered. The annual audit is thus a regulatory requirement, not just a best practice.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Schedule the audit to be completed within 120 days of fiscal year-end to allow timely FDD updating and annual registration renewal. Many states will issue a 'deficiency notice' if the FDD is filed without current audited financials. The audit scope covers the franchisor entity (not individual franchisees).
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: The auditor for FDD purposes must be independent under AICPA standards. The audit report is included in the FDD and the auditor's name is publicly disclosed. Any limitation on scope or qualified audit opinion creates serious franchise sales complications — most prospective franchisees and their advisors review the audit report.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Audit engagement letter, audited financial statements, audit report, FDD Item 21 financial statement presentation, state registration receipts confirming FDD acceptance with current audited financials.

title: Franchisor - International Expansion Costs (New Market Entry)
description: Recording market research, legal, regulatory, and business development costs incurred by the franchisor to enter a new international market prior to signing the first master franchise agreement.

entries.account: International Expansion Expense - Business Development Travel

explanation: International franchise expansion requires significant pre-contract costs including market feasibility studies, local legal review of franchise law compliance (international franchise laws vary significantly by country — some require local FDD equivalents), trademark registration in target countries, and business development travel to identify master franchise partners. These costs are expensed as incurred under ASC 720-15 (start-up costs), not capitalized as pre-contract assets.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Use project codes to track international expansion costs by target country. The total investment to enter a new country can range from $50,000 to $500,000+ depending on legal complexity. Trademark registration in each country is critical — file under the Madrid Protocol for multiple countries simultaneously.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that pre-contract international expansion costs are expensed, not deferred as assets pending the master franchise agreement signing. Capitalization of business development costs is not permitted under ASC 720-15.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Market feasibility study, country-specific franchise law analysis, trademark registration filings (PCT/Madrid Protocol), travel expense reports, legal retainer invoices for international counsel.

title: Franchisor - Trademark Licensing Revenue (Non-Franchise Brand Licensing)
description: Recording revenue from licensing the franchise brand's trademarks to non-franchise third parties (e.g., co-branded products, merchandise, licensing to consumer goods manufacturers).
explanation: Franchise brands with significant consumer recognition may license their trademarks for use in co-branded products (e.g., fast food brand in packaged retail goods, consumer merchandise). These licensing arrangements are separate from the franchise system and generate royalty income based on the licensee's product sales. Under ASC 606, trademark licensing revenue (sales-based royalties) is recognized as the licensee's sales occur, applying the same sales-based royalty constraint as franchise royalties.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Track trademark licensing agreements separately from franchise agreements. Obtain quarterly royalty reports from licensees and verify against audit rights. Trademark licensing revenue should be disclosed separately from franchise royalty revenue if material.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify trademark licensing revenue against licensee sales reports. The brand may need to actively audit licensees to ensure proper royalty reporting. Any royalty-free trademark licenses to related parties require disclosure and arm's-length assessment.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Trademark license agreement, licensee royalty reports, trademark registration certificates, audit rights confirmation, related-party disclosure (if applicable).

title: Franchisor - Segment Reporting (Company-Operated vs. Franchise Segments)
description: Presenting separate segment financial information for company-operated stores and the franchise segment as required by ASC 280 for public franchisors with sufficient discrete financial information.
explanation: Public franchisors with meaningful company-operated stores must report segment financials under ASC 280 if operating performance is reviewed and managed separately. The franchise segment (asset-light, high-margin royalties and fees) typically has dramatically higher operating margins (60%–80%) than the company-operated segment (15%–25%), reflecting the different business model economics. Segment reporting allows investors to understand the underlying profitability of each model.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain segment-level P&Ls in the ERP. Allocate shared corporate overhead between segments using a documented methodology (headcount, revenue, or activity-based). The segment disclosure in the notes must reconcile to the consolidated totals — unexplained reconciling items are a common SEC comment letter topic.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify segment revenue and operating income reconcile to consolidated amounts. The allocation methodology for shared costs between segments must be consistent and reasonable. SEC staff frequently comment on whether segments are appropriately aggregated or require separate reporting.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Segment financial statements, overhead allocation methodology, CODM (Chief Operating Decision Maker) management reports showing segment-level review, reconciliation of segment totals to consolidated financials.

title: Franchisor - Unit Count Roll-Forward Disclosure (FDD Item 20 / SEC)
description: Preparing the unit count roll-forward table showing openings, closures, terminations, transfers, and conversions across company-operated and franchised units — required for FDD Item 20 and SEC investor disclosures.
explanation: Unit count is a fundamental operating metric for franchise systems. The roll-forward (beginning units + openings - closures +/- transfers = ending units) must be disclosed in FDD Item 20 and in SEC filings for public franchisors. The closure rate (closures / beginning units = 6.3% in this example) is a critical franchisee health indicator. High closure rates indicate systemic franchisee distress and are closely scrutinized by prospective franchisees and investors.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Maintain an authoritative unit count database updated in real time as units open and close. The FDD Item 20 tables require 3 years of unit count history by state. For SEC-reporting franchisors, quarterly unit count updates are required in 10-Q and 10-K filings. Reconcile unit count to royalty billing — every open unit should be on the royalty billing roster.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify the unit count roll-forward against actual opening and closing documentation. Units that are open but not on the royalty billing register (royalty waiver recipients, default situations) must be explained. Disconnection between unit count and royalty revenue trends is a red flag.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Unit count database with opening and closing dates by unit, FDD Item 20 tables (3 years), SEC quarterly and annual unit count disclosures, reconciliation of unit count to royalty billing roster.

title: Budgetary Entry - Annual Appropriation & Estimated Revenue Recording
description: Recording the legally adopted annual budget as a memorandum budgetary control entry in the General Fund, establishing estimated revenues and appropriations at the start of the fiscal year.
explanation: This is one of the most fundamental and government-specific entries that does not exist in private sector accounting. When the legislative body (city council, county board, legislature) legally adopts the annual budget, the governmental unit records a formal budgetary entry in the general ledger as a memorandum control mechanism. Estimated Revenues is debited (the expected inflows), Appropriations is credited (the legal spending authority), and the difference is the projected surplus or deficit to Budgetary Fund Balance. This entry has NO equivalent in GAAP-based commercial accounting.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Most government ERP systems (Tyler Munis, Oracle Public Sector, SAP Public Sector, Infor CloudSuite Government) have a separate budgetary ledger module that maintains these control accounts alongside the GAAP ledger. Budget vs. actual reporting is a core function. Appropriations are typically loaded by department, fund, and object code level. The budget entry is reversed at year-end as part of the closing process.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Government auditors (state auditors, OIG, independent CPAs performing GAGAS audits) verify that the budget was legally adopted by the governing body before the fiscal year began. Expenditures exceeding appropriations (over-expenditures by department) are legal violations in most states, not just accounting findings. Auditors also test that the budgetary comparison statement agrees to the adopted budget.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Adopted budget ordinance or resolution signed by governing body, budget document by fund/department/object, budget amendment records, legal authorization for any mid-year budget changes.

title: Budgetary Entry - Mid-Year Budget Amendment (Supplemental Appropriation)
description: Recording a mid-year budget amendment approved by the governing body to increase appropriations for an unanticipated expenditure or to appropriate a grant award received after the original budget adoption.
explanation: When a government receives an unexpected grant or needs additional spending authority mid-year, the governing body must formally amend the budget. The amendment is recorded as an additional budgetary entry adjusting Estimated Revenues upward (if funded by new revenue) and Appropriations upward (new spending authority). In most states, it is unlawful to expend funds without an appropriation — even for emergency purposes — without legislative approval.

practitioner_notes.erp_application: Budget amendment workflow in government ERP systems typically requires departmental request, finance department review, and governing body approval before the amendment is posted. Maintain a log of all budget amendments with the date adopted, ordinance/resolution number, amount, and justification. The budgetary comparison schedule must reflect the FINAL amended budget, not the original.
practitioner_notes.audit_triggers: Auditors verify that all mid-year budget amendments were properly approved by the governing body. Expenditures in excess of original appropriations that were not amended are a finding. GASB 34 requires the budgetary comparison schedule to show original budget, final amended budget, and actual results — missing the 'original' column is a common deficiency.
practitioner_notes.required_documentation: Amendment ordinance or resolution adopted by governing body with vote count, staff memo justifying amendment, original and amended budget comparison, grant award letter (if grant-funded).

title: Encumbrance - Purchase Order Issuance (Commitment of Appropriation)
description: Recording an encumbrance when a purchase order is issued to a vendor, reserving the appropriation balance to ensure funds are not overspent before invoices are received.
explanation: Encumbrance accounting is a government-specific control mechanism with no private sector equivalent. When a purchase order is issued, the estimated cost is 'encumbered' — immediately deducted from the available appropriation balance — even though the vendor hasn't yet delivered and no invoice exists. This prevents departments from over-committing their budgets. The encumbrance is NOT an expenditure or liability under GAAP — it is a budgetary reservation.
